comment: All anchors in great condition. Pools were no more than chest deep, with most being about knee deep. We did 150, Matkat, Panameta, and Olo in a 4 day trip, each day quite long except the day hiking out where we finished around 3pm. Brought 130' webbing and 18 rap rings. Ended up only needing to build one anchor at the first drop into Olo, using 30' of webbing. Everything else was good to go, though I expect that can change pretty quickly.

I'll give it five stars, as the main section of slot - which only lasts maybe 30 minutes - is an absolutely stunning white hallway. But, be prepared to work for it! The approach, and much of the canyon itself, is constantly uneven boulders and awkward climbs and pack passes. Nice reward, but it's a lot of work, like most Grand Canyoneering routes. Anchors were fine. Pools filled, waist-deep at most. You can avoid ever going deeper than mid-thigh with careful stemming. We went down with wetsuits, but ascended up without them after realizing none of the pools are swimmers. It was 70F air temps most of our trip and we were fine. Five days total, including going up and doing Makatamiba.

Did in combination with Panameta. Beautiful narrows just go on and on! The scale in insane! Great life in there too Takes a lot of time to hoist packs. Didn't really need wetsuits this time of year, but they could be nice if you get cold easily
didn't find any webbing so we installed an anchor RDC. pull was fine but the rock was sharp and rubbing a bit on the webbing. unremarkable canyon but cathedral is nice.

This route was short but fun with amazing scenery. The approach was a walk in the park. We quickly found the one and only rap RDC off of two natural arches. The webbing was dry rotted so we replaced it. The rap is fun and has a great view of the river. I wish there was more to it. We boulder hopped and followed the fisherman's trail down river to Cathedral wash where we began the exit hike out. The exit made the route worth it. Highly recommend checking out Cathedral wash! Round trip time was 2.5 hours. We parked at the balance rock pull off which made the approach shorter but the exit longer. We were thinking of doing a longer route nearby but it was very hot so we opted to bust this one out and then drive up to East of State Line and do that immediately after. Definitely not our favorite canyon route, but we had a great time and we enjoyed it. I would do it again.

Just one rappel, but the combination of 2.8 Mile Wash and Cathedral Wash makes for a great half day. The rappel is stunning, the walk along the Colorado is beautiful, and Cathedral Wash is majestic as always. Marble Canyon always delivers.

Canyon was completely dry. Rigged from the largest natural arch. Scenic area and hiking Cathedral is always enjoyable. Wore helmets on the exit as high winds were throwing sand and small rocks into the canyon. Sunglasses were essentially useless against the sand so looking forward to the eye infection later.

Parked one car at the Cathedral Wash TH and shuttled the other to the coordinates given for parking. Fast and easy approach after parking at the start of the wash, which is large enough for two cars. 160ft rappel is beautiful, great view of the Colorado. Pre-existing webbing was solid. Easy social trail to follow to Cathedral and out.

Used this to access Papago Creek camp via Tanner Trail. Butchart mentions a non-technical route through the Redwall but nothing looked inviting. We made 1 rappel of roughly 180' in the Redwall along with several sporty down climbs. Nothing particularly awe-inspiring but was more fun than the Escalante Route.

Great canyon, a big entrance rappel, a good narrows section, and sweeping views from the final sequence. Most drops are bolted or have pitons. Many smaller drops can be downclimbed. This canyon could benefit from a few removed bolts and removal of the large cable in the narrows. Other than that, very pretty.

This is a really great route with lots of memorable features! We drove the dirt trail to the canyon head and camped. Nice and secluded. Have good route finding skills with Gaia to get here. There are no mapped trails and alot of slick rock areas where you can easily loose your way. Rough and rocky. Stock 4wd is fine. Canyon starts with a lovely 200’ wall! There are some neat stemming sections to stay dry above pools. One unavoidable pool we floated across in a packraft. Some really neat rooms. I removed that ugly wire ladder some misguided fool installed... Beautiful big keeper potholes that you wouldnt dare to jump into (avoidable). Neat alcoves and fun down-climbable sculpted cracks. It’s all over too quickly as the river comes into sight. Canyon took is 2hrs to beach. Water level low and we found a stance we could inflate on. In the past in high water levels we had to hike up stream a ways to a beach near the “descending sheep wall” petroglyphs. Took is an hour to get gear situated for the river. Paddle out took 3hrs. More beautiful walls!! River moving quick. 4/3 wetsuits were warm.

Swam the unavoidable pool, pleasant in the heat. Pitons solid, hybrid glue/expansion bolt pair at a short drop stuck the rope but not badly, we freed it with no issues elsewhere. Good route to use retrievable anchor systems to keep grooves from developing. 3 hours on the river.

A sweet rappel that ends in a beautiful basalt amphitheater. There appears to be a narrow section above the falls that looks worthy of exploration! The rap is out of the water and is possible to get off rappel near the rocks DCL without getting too wet. The water is shallow where the drop ends - do not jump/let go of the rope at any height. The approach and exit are fairly obvious and have hand lines.

1 rappel, right off a super short easy trail. Maybe a mile, but it felt shorter. The rappel is a spectacular waterfall into an amphitheater. Truly a wonderful fall, but nothing else. Do not leave webbing or anchors material, please.

Arrived just long enough before sunset to scope out the bottom of the falls and then find the path to the top anchor point. By the time I was on rope and headed down it was just after dark. Nice easy waterfall that can be knocked out without too much hassle.

Had heard rumors awhile back that there might be some more good canyon to see above the big falls on Abiqua... and the rumors did not lie. We found a couple of short rappels, some beautiful rock formations, some great jumps, and a dark slot. A great little canyon! Removed an obnoxious bright yellow sling from the top of the big falls. This is a high tourist area, so please do not leave fixed anchors. If you're uncomfortable with ghosting, it's not hard to hike back to the top of the falls to remove the anchor afterwards. (Or just tie your rope to the tree.) Thanks!
Returning to Abiqua for a chill relaxing day. Saw no one above the main falls. Confirmed the upper two drops can be jumped. Found a better way in via the upper trail.

The road down was slick, we parked just above the switchback. It was raining most of the day and the entrance/ exit trails were very slick. There are no established anchors here but there are plenty of options. You can chose to be in the flow or out of the flow except on R3. I didn't see a good option for running the flow also we didn't want to. the R1 and R2 can be walked around and run more than once, we practiced running it in the flow, out of the flow and jumping! Great place to get some practice and build skills. We used a friction loop for all the anchors. The dark slot was awesome, there was a small log underwater just after the giant root ball above your head. This could present and issue at higher flows. We didn't see any other people! thank you rain!

What a great creek! Yeah it is short, but the pay to play proposition is quite high. Gorgeous slot canyon, fascinating bedrock, stunning deep pools to play in. Measured and updated height of R1, R2, and jumping pool. R3 height was spot on. Beware - R3 drops you on shallow boulders. Water is not deep right at bottom of this rappel. With some care & attention, we were able to navigate the gnargnar switchback in a 2010 Suby Tribeca. Any AWD with 9+ in clearance, carefully driven, should be able to navigate the switchback both down and back up, as of Aug 2025.

Jumped all pools, depth was good and pools clear of debris. The dark slow root ball is gone and only a bit of wood remains towards the end of the slot. Final falls is amazing, tree keeps you way out of the flow sadly but still a beautiful place to drop in and admire! Had I known the pools and rock formations were that cool I would have done this a lot sooner! FYI road still sux…a minivan was stuck when we’re leaving.

Fantastic day. First time in this canyon, will be back soon. We felt like final rap was more like 110-115', based on our rope length and pull cord. Lots of people hanging around at the base, but no one swimming...not sure why. The 'trees' that make up the anchor for R2 are not awesome. Strength in numbers, I suppose.

First known descent! Very high clearance and 4x4 required, with good tires. This would probably be one of the most popular canyons in Arizona if it was easier to access. Set webbing on first 3 or 4 rappels, but rest were done with fiddlestick. Number of fun downclimbs, cool rabbit hole tunnels, in scenic area away from everything. Only for the truest of adventurers. You are welcome, Arizona.

Second known descent!! Fantastically beautiful canyon with a fun 4x4 trail to and from. My lifted Xterra Pro4x on 33" tires had no issues getting there. 2wd and low clearance 4x4 not recommended, need some height for a few obstacles. One waist deep pool that is un avoidable but no wetsuit needed. We updated a few anchors and reflected our changes on beta. Still ghosted a few spots that didnt have webbing.

Mostly dry, with a few ankle to mid-thigh deep pools. We replaced most of the webbing. On rap 6 we went into the rabbit hole and rappelled off a pinch point, about 10 feet. The odds of sticking a rope on the first rappel is very high. I'd recommend splitting it into 2 raps, with farmed rocks for a deadman in the hallway. It has a lot of rappels, which was nice and it was very scenic on the drive. We didn't see another vehicle anywhere out there due to the remoteness. As the description says, you're going to need a capable vehicle and an experienced technical driver with a crew of patient people who don't mind bouncing around for several hours each way. There is cell coverage, but it wouldn't be an easy place for AAA to give roadside assistance. LOL.

Rugged, remote and scenic. We drove in via the South trail from Young. These trails are rough and rocky. I believe a stock high clearance midsize 4x4 with aired down offroad tires would be fine. 4Lo and careful driving needed in a few spots. Both of our trucks (Frontier and 4runner) are lifted and did fine. Trails are too tight for a fullsize truck. Drove past the “car park” way-point all the way up to the cow tank up top and camped. Drive in took 1.5hrs on dirt. A 20min hike brought us to the first rappel. Water seeping and dripping in canyon. Very pretty! One unavoidable knee deep pool. We went down to the Tonto River and hungout down there exploring along the river for 45mins. Beautiful rock all different colors. The river was probably the best part of the route, would be great swimming if it was hot out. Exit out has lots of scrambling and loose rock but pretty easy to follow the game trails. Seemed like we were back to our trucks in no time! Total: 4.5hrs I drove over to camp at the Hell’s Boundary approach and ran that the next day, drove out via the North trail which was less rocky but not as interesting, still took 1.5hrs

2nd documented trip through this canyon. Added several bolts to the canyon for the main drops. Clear water, unusual for the glacier water as it is usually milky. Very cold. Checked out the bonus mini-slot down below as well. Great day and gorgeous canyon, albeit short.
We scouted out Adams gorge and it’s still flowing WAY too extreme to be safely runnable by my crew.

This canyon is short, but burly and every bit of a5. We rappelled in from two spots along the rim to scout the crux from above and below. It is an amazing part of the slot where the creek narrows, dramatically curves, and drops quickly, forming a chain of three incredible and intimidating waterfalls. The creek was running higher than we originally expected and the first two potholes were pure whitewater. We spent time scouting and planning the best approach before dropping in. We entered higher than the R1 drop-in and found ourselves stranded above the R1 falls without an anchor. Oops. We escaped up a steep slope DCR and rappelled back into the creek at the next drop below ‘chockstone falls’. The creek was pushy but not too eventful until the crux rappel. There were advanced swiftwater challenges as well as rope management challenges from linking the three pools that we had to manage through. Anchors were in great condition. There is a nasty siphon about 20 yards downstream from the last pool of R3 where the entire creek pinches and dives underneath a boulder wedged in a narrow slot (see pic attached). Avoid the siphon by climbing out of the previous pool DCL and stemming over the slot. Beta has been updated. All in all, it was a full value day in a short slice of Adams Creek!

Canyon was great. Flows were a bit high but manageable. Flows also rose throughout the day so an early start is a good idea. Sieve area is easier as a log has jammed into it and you can walk by it. Trust zero areas that seem like jumpers. Just below the “boiler room” is a pool that has been filled in with a recent rock fall. All bolts are great.

Great day in Adams. Even at moderate flow it would be challenging for some folks. The first pothole swim can be overwhelming and it would be useful to have an emergency line ready for folks. Bolts and anchors are great. We pulled the rope for the boiler room while in the second pothole and used the large boulder to anchor off of for the last 10 foot rap. Do not jump these as they are very shallow and filled with jagged boulders. We did do the last slotty section further down stream. This could be ran multiple times and great for beginners to feel hydraulics in a safe manner.

Ran Adams. R2 has lost a log and the chockstone moves with ease. We need to either create a traverse to the anchor station or come up with a new anchor station. All bolts were solid. Added a stray log to help with the strainer.

The chockstone now has a fracture and could give way at any point. The rap from this feature needs serious attention. Aka DO NOT CASUALLY ROLL UP AND ATTEMPT. we placed a bolt DCR at the chockstone but one more needs set further down. We escaped after this feature as a team member was above for such an event.

Flow the previous night was Very High. Flow in the morning was High. First we descended a fixed line to the bottom of R2 to assess. Rappeling on DCR was not possible - you would get swept off your feet and have a fast and deep descent into the boil. DCL was a better line, you could stay away from the firehose, stand on a ledge near the surface, jump halfway across the boil, and likely get propelled to the pourover for a safe exit. The boil was very deep. Ascended and went down to look at R3. There is a scramble down just before the canyon makes a 90 degree right hand turn. First tier looked reasonable on DCR; 2nd tier also DCR probably fine but not visible. After 3rd tier there was what appeared to be a new strainer. A fresh log. All water was funneled DCL into a 2 foot wide laminar blast into the log's root ball. Very dangerous, potentially fatal, strainer. You would be on rope to complicate matters. We opted to skip R3 and head back to do R2. R1 was not an issue. The drop in canyon was into a boil in shallow water. Very cold. The Ralstone at R2 was precariously wobbly, more than we expected. Due to the roiling boil, we decided ziplining packs and any rope bags was a must. After gingerly testing the Ralstone, we were spooked and decided to live another day. Did not want it to land on top of someone when it fails, or get pinched like Aaron Ralson in 127 hours. You would not get 127 hours here, you would have about 60 seconds in the cold flow. If the flow was moderate, there may be the possibility to rappel from DCR and avoid the chockstone hazard and also get off rappel in a favorable outwash direction, as the circulation is CCW. R3 would also much more reasonable to attempt at half the flow. Need cooler temps in October, even in this very low year.

We re-equipped the middle section of this canyon that other users had identified as a problem. R1 is the same. R2 has now been split into 2 rappels. We utilized the bolt that had previously been placed DCR, to place a higher bolt, well out of the flow, that leads into a 10 ft in length. This avoids any contact with the “Ralstone” which is still wedged in the slot, very unstable. A brand new station has been placed DCL for the previous R2 (which is now R3). This station is in line with the previous bolts (DCL), with a solid stance for the anchor manager, and allows for line-of-sight for the entirety of the rappel. The original DCL, R2 bolts have been removed. Between this feature, and the original R3, there is nothing to report. We did not go into the boiler room due to increasing flows. The bolts are in good shape at this station. After a run through to re-equip, we ran the canyon until the final sequence. All is ready to go once flows are a touch lower. Future parties could consider placing a bolt for the R1 drop, and begin the canyon 200 feet higher to spend more time in this beautiful slot.

We did not run the canyon. The creek was observed to be extremely low the day before the attempt. We saw it on the previous day at 10:30. By the time we showed up the following day at 8 AM the flow had more than quadrupled. Thinking that we could probably deal with that flow. We suited it up and hike down and watch the creek continue to rise. By the time we made it back to the car, we saw the flow more than doubled in three hours. If you are going to attempt this Canyon, it’s best to observe it from the bridge and when you know, temperatures are going to be very low. Probably best to make it your back up and check it before you run or whatever you were going to run initially.

Sent Adams. Added a bolt DCR to break up the rap so that you can make it to the bottom with 50… need to scramble left to make that work. New bolts at R2 are nice. Log in pool was firm and used it to get to new station DCL. Log in the bottom of R2 wasn’t in play. Boiler room lived up to its name. The second pool was filled with breakdown 3 years ago but that all seemed to clear. Added bolt DCL for last pool entry. Last pool is not deep. The pour over is real. It was surging while we were in there. Sieve at the end is still there and we added a log above to assist. Fun day. Even these flows were pushy so if the level at the bridge is anymore than what you see in the picture then maybe take a pass. In canyon time was 2.25 hours. Only brought one pack and 33 meter ropes.
Easy approach. Even easier exit. Two fun raps with flow into a pool, one could be done as a slide or jump. Many opportunities for more slides and water fun. Beautiful scenery.

Time reflects Agua Caliente and La Milagrosa combo - car to car. Jumped into the first pool. Did two rappels of about 20 and 30 feet. Pools were full with a light trickle. Multiple swimmers. High winds today made for some cold swims. Rock was very slick. Mostly nontechnical canyon, but scenic.
The approach is very hot this time of year, but it's doable. A nice and quick little canyon near Tucson.

Combined with La Milagrosa and never got wet in this one. Schwacked up the hill on canyoneers left exiting LM to combine and hated every second of it. Unsure if there is a better way. Did last rappel of canyon on canyoneer's left from a tree to stay dry.

Trickle had the first two pools clean, the third (second swimmer) was algae filled and will probably stay that way between rainstorms. Swimming LDC will land you on rocks pretty quickly, cuts the swim in half. Time reflects a ropestick on R3, silly of me but so it goes, watch the rock pinch and easier LDC, had to rush home for a lunch, otherwise would be a great combo for La Milagrosa.
Light flow, mostly trickle. Dry at all anchors. Water cold but felt comfy in a wetsuit. All webbing in good shape. Be careful on the last rope pull.
Zero flow. First pool is swimmer but 6 ft lower water level than normal, dead bird floating in water. Last pool waist-deep, nasty slop wading. Rest of canyon completely dry

Heavy, very cold water flow for this canyon. You take a real pounding on the first rappel, the second is easy to stay out of, the third is heavy, but not as intense as the first. Lower canyon is very brushy/overgrown with vines and briars, dead tree fall littering the canyon floor everywhere; very slow. There was still a few inches of snow in the higher elevations. No dangerous hydraulic issues. The 3 creek crossings on the drive were flowing pretty good - probably about a foot deep at the most.
Nice narrows. Road is in good condition now, 2WD can make it all the way to the start of the narrows

Road was dry and easily navigated with high clearance. 4WD not necessary, but would be helpful for one section. The road crosses multiple washes and would likely be impassable after heavy rains. The narrow walls ramp aggressively on both sides creating a runway for loose rock. There was ample evidence of fresh rockfall in the slot - proceed with caution.

Short but fun. There was knee deep water in two spots 200ft into the narrows which was easily bypassed. Took ~1h car to car while brisk walking, with car parked at the 2wd parking spot. A Subaru will have no problems at all.
Two mandatory swimmer pools. Very low bushwacking, easy approach and exit, and nice narrow areas. Fun problem solving downclimbs in boulder areas.

Nearly a dozen wasps were hovering around the anchor for R1. Set a 40’ rap off a tree a bit farther RDC that dropped me into the narrows just before the second swimmer. Downclimbed the next rap and packed out some old webbing. A couple swimmers, a few deep wades, and some opportunities for fun, moderately challenging downclimbs. Water levels appeared low and lots of algae in some pools.

Second anchor was torn out. Downclimb-able but not everyone was comfortable. We replaced it but the rope pull sucks so last person downclimbed anyways. Water was clear and chilly with a layer of algae on top. I wore 3mm wetsuit and was happy, party members did not wear wetsuits and were chilly but fine.

We didn't want to get went and skipped the rap at the beginning of narrows, there is another anchor further down RDC but there was another deep pool so we continued and CEMed of another tree. Didn't help us much, few minutes later there was a waist deep wader and second rap was straight into very cold swimmer.Once out of the water it was very pleasant. If you are into canyon hiking this one is wonderful, real canyon with high, enclosing walls, fun downclimbes and huge boulders forming corridors and even tunnels.

Webbing/anchor at the first rappel was in fine shape. One small deceased mouse in the pool below. Downclimbed R2. A couple refreshing swimmers in not-too-gross water. Spent a lot of time exploring the boulder caves and rescued a garter snake that was trapped in a small but deep pothole. This is certainly one of the more worthwhile and interesting canyons in the area.
Replaced webbing at R1. Multiple swims and several wades. Light flow. 4/3mm wetsuit was comfortable.
Canyon is still in great condition and holding lots of cold water.

Trickle of flow throughout the canyon. Deep cold pools, short swims, unique features. Low effort for some solitude and a unique little canyon. We made a day out of it and paddled into another canyon close by afterwards. All anchors looked good.

Caught this one at the wrong time of year. The pools were very cold, stagnant and pretty gross, with rodents floating in them amongst other things. However, the canyon itself is beautiful and lots of fun. It's likely best done in high flow.

10 years ago, in a warmer, drier season, we didn't enjoy this much. This time, after a lot of recent rain, it was completely different. Catch it wet like this, and it's a solid 4-star experience, given the ease of access and beauty down there.

Hidden gem in the desert, we played with different rappel styles and were able to do 6 rappels in total, low flow with clear beautiful water, gorgeous canyon, we missed the exit and took an unexpected detour out

Great pay to play ratio with this canyon. There were three deep pools, one wade and two full swims, and everything else was less than knee deep and easily avoidable. The variety in this canyon is nice, with some narrows up front, easy yet engaging downclimbs throughout, and some cool "caves" to keep it interesting.

Short but fun canyon. Some thigh-deep pools. Some swimmers. The swimmers were pretty cold and we were glad we had the wetsuits. Otherwise, didn't really need them. R3 appears to have been re-routed through rabbit hole DCR in the last couple years. Better anchor but a tighter fit for those less nimble.
Great time, ran right after a large rain storm and had fun flow. All anchors are in good shape

Hiked in from less busy, (much farther drive) east side - nice fall colors, wading less that knee deep at most - ankle deep mostly. Can stay out of the creek on use trails some of the time, but you must hike directly in the creek a lot and you are constantly crossing - neoprene socks can help. Parking at Turkey Creek trailhead cuts off 1.5 miles of road hiking one way, the only reason I can see for parking at the Aravaipa trailhead is if Turkey Creek lot is full. Many creek crossings on the drive, but not terribly deep. No passenger cars. Used one of 2 nice camp sites just east of Deer Creek as a base camp to hike further west on day trips. Very pleasant hiking.

Backpacked Aravaipa as a 3-day loop from the west trailhead, ascending the old track along the southern boundary of the wilderness and dropping into Turkey Canyon. While a non-technical canyon, Aravaipa is super-scenic and teeming with wildlife. A ringtail came to visit us in camp one evening. Water levels were usually about ankle-deep with occasional knee-deep spots to contend with. There are often trails on one side or the other, but in some areas you'll be forced to hike in the creek. Recommend boots with neoprene socks. Long pants per bushwhacking. A really beautiful canyon. BLM permits required.

Ascended old road along south boundary of the Aravaipa Wilderness. Camped one night at Parson's Grove (water carry required). Day 2: down into Turkey Canyon and camped at mouth of Deer/Hellhole Canyon. Day 3: out. A great loop route, allowing a one-way through-hike of the main canyon. Water ankle to calf-deep. No issues.
First descent? Surprisingly nice little creek. Did 10-12 rappels, plus numerous downclimbs. Worthwhile early season objective to get back in the game. 1hr up; 6hrs in creek.
Stopped by to check out flow rate, but ran Archer West instead.

Road, surprisingly, has been plowed up to the top so we were able to shuttle. Road in good shape, but watch for blind corners on the way up. Started hiking in the snow, but quickly dropped out of the snowline. Dropped in at the right and the going was good for about the first half. The lower half of the canyon has experienced major tree carnage, however, after the recent winter snow-pocalypse event and there's debris everywhere which takes Archer down several notches on the "fun" scale. Might want to give the creek a few years to clean out before attempting it again. Other than that temps (air & water) were perfect. Enough flow to be enjoyable, but beginner-friendly.

Great water today -- the upper falls were nice & splashy. Unfortunately, the creek degenerates past the giant old growth stump and becomes a woody mess at the bottom. Need to get a chainsaw in there to take them out. Would be a marvelous creek, if not for the blow-down.

Took my dad and brother down this canyon while everything else was in high flow. Fairly mellow rappels with straight forward anchors. There was a lot of salmon berry and devil's club to push through and logs to crawl over. On a hot day the wet suit would be needed more to protect you from bushes than keep you warm. Fun for once canyon.

Archers Creek is a masochist's dream right now. There is so much blow down, log jams and brush that it is a constant slog without out much fun. Several of the rappels are now downclimbs through log jams. We think we bypassed the big 120 foot rappel at R6 because it was just a constant stack of log jams with no discernable safe rappel. We scrabbled DCL along a vague game trail to get around it. The anchors were all still good and we added a couple of out own. Overall, although it's always good to get out and canyon, this one is not worth it. It's just too much work fighting the log jams and brush. I'm rather beat up after this one.

Possible first descent? Scoping out a new drainage near Archer that seemed like it had potential. Not an amazing creek, but a nice one. Some cool bedrock and interesting falls, although probably not destined to become a classic. Archer prime sports more drops.
We went on Thanksgiving day and found that the lower pool was at 101F and the upper pool reached 110F! The warmth was welcome after a cold pack raft (3 miles) from Secret Canyon.

The hot springs are wonderful and have several different pools with varying temperatures. It's about an hour hike (give or take) from the parking lot with two main trails to and from. If you're trying to get there quick, stick to the right and go through the hills. My preference is sticking to the left and descending down the canyon, which is very pretty. No ropes needed, just careful downclimbing. The only downside to this canyon is how busy it is. During the weekend during optimal conditions, you'll be sitting in the springs with 20-30 people during the day/evening. After midnight it calms down quite a bit and you might be able to enjoy it in solitude. It's not uncommon to encounter nudists, but I've never had a problem with them. Last time I went, there was a family who brought a loud speaker and strobe lights to the springs. Please do not be this person, nobody wants to hear it.

Great technical canyon when done in high flow! Slept at the campground the night prior to get used to the altitude. On the first rappel into the dark chasm, we added a webbing anchor DCR around a boulder to avoid the flow. The anchor 40 ft down from that rappel was used as a redirect. In high flow, this rappel is the most technical and intimidating due to avoiding the rabbit hole. It is possible to skirt around the rabbit hole DCR to climb up on the boulder. Be sure to use class c techniques when running this class in high flow. A whistle can be heard on most drops despite the roar of the water.The hike out sucked...really sucked. Approach hike 2 hours (we stopped several times to evaluate waterflow) Technical section 4 hours Hike out 3 hours (strong hikers can finish this probably in 2 hours;I slowed the group down considerably :P)

The Dark Chasm is very deserving of its name and is the most technically challenging part in high flow. When we did it, we had to build a new anchor because the standard canyon left tree route would have taken us directly into the flow which was too intense. The canyon was very cold and I wore two 4/3mm wetsuits and was still a little chilled. Luca and Tiffanie wore 4/3's and were cold but comfortable after the Dark Chasm section. Hike out reminds me of the MIA exit. It's long and steep will easily take groups 2-4 hours depending on hiking strength.

Probably the best and most technically challenging class C canyon in Arizona. Lots of down logs on approach and exit due to recent fire, but is now reopened. Dark Chasm is the real deal, technically challenging, very difficult to communicate. Solid class C experience needed. 15' foot meat anchor had fallen tree at time we did it that was easy for everyone to downclimb. Dark Chasm did 70' rappel off of chockstone to VW size chokestone below. Anchor material at VW chokestone was low, would be very difficult to setup anchor under high flow. From VW chokestone, last person down must watch rope placement. Pull is difficult, moderate chance of rope getting stuck. Easy going after Dark Chasm with a couple more fun waterfall rappels before the hike out. Setup all new bomber anchors on all the rappels. Only one anchor on one rappel after Dark Chasm existed. You are welcome Arizona.

Beautiful and unique Class C canyon nestled in the cool high mountain forest of Mt Graham! We camped at the campground both nights which was incredibly convenient! Following Ash Creek Trail down is easy and fairly obvious with lots of log hopping over fallen trees. Easy to stay out of the creek bed and walk along the trail on the side up until you must turn off. Couple of old mill relics scattered here and there, very cool! The forest and meadows made for very enjoyable scenery! Slick rock section was very slick but pretty! Once at the top of the "Dark Chasm" we deliberated for a bit about how to best handle it; trying to make sense of the confusing and slightly inaccurate old beta. Once in the Chasm, we found it to be fairly straight forward. Had to build an anchor at R3 in water course. The rap sequence was smooth and tons of fun!! A total gem!! The "Dark Chasm" is a narrow slot with multiple small ledges but wasn't really all that "dark". It is dimly lit, but there is enough light from above to see what you are doing. The other rappels were pretty fun too but the Chasm was definitely the best part! I wore a 4/3 wetsuit and rain jacket. I was plenty comfortable, water was not as cold as I thought it'd be. On the exit, we followed the hiking trails as it was mapped out on the GPS file. About 1/2 way up the first drainage the trail fizzles out so we cut over to the right on a less bushy line to pick up the ridge trail above. Following the ridge trail for awhile eventually takes you to a spot where GPS file has you take the switchback up to the right instead of re-joining the slick rock watercourse you took on the approach. We again followed the GPS but this seemed to take us up and out of the way, gaining an unnecessary amount of elevation when we could have probably shaved some time off by re-tracing the approach trail at this point. Much further down, after following faint remnants of trail we eventually re-joined the approach trail as per the GPS track. We definitely enjoyed this one! The combination of big cascading water falls, the "Dark Chasm", intricate rock, and beautiful lush forest scenery with easy camping made this a memorable trip! Beta has been updated!

We changed the location of the 1st dark chasm anchor to a small chockstone RDC and above the large chockstone that the webbing previously draped over. The new one goes down a rabbit hole and provides a cleaner pull. It's challenging to lower yourself down to weight it though, so some may want courtesy rigging. Unfortunately, it didn't help us avoid a stuck rope. After pulling the rope through the rap ring (from the mid-chasm anchor at the next big chockstone) the end fell down and got thoroughly stuck up in the first pool. We eventually cut it and continued down. The difficult geometry of the chasm makes it a good candidate for some thoughtful bolting. Flow was fun but not very intense. We didn't find an anchor at the drop right after the chasm but installed a bomber knot chock. The exit up to the ridge trail kinda sucks. We scrambled up a drainage most of the way then bushwhacked the rest. We cut over to the Ash drainage right above the slickrock rather than following the kml track up further, which I think was a good call. All in all it's very beautiful but a lot of work.

Overall I enjoyed the canyon, here are some take aways: Water flow was listed as moderate to low, which is about as high as I would want to rapell through this canyon. The raps are technical which made them fun for our team, but in high flow I think they would be a little much. Wetsuits, 3 of us had on 3mm, and 1 person had a 5 mm. Spent a little extra time in the chasm solving a problem, and that was the only time anyone was at all cold. The canyon/approach was super slippery. Rocks that seem stable at first were giving out. Exit-- don't do what we did. We decided to follow trail 307 down hill, and do a shuttle. There is no trail 307 right now, we thought it would get better but it kept getting worse, until it got dangerous. We ended up sleeping on rocks, it took us about 24 hours to move four miles, and without ropes we would of been screwed. Ended up doing 4 raps to get down on a "hiking" trail.

I created an account to write this review because I wanted to share trail conditions with folks. First off, we had it easy; the water was still flowing but very low compared with any photos that I’ve seen. Part of why we chose this canyon was that it looked like a loop almost all on trails. How very appealing! This area faced some of the most extreme areas of fire damage that I saw on the mountain. Entire slopes were barren of trees. The switchbacks bringing you from the end of the canyon to the main trail are gone. Even the benching is gone. We occasionally found the old trail and then lost it within a few hundred feet. We spoke to a volunteer at the visitor center and she said that there are zero trail crew employees and that this trail will never be brought back. Additionally, we felt fortunate; she explained to us that about a year ago a volunteer brushed trail 307 and so we took this trail. Being familiar with ecosystems after intense burns, I would not expect this trailwork to hold back the spiny brush for more than another 2 or 3 years. Expect the trails to take significantly longer than you’d expect from a maintained trail.

Fantastic class-C route, if you're up for the exit and (for most) long drive. Despite the fire damage, the area is stunningly subalpine for AZ. Beautiful canyon, and the dark chasm bit is both rewarding and unusually class-C challenging for AZ. Suggestions for a safe, fun outing, based on our experience: 1. Dark chasm - talk the whole sequence through before going in. Absolutely break out radios. You'll want to communicate. There's no line of sight through a lot of it, and it's loud enough to drown out whistles. Our 5W Rocky Talkies on full blast were barely audible over the noise. We mixed up the rap numbers in the chasm, and I rapped past R3 station. Had to convert to ascend back up and over the chockstone in flow. Memorable... 2. The granite is often perilously slick near the watercourse, especially where overgrown with algae (which is abundant), and often in places where a slip could be mortal. Traditional feet-on-wall rappelling is often near-impossible in flow lines, so we often ragdoll rapped those parts. 3. Water was cold enough to feel like rocks coming down on my head when in flow. Wish I'd had my rainjacket on. 4. Take plenty of webbing. We found anchors everywhere expected. Most were well-placed but with seriously bleached and/or frayed webbing. 5. The initial exit is ugly. We entered the drainage south of the trail, and stayed in it all the way to where the drainage crossed the trail, thinking the trail must be an overgrown wreck. This was a mistake, and put us through the worst thrash either of us have ever traversed. While we can't speak for the trail below that point, what was above that gully was mildly overgrown but not bad at all. So on the exit, prioritize finding the trail. https://youtu.be/9XH8ys3l9bE?si=VS04gth-rAi4J6O6
The technical section was swarming with bees and mosquitoes. Nice, clear water in the pool - swimmer.
One swimmer pool, clear water. Interesting area and scenic views. Some class 4 hiking on approach. All anchors looked good. No rope issues.

rappel #1 has an exciting start cause the anchor hangs a bit over the lip (makes for a very easy pull). we sent one down through the pool (a swimmer and cold) and then down rappel #3 while on rope. this person then tensioned the rope and provided a guided "rappel" (just went on a carabiner) across the pool. she managed to stay dry! check the webbing on rappel #4. was starting to look a bit crispy.

It’s a crumbly, thorny thrash up, and a thrash between final rap and decent trail back. Still, some decent raps, and it’s close to town. There are worse ways to spend a winter day! https://youtu.be/g5u-hy4PyIw
Lots of poor quality rock in the approach gully. Pool at the base of R2 was mostly chest depth but required a short swim in the middle. Moved at a casual pace. Rugged approach and exit. Scenic area.

One mandatory swimmer. The rock quality is ok and the natural anchors were super good enough. We sent the heaviest person down first with a back up on most rappels. We had to extend a 100 and pass a knot on the ledge for the last rapp, no biggie. Super fun little outing and less commiting than some other options in the area. We found a ram skull, and a dead fox.

south fork with north exit: replaced webbing at a couple anchors. at the second rap we installed a new anchor another ~50 feet further down the ledge LDC from where we found webbing to avoid some muddy-looking areas. the limestone is pretty sharp over there so be aware of that. having done both the south and north exits, i definitely prefer the south. it's shorter and way less sketchy. that traverse back to the exit drainage above the cliffs on the north exit is scary! steep and unstable feeling, though i'll admit we never slid or anything. My vote is for the North fork for scenery, though both are beautiful.

Took the North fork from the road and really enjoyed it. Counted 4 rappels to date 3 in the north fork, and one in the continuation. We opted for the Souther exit #2. Exit beta on the page is all we needed and was spot on. We may have taken the ledge too far toward the river but went up and exposed section. Did not see a handline or bolt, but they may have been missed. Brought a set of climbing nuts which is all you would need to protect the top. I just climbed the short section then belayed my partner. The loose and steep terrain after the 5th class move and before the cliff band was the spot where we took most time navigating the choss. Potholes were waist deep in areas. Anchors in good condition.

We took the South fork and the North exit. Once dropping into the canyon the scenery was beautiful!! Intricate walls, overhung layered rock shelves and picturesque pools! All pools were avoidable. Lots of crusted over mud flats further down that, if not paying attention, you can walk into and find yourself waist deep in the chocolate milk hidden below as a few of our group did! Most of the raps were pleasantly over hung and very enjoyable despite being less than 100'. We took our time, did some ascending practice and hung out. Coming out to the Colorado River was awesome! Great chance to filter water and take a dip! We spent a bit of time enjoying the river before starting on the exit. The North exit was a long annoying rock hop along the shore in between sections of soft sand. The sun was beating down and there was little shade, luckily we could take dips in the still freezing river. The scree slope up to the cliff band was VERY loose and has lots of big rocks that moved easily, take care! Once up top the desert walk was long, monotonous, and had lots of soft sand that made hiking less than enjoyable. We did not leave a shuttle. I would do it again, go down and visit the river but would definitely leave ropes and ascend back out. The exit was not very enjoyable. In my opinion, the scenery was alright but not worth the hike. The canyon technical section is way more interesting and much prettier! Beta has been updated!

We did the loop from the river via the southern exit. The class-5 move had a webbing handline installed. All pools were avoidable, though the last rap required assistance from folks on the bottom to swing the person on rope clear of a mud puddle.

Descended the North fork and took the South exit. Light flow through the canyon and pools were all at or under waist depth. Deep mud throughout. Handline is in place rigged from a bolt to aid with the exit climb. Set a shuttle at the highway which after the relatively long and arduous hike through soft sand and against high winds (20-30mph) we all agreed was a great call.
All pools avoidable in the North Fork. We did the south entrance, and the webbing was in place and helpful.
All pools and mud avoidable. We fixed and ascended/hauled up, which added a fun bit of practice and learning.

North fork to river took about an hour and a half, webbing at all drops. A few are getting crispy but good enough for now. Canyon is interesting but not quite as sculpted as the short narrows in north soap. Inflated packraft and had a leisurely float down to soap. No raft groups came by, so had an excellent beach camp all to myself for the night. Next morning about 4 hours from soap beach to lunch at Cliffdwellers with a nap stop in the middle. Great solo loop.
North Fork quality is great, did 3 rappels to the river. Please don't add bolts, and keep the rappels clean and limit trash. Cool canyon.

We did the south route starting from the river. The handline on the approach is getting worn and needs replacing. Also be careful of loose rock, we had a close call with a ~20 lbs sized stone falling while passing packs.

Badger was mostly dry, except for a few easily avoidable pools. Packrafted from Badger to Soap and exited the South Fork. The upclimbs on the exit are all straightforward but were quite strenuous with heavy packs full of wet whitewater gear. Received some sun on the river but minimal light in both side canyons this time of year. Condition report time reflects the full loop, car-to-car.

Descended the North Fork via South entry point (North entry point looks to yield an additional 60’ drop that looks nice!) We fixed all our ropes, went down to the river then ascended back up and out. Fun quick day but I feel South Fork is slightly longer and more interesting. All water avoidable, minimal surface mud.

Time is car-to-car doing Bantam and Mini Hollow (MH) as a loop (up Bantam and down MH - this is recommended). Bantam was unremarkable, but I found MH to be interesting and enjoyed the final semi-deep narrows section. Pools of water throughout both canyons, was able to avoid with some stemming and a few full-length bridges. Still plenty of loose, crumbly rock present. Overall worthwhile for the unique slotted section in MH. 4x4 probably not necessary for the access wash unless saturated but high clearance could be helpful.

Low flow, cold water. Some residual ice above R1. Removed quite a bit of old webbing and rusted quicklinks. Many fun downclimbs. A few swimmers and several deep wades as well as a couple optional jumps and small slides. Only ended up doing two rappels. Very pretty and interesting geology throughout the canyon. Exited up Big Kahuna drainage to the trail.

FUN AND BEAUTIFUL. Low but fun flow. We did the route with 2 rappels, one of our team members bypassed every pool/ rappel besides r3 90'. Depending on experience and downclimb abilities of the team, you may want to being extra webbing. Take some of the old stuff with you. Wool socks under the neoprene socks for the win!

Plenty of cold water. "High" water for this canyon isn't that intense as it never really constricts all that much. Definitely a bit pushy at times but no other water hazards. We did 4 rappels. Found webbing at the first three and put in webbing on the last. Found a better exit, which I'll add to the page. Warm day, fun canyon.

Nice day out. Typical Mazzy action: colorful, sometimes slick, but solid quality rock, manzanita all over, and fsurprisingly cold and flowing water, given the amount of mountain above it. We did 6 raps, Downclimbed R1. Several that we rappelled were visibly downclimbable from below, but had looked difficult from above. Anchors good all the way. Barnhardt trail has been meticulously groomed. Only real slow going is the ravine from that trail to the start of the canyon. We exited up Kahuna where that creek comes in. Light whackage up 100’ and about 500’ horizontal was totally worth it to escape further creek walking for that good trail back.

Under 7 hours car to car using the early exit up Big Kahuna Canyon and back to the trail. That early exit really helps the pay-to-play ratio on this one. Long hike in, decently long time in canyon, short hike out. Water was at moderate flow which made for a super fun day without any true challenges. Fun downclimbs and water features throughout. Very cold water - I started the day thinking I might have been alright in a shorty wet suit (I normally run warm), and by the end of the day I was very glad to have brought the full 4/3.

Bearwater is fun. Lots of downclimbs with a few rappels and some creativity needed for rigging rappels for questionable downclimbs. Bearwater is an easy canyon with enough to keep a team interested. Nothing stood out as amazing, but it's a solid 2* canyon with the benefit of an old road exit that is much easier for the return than Bull Draw or Snake Drop. It makes a nice combo with Kingsnake for a full day out. The 4 hour time started from the parking, included bashing through the upper reaches of the drainage (not recommended) and stopping the clock at the top of Kingsnake.

Descended Bedrock and South over two days, exiting via the UPS route. Navigating the large boulders in the upper section of Bedrock was slow-going. A couple difficult downclimbs where a rope came in handy and some exposed moves on heavily eroded terrain. A few anchors were in place yet in poor condition and we did not use them.
Ran the canyon on the way home from kayaking in the area. Water was high but very manageable, making the narrows at the bottom very fun. The bushwhack in had quite a few overgrown thorny plants.

Thrashed up an overgrown logging grade along the west side of the creek and dropped in above the falls. Lots of woody debris in the creek - likely damage from winter storms - which causes Big Alder to lose some points. If more people were running it, would likely clean it out a bit. Once the rappels start they're rapidfire, culminating in Big Alder Falls and the short narrows which is the best bit. Fun way to spend a few hours if you're passing through the area. Probably more interesting with more water.

We did this canyon in the summer and it wasn’t tons of fun. We came back in the winter and it’s much more fun in the winter. Perfect training ground for higher water. The falls all had some push to them but there really aren’t any pools. On caution I. High water is you could get pushed off the last set of falls, just stay on rappel until you get to the anchor. Way more fun with more water. It’s a quick run! All anchors looked great. Last photo is the last set of bolts(in the water course in higher flows)

Great day! I love Big Alder for how many raps you get in a short run. Great for showing friends how to set up anchors. There’s a big log on R4 now that makes you either take the mossy slide or go directly in the flow. But no problems. All anchors look good. R6 had a lot of junk on the anchor, but we cleaned it out. Less than 2 hours car to car!

Day one 8 hours to camp near Salt Trail Canyon, hike out day 2 in 3-4 hours for the group. We found Salt Trail to be easy to follow and well marked. LCR was a beautiful, milky blue. There are a few deep pools in the canyon, but almost all were able to avoid wading more than waist deep with a few tricky moves. The hallway after the second rappel is quite deep, but it is possible to cling to the canyon right wall, then bridge across the remainder of the pool, but the moves are a little difficult. A Navajo ranger was checking permits at Salt Trail camp - do not attempt this trip without permits! Don't bother trying to filter or purify any of the water - bring all you need for the trip. All in our group took an average of about 6 liters per person for the 2 days.

LCR flowing high, chocolate color. Connecting trail to salt trail canyon completely washed out. This lead to the worst bushwhacking of our lives. Morning temp 18 degrees, no snow in canyon but some icicles.

Didn’t have a shuttle vehicle. We approached via the salt trail and broke off as the red wall began to soar. We skirted the terrace for a few hours enjoying some of the best views I’ve been blessed to see. You get some sick perspectives of bekihatso wash and big canyon too! After working all the way around and into the area above the technical portion you could potentially drop directly down to the first rappel via the first major drainage. With fading daylight we chose to find a walkable way down. This would add a rappel or two not more in length than 70’ feet from the looks of it from the first rap station. Pick your way down to meet the proper approach for big canyon. If you walked all the way to the gentle gully, there may be some water obstacles ahead of you. We were met with multiple avoidable pools and some dicey climbs around murky, cold water. We came to a dry fall with a large pool below it. Opposed to trying to downclimb, we went high up LDC wall to a thick but not so happy tree and fiddle sticked to the base of the pool (60’ ish). Shortly after this we were faced with our first mandatory swim. At this time it was already dark and windy. Shortly thereafter we made it to the first rappel and both made it to a sliver of rock in a long, deep, clear pool. It had steam coming off in some spots and was seeping from the walls. We were both freezing and not looking forward to getting our sleep system wet. After some thought we quickly jumared up and spent the night up and in a corner out of the watercourse. Upon waking we made the mention looooooong swim through the spring room. Ropewiki has been accurate for awhile but this first rappel is now a mandatory swimmer for at least one person! The anchor is mud glued old tat and new webbing on a tree far back from the edge rigged all the way to the lip. The second rappel was the longest and really a gorgeous one. You have to skirt some deep water to the right for anchor access, it is a thread at the lip in the watercourse RDC. Once on you pass a pool then make your way down a twisty, lichen-y, vibrant waterfall to a deep pool in a tight corridor (80’ ish). Skirt wall right again then stem your way to the next rappel. I set a rock to use as a rock-chock directly in the watercourse. It is still there. My partner found an existing anchor LDC out of the water course. It is a rock chock in some small boulders. This rappel is about 60’ feet. It skirts a waterfall. Beneath this waterfall is neat cave. The next rap is just down canyon and to the left. If you send one of your party members up RDC there is a perfect position for excellent photographs of the rappel. This also skirts you around a waterfall with a big pool. You are immediately faced with options for you last rappel. LDC brings you to a rappel into the shallow side of the big pool and involves some more navigating to get to shore. RDC brings you to a rap that drops you on some dry rocks on the beach area. Stay left from here and eventually get into the hillside to find some cairns that take you to the delightful river. Follow the trail, don’t forget to check out at the big ca ton lodge front desk. We got into dry clothes at the marked campsite. We blasted to the top in 3.5 hours. We got lucky, the gauge had some cfs flow for a week or two prior.

Started late in the day, used the standard approach (w/ shuttle) and finished the canyon in the dark by headlamp (11h to reach camp at the LCR). Carried 9L of water which in this heat was critical. I had nearly completely ran out by the time I reached the Salt TH on day two, as I gave away 2L to hikers who were becoming dangerously dehydrated. Multiple deep pools, but water temp was comfortable. The spring room had a 50 foot swim. Replaced R2 anchor. There was thigh-depth mud by the mouth of the canyon, best to find the social trail CL.

Approached via the redwall this time around, which was loose and exposed in sections but more engaging and IMO more scenic than the shuttle/loop approach. The canyon appeared to have flooded recently and was nearly at its water-holding capacity. We filtered rainwater from a pool above the first rappel. We then managed to bypass most the pools and get by with only a couple swims and some shallow wading. One person in our group wore a thin wetsuit. Others went without neoprene and were only marginally chilled. We excavated and replaced the aging anchor at R1. Some twists around the pull side led to a stuck rope at R2, and one of us ascended to re-rig. After the final rap we found the cairned trail high canyon left (highly recommend over attempting to navigate large boulders and deep mud near the mouth of the canyon). The LCR was running brown from recent rains. Despite more promising forecasts, we were thoroughly saturated by a substantial thunderstorm which rolled through as we were exiting the canyon and approaching the LCR.

All Anchors in good condition, though R2 anchor is under running water, and likely should be replaced each trip through. Water is much warmer after the spring. Muddy pools above much colder. We both had 2mm shorties, and could have easily done without, as the water from the spring was quite comfortable.

Amazing day. The travertine pools and flowing spring water were outstandingly beautiful. Our time is from the Big Canyon TH to the Salt Creek TH. We made our way up the Salt Trail at a leisurely pace.

Light flow right now through some beautiful twisty falls, replaced all webbing. We encountered a tricky meat anchor+partner assist downclimb below a deep pool on the way in - it seems like it would be possible to anchor off a small tree high LDC. Did this as a 2 day backpacking trip, camping right below the salt trail and exiting in the morning. The exit in the sun was brutal. 8 liters total was not enough water for me to be comfortable(though I am quite a water hog). Certainly wouldn't do this canyon any later in the year.

25 hours car to car, includes playing in the river and an overnight near the heli-pad. We took the Redwall approach and it was all good until we left the shade. Sun exposure got intense and we had to take breaks and huddle under boulders which slowed down our progress. Once we made it into the canyon it was like entering another world. Shade and water were in abundance the rest of the day. The morale of the team quickly improved and we thoroughly enjoyed the canyon. The LCR was neon blue and we arrived at the river a couple hours before sunset. We set camp around 8 pm with the little bit of sunlight we had left. We fell asleep by 10pm and left our camp at 4 am. Leaving by 4 provided shade for the entire exit up the rugged salt trail. The water is palatable once filtered, but our team brought enough water. This was a good canyon and I would do it again in the early spring or late fall.
Pretty cool. Slow movers will want thin wetsuits.

Approached via redwall. Some frozen pools above the first rap. Replaced R1 anchor. R2 chockstone is loose so carefully inspect before use. We found R4 anchor repositioned on CR - recommend the CL rappel line which is in the watercourse and avoids sharp ledges and loose rock. LCR was blue, though it may turn brown after this weekend's storm. It rained for a portion of the exit hike, transitioning to light snow as we reached the TH. We did not encounter any other hikers this trip.

Took the "shortcut route" in. New logjam has appeared not too far inside the entrance. Might be able to downclimb underneath it somehow, but was easier to just rappel off a log and drop back in. Fun to float along in the current through a beautiful narrows. Nice chaser just after running the Middle Fork just upstream.

The log jam has shifted a bit and the rappel is now optional. There is a rock shelf DCR you can step down to and down climb into the creek, or a deep pool you can make an approx. 10ft jump into DCL. Water is cold and be mindful of being polite to the campsites you pass on the creek walk out. Also saw an otter on the logjam after swimming downstream a bit. He apparently thought my jump looked fun and followed suit before climbing back up and over. Good reminder we share the canyons and to be aware and respectful of wildlife.

Nice, short, easy route. Had a light flow, making the canyon more scenic, but not enough to push you around - a few short swims. Most anchors in good shape, replaced one where webbing was damaged from maybe rockfall - webbing was fairly new. Anchors are easy - if you are not familiar with construction or evaluation of knot chock anchors (there were 2 - placements were bomber) or if you just don't think they are safe, simply bring webbing and make an anchor from the many boulders in the canyon - though you may have to extend a bit. "Ghosting is required" doesn't make any sense to me here. There is no reason why you would be required to use a releasable anchor in a remote place like this; there are no popular hiking trails nearby, etc. Exited after rap 6 on the left to gain the rim as beta suggested, no class 4, but a few moves of class 3. I was a bit confused as to why we were climbing out where we did instead of finishing the main arm of the canyon, then hiking up the drainage coming up on the left very shortly after, it looked like it goes with no problems. Did not find the mine camp or whatever the author mentions in the beta (we looked all over on the exit), but that appeared to be the idea.
Very cold water. Nice area and scenic. At least one swimmer pool.

Expected it to be dry in July but water was flowing, clear, and not too cold. Existing anchor pts on all but R4. Ideal hot weather locale due to cooler high elevation temps, light flowing water, & free misting on descents.

Beautiful fun little canyon with some flow! Every rappel was a lovely waterfall! Lots of deep pools and swimmers. Replaced webbing and anchors where needed. Knot chocks are totally bomber, no worries there! We were definitely happy to have 7mm wetsuits, very cold water. Hung out a bit to enjoy the falls and drink beer! Approach and exit were easy. No shuttle needed. We exited up the downstream drainage instead of following the KML. This was fairly clean and not too brushy. Beta has been updated.

Low flow and deep pools. Multiple short swims. Slid R4 and jumped R5. Pulled the rope at first stage of R6 and jumped from the second stage. Some pools were just deep enough for jumps and slides, check depth first. Comfortable in 5mm wetsuit. Others had less neoprene and managed. A pretty canyon with lots of tiered pools and sculpted quartzite.

Anchors in good shape. Knot chock looked good and easy to inspect. Blue webbing is fading and has some good stretch to it, maybe need replacing soon. The jumps and slides were the highlight, but getting shallow so best to wade it out first. Thin wet suits kept us from shivering but we warmed up quick with jumping jacks. Rock is polished and the slides are slick. Exiting LUC just after last rapp was loose in spots but short and quick.
Fun colorful canyon with low flowing cold water. Only saw a few anchors in there but was able to wrap the rope around trees for the other few rappels.

Likely first descent of Big Huckleberry as no other anchors were found. Had somewhat low expectations dropping in, but turned out to be a quite lovely creek. Not destined to be one of the all-time classics, but enjoyable. Water levels were were great (but cold)... guessing we hit it in flood stage. Water levels likely drop off significantly heading into summer, but the creek was clear enough to suggest it runs all year. Might be worth checking out the east tributary as it had equivalent flow to the main fork.

Flow was strong and made creek walking a challenge. Below the top confluence we avoided some narrow spots due to the flow. The upper drop, at the confluence, on the north side was not runnable due to the flow however the south one was perfect. Rappels were great and anchors offer options to be in the water but out of the main flow.

Water was a little lower than expected with all the rain but it was at a nice level. All anchors are in good shape. There is a bit of a creek walk out but there are some decent little slides and pools along the way. Added a sling to a log across the creek DCL for the last little drop.

Lovely, cold day in Big Huck. We replaced the anchor for R2. R3 was a bit spicy as we were right in the flow and it was ice cream headache level cold! The road up to the top was bumpy but clear. Unfortunately, when we tried to return with the shuttle someone had a cut a large tree that fully blocked the road four miles from the top! We waited for two hours until help arrived with a saw. Our poor driver hiked the four miles up while the tree was cut. Beware of idiots with chain saws!

Flowing at a trickle and most were able to avoid the deep pools. The first anchor for the upper falls has one improperly placed bolt that needs to be removed - the other 2 bolts for the anchor are good. Day hikers tend to fill the hallway at the bottom of the first drop especially when it is flowing - don't throw the rope for this first drop, but bring it down with you so you don't hit them with the rope. Even when flowing good I've never seen nor heard of any dangerous hydraulic situations in the canyon.

All anchors in good shape. Low flow but enjoyable in thin wet suits. Still snow melting in the canyon. R1. from the top Falls was maybe worth the bushwhack. 50% said yes, 50% said no. A handful of fun downclimbs. The exit came up pretty quick after the rabbit hole and put us right on the trail.

Adding the optional big rappel entailed an arduous bushwack through a thicket of manzanita and added an extra 45min to the approach. Not worth it for the single added rap IMO. Lower section of canyon is scenic and worthwhile in and of itself. Multiple cold, deep pools and some residual ice coating rocks and plants. https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=vHmf_76fCL8&t=27s
Time is doing full (first big rappel included). Replaced webbing on rappels 2 and 3. Low flow made for easy enjoyable day cooling off in the water.

Pleasant trip. 2 people at a moderate pace. Barnhardt Trail isn't hard and elevation gain is gradual but it feels like it takes FOREVER! Lots of other hikers on trail. Once at the main waterfall, we hiked up around the mountain, cutting over early up a gully rather than following full GPS track to do the optional big 2 stage rappel. Lots of people chilling at the bottom of the falls. We stopped here and hung out for quite awhile before continuing on. At first, rock hopping down the creek after initial rap was lack-luster, but once arriving at the next rappel the walls grow tall and the scenery really comes to life! Beautiful water falls cascading from multiple angles into deep pools, intricate layered rock everywhere. We only wore rain jackets and were comfortable. Water was cold but no where close to unbearable. This technical section was super pretty, the raps were wet and fun, even with the low flow, but it was over fairly quick. Back to rock hopping down a fairly average pretty enough drainage then up to Barnhardt Trail.

A very cold day in Big Kahuna. My partner and I opted to skip R1 as the trail up to R2 became increasingly snowy and icy as we arrived at the confluence of the trail and the watercourse. There was enough hard-packed snow and ice on upper portions of the trail that the addition of microspikes would have increased our speed enough to warrant carrying them. There was ice in the watercourse throughout the sequence, only abating after the technical section was complete, enough to cause us to slow down and use caution as some rocks were wet and others frozen, but it was tough to tell the difference without testing each one. We each had merino wool underneath 4/3 wetsuits. 5mm+ would have been far more comfortable, but we finished with only mild discomfort. Watch out for the large pile of human shit frozen at the top of R4. (Please learn how to poop out of the watercourse) Waterflow was fun and fantastic and consisted primarily of fresh snowmelt.

About a foot of snow at the drop in for lower section. Ice within the watercourse was minimal and did not present any issues. Snowmelt had produced some appreciable flow. Webbing was in decent condition. We were comfortable in 4/3 wetsuits w/ rain jackets.
Low flow, beautiful water, very cold. Ice on the rocks and logs. A handful of short swimmers. Replaced anchors on R1 and R5.

Low flow but perfect weather. We did not do the first rap (140 ft rap) as we were not inclined for the bushwacking on this day. VERY casual paced day as we were enjoying the beauty of the canyon. Wore 3/2 wetsuits which were unnecessary--neoprene booties and a rain jacket would have been sufficient. Water was avoidable until bottom of second rap which was shin deep at most as it was at the bottom of all other raps except for the third. Lower pinch point part of anchor at rap 2 replaced. Waist-deep water at bottom of rap 3.

We started from Secret canyon, packrafted down past Black Jack exit, hiked up to the canyon, and once down, packrafted back up to Lone Palm where we hiked out (wind was S-N so was pushing us upriver). Approach was much shorter than we expected, but all rappels were scenic, nice, and have good webbing. Watch for a chockstone rappel for possible pull issues, and avoid the velcro plants near the end of the canyon.
Went out for the day to explore around black point and see if we could find the fissures. We found this main one, crawled into a 100 ft long, 40ft deep squeeze and another short box canyon.

Great adventure in rugged Superstition Mountains. Cave, double bridge, cool big rappel, great scenery, not too brushy, Crucifix canyon fun and scenic. Whole experience was pretty great. Replaced webbing on only rappel. Got one foot wet to ankle.
Fantastic trip from Blacktail river camp, easy access short approach and beautiful canyon. One swimmer but didn't need wetsuit on last rap
Done from the South Rim, not via river trip.
Done starting from the river. The last rap was a jumpable swimmer, there was no reasonable bypass to stay dry. Drysuits were nice to have.

A really great route for the area with narrow slotty corridors that twist around rapidly plunging towards the river. We did this route as a part of a 3 day trip paddling down the river from the dam camping and hitting canyons along the way. We started the day from the AZ Hotsprings Beach. The hike from beach to turn off took maybe 30mins going though the hot springs. From the turn off it took us 2.5hrs for the approach/descent at a casual pace, back down to the river. The approach does follow a very faint trail crosscountry. The descent is short and committing, rappels all enjoyable enough, many of which down large chutes. We were able to stay dry, one pothole had water but was avoidable, may not be in wet season. All webbing in good shape, we added an anchor on the questionable 2 stage to break up the pulls. The route ends with a short stemming down climb though a crack LDC to a small beach to inflate rafts. Paddle upstream to AZ Hotsprings was short and despite the opposing wind, we managed just fine. I feel this is a great full featured route for those wanting a short descent with day trip to the hotsprings that is a much more interesting option than Secret Canyon.
Lovely canyon! Floated out to Crane's rest on a no-wind day. Mixed crew of alpackas and explorer 200s. Time includes the rather leisurely float.

Great first trip through Blue Velvet! The approach is the same as the Arizona Hot Springs hike, but about three-quarters of the way in, you’ll cut up and to the left. From there, it’s pretty exposed with a few scree-covered hills to climb up and down. Once you drop into the canyon, though, it’s nicely shaded and much cooler. We tried to keep webbing use to a minimum by partner-assisting and down-climbing where possible. A couple of old slings were pretty fried and crusty, which we replaced, but most were still in solid shape. We checked the wind forecast beforehand — it was blowing south to north, which turned out to be perfect. Once we inflated the packrafts, the wind practically pushed us upriver, making for a super chill float out. One from our group decided to pack a thick wetsuit and swim the whole way instead. Probably not something I’d recommend, but he called it great triathlon training.

We descended this route as a part of a multiday trip paddling down the river. From AZ HotSpring Beach it took us 1hr to hike up to drop in. FYI sandbags have been removed from AZ Hot Springs as the water has been tested to have too many contaminants. Approach is getting worn in and is fairly easy to follow for the most part. Descent is short but fun and took us 1.5hr to reach the water. There are a few knee to waist deep pools. Not cold but refreshing! Can confirm largest rap is 60’ and there is a few that length so 120’ ropes work great! Great bang for buck on this one! Great canyon for the area.

First known descent. Accessed by bike. Decent spot to lock up bikes near start of bushwhack. Water wasn't too cold, and no deep pools. Done with all natural anchors. Don't even think about bringing a bolt kit - it won't help you. Rock is absolute choss.

Great day. Did the canyon solo for work. Quite the schlep carrying 600 ft of rope/pull cord just to do the last rap (in addition to all the equipment I needed). Water isn't cold, but it was kind of windy in the canyon. Was comfortable in a 3mm shortie and a rain jacket while moving, but cold while standing around for long periods of time. Removed two things of cord/maelon - gotta love that fiddlestick! I think there's only 3 raps left with tat on them. Would be a fun challenge to try to ghost those drops - might be doable, but I didn't want to push it solo.

It was a warm weekday afternoon. We biked from Mt. Washington trailhead and locked the bikes to the bridge. Followed the existing approach coordinates for the most part. Just the approach took us 2h. It is steep bushwhacking, most use hands and feet often. We got lots of scratches. Once we got to the canyon, it was fun. All rappels are back to back. Replaced the existing anchors with new webbing. If it wasn't for the approach this canyon would be a whole a lot more fun.

A decent canyon for the area. We did this route as a part of a 3 day trip paddling down the river from the dam camping and hitting canyons along the way. We started the day from the BoyScout Canyon Beach. From beach to drop in took us about 2 hours. The descent also took us about 2 hours. Webbing thoughout canyon is still okayish but probably wont last the summer. We encountered one unavoidable waist deep pool. Much of this route is wide and cliffy except a very short spot at the end where the spring is. Much of that area is a bit over grown. The calcite formations were neat. Final “rappel” into BoyScout could be ghosted off a tree but it was a low angle walk down and there is an easy walk off DCR. All in all a decent but unremarkable route.

The technical section was okay for the area, with a couple of good rappels. However if you are a scrambler like us and enjoy climbing up dry falls, side-hilling, ledge traverse, tricky downclimbs, and class 4 exit-- then this canyon is a worthy adventure. All anchors, webbings, and fixed ropes were in good condition. A few of us got wet in the "unavoidable pothole" and stemming section after, but only ankle deep. Also, big thanks to whoever put more sandbags in Boy Scout hotsprings area.

We descended this canyon as a part of a multiday packraft trip on the river. Starting from BoyScout Beach it took us 1hr to travel up the BoyScout Canyon exit. From there we followed the BMD approach originally trying to find a way over to Wildside Canyon. Being faced with some sketchy ridges and challenging terrain we decided to descend this canyon instead. This canyon has a few decent drops but as I mentioned in my previous report, it is fairly unremarkable and a 3 star canyon compared to others in the area. Can confirm largest drop is slightly less than 120’. We got wet to waist deep. Water not cold but refreshing.

We totally missed the trail up. We started to the left of where we parked our cars, and I think the faint trail is even farther left. Brutal hike up, wearing our wetsuits after doing clark. We hiked up per GIA, but we ended up crawling back down the creek 200 ft lower in elevation before the first Rap. At this flow, we did not need the R4 traverse line to access the bolt.

Enjoyable little outing - fun weekend combo with Clark Creek next door. Didn't bother looking for a route, but thrashed up wherever was easiest then followed the "shortcut route" into the creek, dropping in just above the first rappel. As bushwhacks go in the Olympics, it's pretty easy. Probably a 2 out of 5 (where 1 is open forest and 5 is "I can't see my feet"). Re-rigged goofy anchor DCL on R1. Traverse line was unnecessary at current flow. Could have used a little more water. :-)
Canyon has obviously been run this season - new 5/8” webbing on all rappels. Started 5am from South Kaibab, finished technical section by 12:45. Forecast rain made an appearance around 3:30 pm.

Unfortunately, due to an injury on the approach via N. Kaibab-Tonto we only completed the first rap in the daylight. Thus, we were unable to see the bulk of the canyon as we completed it. It was a ton of fun to do under headlamp but all of its aesthetic was lost in the dark. We camped on the beach and packrafted to boat beach to hike out Bright Angel the next day.

Took a canoe over, left it at the bottom of the canyon and convinced some fisherman to give us a ride around to the approach. Worked out well. Pool at the base of rappel #1 was about knee deep. Was a bit difficult to find a good place to leave the boat. Ended up having to bushwack pretty hard to get back to it at the end.

Accessed via packraft. About 1h to R1 moving at a leisurely pace. Tons of wildflowers on approach, which was steep and loose. A couple tricky starts. Did not replace any webbing but did clean up some loose rock by R2. Short but pretty narrows and some interesting geology.

2 star canyon, 3 star route. The packraft in and out of this canyon make it a good adventure! I brought my packraft, broke it down after crossing the lake and packed it through the canyon. My friend kayaked across, left his kayak at the approach, carried an ultralight packraft (barely more than a pool tube) through the canyon, and paddled the fancy tube back to his kayak, which was better suited for crossing the width of the lake. The hike up the hill was loose but short. The loose footing is even more evident after crossing the saddle - I slipped a few times hiking down the hill into the canyon. Put in new webbing at 2 out of 3 anchors. Second rappel has a slightly awkward start. Stepping back towards R3 made the pull super easy, no issues with the pinch.
