After R3, stay right moving towards the water and you'll land on a small rocky beach with room for 1-2 people to inflate packrafts.

We encountered a freak rain/hail/thunderstorm which made things interesting. We decided to explore a new exit route past the vegetation and to the right. Started with a easy scramble over some loose scree, then transitioned to steep gully with VERY loose beach ball sized rocks that will rocket right towards your buddies. Which then becomes an exposed fifth class climb (X- no turn around extreme exposure, was quite chossy). Then a quick half mile to the ridge to a herd path that leads to the road. Those that went down canyon enjoyed some more advanced scrambles with the precipitation loosening the holds. The portions with hand-lines became less of a DC and more of a fun slide. Overall a good canyon that might give you a great excuse for a marg!

Only rappel is big one. Bolts on both sides of big rap, used ones on DCL and had no pull issues. Did see bolts and chains about halfway down on super small ledge, so looks like could be done as multi stage with smaller rope. Fixed rope lines on about 4 places down to river.

The short fixed rope to the big drop is getting pretty ragged and should be replaced. With a 220' rope from the first rebelay we came up short but were able to downclimb the rest of the drop. We did this as a river trip so our time doesn't include the hike through the wash, but we were going at a pretty relaxed pace.

Started down at the river on a canoeing trip. Approach up to the canyon rim had some fun route finding. The main drop was incredibly dramatic and tall into an amphitheater with an audience of other canoeists. We rigged it as a single pitch with a knot. Noticed that there is a rebelay station right around 195' from the anchor. Relaxing in the hot springs on the way out was a unique end to the adventure.

Time reflects a descent of Boyscout, packraft to AZ hot springs, and exit up White Rock Canyon. Weather was perfect. Hot springs were hot. The one special challenge was that between the 350' rope, cord, rafts, oars, PFDs, and copious amounts of gummy bears we were left with minimal beer carrying capacity. Prepare accordingly.

All anchors and fixed ropes were in good condition. Sand bags were present to make some nice hot soak pools. Several people camping and partying near the river, but the canyon was in nice clean condition - please keep it that way if you head down there. Exit route had a couple of climbing moves with some mild exposure, but nothing too bad.

Dry canyon above the hot springs. Easy approach, some of the handlines are old but good enough. Used a 400ft rope for the big rappel and collected info on the chain anchors along the route. Middle anchor had a spinning hanger on the bolt, but otherwise looks solid. Hot springs in great condition. Be aware that there is no way to get below the hot springs without wading through them. About 3ft of water in the pools.
Only the big rap is mandatory. Rest are fixed handlines or bypass able.
Amazing time! All fixed ropes in good condition, and hotsprings were great. Could use a few more sandbags (probably washed downstream from recent rains)

We ran Bozoo again as part of our Freeze Fest. Air temps were in the upper 40s when we got to the starting point around 9:30 AM. There was minimal packed snow on the ground so a passenger car could likely make it. We decided to hike directly up to about ~4000ft instead of taking the overgrown road and making the switch back since the road was covered in about 2 feet of snow. The post holing would have drained our energy/slowed us down so we proceeded directly up the mountain. The post holing when cutting across to our drop in around 4000 was terrible. Waist deep postholing, stick close to your team so you can help them out of the holes. The canyon packs in a lot of rappels if you're crazy enough to run canyons in these conditions. The cold will catch up to you by the last 3-4 drops and these are some rappels where you are completely in the water. Dry suit with rain jacket + hood highly recommended also we had several of our team where an insane amount of neoprene. Freezefest II complete.
Brian French, Jared Smith, Benjamin Trapanese, John Waller, Mike WIlliams

We encountered a lot more water than we expected. The canyon was extremely technical and every obstacle had to be carefully analyzed to find best solution. We had to rebuild all previous anchors and found no evidence of prior anchors anywhere in the canyon. We did find a watch and forgot a 200ft rope at the top of R3 rappel. If you find it please contact [[User:Lucach]]

While not for beginners or those faint of heart, not really a problem for those experienced with high water. No hydraulics found and water was either endurable or avoidable depending on desires. Run at appx 146cfs. DCs at the end may require some above average skill.

This canyon is like a smaller version of Big Creek in WA. The flow was mod-low, and the waterfalls presented no issues. R2 was intimidating for some people in the group, but as they rappelled they stayed mostly out of the water and had no issue. For R4 we went off the existing webbing and deadman, and were completely out of the flow, so for us R2 was the 'crux' rappel. On the exit, we followed the recently changed exit description in the beta from the group a few weeks before us, and were just about killed by falling rock and sliding down the extremely bad scree and loose dirt below the cliffs. Twice we had to lay down a rope and anchor on a tree for the rest of the group to batman up the rope and avoid dropping rocks below. Therefore I changed the beta back to the original beta that has a much safer exit through the vegetation just downstream of the talus field below the cliffs. Otherwise, the walking through the forest was very pleasant as there is no underbrush whatsoever, just some minor bushwacking right near the parking area.

Very wild and untouched canyon run. Removed several old and very unsatisfactory anchors. Ghosted most of it so if I remember correctly we only left anchors on 2 of the raps. One on a rock DCL of the rap in the cover photo and one on DCR tree of R2. Very tricky set ups, traverse lines, slippery rock, and dangerous log jams. The canyon itself is quite beautiful almost the entire way through but def not a trek I will ever do again. The road is open now, the hike in is not too bad but takes about an hr. The exit is a HUGE pain in the butt. The post fire damage has made the initial climb out very treacherous. We had to set up 3 hand lines for the 1st 500 feet of vertical. Very loose and crumbly dirt/rock. A fall here would be disastrous, possibly leading to a helo flight out. After reaching the top of that ridge you only have about 1300 more feet vertical to get over. IMO if you’re an advanced group and want to see something rare…it’s worth a go but be prepared to walk out in the dark and take plenty of H2O for that hike out! Def not a beginner friendly canyon.

1. There are several handlines going to the Upper Falls. Not overly difficult, but not for inexperienced people or those lacking in hand or upper body strength. 2. The large ledge 30' above the bottom of the Upper Falls accumulates significant debris in the form of rocks and small trees. Rope catch potential when pulling is HIGH. You can get off rappel and try to pull from a narrower ledge ABOVE this bigger ledge (may be a bad angle?) And then set up a 60' rap using bolts in the wall DCL. Or, maybe better, pull the rope from the bottom, cross your fingers that it pulls. If not, be prepared with a short rope to descend about 30' to the ledge from this bolt (after reascending the 1st handline), free or retrieve the rope and either reascend OR continue for a total of about 60' back to the bottom. 3. Can confirm that the bolts DCR of the Upper Falls put you in the main flow at times. Be prepared to manage your rappel. 4. The anchor for the Lower Falls is bolts set well back. If you want to pull from the bottom of the drop, come prepared with about 30' of webbing to make it to the edge. 5. Our time of 3hrs was partly spent retrieving our rope. If everything goes perfectly, could do this easily in under 2hrs.

Some tiring chimneying in the lower section. I had a pack with rope as I started up at the top section. This made it very tricky. Lots of swimming at the end. The walls were slick with mud, forcing me to swim sideways in the slot, pushing my pack in front of me.

Did the Lower Brimstone "The Beast" route as described in the Bluugnome beta. Our drop-in point was a couple hundred feet up canyon of where the confluence is marked on that map, scrambling down a minor side drainage instead, which seemed a bit easier. Three of us stayed low the whole time, the rest of us stemmed over the tightest section, which was generally quite easy--there's a very comfortable stemming level something around 30-40 ft off the ground. Canyon was totally dry.

An amazing day in Lower Brimstone! We did Spooky, The Beast, and ROC in a day. Time is for all 3. It was a group that was somewhat new to desert canyons so going was quite slow. Overall lots of movement to different levels vertically to find passages wide enough to go through and comfortable to stem. The lower section was amazing. A great Subway room and some very cool slots. It is definitely worth hiking in from the bottom even if you don't want to try the R+ stemming. We saw 3 baby snakes and one parkouring mammal which helped to keep things lively. Bring lots of water obviously. We brought ~2 liters and our party wanted more for sure.

Oct 31, 2025. Most of the lower section was flooded with lot of water, as is the case with most stuff in Escalante this fall. A lot of the water can be avoided by tricky darkness stemming but eventually you will need to drop into the water and swim through the anxiety inducing darkness. Type 2 fun for sure. We didn’t wear wetsuits and were very cold in the moment but warmed up quickly after leaving the canyon so I’d recommend leaving the suits behind unless you run this in the winter.

First descent by Jay Wisocki and Erek Burek. Some minor flow in the canyon on first two rappels, with a little more flow accumulating for the last five rappels. Lower section had three swimmer pools with little sun exposure, wetsuit not needed but non-cotton layers was nice to have. Most rappels were very slippery. Good anchors on all rappels. R3 be mindful of rope placement, but low chance of rope pull issues overall.

A rugged backcountry oasis! Second known descent!!! After a previous scouting trip, we found an adjacent 4x4 trail that we were able to drive leading over to the main trail, bypassing the worst of the road in the very beginning. We were able to drive the 4mile 4x4 trail that the FD team had hiked, all the way up to the canyon loop. The road is rocky and rutted but doable with our lifted Frontier and 4runner and careful driving. It took us 38mins from Hwy 260 to get to the exit where we left our shuttle vehicle and continued driving an additional 13mins to the approach, parking at “Neal’s Cabin” as labeled on the map. (The cabin is long gone) Approach from here was 1.5 miles following faint game trails and a trickling stream. It took us 1.5hrs to get to drop in, at a leisurely pace. Canyon was flowing a bit after subsequent storms this week. All drops very slippery. The route features very interesting geometrically cut basalt rock rock with cracks everywhere that easily catches and entraps a fiddlestick! We didnt put on our wetsuits until middle of the canyon where the deep swimming pools presented themselves. We were all very comfy in 4/3s. We all agreed, the best part of the canyon was the last 2 rappels, a really gorgeous sequence separated by a deep swimming pool surrounded by sculpted basalt rock. This alone brought what would have been a 3 star canyon up to 4 stars. We had to rebuild all but 2 anchors and the descent took us 5 hours at a very leisurely pace. Exit out has lots of loose scree and boulders to scramble up to gain the ridge. The ridge line views are incredible! Once on the ridge, more rolling hills of loose rock presnt themselves. Exit took us nearly 3hrs at a very leisurely pace. Over all this is an interesting and rugged route with neat features throughout. Although definitely not worth doing without a 4x4 to drive up the trail and another for the short shuttle, in my opinion.

A little flow throughout the canyon made it worth doing. Due to the cracks near the edge and poor rock quality, double rope rappels might work best for retrieval. We did the last rappel as an 80' and 100' when it can likely be done as one rappel with a 220' rope. Definitely a good find and perhaps 4 stars to some, in the right conditions. We got it in good conditions and I still dont think I'de repeat it, so 3 stars for me..
Nice scenic canyon from the bottom up.

A wonderfully scenic and adventurous trip. 3 days car to car. As one in our group mentioned after exiting "that's the most effort I've ever put forth for 6 rappels." We car camped the night before at the drop in point. The scramble down is steep, loose, and the sketchiest part of the trip in my opinion. Good route finding and exposure tolerance is required. The technical section through the Redwall is stunning. The lower section through some Tapeats narrows are beautiful as well. Because we were exiting Nankoweap, which is 11 miles downstream, we stuck to the rules and hiked the first 3 miles along the river to put in at President Harding Rapid to float the last 8 miles. Those 3 miles of river hiking were the most miserable I've experienced in all of my Grand Canyoneering.

We did only the lower part of Buckfarm Canyon from a river trip. It is beautiful, through canyon walls with undulating ledges of Muav limestone. However, it felt more like a sporty hike than a canyoneering route. On the way up, we bypassed on the left (then saw a trail on the right looking up canyon, which we didn't see earlier). Our route was exposed. On the way down, we did one short rappel, waded through a couple pools. We were all comfortable without cold water protection since the air was warm and the water sections were not swimmers.
Meh.

Hit knee deep quicksand at one point! Water was flowing and clear. No anchors found at top of drop (didn't look for very long). I opted to just downclimb as all the good anchor points would have dropped me into the pool at the base and I didn't want to get wet. Didn't see the rock art ☹️

Light flow of water. Avoidable pools to only rappel. Big pool at bottom of only rappel, but avoidable if make way LDC before going into pool to rock ledges. Only got slightly wet on light flow coming down. Easy hiking with a rappel. Great for beginners.
Stagnant pools that were avoidable. Replaced old, crusty webbing.

My first descent of Butte Creek; based on limited beta. Flow was slightly higher than optimal in some locations. R1 was in ideal conditions, current but no hydraulics at the base of the falls. R1 was extremely fun and we repeated it at least 3 times. Extra time was spent locating and moving anchor locations. We stayed out of the narrows above R2 as the water was moving somewhat swiftly. R2 flow was quite strong and not suitable for beginners and may have proven challenging for advanced canyoneers as well. Tried to bypass falls with a rap DCR, but a 200' rope halved proved to be slightly insufficient. It's possible it could have been done, but rope retrieval would have been tricky as you would have had to pull from the base of the falls while swimming and treading water in a slight hydraulic. I decided it wasn't worth it and ascended the rope instead.

Flow on the gauge at monitor was around 12 cfs, so we stayed out of the flow for R2. The mossy anchor DCR on R2 didn't look particularly strong so we backed it up with a meat anchor. For R3 we downclimbed ~10 ft DCL and then jumped into the pool. Fun times!

Coming here on a weekend was likely a mistake. The parking lot was quite crowded. We avoided families swimming and planing in the falls, but in the future, I wouldn't do this on a weekend again. It is not nearly as much fun to canyoneer around others...

This is a really great canyon! In California this would be a 5-star canyon, but in comparison with Big Creek, Davis etc. an in mod/low water we decided it was "good", not "great" based on a lack of technical difficulty.

For rappel 2 we anchored from a tree further DCL not in the direct flow. To get to this tree there is an exposed traverse through devils club. This rappel is mostly fee hanging and out of the flow except for the last bit you get a little wet in low conditions but not much. I am not sure what it is like in high conditions. It appeared to have been rappelled from before and is about 70'.

Water levels are fun, R1 can def be J1 currently. The small falls right before middle Butte falls is about a 4ft jump from DCR. R2 anchor tree DCL is solid however the webbing looked to be very old and I didn't use it. Currently the water iwill push you around a bit and the small inset about half way down is a cool place to take a photo from behind the falls. Beware the water just under the falls DCL is not deep enough to jump even the final few feet should not be jumped. DCR may be more favorable if jumping from the half way point if that was desired, depth check 1st. R3 was flowing well however there is a 8ft fat log stuck in the flow so I opted to jump it. Great little canyon with nice views and a very fun inflow rappel!!

Never had a chance to run the creek pre-fire in 2020, so was keen to jump at the opportunity when the area reopened. Butte is a lovely little canyon with some interesting rappels and fun water features. Given the high tourist factor, it may be good idea to avoid leaving fixed anchors where possible. Replaced anchor on R2.

This was a great canyon! I’m new ish so I chose it because most features you can walk around if you’re not having a good time. We had a great time! The flow was fun and pretty easy. There is no webbing on R1, R2 webbing looks good. R3 DCR there is good webbing for the rappel out of the flow. We did the jump and it was great! Confirmed the gate to Abiqua Falls is closed. So I ran it twice!

High flow today and we decided not to run R2 due to the volume. R1 was manageable and fun to start in the flow, just off the edge DCL because you would be in the high water just for a second, and come out under it immediately due to the overhung (cave) shape. For R2, we rappelled DCR from a tree away from the flow to the pool with a 300' rope. Not sure if 230' would have made it. Probably need a pull cord. For R3, two members of the group jumped from the rock, and the rest rappelled to the ledge DCR from a tree, and jumped from the ledge, which was about 15' or so high. The water immediately below the ledge was very frothy, but we all swam away no problem. No log or obstacle in the pool today.

Some icy conditions. Water was higher than anticipated since we haven’t had rain for 15+ days and it has been cold. Overall, it was a fun day. Couldn’t jump R3 from the pinnacle due to ice. Jumped from anchor station on other side. Normal exit was iced over and we used the gulley that leads up to the small bridge on the trail.
Cool canyon with interesting geology. R2 had a hydraulic at the bottom of the DCL line, after 1 person down we decided to send the rest of the group down DCR.

Flow was lower than anticipated, but still made for a fun day. R1 is a great place to do laps. R1 can be jumped, but it is technical as there are rock ledges and outcroppings. You definitely want to have someone scout the landing zone before attempting. The log in the R3 chute from 2024 is no longer there. The current must’ve taken it further down creek as I did not see it in the deep pool below. We opted to take the R3 tree rap / ledge jump combo then swam across the pool, scrambled carefully up the backside of the tower, then made the 45’ jump.

Super fun day. Zero people. Nice to have great trails for approach and exit, with zero bushwhacking. R1 was easy, fun to check out the cave behind the falls. R2 was a real highlight. For R3, we did the 'slot.' I was first and faffed around a bunch since it is a tight slot with very poor views (the canyon was new to us all). In the end, not bad, but a bit of a 'cork in a bottle' feel. A wonderful short adventure.

First known descent with Patrick Buttermore, Cole Anderson, and Jacob Cowan. Approach not to bad, brush is spare and easy to avoid. Spring trickle coming down canyon. Easy to stay dry or avoid getting too wet with careful footing. Exit brush very few if stay in drainage. Beautiful area of Superstition Mountain Wilderness.
Entry into the canyon required two rappels. (~100ft, ~190ft) Anchor retrieval required consideration on several of the rappels. False floors would present a risk at higher flow.
Great day to get a last run in on this canyon. Put in webbing on trees for the entrance rappels. Jumps are all in and no issues.
Flow lower than we anticapated. Neat bedrock in the upper section

We had just a few hours and after looking at pictures decided to run this canyon rather than headdress or minnow. It was surprisingly pretty rock, but very short. We had harness and rope but did not need to use them, we downclimbed everything. Also had wetsuits (the canyon was flowing) but did not use them as we decided to endure with waist deep pools in shorts.
Water ankle-deep. We guessed that most of the pools had been filled in. Some fun downclimbs. Did one very short rappel, although probably could be downclimbed by the determined. Not too challenging.

Parked a vehicle at the exit and walked the road to the start (20min). Car-to-car just over an hour moving at a moderate pace. Flowing water and no unavoidable deep pools. Shin depth at most. Lots of flood evidence. As others have mentioned deeper pools must have been filled in over time. Brought a short rope but didn’t need to use it.

We did a bottom up walk through of the canyon, the canyon has a light flow of water with no pools, was able to avoid all water. The down climbs were easy with no handline. Only issue we had was overgrowth of a large grass that was very sharp on the edges
Completely dry. A few dark areas with spiderwebs - bring a headlight and watch out for black widow spiders.

My vehicle wasn't broken down...but decided to check out Cane Spring Canyon anyways. Definitely rugged. Plenty of thrashing through brush and scrambling along the approach as you ascend the canyon. There were 2 or 3 class IV climbs required to navigate around some of the rappels - hence the difficulty rating. R1 webbing is getting crispy and should be replaced soon. The water knot at R2 looked like it had been purposefully cut. I replaced the webbing and packed out the junk anchor material. R3 anchor was solid and webbing was in fine shape. There was a black rattlesnake coiled up in a rock nook at the base of this rappel about three feet from where my rope bag landed. I added a 4th rappel at the first major obstacle of the canyon that avoids an exposed, advanced downclimb. Most people would probably hesitate climbing up the section, never mind down it. Details for this rappel have been added to the main page. It would be nice if there was an alternative approach that didn't ascend the canyon. Maybe taking a separate drainage off FR 177, or descending from above via FR 191?

After bailing out on Barnhardt due to too much flow we still wanted to do something and checked out Cane Springs on the way home. It turned into good albeit strange adventure. The current beta leads you into in and out route. You boulder hop up stream, scramble around obstacles until you get to the big, final wall which is the first rappel. At this point we felt we "conquered" the canyon and obviously if you can make it to this point you can retrace your steps and bypass/downclimb everything so there are no mandatory rappels. We were lucky to caught it at the highest flow this canyon can possibly see and it was really good. Not having our wetsuits with us and not feeling like getting wet we ended up...hiking instead of canyoneering. This canyon would make much more sense with different approach. From the parking lot I would try to gain the ridge on either side, high above canyon floor and try to drop back down after the big wall, then forget about the fact that everything is passable and turn every obstacle into raps. And with the flow we encountered it would be fantastic outing.

Likely a first descent of Canyon Creek. The first part of the creek was brushy where we dropped in, but it starts to get better as soon as one reaches the first falls. When you hit the main line, the creek abruptly plunges into a tight narrows with no escape and it's pretty fun. No big rappels in the narrows, but some very cool potholes. Between the sections of narrows are some long creekwalks which can be a little tedious, but the scenery is pretty. Canyon Creek Falls is quite excellent as well: beginning through a narrow slot which leads to a vertical pitch. Not destined to be a classic, but still a nice day out. I'd run it again... Would be fun to visit with a bit more water.

Day one we hiked to Bonita Creek, dropped camping gear and hiked up to descend the canyon and return to camp in about seven hours and 15 miles of hiking. We hiked out on the second day in about three hours. There may be better ways to reach the area where the canyons are - north of Midnight Canyon - this was my first visit to the area and I was a bit surprised by the well maintained roads far along on the hike. If they are not private roads it may be possible to get much closer to the Midnight Canyon area making visiting the slots north of there much more worth while - though the low quality of this one doesn't give me much hope that the others will be much better. Most of the trail is in good shape, well marked and easy to follow, but was a bit overgrown with thorny brush. Hiking down Johnny Creek is fairly tedious with the worst size cobbles and small boulders that move underfoot and threaten to twist ankles. Don't underestimate how tiring all the creek and canyon hiking can be in this area.
Flowing water. Waist to chest deep at max for short distance. Found cairned trail on the way out
More than a trickle made for very nice waterfalls but still straightforward. Briefly chest deep

Lots of cactus on approach and exit. Scenic canyon with some interesting sculpted sections, pools, and high walls. Little rappel or canyoneering appeal, short easy raps. A few interesting downclimbs. Overall, not worth the hell that is the approach and exit. Downclimbed rappel #1 LDC about 15' rappel. Downclimbed rap #2 LDC in challenging slopping crack about 25' rappel. Rappelled #3 about 90' from tree LDC, instead of doing bushy downclimb more LCD. Replaced webbing on rappel #1, #3, and #4. One and done for this canyon for me.

A few waist and chest depth wades. No swimmers that were mandatory but a couple optional ones if staying directly in the watercourse. 3/2mm wetsuit was a luxury, but not completely necessary. Very light trickle of flow, with algae beginning to develop in some of the pools. Some interesting sculpted rock and optional fun chimney downclimbs. Approach and exit (particularly the exit) were rough. Won’t be recommending this canyon to anyone who isn’t a masochist.

I think this canyon would be a much more fun romp in better conditions, but as most people could tell you 101 degrees isn’t ideal conditions. The approach and exit are what I imagine a prison gang fight to be (everything is trying to stab you) and a cholla cactus managed to sink a needle in my heel on the exit. The canyon itself had a lot of cool geology, although the rock was shockingly slick, even with the water being only a few small pools inhabited by garter snakes and tadpoles. Found two sets of webbing on the first rap, removed one, replaced the other with new webbing, downclimbed everything else, I think all DCL. Packed 4 liters of water, went though a little over two of it. This one dissuaded me from further canyon exploration while I was in the area, but would love to come back in better conditions.

Started at 6:30 was a great temperature. Started getting warmer when we got back to the vehicle at 9:00. Beautiful canyon! Can’t wait to come back when it has a lot of flow. A great quick fun canyon to do.

Plan on webbing and hardware, floods seem to wipe the drainage of existing anchor material. Warm winter day, no neoprene or windbreaker, felt very hot. No mandatory swimmers, though higher water levels would require a chest deep wade (I am 5'11"). It only hit my calves. Optional swimmers adjacent to the final rappel and exit should you get too toasty. Very leisurely pace allowed for a very enjoyable canyon experience. While I still rated this canyon as a 2 star, the geology and sustained water presence make this canyon very enjoyable. I was surprised with how much fun I had (downclimbs very pleasant too). There was no algae, though some developing in an unavoidable wade that will surely turn in a few weeks. Despite there being no consistent through flow up top, many springs seem to continue to provide flow in intermediate points in the canyon. There was really only 1 mandatory rappel, though the first downclimb will leave you questioning if you are new to slick granite and soloing (easy with a partner assist). The rest of the rappels are not technically downclimbable but bypassable. I found the granite in some of the sections beautifully striped. There is an easy exit with a use trail just before the exit provided in the kml, I may update it if I can. This may be the source of the aforementioned disgruntled attitude towards the canyon. Otherwise, if you take your time, you should not have many issues picking your way through the cactus and thorn. I would recommend this canyon to any who have patience. Oh, and your minivan or sedan or what you have will absolutely make it to the trailhead. No issues. Very well graded road, simply go when dry.

Barely technical canyon with just one short rappel, but this was a nice canyon, good narrows, fun (though relatively short) downclimb challenges. Very little water in the canyon - just 2 pools with a few easy moves to stem over.

Shallow avoidable pools. Lots of spiders along the walls. Some spicy downclimbs. Encountered a juvenile owl in the watercourse (not yet fledged) which we were able to climb up and around. Explored Turkey Creek for a bit and saw some deer and coatimundi. Combined with Canyon Sem Decepção and Upper Turkey Creek narrows for a high value day.
Lots of avoidable pools. It was a bit of a game of keeping my feet dry. Only 1 small rap but the narrows here made this one a delight
Might as well do this one if you're already here for Sem Alma. Although I found the Turkey Creek narrows to be more worth the time.
Would call it 2.5 if we could. It's not a banner route, but fun enough for what it is - an easy, noncommitting route with some fun drops, close to the east valley.

Accessed via Tanner / Beamer Trails and combined with Lava. The cairned bypass trail at the one drop is well established and clearly seeing heavy use. No water was present in Carbon. Floated from Carbon back to Tanner with a light boat and portaged the larger rapids (of which there are several). Didn't see a single other hiker on this trip - only boaters.
Light flow, very cold water probably from snow melt. Rebuilt all anchors. Dirt road getting out there is very rough, requiring heavy-duty 4x4.

Did an alternate approach from the top of the drainage which added 2 extra rappels. Fiddled these with a boulder for the first and a cairn for the 2nd. Rebuilt the last rap anchor in the canyon to get rid of the terrible rope pull from the 130ft rappel. This is now a ~65ft free hanging rappel into the alcove from a deadman anchor with webbing extended over the edge and courtesy rigging. Would highly recommend this route over the original anchor described in the beta on ropewiki.

Aborted, for reasons I believe are worth reporting. Hiked up to the “Option A” level, not wanting to throw hands with owls. No anchor at R1. Thought maybe I was in wrong spot, scouted west a bit, found a cairn anchor at top of next ravine over, set pretty far back from lip. Got the difficult pull I expected, and the cracks rap was prob more like 70-80. Advancing down this drainage, I reached the next drop. It had grooves forming, but no anchor. Looked like a good sandtrap spot, but I didn’t have mine on me. The next drop also had no anchor, but likely enough sand. Thankfully, I was able to class 4 my way up the fin between this and what turned out to be the real route. Took a good look around, still couldn’t find any anchor. Natural options are limited - it’s all super crumbly Navajo sandstone, eroding more into wafers than blocks. Decided that soloing this route without a sandtrap or similar gear would be unwise, and walked off. So come ready to build all anchors, as I can’t speak for the other 2.
It was a little muddy, but you could stay on ledges for the most part. There were more people than I expected. Very pretty narrows and fun spot to check out on the way through.

Be prepared to farm an anchor at the one rappel. The final narrow section is pretty and had a few waist depth pools, but remainder of the canyon was dry and disinteresting. It was hot as hell outside so I turned around at the rappel. Also, a bug flew into my eye and I'm fairly sure (in writing this three days later) that it's still in there. Not that fun.
Descended this canyon then packrafted the salt to the takeout at the 288 Bridge. The section on the Salt River is the highlight fun class 2 sections beautiful float out.

Descended Chalk Creek to the river and packrafted about 7.5 miles to the 288 bridge. Enough water for packrafts at 135 CFS and a few sections of mellow Class II. A trickle of water through Chalk and pools less than waist depth. Thick brush at the mouth of the canyon.

Frozen waterfalls and ice rivers made for an exceptionally pretty and interesting run. The only unfrozen pool was waist depth and required a short, but frigid wade that involved breaking through a thin sheet of ice. R5 was coated with a beautiful curtain of ice with large icicles formed at the base. Loose rock was cleared from the top ~40’ of R1, but caution should still be used here. All webbing new. Exit drainage was scenic and had several easy climbing obstacles. Try and catch this one in flowing or winterized conditions for the best experience!

If you can catch this with the beauty of the ice it is a truly special experience. If in good flow I feel this could be 4 stars, the exit packs in a lot of beauty as well. One pool was a mandatory wade but short. Overall really enjoyable and fun canyon.

Super quick, easy approach. We did the first 3 raps, then down climbed the rest. Lots of really interesting rock/water features. The exit drainage has some neat stuff as well. At one point on the exit, you'll be forced to go high then drop back down. We chose the sneak GPS exit route. Lots of loose rock and dirt, but nothing terrible. We thought this exit route would be easier than boulder hopping and following a road to get out. We enjoyed the canyon, but it would be even more interesting with some water flow.
People will thrash through every drainage in the Anchas instead of going to therapy.
2 in 1 because exit canyon is more pretty than the technical one. We changed the webbing on R 1,2,4,5. Deep pools, all easily avoidable except the R 3. We bypassed the whole thing on DCL.

The water was cold, but it was a warm, sunny day. We had a relaxing few hours in the creek and also cleaned up some of the anchors. This canyon could be best enjoyed by advanced canyoners just a little earlier in the season, or perhaps after a rain.

We ran this canyon after it had rained continuously for the past 2 days. Flows were moderate high but most of the rappels are out of the water/lower flow section of the waterfalls. Great canyon to take beginners. Those more advanced may want to adding anchors that will set the rappel line directly in the water course for more fun. The last rappel (going through the chute) is fun!

The approach route is still flagged at the beginning and is well-flagged at the end, but the trail is hard to follow in some places in between, we definitely lost some time to route-finding. Flow level was good, most rappel anchors are currently set for dry or nearly-dry rappels; in retrospect it would've been more fun to re-set them to go down the watercourse. Very little debris/brush in the canyon, mostly easy (albeit slippery) walking between drops.

We chose this creek for the purpose of playing around with rigging systems and practicing new skills, so we took our time. We also definitely lost 45 minutes to route finding in the beginning. The creek was running enough to be fun but not at all intimidating. Definitely a good beginner waterfall canyon, though the slippery blocky rappels were a bit heady for the climbers in our group who are used to dry rock and would be quite heady for someone new to rappelling and rope management. Still, everyone had a lot of fun, tried out some new things and got to gain experience in a pretty benign canyoning environment.

The new Trail up is in great condition. Checked all webbing and did not need to replace any. Placing bolts in the future would improve safety and rappel routes. Anchors were easy to locate. We ran the canyon with 3 rope teams, constantly moving ahead to set the next rappel. Hike up with a pack will test your legs but the scenery and quick succession of rappels made it worth while. Rocks not that slippery compared to Hall creek next canyon over.

The newer trail up is in great shape and dead ends at first rappel. Brushed some of the branches out on the way down, for easier pulls. This canyon could use the benefit of a couple bolts. Beta is good, water is cold. There is no swims, waste deep at best, and a couple of good shower style rappels, with options to stay dryer on more slick rocks. Third trip this year :)

I was lured by the "58 and sunny" forecast and envisioned myself rappelling in moderate-high flow with sunshine greeting me at the bottom of each drop... but if I had used my head I would have realized that of course a north facing creek doesn't get a single minute of sun on November 1. Definitely a dry suit day and two of us were in wetsuits. We stayed just above shivering but only by choosing the dry lines. The water was very cold. Still, it was nice to get out canyoning. I'm skeptical that the stream gauges mean much for flow indication in this creek, so I'll share the recent weather patterns: In the middle two weeks of October, North Bend got something like 7" of rain. The water tables were definitely saturated. This past week has been dry and sunny, with the exception of 1/4" rain early Friday morning. With a 1 mile catchment, the flows drop quickly - just a week of dry weather and it was a solid moderate flow. Change creek is really improving thanks to the efforts of a handful of folks in the community. Great to see well-placed bolts taking the place of some natural anchors that were causing quite a bit of erosion.

First time down Change Creek for the group. Started hiking up at ~10am; fully geared-up at the top by 11am; and back at the car for 2pm. We spotted the reference point from the path (and even had cell signal to compare it to ropewiki). We had fun getting pounded in the (cold) flow, but it wasn't a problematic volume, nor was it unavoidable if desired. Probably at the limit of an average wet suit, I was glad to have a dry suit on. Despite a fun & fast day we still had some rope grief. The rope wouldn't pull on R2, so had to be ascended and re-rigged with less friction. Then on R3 we core-shot the rope on the sharp lip at the top, so we "creeped" the ropes from then on. An all round great (half) day out!

The trail is in good condition, well marked. It had rained hard the night before so the creek was full at the parking lot. Not a real issue because most rappels have the option to go to the side of the flow. Stuck a rope on the large rappel on the multi stage, but were able to release it because there was a rope going down the dry side and folks still up top to give it a yank. Really like this canyon for beginners

good, friendly flow. Note: R2 does not need webbing, and the bolts are best left unlinked. While it's out of the main flow, it's not out of the path of potential winter flood damage. If a tree limb gets caught on webbing left behind, it will destroy these bolts. Please leave them unlinked. The rope pulls just fine if you back away from the base of the rappel for a good pull angle.
Good conditions. Even though water is low, it still made a fun outing. 3 of us had 3mm wetsuits on, while 2 people did not wear a wetsuit.

Fun flow right now! There were huge floods in Change Creek this winter/spring and a lot of new logs and debris in the canyon. We spent time cleaning up and sawing away the most annoying stuff. R1 anchor was buried under 4” of gravel. R3 flow has been completely diverted to DCL with no flow at the rootball anchor, due to new tree fall that caused gravel to build up DCR. R7 bolts were buried by a landslide and fallen trees. We excavated them but we do NOT recommend using them as after we moved on we saw rockfall coming down right where the bolts were — use the DCR tree instead and link R7 and R8. We cleared the approach trail of overgrowth, checked and fixed up all of the anchors, and the canyon’s good to go.

Water levels were on the low side, but we had a great time taking someone very new to canyoning through the creek. Anchors were in great condition (thanks, Becca). Compared to last year, there's been some upheaval with tree and rock falls in the lower part of the canyon. The approach is also a bit more of a slog than it was in the past, thanks to giant fallen trees. We were grateful for the flagging a couple of times where the trail was hard to spot. At R8 the bolt station is very exposed. We opted to rig a traverse line from a log instead of multi-staging from the R7 anchors.
Great day in the canyon. High flow was very fun.

Figured I’d check out flow on a rainy day. Was still low, but not very low as it would’ve been otherwise. Probably not worth doing aside from beginner trips until it rains more. Quick afternoon trip, all anchors in good condition. Very slippery
Fun day in low flow conditions introducing some new people to the sport! As other reports mention, there are a lot of downed trees on both the hike in and the creek walk out.
Fun day with mostly clear skies and good flow. Hiked directly up canyon right (UCR). Avoided water on R5. Fun with the water flowing.
Rap 1 rappeller’s left bolt spins a little. Placed webbing DCR prior to rap 3 bolts, otherwise all anchors in good shape.
More water than we thought there would be. Had to bypass one rappel that woulda been dangerous due to flow levels. Great conditions to play in the water.
Nice place on a hot day. All anchors seemed to be in good condition. Flow was low but still fun. Attempted to tighten the spinner on the last rappel to no avail.
Flow was fun, things were slightly less slippery than usual. Anchors were in good/useable condition although the webbing for R1 should be replaced soon.

Fun day with descent flow in nice weather. First time in this canyon, anchors in good shape but seems some are missing, maybe a local can update beta. Quite slippery but sounds like that's normal for this canyon. We used this as a training op for newer folks. R1: did not see boulder webbing, used the traverse line bolts and the deviation. R3: did not see Option 2 webbing, rigged a dry line off a tree DCL for one person but removed it after. R5: both anchors in good shape, pushed a log off that was in the rap line for the first 15 ft. R6: has really sharp rock on both anchors. If using DCR anchor recommend going off the rock ledge further DCR. Might be nice to find a better anchor location.

Practice rigging day for a very inexperienced group trying to shake off some rust after an off-season. All anchors in great shape except: R1 webbing in good shape, quicklink may need replacement soon (surface rust with some small pit Several bolted anchors could use a little TLC with hammer/wrenching (a handfull of spinners on a single bolt of a pair. no obvious rust or concerns.)

Couldn’t find a flagged trail at all. The landscape forced us down into the creek at the bottom of the big drop. Bit of a bummer because it was RIPPING! R6 was a blast. At this flow definitely stay on rappel between R7 and R8. Definitely need to return with someone who knows the approach trail as the lines on CalTopo weren’t helpful after leaving the Mt Washington climbers trail.

2 hrs on the approach, 4 hrs in canyon. There has to be a better way of approach. There’s no "trail", flagged or otherwise, where the kml lists it. Slopes are covered in deadfall, soil has been significantly weakened over the winter and cliff bands force one to constantly switch between hiking down towards the creek and scrambling up away from the drop in. People who care about this as a training venue need to do some serious work reestablishing the “alternate flagged trail”. I'm claiming "special challenges" based solely on the approach. Glad I came back to finish it in its entirety, most rappels are delightful albeit very slippery. The pool between R7 and R8 is now full of wood. All pre existing anchors are unharmed. We left two webbing anchors up creek from the established drop in as that’s where our “trail floundering” dropped us in and we needed to do some nuisance raps. Could we have ghosted them, sure, but I opted for guaranteed clean pulls. Great flow nonetheless.
No water. Some snow. Cut off an ugly stretch of webbing coated with flood debris from the two bolts at R7.
All anchors in good condition, some other group must have gone through it since the September flash floods.

Looked like most of the cairn anchors had been wiped out by the storm the week prior. However we did see a car at the trailhead the day before and all of the cairn anchors/webbing looked brand new. I'm guessing the part before us replaced a good portion of the webbing. Crossing the Virgin River was about knee deep.

All natural anchors are in great shape. Most are "rock cairn" type anchors which is basically a deadman anchor "buried" in rocks rather than sand. You should know how to evaluate this type of anchor for safety before you go. The webbing will need to be replaced on occasion and so you should be comfortable with this arrangement. There are some tree raps but these are smaller trees or wrapping several smaller trunks together which also need evaluation. Some rock anchors as well. There are multi level raps in this canyon. This requires more rope than a straight drop. We used a 65 M (213ft) rope that covered everything. If you take a 200 foot rope you will need to extend the pull side with webbing etc.. for a couple of drops. The old report of 80 foot max rap is no longer valid.

Perfect day to run the canyon. Water level in the river was mid calf. The natural condition. We rebuilt all of the cairn anchors as they were covered in gravel and sand could not be properly evaluated. should not be properly evaluated.
Fun canyon. Crossing the river was easy, then a little nav to get up to the drop-in point. Lots of rappels. Pretty fun day out.
super fun canyon, didnt really expect this for the area, river was almost knee deep. something like 15 raps in fairly quick succesion. the hike up wasnt too bad either, fairly worn trail all the way.

Fun canyon for sure. We began the approach at sunrise and were back to the cars late afternoon. 15 Raps. Added element of challenge as every anchor was a natural anchor. We inspected and rebuilt each and every one of them, one of them twice. The creek water level was about knee/thigh deep to cross at the beginning and end.
River was only ankle deep. Leaves were beginning to turn orange. Very beautiful.

Did Portal the day before. We went up the drainage according to the GPX route. We took an obvious trail we saw that day up instead of the drainage. It was much faster and easier. Replaced a couple of deadman anchors but otherwise, all anchors are good. Fun canyon. I gave it a 5 for the Virgin River Gorge area only.

A very nice canyon with great views. We experienced very favorable temps with just a long sleeve t-shirt. The river crossing was knee deep and about 40 feet wide. Could be done bare foot if needed as the river bottom is soft sand with some smooth rocks sprinkled about. We choose to wade through with water shoes and stash them on the other side. Ascent was 1.5 hours hiking at an easy pace but it is indeed strenuous. All GPS way points are spot on including where to park. Spent about an hour total filming and taking pics on the descent so our real moving time would have been about 4.5 hours at a leisurely pace car to car. All anchors were in place with seemingly brand new webbing. Current description of many anchor placements is fairly out of date as it appears that many of them have been rebuilt in different locations. All are very easy to find but don't rely upon posted drop distances as the anchor has position has quite likely changed. The 100 foot drop does appear to be slightly longer so a 200 foot rope likely will not make it without an extension or a separate pull. Every pull was non-dramatic.
River water was at about knee deep, but very cold. Passed a family with 3 girls under age 10 going through the canyon. Very impressive!

The Virgin River was running a little high and muddy after some rains, but still relatively easy to cross. The approach was mostly effortless following a trail along the ridge to the drop-in. The canyon itself was dry except for some tiny pools, and all anchors were in great condition. Someone has built some excellent cairn anchors (kudos) and all the webbing was fresh and in great condition.

A great canyon for a 7 person group with mixed levels! We used 3 ropes of lengths 90ft - 200ft and we could easily sequence as the rappels are pretty close together. The Virgin River crossing was about knee height, a few of us brought a pair of sandals or wetsuit socks for the crossing and left them by the river. We took the use trail up the ridge rather than following the GPS trail that stays in the wash. We did not get cliffed out, and that route is fine. We replaced the webbing on ~4 anchors. The air was chilly enough to not need an early start to avoid the heat, and we used light jackets in the shade of the canyon.

Virgin was flowing at about 90 cfs. Some snow on the upper part of the approach. Rebuilt a few cairns to allow for easier inspection, but existing webbing was all in decent condition. Encountered two other groups throughout the day.

Finding the drop in was a pain in the butt. GPS and that canyon just don't like each other, kept telling us we are on route to Portal. Pretty much just have to drop in the canyon on your right where Cherry splits off from Portal. Once we dropped in, it was easy breezy. The canyon's pretty and most of the raps are pretty simple. Thanks to all the long webbings, there's no pull issue. Contrary to the beta description, a few rappels do have bolted anchors. The river crossing at this time was mild and below knee deep.

River Crossing was super easy, about 60 cfs, maybe mid shin deep. Nice Sandy bottom, so easy to cross barefoot. A nice social trail has formed on much of the approach trail. We lost it towards the top, but the approach was overall scenic and peasant (albeit steep). All anchors in place and well built except the final drop, which is easily bypassed DCL. Webbing in fair condition. Several anchors could have webbing replaced soon. With cooler weather in Zion this week, this was a great alternate option!
Great trip! Weather was nice. No water. River was easy to cross.

Great day, perfect weather and temps. Virgin River flow was about 80cfs (knee deep at crossing), climb was a little tiring (as expected), anchors and webbing were all in good condition (near new) with the exception of a couple where we replaced webbing or improved cairns. Of note: - Rap 9: Don’t love the courtesy anchor setup. If I return, I’ll setup a frost knot rather than the hard-to-inspect second strand of webbing and knot tied to the original strand. - Rap 12a: Built a quick cairn anchor at the start of the second section of narrows (before the actual 12). Calling it 12a - which is optional since you can down climb and proceed to the actual R12. If I did it again I’d move it back a bit for better angle and then make a V setup for both easier inspection and steeper start angle of the webbing. Someone else can fix it. - Rap 14: Don’t love the tight webbing and ring. Needs more slack to minimize the force on the webbing with minimal angle for force distribution. If we had more time, would have redone with closer to a 60 degree (or better) angle. - Rap 15: Added another line of webbing to the existing quicklink/rapide and the short ring of webbing around the edge of a boulder against a slab. Didn’t love the original setup so added some redundancy with the second strand girth hitched around a larger and further up boulder.

Cherry was a very fun canyon! All anchors are in good condition when inspected. The Virgin River is cold, but no wetsuit is needed. It was just under waist high for a 6' male. Watch out for R12. Our rope got stuck, and I had to ascend. The cairn may want to be shifted slightly more to face LDC, as the RDC is pretty pinchy with the rope and biners.
After hitting S55, decided to hit Cherry. Approach took 1 hr, canyon took 1.5 hrs, and exit was 29 minutes to get back to the car. All webbing in good condition.

Agree Cherry is a great canyon! Definitely recommend a test pull on R10 (R11?) as there are two obvious cracks in succession. One crack may be eliminated if rigging to the cairn anchor at the edge of the drop, which requires an awkward and somewhat exposed downclimb about 6ft into the crack to access (which I did tethered/belayed…see picture attached), only to climb back up and opt for a tree/bush about 10ft up-canyon right (we replaced the webbing on this one). Likely due to the awkwardness in getting good rocks down, I found the cairn somewhat lacking. However, even with test pulling the rope when rappelling anchored to the tree up-canyon right, we almost stuck a rope but fortunately were able to whip it out. High was about 95degF - we started at 0700 to avoid the heat for the approach, which was mostly straightforward if you generally head towards the correct ridge. Made it to the start of the canyon by 0845. The canyon was plenty shaded until it opened up around R11...felt warm, but not too bad. Virgin River was running at about 50cfps and made for about a shin-deep easy and refreshing crossing.

No other anchors were found, so possible first descent. Dropped in upstream of the narrows to catch several rappels. R2 is excellent, dropping into a neat grotto. Would be fun to find a line to run the flow. The remainder are mostly boulder problems, requiring the odd nuisance rappel to get down. Suddenly, Chikamin narrows up with walls rising vertically 60-80ft on both sides. Gorgeous narrows and very photogenic, but not very technical. Needs more jumps and swimming! Easy thrash back up to the road.

Time is car to car. Super scenic and fun; high pay to play. Highs about 90, did not bring nor want wetsuits despite some swims. One of the swims was truly revolting and left us smelling like cow shit. Still - a very beautiful day. Used sand traps several times and a water anchor once, instead of the knot chock.

Clear skies, winds variable 5-10 mph, Air temp 72F, water temp aprx 45F Large group size with new canyoneers unprepared for slippery surfaces, jumps, swims etc slowed the group down and made our descent about a 5 hour trip. The was is always cold as its up in elevation above 6k and snow melt. The canyon was flowing what I would have assumed to be Medium flow according to the reference photos and was higher than the last three times at the same time of year I have descended it. No major challenges where present but checking water depth as always can be critical in places as the water can be murky. Two groups lost gear in the canyon including a wallet and a camera.

Busy Saturday, 3 groups in the canyon at that time, raps have multiple anchors for each group, felt comfy(not cold) in 4/3 + 3mil jacket, rainshell, 5 mil booties, 2mil gloves with 2nd layer glove, even wit hydrolace shoes had a lot of slipping on granite & green algae, raps in water are heavy but alternate anchors available if some groups prefer to avoid flow
Didn't use our rope. Jumped every Rappel.

Water was quite cold - wetsuit highly recommended. Always check jump landing zones before jumping - one spot was more shallow than we remembered where a jump in the wrong spot could have resulted in injury. You really don't go through the waterfall on the last rappel anymore, this drop changed a bit about 2 years ago - still nice, though.

Absolutely FREEZING cold. The water was probably in the 30's. We camped nearby and our water gallons froze solid overnight. The low rating reflects the lack of technical challenges and the cold water.
flow is like mid summer flow. water cold. windy; wind jackets over wetsuits recommended. encountered no problems with any of the jumps.
We jumped the last two rappels. Water was brown, but not quite chocolate milk. Cold in the morning, but warm during the day. Wetsuits advised, but not required.
Water temps great and water is very clear. Water level is still a little high for beginners, rappelled only first rappel in the water flow.

Time includes lazing around on rocks and jumping multiple times at some of the drops. Flow was enough to be fun. One of us wore a shorty 3mm and the other went without and was a bit cold a times but it was easy to warm up in the June sun. All but the first rap can be jumped but we rapped in the flow because we brought a rope and why not? Could probably downclimb the first rap RDC but it looked a little sketchy. Pickle hole is way cool

The Water level is still high enough for the jumps. The water was cold enough that the wetsuit made it nice and comfortable. I Ran it solo so I either rapped or downclimbed where it was faster to get around people. Lots of people were out and watching a couple of groups made for some cringe worthy moments. I spent lots of time taking in the scenery as well as a bit of people watching. There were also some friendly friendly climbers near the exit.

Canyon was in decent flow. There were a lot of guiding groups moving through the canyon and a decent amount of climbers at the end. The parking area was maxed out. Surprised to see so many people there although it's been a few years since I've done this canyon. The flow is still good enough for all jumps but not high enough to demand any special considerations.

Done with two adults and two kids (age 8 and 11). The kids wore wetsuits for comfort. Such a fun canyon, we spent a lot of time playing around in the pools and the natural slides. Water levels were very low but flowing. There were three waterfall rappels although one could be avoided from a bolt DCR. The rock was very slippery in many spots. Really fun day to play around in the water.
Water temps are perfect. No wetsuits. Enjoyed having the canyon to ourselves on a sunday! jumping was fun!
Most spots are jumpable. descent flow. no major issues or reports. no wetsuits= no problem. Enjoy!

This was the first technical canyon for a couple in the group. We did this without wetsuits. The pools were cool, but the warm air temp and sun balanced that out. A bolt has been added RDC on the final rappel which replaced the previous pinch point set up. The 10ish ft. exit climb (off the house sized boulder) has a wasp nest at the top that was disturbed. Avoid them by moving down canyon another 50ft or so and scrambling up RDC to exit.

Started off following the powerline track, then went through a gate and along the highway until we picked up the trail and the special "canyoneer's gate" through the fence. Followed obvious trail, staying off private land, to the drop-in. Water was very cold today, so we didn't dawdle. Did all rappels out of the water. Super fun canyon. Will have to come back again on a nicer day to run the flow. Got a bit confused on the exit trail... think we went upward when we should have stayed low near the watercourse.

Approach via the road was very straightforward. Wore a 5mm wetsuit and was comfortable. Others in the group were cold in their 3mms. Saw multiple other parties out today, including some climbers near the exit. Super fun, accessible canyon. Great that the flow is reliable even during an extreme drought.
Great day! A little windy but enough flow to have fun but not scary.
Wetsuits required. All anchors and webbing good condition. Some spots had a few algae mats.

Slightly overcast weather but air temperature was warm. The group managed fine in 3/2 wetsuits. Be cautious on some of the jumps (i.e. very first jump opportunity at start of canyon), water level is a bit low. Had the canyon to ourselves today. Also pulled some trash that had been left behind. Pack it out people!

The pools were definitely cold, but we went through them wearing shorts and t-shirts without any consequence. The air was warm enough that we dried off and warmed up quickly by the end. Water flow was fairly low, but still enough of a flow that the waterfalls required some caution. This was the first time either of us can gone canyoning and the very first time my girlfriend had gone rappelling. A great trip was had by both of us.

Another fun run of Christopher Creek. Water was pretty cold, but air temps were high, so definitely a good idea to get of the water and warm up on occasion. We ended out doing all rappels out of the water.

We celebrated Easter by running Christopher Creek and had an absolute blast! We had the entire canyon to ourselves. The water was high and cold but we were comfortable in full wet suits. The only thing that got painfully cold were our hands. The webbing at the pinch point on rap one needs to be replaced soon. All other anchors / webbing were in good condition. We stopped halfway through the route to eat some lunch and enjoy the scenery. We rappelled most of the waterfalls with the exception of the last one. There was a spot to jump LDC into a huge pool. It was the best decision until... my phone sank to the bottom of that pool... So that was a major buzz kill to an otherwise amazing trip. We will definitely be back to enjoy this route again.

Flow has lowered significantly in the past few weeks but is still fun. Wore a 5mm wetsuit + rain jacket and was comfortable. Everyone in the group who wore 3mm suits was cold. All rappels were done outside of the watercourse due to having a few beginners and one first-timer. One member of the group jumped all rappels.
Always a good time. Anchors are good and in abundance. Beta on this page is quite a bit to decipher. New exit alternative will be added to the kml.

Ran twice in 3 days. Plan was to move to Rim canyons, but fire closure… Did Sun Bear, then the boys wanted to come back and cool off in this one again. Everyone loved this canyon. Standard “first guy raps, checks depth, then jumping is an option of deep enough” procedure worked well (and kept them from jumping into one spot that looked deep, but would have broken legs). Hiking along highway is dull, but very quick. All anchors and pulls were great. Flow high enough, and rocks slick enough to make on-rappel ragdolling/left hip sliding preferable to standard rappel posture at several drops.

The right bolt on the first rap is starting to spin. I'm not sure if it can be wrenched because I didn't have a wrench to try. It's not very loose, but it does move. *Please ensure that the webbing on the first anchor is tied in a way that is redundant and won't add undue force on the good bolt should there be a failure of the right bolt.* People have been tying death triangles and sliding/magic X's on these bolts and they aren't the safest for the situation.

Water was great! All webbing good, hangar is able to spin as mentioned before but the bolt itself does not move, rapped and were safe. Alternative bolts are available at this rappel as well. We ended up doing 7 rappels and skipping all the jumps, there is an additional rappel prior to R1, maybe able to downclimb but water felt great and made it a fun intro for the beginners.
Water was murky due to recent rain, nice and warm with a air temp of 81 and sunny, looks like the issue with the first webbing had been addressed
Had the choco milk look from recent rain, and flow was nicely up a bit, but still beginner friendly. The combo slide-jump on DCL at the last drop is fun!

Hot weather and (very!) cold pools made for an amazing day. Water was a bit cloudy so be sure to check every jump. Most people chose to rappel but it's possible to jump everything. Anchors are in good shape. R4 slide is a highlight!
was comfy in my 5mm but too cold in a 3/2. slippery. skipped the last rap and scrambled out, steep but doable.

Team of two, took us five hours, we took our time and just enjoyed the day. I run warm, I had a 4/3, and was good the whole day. My friend had a 5mm and she got a little cold on the swimmer between the 3rd and 4th rap. Even though it's early in the season, the water level was the lowest I've seen it, so be cautious when jumping.
My first report. T'was a great day. Water was chilly but with a 3mm shorty, I was fine. If you run cool, a full suit would be better.
Water was not muddy and pretty warm, I regretted bringing my shortie.
No water. Christopher Creek had a very small trickle at the top that disappeared by the end into stagnant pools with a film and bubbly algae. All of the waterfall rappels were dry.

Low-moderate, nonthreatening flow of relatively clear and cold water for this place. Nice to be able to see bottom more and bark our shins less than usual. Team was in everything from a 3mm shortie to 5mm full. We were all chilled by halfway or so, which made us more water-avoidant than I like, choosing out-of-watercourse raps and rarely jumping. Still, great weather! One of our group forgot my 5mm full body Henderson suit in or near the parking lot. In the unlikely event the finder sees this, I’d love to get it back! https://youtu.be/iYpp7mc5BhY?si=QuY5usZCJMeCxXha
65 degree day, partial overcast, cooler water temps, but 5/4's kept most of us warm. Several of the women in the group got chilled hands and feet around the 4th rap. Fun flow. Fun day.
4.5 hours car to car. Wetsuits ranged from 2.5mm shorty up to 4/3, with the 4/3 folks being much more comfortable. Low flow.
We did the canyon by hiking up from the bottom, bypassing around the falls then descending back down through the watercourse. Water flow was around 12 CFS and everyone thought it was perfect.

The low water (12 cfs) for this time of year made for some great opportunities. We were able to be the first group to rappel into the final waterfall. We also found an additional hallway above the final fall that can be rappelled off a rock into the watercourse, or also carefully stemmed. (There is an alternate jump into the pool below, or the traditional walk around). Anthony was very kind to slow us as much freedom as we wanted to play and create technical elements to challenge.

Water was at least 10F degrees greater than Tank and most of the canyon is sunny making it much warmer. Water flow was low enough that jumping (on rope) across the flow on R1 was not needed, but good practice and fun anyway. We rigged a hand line from a single glue-in bolt RDC before the corridor which helped getting to the submerged footing RDC beyond the lip (above siphon). We spent an hour getting everyone down the corridor, stemming down to the pool below; most did arms to feet stem, I did wide leg split stem. Note: LMAR needs to pull hand line from top of 10 foot fall in the corridor (just beyond siphon), because excess rope hanging over the 10 foot fall will create too much rope drag, and the rope end may get sucked down the siphon. Cibecue took 5.5 hours, 10.5 hours included Tank and Cibecue.

A great day with a great team running the 2nd descent of this canyon to complete the final few rappels in the canyon that we bypassed the first time around due to high water flow and a late day. Water was much more manageable and we were able to rappel multiple features that we bypassed the first time.

Another great canyon. Hike up could be flagged but able to follow track from Jakes trip. Steep hike up, but quick to put in. R4 access and transfer over to the rappel is not easy. Very slipper and errors will hurt you. I sat on station for all 6 folks, making sure transfer went well. My hand saw fell off my belt near R2, if you find one at lower levels. We were able to combine this with Bottleneck canyon

Great day! Conditions looked just slighlty lower than the moderate water reference picture, which is a great flow for beginners. R4 at the hanging belay is now unlinked - there was a Y-hang that used to be at R4, but we removed it since the webbing was rubbing pretty badly on the rock. Very important that next team in can manage the traverse and unlinked bolts properly. Next team in should definitely bring a wrench. The R4 nut and hanger on the right was loose (bolt still good) and only hand-tightened (we forgot a wrench), but the left bolt/hanger was solid. There are small, 5mm quicklinks from the Y-hang left on the hanger; it would be great if the next team could switch those out for larger 8mm quicklinks (sorry, we couldn't loosen it without a wrench). We could not find R7, assumed tree root may have slipped down due to erosion. But the webbing that was added on 7/17/2021 was fine. Please note, going that route is tiered, so be sure to continue down to the second tier as that second tier is not bolted.

Followed the faint trail up to intersect the road and dropped in below R0. Some very enjoyable rappels and cool water features (ex: climbing down though the siphon and the Rooster Tail - a human-sized water spout). We took our time and had fun. We didn't see the anchor and accidentally downclimbed R3. Ooops. I wasn't fond of the anchor station at R4 -- it's awkward and management feels unnecessarily complicated. (Were I to run it again, I'd ponder finding a better anchor location higher up.) We were planning to replace the quick links on R4 with larger ones (i.e. turn it into an unlinked station), but the bolts are positioned for a Y-hang, so we ultimately decided to re-establish the webbing. The anchor is not subject to flood events and abrasion didn't seem to be an issue in its new position. Folks running the creek currently should *definitely* know how to rig retrievable safety lines and be comfortable managing a hanging rappel station.
I don't recommend this one. Its a really long drive on a bad road. I didn't like the sloping rappels. The hanging anchor position is not good and makes it time consuming and overly complicated.

No anchors were found, so possible first descent. Easy shortie creek; nothing too remarkable. R2 is the real reason to run this one where the creek plunges into a 80ft slot. Would have been more fun with some more water. Probably better earlier in the season.

Visiting from Utah, I decided to do the old hike up to Cora Lake that I used to do as a kid. I brought my rope with me as even when I was little I thought it would be fun to descend through the creek. I figured the creek was pretty mild and would be a good transition from the desert canyons I am used to. I hiked up, swam around in the lake for a while and then headed down the creek. There are plenty of trees that can be slung for anchors so I didn't leave any gear. Though I did almost stick my rope on the log in Rap 2.

The single bolt and hanger for the 240' rappel was sheared off from potentially a previous flash flood. Extended the previous anchor from the 30' rappel directly above it an extra 10' with webbing. Ended up with approx. 260' rappel. Recommend carrying a 300' rope or planning on replacing bolts.

Cortright turned out to be a delightful descent with neat pools, cool geology, and much luscious green moss. It would probably be run all the time if there was a nice exit after the final rappel. (Would probably give it another star.) The exit down Cool Creek is a nice forested canyon, but is more of a creek walk / float and somewhat lacking in technical interest. Recommend swiftwater training as there's strong current in places. We saw a number of spawning salmon.

The upper Tapeats narrows are short, easy and scenic and well worth the quick detour to check out if you're in the area. The lower basement rock narrows are not as scenic or fun as others, and contain three difficult bypasses, the second of which I found to be quite horrible. This second bypass takes you a few hundred feet above the canyon on a long, steep, crumbly, climby and very difficult to follow traverse before dropping back down into the canyon below. The exposure on this route isn't all that bad except for a spot or two, but it is very complex, the way isn't obvious at all in many spots (the cairns are easier to follow on the way down canyon than the way back) and on my visit a few cairns led to more difficult and dangerous options than were available. I made the mistake of taking the gully that was cairned to go back up, this gully has a few 20 foot vertical climbs, whereas the next gully down canyon is much more straight forward and safe. Even though it isn't all that long (mileage-wise anyway, it still took something like 45 friggin' minutes one way), I thought this route was more frustrating, difficult and dangerous than either the Eminence Break or Scotty's hollow exit routes. There is a bolt on canyon left at the top of the first 25 foot drop - if I were to do this one again (I won't) I would consider fixing ropes on the few drops in the canyon and ascending if going back up canyon. Heed the parks warnings about ravens and take your food or anything that could be mistaken food with you if hiking the canyon from a base camp - or leave it behind in a bomb-proof container.

Reached the confluence with Burro Canyon in 9 hours on day one, finished the canyon, float and hike out in 9 hours again on day 2. Very little water in the canyon, we only encountered one pool where we had to get our feet wet, about knee deep, springs were relatively dry, no wet suits needed for the canyon. One small, but decent pool under a rock at Burro confluence. Reconstructed many terrifyingly poorly tied/constructed anchors throughout the canyon - this is not a great canyon to do if you are not very familiar with anchor construction. Wet suits are needed for the packraft, the river was quite cold and had a few wild rides through the riffles where all in the party took on quite a bit of water in their rafts - fun! Found the canyon narrows to be good, but short with long sections of hiking in between. *Additional notes by Danielson: Check with locals about road conditions ahead of time. Google Maps sent those of us coming from LA down a road that doesn't exist anymore. It would be best to have a map that is not dependent on cell phone signal. Temperatures above the rim at night were mid 20s Fahrenheit, and not fun; however, it was enjoyable during the day. While the temperature at Phantom Ranch was reported to be 30F the night we spent at the Burro confluence, it felt like a comfortable mid 40s in the canyon, and the group had no complaints. As Pat states above, water was scarce and green at the Burro camp, but about an 1hr 45min before Burro there is another nice flat camp spot DCR on a rock slab where more water was available. The raft trip was pretty chilly. I think everyone was cold in 4/3 wetsuits. I had a full rainshell over my wetsuit and was ok except for my hands (in retrospect I would consider adding the weight of some nice neogloves). The raft trip was far rougher than what I expected. I spent most of the time bailing my raft out with a mug I clipped to my backpack. One group member flipped twice, so by prepared for a swim. The exit up is fun. not. We counted 14 rappels which I believe is +1 to Todd's book.

A really beautiful canyoneering backpacking trip in the Grand! We aired down our tires and drove about 2hrs on dirt to get out to Lava Falls TH where we left our shuttle then drove over to Tuckup TH to start the approach. The roads start out graded then progressively get worse. Lots of sharp limestone rock notorious for slashing tires further on. Heavy duty offroad tires and airing down highly recommended. High clearance 4x4 needed to negotiate some small obstacles, ruts, and rock steps near Tuckup/Lava. Approach is 7.5miles and took us 3 hours to get to our chosen campsite, half a mile from drop in. The site we dubbed “TajMahal” featured multiple overhanging alcoves nestled around a drainage, perfect camping! It had rained recently so there were pools nearby to filter from. Last time my friends went, it hadnt rained in some time and they had to start down the descent before finding water… Next day we descended the canyon. There are some really neat sculpted rooms, some beautiful fluted drops, and a couple pools of water waist deep. Water wasnt cold, we were happy to have forgone the wetsuits! The canyon goes on and on much of it large open rock hopping or bouldery downclimbs with short narrowiah sections scattered between. A couple larger raps were particularly memorable. My favorite was the sculpted white narrows near the beginning. This was very pretty but over quick. We eventually arrived at the beach of the Colorado in 6 hours. We hiked up stream along the beach set up camp in a nook by the water where the river turns. The flow was up quite a bit after recent storms. In the morning of the next day we dragged our feet and eventually inflated our packrafts to paddle down river. We all were happy to have our Alpacka rafts to enjoy the playful rapids with carefree ease! The river was quick and despite trying to drag it out, we arrived at Lava Falls in 1 hour, 20 minutes. Where we hung out and watched other rafters tackle the infamous rapid. The exit out from Lava is just over a mile but is all straight up. It took us 2 hours to get back to our vehicles. Over all a very enjoyable and worthwhile trip!

Overall, this was an excellent canyon, and well worth repeating. I think it falls short of other five star canyons in the area though, as the majority of the rappels were short and not expecially beautiful by GC standards. Day one, our group of three hiked along the Esplanade and completed the first two rappels. Approaching on the Esplanade was generally straightforward, and the Tuckup Trail is overall well cairned. Day two, we descended into the Muav and stopped in the early afternoon at a beautiful camp in a large flat area with a trickling waterfall next to it, Just before the confluence with Burro Canyon. Highly recommend spending the night here if the timing works out for your group. Day three, we descended the rest of the canyon and completed the float.
