We did not have to portage any rapids/riffles despite the water level in the Colorado river being relatively low at 10,000 CFS. Day 4, we hiked out the sun-exposed and very hot lava Falls Trail. Highly recommend you get an early start on this one! Water: the canyon was overall in a very dry state, however there were still clear pools of high quality water starting in the Supai and extending all the way to the river, intermittently disappearing underground. We chose to carry two days worth of water in the beginning of the trip, though this was likely not necessary if we had been willing to hike a little bit farther on day one and get into the Supai. Wetsuits: not necessary for the canyon, but nice to have on the float in case one were to capsize. Other logistics: Definitely recommend the car shuttle if feasible. Two Subarus made the trip with airing down tires and using a spotter for the rockier sections. Shuttle not possible if there has been recent rain, as the section to lava Falls trailhead would become impassible with thick mud if wet. Anchors: this canyon requires excellent natural anchor skills. Our group cleaned lots of messy anchors with tan/green/red webbing. Please remember that only black webbing is permitted in the Grand Canyon for safety and visibility reasons, and please clean old tat off anchors when adding new webbing, don't just add a new layer of webbing on top of the old one. Have fun and be safe out there!

Possible first descent of the main fork on Covell Creek as no anchors or signs of previous passage were found. Access is excellent and this opens more options in the Covell complex. Anchors are a little thin in the upper main fork; the lack of significant vegetation along the sides indicates that this creek can really rip from time to time. Main fork joins the west fork below the confluence, so we enjoyed the narrows and rappelling Covell Creek Falls. If you have time, it's fun to continue down-canyon and hit some of the smaller drops too.

The rappels in this canyon are incredibly fun! Keep in mind that the flow will double once you get to R3. R3 is pushy in high flow. After R4, we hiked out on the trail. The overall time of 5 hours includes route finding, finding and building anchors. I wore about 8 mm in the core and was decently warm. However, other members in our group wore 5 mm and no gloves(!) and were fine.
Ran the creek. Dropped in higher than last time and it wasn’t worth it. Built new anchor DCL for R2 which makes it just shy of 200”

Flow was great today, but very cold per coming right out of the snowpack. Anchor for the multi-pitch at R2 is in a somewhat awkward location. Next time need to look at breaking up R1 into two shorter rappels. This would get you down to a nice platform right at the top of the big falls which would probably be a better place to stage a group without tearing up the slope. The big falls was a joy to rappel. The lower narrows and exit rappel also were great. Found a sign just adjacent to Covell Falls requesting hikers to please stay on the trail, so next time we need to stay in the watercourse and continue down-canyon instead (will add +2 short rappels that looked fun). Lots of hikers about. We were informed that we would be going on someone's facebook page for sure. :-) A great little canyon!
Dropped in high, has potential for slide rappels. Also has high potential for very public moss scraping on scenic touristy waterfalls. One and done.
Big falls was completely frozen over. All anchors good. Lots of ice. Was plan C for the day. Old flagging on exit is now a full trail back to the parking area.

Running at low now. Note there's a rigging for an R0 access rap DCR just after the suit up area. Don't confuse that for R1. We also dislodged a very large rock in the middle of Angel Falls. Luckily people and ropes were out of the way. BC-R2 (our final rap) was the highlight.

Canyon terrain seems drastically different from the last reported trip. Landslide/blowdowns have clogged the canyon with debris. Handful of anchors needed to be rebuilt. Specifically, the R2 Option B (rootball) anchor has been washed away and the surrounding terrain didn’t provide for an alternative anchor placement, so we opted for Option A DCL. The fallen log anchor for BC-R3 has snapped and is no longer a viable anchor point, so we opted to bypass the final two rappels and exited the canyon directly after Covell Creek Falls. We were able to walk the rim of the canyon on the exit trail and peer into the remainder of the canyon (from BC-R3 through the creek walk exit) and the creek was filled with debris and log jams that would have easily added another hour to our exit if we had not exited at the bottom of Covell Creek Falls. Overall, a fun and interesting day of on-site problem solving but definitely exhausting having to navigate a canyon that felt log jammed start to finish.

One or two cold, borderline swimmer potholes. On this particular trip the final pothole was the closest water available between Crack Baby x Kanab Creek and Rattlesnake Canyon x Kanab Creek. Note that this is an observation only and should not be relied upon as a guarantee of future conditions or water availability. Research before you go!

Seven or so hours from the car park at the top of the Flipoff route to the junction of Jumpup Canyon and Kanab Creek. There is a good trail on top of the redwall when approaching the canyon. A few deep pools despite water level being very low - anchors mostly in good shape - many awkward starts to rappels.

Ended up doing 6 rappels. Replaced most of the webbing on knot chock on tricky start rappel. It's backed up on a finger sized rock loop that may be well below water line in normal conditions. Low water for us, filtered at the last pool which was the only swim. No easy starts on rappels in the canyon though, all somewhat awkward. Didn't really need wetsuits in the 85 degree heat and low water.
Single bolt at R3 anchor ledge in good condition as of this trip.

A good overnight Grand Canyoneering intro canyon. Big raps back to back to back, some pretty Zoroaster/Vishnu striping, easy packrafting, and aside from the technical descent, no off trail hiking. We surprisingly got a permit for Bright Angel Campground so did 1 day down Cremation and floated to boat beach then hiked out BA the second day.

Completed with an overnight at Phantom Ranch. 8 degrees leaving the snow-covered rim at 0400 and starting the descent down S Kaibab. Microspikes were useful. About 50 degrees at the head of Cremation. The canyon was dry with the exception of some shallow, avoidable pools. The Colorado was cold and those who wore wetsuits for the pack-raft were far more comfortable. Didn’t see a single other person until Boat Beach. Reached Phantom Ranch around 1700 (moving at a casual pace). Ascended via Bright Angel the following day.
Amazing desert oasis!

We have navigation issues finding the road to the 4x4 parking, wound up hiking from the 4x2 parking along one road, then cross country to 4x4 road. We also checked out 35 foot jump (pool 10 to 15 feet deep) RDC from the 12 foot waterfall jump after the first slide. I did not get the instructions to stay out of the pothole (boil) on the 2nd rappel, but it's easy enough to escape the pothole at moderate flow. After the exit swim, hike well downstream RDC, climb up the talus to the shelf high above to get back.

Started from "4wd" parking. 4wd not necessarily, high clearance all that is needed. Did not take petroglyphs route, but one in team went most of way up on exit and looks not too bad. Nats are bad on hike, highly recommend bug spray and long sleeves. Bolt on last rappel is very rusted and hanger moves, check it before going. Replaced webbing on first mandatory rappel. Replaced quicklink on bolt for safety line to last rappel. Lots of jump options. Super fun.
Scouting trip; low-moderate flow; very low snowpack year, most of the snow in the drainage area has melted
Wading to waist deep, but only one mandatory pool. Water level was probably higher than normal with recent rain. Replaced a few shredded anchors.

2 waist-deep pools. all webbing was old and needed replacing (5 anchors). read warning for the big rap, our rope almost got stuck on a little outcrop as it fell. blood drawn by approximately 4 aggressive cholla babies. they're sneaky.
A couple swims and a few deep wades. Replaced webbing at all anchors.

Can be done without getting wet, and without rope if you're a good down climber with reasonably grippy shoes (we had 5.10s, and we did bring a shortie rope just in case). If you opt not to go out of your way to avoid the water, there's one chilly swimmer (maybe 15 feet across). Fun to do if you don't have time for anything else.
Straightforward and quick (15min) approach. Multiple deep pools. A couple required swimmers. Didn’t use a rope for any of the obstacles. Water was fairly clear and refreshing.
Low water, but still a few wades and a couple swimmers. Lots of tadpoles, snakes, and other aquatic critters. Removed some old crusty webbing from above the first optional rappel.
Small trickle of water flowing in canyon, clear pools, fun spot for kids
Surprisingly awesome little canyon! Definitely could be dangerous in higher water per hydraulics. Flow was just about perfect today.
Ran Dark and added a bolt on R4 to help with the lack of natural anchors. Fun creek and was at moderate flow. Need to go back with a bit more flor in the future.
A very pleasant canyon with a short rapid-fire technical section. Added a backup bolt to the single bolted anchor. Moderate low flow. A very nice time.
The approach is on an OHV trail, heads up for dirt bikes. Flow was on the low side but still fun. Great rock formations throughout.

Returning to Dark Creek after a couple of years and it doesn't disappoint. A fabulous creek in the Dark Divide. Water colder than I remember; we're suspicious it must be spring-fed. Once out of the water, you'd warm up relatively quickly. Stuck a rope on the bolted anchor that we were able to free with a great deal of hauling and thrashing the rope from below. Might be good to extend this anchor so the master point hangs in space to keep this from happening in future. Great rappel.
Always a good time on Dark Creek. Reasonable approach/exit, minimal bushwhacking, and some quality rappels. A must-do if you're in the Dark Divide.

There's a new log perched precariously in the middle of the pitch on R8 -- just above the funnel chamber. Looks like a rappeller accidentally bumping into it could knock it down. We used caution and detoured around it... the rope will run clean underneath it. On the same pitch, we discovered a doubled 200ft rope will just reach the bottom. Extending the pull side may be recommended for a clean pull in a better location just downstream of the slot. All anchors in good shape.

Dark was awesome! I wouldn't have wanted to run it at any higher flow. We couldn't find a lot of the anchors and the ones that we found were "baconed" so we built or rebuilt most of them. R7 hanging pothole was not a problem. The flow was going mostly over and beside the pothole. R8 rappelled out of the flow into the slot room. Very splashy and no hydraulic. Easy to swim into and out of the area. R8 wouldn't pull. We had to ascend back up to check it. It looked fine, block was on top and it wasn't caught on anything. I rerouted the pull over the log (above the slot room) and we pulled from far back. Ended up building a 3:1 to get it to pull. Extremely fun creek at this flow!

Another great day in Dark. Flow was perfect. A lot of fallen logs that were present last time I was here have been blown out, so the rappels are all clear and good to go. There have been endless reports of people sticking ropes on R8, so we re-rigged the bolts as a Y-hang anchor. Rope pulled like a dream. Be aware: the log on R8 is now about 10ft lower wedged between walls of the chasm. If you run the flow, there's a great line all the way down into the "Dark Room" at the bottom although you have to slide under the log. Played around doing some jumps on the very last drop in the canyon. Ran East Canyon Creek as a chaser.

What a beautiful canyon. I was pleasantly suprised at the bang for your buck on this one. Approach is 3 miles but on all established trails. I want to know who did this approach in 1 hr (STUDS!) More flow would have been better but just on the edge of moderate flow and still super fun. Closing in on one of the PNW classics if you ask me!!!

We did a low-flow run of Dark and it was happily more than the trickle we expected (slightly more). We found it to be great for canyoneers who are nervous about water but want to gain experience rappelling in the flow. It’s a beautiful canyon. We removed the webbing from the rotten wobbly log at the top of R7. We had intended to bolt it (per requests in the beta) but found the rock quality to be insufficient for bolting. We built a cairn anchor and used that. Unless someone else is able to find suitable rock that we didn’t find, it may be that a cairn anchor is the long-term solution here, as there is ample mateial.
Fun day on Dark. Performed anchor maintenance as required. The last anchor should be replaced. We should have done it but by the time I got there, most of the team was already down.

First time actually seeing motorbikes at the trailhead; they took off in front of us and we never saw them again. Flow low to moderate-low, no issues, all anchors in good shape. Surprisingly nice cairn at R7. Current line takes you right down a rock rib between the main part of the waterfall and pothole to rappeller's right. (Going in the pothole is fun at this level.) The funnel rappel at R8 has a log spanning the canyon from one side to the other which is not visible from the rappel station. If following the watercourse, you'll need to squeak underneath it. Best to extend your rope on this anchor for the pull and toss the pull side over the log. A large number of mountain bikers showed up at the trailhead just after we got out and were picked up by a shuttle. Looked like a guide service.
Dark Creek was good, but the flow is pretty wimpy. Anchors were in good shape.
Surprisingly fun canyon with some really scenic rappels, although flow was very low.

Fun day in Dark. Rebuilt anchors as required. There is a new log jammed under the old log on R7 which is stabilizing it or at least it doesn't bounce anymore. The carin anchor is gone so we added one to the log. We did not run the flow in R8. It looked way to powerful!
We ran Dark both last weekend and this weekend. The flow is really dropping. Get in there soon!

Great day in the creek! Splashy but not pushy. All were surprised at how cold the water was, and grateful for the frequent sunny areas to warm up in. All anchors had relatively new webbing. R7 is a "cairn" anchor of a small dishwasher size boulder with long webbing; bouncy, but good & solid.

R1 looked just like the "high" reference photo from Jun 2023, which tracks with 2026 being a month ahead of "usual". The trail is in great condition. At R7 the cairn/threaded anchor has gone, but the log spanning the creek is stable and provides a good anchor. A bolt was damaged at R8, we took the opportunity to repurpose the remaining bolt as a traverse line, and commissioned a new rappel station on a ledge further DCL with good line of sight of the drop. The bottom of R8 was rowdy but was easy to get flushed out with a good jump across. Ran East afterwards.

Fun day in Dark. All anchors in good shape except R8. New anchor station DCL for last pitch of Dark Creek Falls was used and ruins the rappel. I recommend not using it. If the next team has the capability recommend placing additional bolt at the original anchor or back up the single for all but the last person and rappel in the flow. You can rappel the dry (high flow line) from the original anchor without problem. I think the new station is a case of unnecessary over-bolting. It overcomplicates the drop with a traverse line, makes the rappel dry, and runs the rope over several sharp edges. The benefit it provides of better line of sight can be achieved in other ways. The new station was placed 3 weeks ago and already failed as one lose bolt wouldn't tighten and popped out with barely loading it with my personal pro. I put the bolt in my vice to loosen the nut and get the hanger off signaling the threads were damaged possibly from over-tightening.

We ran the canyon replacing all mallion anchors with rings. We established the cairn anchor after getting freaked by the flex of the log. Feel free to rig the log but it gave us bad vibes. A future party could bring in more webbing to lengthen the cairn anchor. We then replaced the lower rebelay above the Dark Room which resolved the incident of the bolt pulling out in the previous trip report.

We did the shuttle approach so we could check out China Garden, where we enjoyed a leisurely lunch by the oasis. This stop was worth it- be sure to see all the surprisingly large goldfish in the pond! We didn't find the first anchor, so had to build one. We swam a few times in the very cold water... all had 2mm shortys but were freezing after short exposure. Any longer exposure would've been problematic. IMO, optimal season should be considered beyond March if doing the shuttle, as this is a WET canyon.

Darwin Falls was quite the surprise. For the entire first part of the decent there was no clear use trail through the thick brush consisting of 8 ft reeds, trees/branches and a difficult to see stream through the canyon proper. Not entirely enjoyable for me personally. The overhung long rappel next to the waterfall is quite asthetic. You cannot see from the top, but there is an alcove at the bottom to avoid dunking into the pool. At least the first person needs to do a swing/swim to get across the waterfall (left). The rest can be pulled over by the first person to bypass. The second rappel also leads to a little standing position, but then becomes a deep pool over 10 ft deep. I had a 15 ft long swim to reach a tree. At this tree I fixed the rope and my partner did a part Tyrolean traverse, part zip line down to the tree to avoid the water.

Fun novelty to have so much water in the desert. The first waterfall is beautiful and worth the trip. I wore a rain jacket over a 6/5 long-sleeved neoprene jacket with air temperatures in the mid-60s and felt great. Direct water exposure beyond deep wading is over quickly, so the main issue is how cold it is to be wet afterward.

One washed out section of the road in beginning of road to Darwin, not passable by a car but a truck made it okay. Canyon no longer has the tall reeds, they are all flattened. A thin layer of mud on all rocks makes things look more slippery than actually were. Replaced all webbing at the main falls. Pool was 4 ft at deepest at base of main falls rappel. Option to skip last rappel (Darwin Falls) by R-rated traverse on right. No wet suites needed, just a dry layer for the person who swam / wadded.

The canyon is completely different than previous conditions. Flash flood in August 2022 scoured out most of the vegetation. No bush whacking, just open canyon corridors. Then one of the atmospheric river storms in winter 2023 deposited gravel. Every place I waded or swam on prior trips is now just ankle deep walk on gravel. Road is a little rougher than normal to trailhead. Recommend high clearance. Won’t need 4x4 though.

Canyon changed by flash floods: vegetation has grown back noticeably since my last trip in April, but still is very reduced compared to past years. There are still no pools deeper than knee-deep. We set up anchor to rap down the big waterfall directly (as opposed to normal route on RDC side of waterfall) because it was hot out and we wanted to get wet. It was awesome!

Road to Darwin Falls is almost completely gone. Access is closed to the public except via walk in. Canyon was once again scoured by the Aug Hurricane Hilary flooding. Most vegetation has been washed out and the water course has changed in places. Still no deep pools as per last years reports. All anchors had webbing replaced. We opted to skip the water course rappel on the big falls and rig the standard RDC location. Great day to play in the water.
Great canyon. It rained a lot the previous day but the water levels were pretty much the same.

Water flow was moderate low and the water was chilly. The canyon is likely one of the most beautiful canyons in Washington and it's a must run! We went with a film crew so it took us a little longer than normal. Anchors are in good condition but webbing may need to be replaced in the next few months.

Despite the very dry Summer, we went in during the first weekend rainstorm (50% chance of rain during the time we were actually in the canyon). The canyons in the PNW typically do not have a high chance of flashing since the ground is absorbent. The gauge USGS:14231900 was reading at 110% when we arrived (it had been raining in the entire night/ morning) so we decided to give it a go. We were lucky and it only rained briefly (5 minutes) on us throughout the canyon and we even got some blue sky patches! The increased flow is more noticeable and our group was cautious about proceeding to the slotty area. The first two-tiered rappel (R3) we were able to completely avoid by rappelling to the side/around. With this flow, rappelling in the water is not advised - there is a square-looking crack where canyoneers should be careful not to get stuck in! In low flow, rappelling into the crack will not be a problem. For R4, we went over the log instead due avoid the high water. For R5, care must be taken when rappelling into the pothole. You can rappel 1/2 way down and slide down the rest. The current will spit you out but have a line ready to throw / throw down the pull slide ahead of time to help others swim out. R7 is the rappel you see from the bridge. Is the by far the strongest hydraulic of all the other rappels. It is difficult to set rope length due to the amount of white water but still do your best. It is advisable to have people lock off about 1/2 way down (where you can stay to the side of the water course), pull up the rope to ensure there are no knots/rope is long enough, before entering in the lower 1/2 of the waterfall.

This canyon is flowing at extreme levels and should only be run is very, very competent Class C canyoneers. At least two people need to be familiar with reading currents, escaping very powerful swirling potholes, setting up guide lines, and have throw ropes ready. Do not underestimate the power of the water in this flow. The canyon has changed significantly from the 2015 season - log anchors are gone, and other logs have accumulated in other places, some rappels are now jumps, footholds are buried 2 feet below the water. The 80' waterfall underneath the bridge is very dangerous due to the slippery rock and risk of slipping/falling and setting up a meat guided rappel is highly recommended.

Canyon is flowing about the same flow about a week ago. We experimented with different hydraulic avoidance techniques now that we didn't have Thor with us. For swirling pothole #1, we used a floating anchor to zipline the packs down. The first person still went into the hydraulic unaided. For swirling pothole #2, we used a potshot to setup a guided rappel and then switched to a meat guided rappel afterwards. For the 80' waterfall, the first person down rappeled with the rope bag in order to deploy the guide line. First person still had to cross the waterfall, toss the rope bag, and then jump about 20 feet (away from hydraulic). Meat guided rappel worked perfect and should always be done in this type of flow. Many expert canyoneers have slipped on this waterfall - be prepared to add friction, lockoff, take small steps, etc...
We played quite a bit and took longer than we could have. It is a gorgeous canyon with beautiful lighting and several narrow gorgeous lined with ferns and moss.

Moderate flow - you can enter all the pools without concern of hydraulics. A really fun day with a large group! Anchors are in good shape. There’s a little giant sequoia sapling that has been planted in memory of Luca Chiarabini. It is tucked away DCL between the 2nd swirling pothole and the rap underneath the bridge. Please water it if you are there and send pics to Tiffanie Lin (cinnybear[at]gmail[dot]com). Thanks!
Beautiful canyon, not to be missed. It holds a little piece of our hearts.

Terrific canyon and a lot of fun. Can see how this creek would be extremely dangerous in high flow. Ran into an issue with the rope getting stuck on R4. Block was caught in a crack up near the top requiring one of our party to ascend back up to fix it. Otherwise smooth sailing. First pothole was a joy to rappel. White water & some current, but no serious hydraulic effects to be concerned about. Two thumbs up!

Chilly day with air temperatures at the upper 50s. It rained prior and during our time in the canyon. When we entered, flow was moderate and by the time we exited, it probably had gotten to about high flow. Thicker neoprene was appreciated in these conditions. Potholes were recirculating but nothing our group hadn't encountered/overcome before. We entered both potholes and had throw ropes ready. Great day despite the rain!

Flow was moderate high and lower than expected compared to last year conditions around the same time. There are new logs and gravel that have changed some of the rappels. The beta has been updated with the changes. Take note that the first swirling pothole has logs that obstruct the original rappel/jump. Rappel DCR instead.

All the anchors are in great shape. Flow was relatively low so the hydraulics and swirling potholes were not very significant . Super fun Canyon, although a little more water would have been more fun.

This was the first time any in our group ran this canyon, the beta made us very cautious about the potential water hazards. So some of the rappels were discussed heavily and contingencies were pre-rigged, ready for rescue. But it actually was a pretty calm canyon. We took our time, were safe, enjoyed the beauty of it all. We looked for Luca's tree but it appears to be gone. Found a ring of rocks where I believe the tree would have been, but no tree to be found :(

Great canyon. All anchors in good shape, although couple of bolts could use tightening. The narrows have a good bit more woody debris than last year. After exiting the canyon ran into a woman driving down from above who reported a cougar sighting along the road.
No issues with hydraulics. Moderate high to moderate flow. Pothole #1 still has visible and hidden logs. The large white boulder has shifted down so there is no longer a rappel from the boulder.

Another great day in Davis. There was much rappelling and jumping into pools. Approached a few of the falls with caution. Found a snake in Swirling Pothole #1, who, from body language, just wanted to get the hell out of there. Swirling pothole #2 has some good current; better to send packs down separately. Good to have someone at the exit to help people out. Water temps were perfect for a hot day. We took a "wrong turn" on the way in and accidentally found a steep dirty trail leading right to the top of R1.

Anchors and webbing in good shape. You can see water swirling in swirly potholes, but they weren't difficult to swim through or escape. The rocks in the watercourse are very slippery and this is difficult to see (I'm not sure if this is normal as it was my first descent and I'm not from the area). All 5 of us were slipping throughout the day despite having shoes with sticky rubber and experience creek walking. I fell and broke my hand (see incident report). Note, it rained a lot the night after we ran this. We drove back into the bridge checkpoint the next day and the water was running a fair bit higher.

My fat thumb accidentally hit save on a condition report before I was done, so this is an incomplete report. In the future I promise to take Daniel on more trips because his trip reports are the best. Also, his photos are the best. Really tho, all around, he is the best. Thank you for your time. Namaste

we bipassed R5 & R6 log jams rappels. Above R5 is a trail Rh that you follow 140 yds downstream. one slippery section. Two big tree at end of shelf, green webbing on last tree 60 ft rappel just past log jams 🤠. We climbed back on top of log jam and out green webbing with slings to pull u up in case you go the traditional route. Also note. you can exit out on this trial

At this flow, the log jam below R6 was very easy to climb over and no more difficult than any other part of the canyon. There is a small submerged rock to the left of the jam that you can use to climb over. There is a new optional jump off of the log jam as well (check water levels first).

Fun new rappel now that the logjam has blown out. Found the boulder submerged deep in the pool below the new falls. Climbed up over the logjam. You can either downclimb a sloping log or rappel. Super fun day and great group. Exciting new full-size freezer that someone dumped off the bridge.

Great day! The new log jam at the bottom of swirling pothole #1 is easy to climb over. Davis received 0.15" rain the day before, ending around 10pm, but since the forest has been so dry for weeks, it didn't appear to impact the water levels at all. We removed a mailbox from under the bridge. No mail though.
All anchors in good condition. R6 with the giant log jam is a really fun jump. Highly recommended.

Scoped the flows last week after running Covell and decided to give it a go. Added redirects on the big drop to stay out of the powerful flow and on the lower drop below the bridge. Next group should bring some natural looking 5 mm dyneema cord to replace the white that was used. The two dangerous potholes were swam without issue but a strong swimmer should go first. The first pothole was jumped by half of the group after a depth check. There is still a long in there that spans the pothole. The slide that was once blocked by debris and the big boulder has become a horse tailing drop at extreme flow so that added some spice.

Ran Davis Sunday July 18th in moderate high flow. Manageable pothole swirls and currents with some care and assists. Bypass not used on R4 Davis Falls, used the re-direct for R8 Lower Creek Falls to stay out of the whitewater flow. Great day out in this classic canyon. It's in a good shape, no changed conditions from previous couple of years.

First time running it. Would estimate moderate/moderate-high flow. Didn't need the R4 redirect. R6 pothole was jumpable with an unproblematic hydraulic. R7 recirculating hydraulic was stronger and team assists helped. Added thicker black webbing to the R8 redirect, and now the white cord can be removed by a group descending in lower flow.

As a result of a dry spring and summer, water levels are probably a month lower than where they'd be on a normal year. Some current in the swirling pools and on R8, but easy to swim out of. Spent some time playing in the horizontal recirculation on R7. Can still get pummeled on the big falls, so the redirect on R8 was appreciated.
Ran Davis, paired nicely with Hager. All was good.
Davis is a great creek. Dress warm the water is cold. The beta is accurate and beginner friendly this time of year. Our time was 5 hours due to having some training discussions at each anchor.

Fun time through Davis. Added a bolt station for the second tier of Davis Creek Falls. You can either rappel in the flow or alongside of in higher water. This made for a great time. Just beyond the notable log jam, be mindful of getting through the logs upon exit as it is a tight fit. Check Swirpool 1 for sliding and it was great. We added a V thread anchor for Swirlpool 2 that will assist in getting down. The pool is SHALLOW. The sun was out and the day was great. Met the new landowner and he was cool with people continuing to run the can but did ask that we clean up any trash that we see. His name is Wes and he is very friendly. Please be kind and respectful of anyone you encounter. He does have children who swim in the lower section like the locals.

Great trip and great conditions. Chilly swims but it was 100°F out. We added/replaced a hanger and rapide on R4+. Some raps jumpable. No worrisome hydraulics encountered, tho the chute in R5 was pushy. I reeeaally want R5 to be a slide... can we move that boulder? ;-)

The flow is down considerably from one week ago. No need for redirect on only get wet if you intentionally push right. New anchor at R4.5 is fantastic. Better rope pull and allows for rappelling in or next to the 2nd tier 40ft drop. The recirc in R5 is easily crossed by bending the knees and jumping onto one’s back while letting go of the rope to completely cross the pool. R8 is an easy walk down on rappel in the middle of the flow, boil at the bottom is fairly benign. Great fun level will not last long with the dry heat.

Enjoyable, leisurely descent through Davis on a hot summer's day. No anchor was found at R2, so guessing folks are downclimbing through the logjam this year. Added an anchor on a tree far DCL to rappel in below the jam. Avoids any need to fool about climbing around on slippery logs. New bolt station (R4a) is terrific and a fun addition to the canyon. Water conditions were great, little pushy in places. Much lounging in pools.

Glorious 5* day. Echoing others in saying the new R4 option is much more enjoyable. Two of the group swung at the end of R8 as they tried to stay out the pummel. Getting into the flow and dropping straight down through it is the better option. Other than that, I'd call it mod-low everywhere else.

Such a beautiful canyon! Davis has moderate-low flow at this point in the season, which makes it a quick and easy descent. There were no points where we felt the flow was dangerous or the hydraulics concerning. There were plenty of swimmers though, so with lower night temperatures the water was chilly and the members of our party with thicker wetsuits were the happiest. For the 120ft rappel, we used the DCR bolt station for the full drop and the pull was fine from the bottom.

The pictures vary too much to land on what the levels were. That being said, it was definitely high and sporty. Notable items, the second tier on Davis Creek Falls has a large stump in it which makes it a bit easier to get to the new anchor. The big Boulder hasn’t moved. The logjam has progressed but very doable to get around. We jumped the first swirl pool DCL. The infinity pool and the V thread to help get out . Rap below bridge was straightforward and the log in the pocket is gone. The slidey log now has friends so as a doe see doe to get around it. All anchors are good to go.

All bolts good, like the new additions. Not sure when looking at water levels they consider the cowlitz (dam regulated) as an indicator. I would use the Cispus River, flows into cowlitz from the south, but similar drainage. My 2 cents. Water level was fun, but moderate. Never really pushy. Beta is good, recommend on the two swirling pools to jump them (into the sweet exiting spot) over rappelling into the SOB swim into the recirculation wall.
Edit on previous post, Cowlitz dam is downstream of the Davis creek, my bad. Cowlitz has much larger drainage basin up towards white pass. Cispus is a more similar drainage.

Davis was fantastic. Cold water (8mm wetsuits were appreciated). Swirling Potholes #1 and #2 were not as strong as anticipated and felt closer to moderate/moderate-high in terms of strength. The anchors were in good shape.

Great way to spend July 4th! Very fun creek with great drops. Swirling potholes were not as strong as expected. 2 team members had issues with SP2, but eventually were able to swim out. R4a is a great rap and worth the drive itself. Team enjoyed the raps, the pools, and the pushy but manageable flow.

Great day in the canyon. The log rappel in the begin has a hornet nest in or close to it. Be mindful! Also, a bolt was installed that wasn’t there a month ago when we opened it. Not sure what the point is. It was at the same location as the old redirect for high flow sequence for Lower Davis Falls under the bridge.

Flow was a2, moderate-low, easy to walk in the middle of the flow anywhere, very grippy. Everyone had a blast. This canyon never gets old. 5 noteworthy jumps, 5 downclimbs, 1 waterslide, and 3 longish rappels, all in a deep beautiful canyon. Upper Section: 1. DC left 10' on natural staircase, 2. DC left 10' through logjam easier than it looks, 3. DC right down natural ladder, 4. 70' rappel on side of waterfall do a J-tie at next botls, 5. 50' into plunge pool (can off rope on ledge and jump 15') multiple jumping perches from 5' to 20'. Lower Section: 1. Chute can be downclimbed almost all the way but rope needed at bottom, 2. slide on right 15' (or jump from left 20'), 3. downclimb on right 10' through logjam, 4. jump 12' into hanging pool (CW circulation) then DC 4' at pouroff, 5. rappel 50' at bridge, 6. jumping pool up to 10', 7. short downclimb/scooch logs, 8. small jumping pool, 9. finale is an overhang rappel 15' into a grotto and cave accessed through rabbit hole.

Beautiful trip down Davis in the late afternoon. We suited up at the car and left down the trail at 4:45pm, and were surprised to finish only 1.5hr later. So I updated the beta to reduce the time range to 2-5hr, from the previous 3-5hr. We had done Lewis earlier in the day. Davis was quite markedly a much better canyon than Lewis. We also camped that night in the field at the start of Davis.
Great day in Davis. Flow was low enough to not be too challenging but still fun.

Davis: classic fun as always. There is a new giant rootball jammed in the opening of the hanging pool on the big rappel (just below unlinked anchor at R4b). Didn't affect the rappel or pull, but could cause issues for anyone trying to climb out of the hanging pool in high water. Found part of a rusty metal cash box (?) at the final rappel and packed it out.

Very fun day in Davis! All the anchors looked to be in great condition and the jumps were great! We used the redirect for R4, but the flow was plenty tame to have gone straight down. The swirling pothole for R7 presented no challenges to any of the members.
Great day out. Wonderful weather and terrain. Water is relatively low, still great swimming. Skipped the redirect on R4. No special challenges.

R9 anchor log unstable. It is balanced on a pile of smaller logs right at the anchor webbing. Anchor Log shifted down 3 inches when 205lb rappeller went vertical. The rest of the team down climbed. Water is low but still fun. R5 pool rock exposed about 1ft above the water. Great day in Davis. View in attached photo is from midway down the down climb, looking back up-canyon at anchor. The anchor log is the horizontal log in the photo. Webbing is just visible at top of anchor log.

Due to recent rainfall, water levels were pretty fun but not overwhelming. All anchors were in good shape, I avoided the bees by using the alt DCL tree. Might have been the last run for 2023, the air temps were quite cool and I def felt the chill towards the end of the canyon even in a dry suit. Amazing place, I can see why everyone goes here, I hope to get out there next season again!! I also did my best to look for trash under the bridge and didn't see anything laying around. I documented this canyon with current conditions and obstacles which I'm sure will change but I will add the vid to the media subpart of this canyon page.

Great day in high flow. Cleared the R4 anchor station of hundreds of lbs of debris. Used the redirect to stay out the flow. Crux was the R5 slot/chute which needed careful footwork. The logjam exit from the R6 slide-pool has shifted downstream. What remains is much less stable than before, and has a siphon under the surface. Be cautious here. The down-climb on the other side is similar to the last few years. A bolt DCR up on the wall may help protect swimmers, up-climbers, and down-climbers from shifting debris. R7 recirc pool wasn’t problematic with a flat jump. We put a hanger & carabiner (no webbing) on the naked R8 redirect bolt - it wasn’t clear from its ambiguous state that it was meant to be decommissioned. Don’t mind if it stays or goes, but I suspect more confusion if it’s partially decommissioned again. Perhaps a little project for the WCC to handle the physical & community communications around (the R6 slide also has some bolts that also need decommissioning). Water temps weren’t nearly as cold as we expected.
Great canyon. Beautiful and fun. Anchors were in good shape. Log jams are kind of sketchy.
Returned to Davis after 5 years to introduce some cavers to canyoneering as was done for me.

Of many trips through Davis this was the funnest. Finally jumped R4b from the pour off - it was plenty deep. Did the slide at R6 for the first time as well - usually do the jump, plenty deep in any case. With jumps at R4b, R6, R7, and after R8 and R9, that's a lot of jumps. With the 5 minute approach and 10 minute exit must be the lowest pay-to-play ratio in WA. I've changed my rating to 5-stars due to the higher jump and slide. We were in canyon 2.5 hrs. Another 1/2 hr for shuttle and gearing up.
Great day as always. Did observe some spontaneous rockfall at the bottom of R4 coming down off the cliffs DCR onto the ledges above the pool. Signs of recent rockfall - use caution.
Lovely day in Davis! All the normal jumps and slides are in. All anchors in good condition.
What an amazing place! Went in loaded for bear, being new to PNW canyons. Overkill, but helped us proceed confidently Anchors were all great, and beta spot on.

Some recent rains bumped the water level up to moderate or more likely moderate-low. Rain also left everything in the canyon wet and slippery. This was my third time through Davis, and I have noticed this canyon is pretty slippery to begin with, combine that with a light moister coating on everything and it's even worse. I've updated the beta for rappel lengths for R1 and R5. I brought my ultra-light 70foot rope for all the short rappels as listed previously on the beta and was short at R1 and R5 and had to extend my pulls, so the beta has been updated. Cleaned up several glass bottles and trash under the bridge. We did not have a safe way to carry out some broken glass. Future canyoners watch out for glass and if you have a spare empty keg to remove the glass that would be great. Found new anchor at the drop between R9 and R10 (Webbing DCL from root ball). This is the drop with the big knobby log that we all used to butt skootch down in years past. With new wood on this log and it being wet and slippery the new anchor was much appreciated and made for a safer drop rappelling.
Overcast slightly rainy day but all were comfortable. The redirects have no caribeaners.

Did not descend Davis today, although the flow was likely doable for a strong team. More notably there is now a large log in the recirculating pool. It may get washed away before the season, however if you are the first team down this summer, make sure to get a look of the log from the road before descending! More logs/debris may arrive and be deposited here from the up stream log jame

Great day in Davis, curiously lower flow than when we ran it early July last year, despite the Cowlitz gauge reading nearly double. We replace the webbing and quicklinks above the roundy pool with stainless links, swapped the webbing for the log slide rappel, and the redirect had the nut spun nearly completely off so we removed the hanger and link, the bare bolt is still there. Even at this flow jumping into the pool at the bottom of the bridge simply flushes you straight out, and the redirect seems like it creates more of a hazard than anything. The log in the roundy pool is wedged up well above the water. It does impede the jump, but isn’t a hazard in the pool.
We spent 3 hours hauling and cutting the log out of the whirlypool. Would’ve been nice to still have a redirect, even if decommissioned, on R8. Great trip, great team, great canyon!
Davis was fun. Anchors are all good.
All anchors are in great condition. Kudos to the team who placed the new bolts and removed the previous ones.

Canyon is in great shape. The maintenance and newer bolts have improved it a lot and made the canyon easier to run than I remember from a couple years ago. The water felt and looked like a strong moderate. It may have picked up a little from the rain. Was a good day!

Flow looked moderate according to reference photos but felt more on the moderate-low side. Incredibly slippery creek. 5/4mm was comfortable. Approximately 2.5h in canyon and 30 min to gear down and walk the road and retrieve our vehicle.
Agree with Nclegg's condition report with regard to flow. From the photo comparison, could make a case for moderate flow, but conditions in canyon were evaluated as moderate-low.

The new bolts are indeed lovely! The water level is definelty low at this point. The boulder at the bottom of the V slot is above water. Make sure to scout the jumo at the bottom of Upper Davis Falls as there is new(ish) rock just below the surface DCL.
Another late season run. I replaced the webbing on R2 (root ball above Upper Davis Falls) as the current webbing was pretty damaged.

-The logs at R2 have shifted to over the pool, now goes as an L sit shallow jump, in lower water consider bolting wet line dcr. -R3 log anchor gone, still a down climb. The convenient log we sit while rigging R4 has shifted away from the wall. -To make accessible at higher flows consider bolting a traverse anchor to R5, a meat traverse anchor was required here. -The log jam between R6 and R7 is gone. Apparently it’s what it was like 10 yrs ago. At this flow it looked like white death. So we scrambled up the side of the canyon dcl and bolted an anchor that dropped us into the pool beneath where the log jam used to be. -At higher flow consider using a bag anchor at R7 instead of sending a swimmer down to set up a guided off the dyneema hand loop as we did. Even with flotation (or because of it) one can become temporarily stuck in this recirc. -R8 isn’t the crux everyone makes it out to be, but we meet anchor guide rapped it due to a drysuit malfunction. -The log jam at “R9” is gone, it’s now a very very shallow slide and could use a bolt station. -R10 is cleaned out a bit, in lower flow a wet line dcr might be nice. -All pre existing bolts are intact, more will be needed because so much wood has been cleared out. -We left a rim rope as an escape line just below R4 which we didn’t end up using. After running the canyon we were pooped. I’ll come back for it.

NEXT TEAMS PLEASE BRING: * A good ring hanger and variety of different nuts for R3b. We tried the nut from a 3/8" Simpson Strong Bolt 2 - it almost fit, but it binded up after one rotation. Maybe it's an M10, or 9/16" with a different thread. * 5mm sheathed Dyneema cord for the exit of pothole #2. It's still functional but the sheath is heavily core-shot. Davis is still running high, and swift-water skills are necessary. We bypassed pothole #1 using the high DCL anchor the team earlier this year added. However we did confirm the exit of the pool is a fun slide! If the log blocks the entrance slide is TBD. Excellent day with just the right level of challenges that let us take our time to practice skills & decision making. Only real negative is that the creek is frigging slippery.

Conditions remarkably similar to ~3 weeks ago. Completed previously reported anchor maintenance, everything should be in great shape. Also added anchors to the exit of the first pothole to facilitate staging people & packs. It's still unclear if the log in this pool blocks the entrance slide or not.

Great day in Davis flow was still playful but not bad. Anchors were in good shape…new single hanger by the old log jam spins but usable. Checked this pool with goggles and only one log is in the pool so slide goes and a precision jump it also possible, just don’t hit the log! The slide is probably safer option. Didn’t see a bolt at the R9 sketchy down climb, would be great for a single here unless we are blind and missed it.

One team member sprained their thumb after slipping while creek walking at the bottom of upper davis falls. He was able to rappel left handed and we made our way slowly down the rest of the canyon. The R5 traverse line bolt, R6 bypass anchor and R7 v thread loop were all essential to finishing strong. Thankfully non catastrophic albeit still a wake up call to take creek walking very seriously.

So two things. The pothole slide dcr with a log probably goes? But I wasn't brave enough to check it. It also makes jumping really awkward. The famous Davis boulder has once again moved! It's now about 6-7' underwater and has been pushed about 6 feet back in the pool. This rappel might finally go as a slide but we didn't test it. I would be keen to go back and try it through. Breaking a tailbone or leg is def possible though if it doesn't go.

Beautiful canyon! High walls, high quality raps, gorgeous slot with a big grandiose feel! Was definitely floored by this one! 5/4 wetsuit was comfy. We helped a beginner through and took our time. Loved the scenery! Plenty of unique and memorable features in this one! We suited up at parking and desuited at cars, hardly any dry hiking no bush. Wish it lasted longer!

Excellent canyon, shitty bushwhack on exit. I would definitely recommend heading back up canyon. This should be easy, we never pulled out our rope on the way down. I would not consider subjecting myself to that bushwhack on the exit again. Another option is to take moki steps to an exposed ridge exit on the left down canyon a little ways beyond the slot.
Bushwack from hell. Don’t do it.

Ambitiously attempted in 1.5 days. Hiked in about 7 miles from Barnhardt TH on the afternoon of day 1. With only limited beta, we intended to descend the full canyon and exit to Barnhardt TH on day 2. Above the lower narrows, we got eyes on the final large rap / rap sequence as well as our not-so-confidence-inspiring exit route. We decided to bail from the canyon up a steep gully CL, rather than bivy mid-canyon, after checking our rapidly dwindling food supply and assessing how much additional time would be required to complete the endeavor. Our sprits were then further compromised upon being thoroughly assaulted by a series of thick and particularly hostile manzanita groves. One team member cursed these mountains and swore to never return. Next year he returned to descend Shake Tree.
Though the area is heavily burn damaged from the Frye Creek fire, the canyon is still choked with brush. Rebuilt both anchors.

The first rappel was spectacular at this flow and canyon overall was better than anticipated. Plenty of brush and poison ivy throughout the watercourse, however it was all forgiving. Approach trail is faint initially but then well-marked by cairns. Replaced R1 webbing. Last person down pulled from the pool at the first stage and then downclimbed on canyon left due to concerns over a stuck rope - either in cacti / plants on the first stage or around the limbs of a large dead tree at the base of the second stage. Rebuilt R2 anchor, and then pulled from the first stage and rigged another rappel from a pinch point canyon left to (again) prevent potential rope retrieval issues. This time the force of the waterflow was pulling our rope down into two separate cracks.

We descended the canyon in two days with a bivy at Moonshadow. Incredibly beautiful and very isolated canyon with many interesting swims. Felt like a mini Zion with no other people. Can't say enough good things about this hike.

We completed a round-trip, no-shuttle variation that included a packraft - see Ianello's book for the best beta. Death Pit is notable for the beautiful glimpses of the Colorado River that you get as you descend. The death pit itself is easy to avoid by traversing DCL to an obvious ledge and then descending, being careful not to pendulum toward the pit. If you wait until you are below the ledge to traverse, it will be more difficult. All anchors were in good condition except the last, which we could not locate. We constructed a new knot chock anchor adjacent to a large boulder. We enjoyed an easy ~25 min packraft to the mouth of Bighorn Canyon, then up Bighorn and back to the car via the route described in the Weeping Springs beta. Bighorn was a lovely canyon in itself, lush with salt cedar and a trickle of water in its lower reaches, plus some sections of colorful twisty narrows with fun upclimbs. The point at which you leave Bighorn to cross back over into Weeping Springs can be difficult to find - it's located in an open section of canyon just before another section of narrows. The final climb back up the steep headwall to the car was long and grueling.

Completed loop version. Did canyon and packraft with 10 people, 4 of us hiked faster back to vehicles faster to drive home. Death pit is super easy to avoid, as long as you have more than beginner rappel skills. Death pit was completely full of water, and probably able to swim out, but not tested. Can do rappel #2 as one 180ish rap or split it up into 60 and 120. We split it thinking it would be easier pull situation, no pull issues. Replaced webbing at bottom stage. Rest of canyon could stay dry to river. Brought thin wetsuit for packraft, but not necessary. Nice exit hike back. Rest of anchors looked fine. No pull issues on any raps.

It was the first run of the season and we built new anchors on R1, R2 and R3, which we completed as the full 180' rappel. We moved the R3 anchor to a boulder at the edge of the drop and had no issues with the pull. The highlight of our day was the easy float down the Colorado River (20 mins) and spending a full hour at the exit beach hanging out and jumping off the rocks into the beautiful blue-green water. No need for any cold water protection for the float on a 75° day. The scramble up Bighorn was fun and then it was a 1,000' slog back up to the cars.

With a team of 2 (one of us needing twice as long as the other to hike out), we were car to car in 8 hours using the Bighorn Canyon exit. Similar to Weeping Spring, the exit up Bighorn was the scenic highlight of the outing for me. The "Death Pit" poses an interesting problem especially for first person down. Although not terribly difficult to avoid, the risk of pendulum is not without consequence. After trying the high line (the obvious line when looking down) and deciding there must be an easier way, I descended below to check for a lower line. This resulted in the need to ascend back to the high (only) line. It is very doable but the first person needs to be competent. The second (and last) person in our group had no trouble with the confidence of a fireman belay and a redirect. The "canyon" below here is very straight forward. We went only days after some significant rain and were able to stay completely dry. The lower portion of Bighorn is considerably more overgrown with Tamarisk than I remember from 6 or 7 years ago despite there being some obvious restoration work not far up from the beach.
All anchors in good shape, webbing on the 4th is a little crusty but still okay. 200' rope was short on the first rappel, we needed to use a pull cord. Exited up bighorn.

Ran it in 7 hours C2C (30min shuttle each way) including 20 min packraft up river to the AZ hot springs and 1.5 hour break at the hot springs. All webbing and anchors in great conditions. No water present until the river.

First off, this is in no way a 4 canyon. The death pit is not enough of a challenge to warrant that designation, and is easily overcome with basic traversing moves common in 3 level canyons. All that being said, this was a great day. Worth noting our time includes exiting via a leisurely 3.5 mile packraft to cranes nest as opposed to up AZ or Bighorn, so times are a bit longer. Car to river was 4 hours give or take. The cairn anchors were all torn apart and rebuilt, and webbing at the death pit rappel was replaced. Other webbing could go for replacement but we had limited supply and hadn’t run the canyon before so we backed up raps to gain confidence instead. We did 4 rappels, treating the death pit as one 180’ rappel as well as the subsequent one which beta says can be split up. Very enjoyable day.

The actual time was 4.5 hours, car to car at the upper TH. We DIDN'T exit to the river, pack raft and then exit up Big Horn Canyon. We found a Class 2 exit to get back up to the main canyon to the TH. Part of this exit is the same as the Class 2 exit out of Weeping Springs Canyon. Death Pit canyon was in good condition. There is one anchor that the webbing will probably need to be replaced soon. Interesting traverse around the Death Pit. It certainly helps to have some knowledge of basic rock climbing...so that you can identify the small foot and hand holds necessary to cross the wall. I have attached a KML file for the Class 2 exit. Enjoy!

The actual time was 6.5 hours. BUT, this included running Weeping Springs too. We ran Death Pit first and then took the class 2 exit back up to an easy connection point with Weeping Springs. From this point you can be at rap 1 of Weeping Springs in just a few minutes. Running the 2 canyons together added about 2 hours to running only one canyon. We took a different approach to crossing the Death Pit. As already reported, there is a ledge LDC above the pit that is easy to get on, traverse and continue on rope. The pendulum pull on the rope while doing this is minimal. For those that don't want to traverse the sheer rock face of the pit, this is a great alternative.

We went well above the pin for the canyon location and did not experience anything more technical than some straightforward downclimbs. We did encounter some deep pools that can be avoided with wide stemming or via bypass. The canyon is pretty and has some neat sculpted sections. A lot of bones and boulder hopping throughout. The road out to the start is overgrown with vegetation. Would be a fun non-tech solo for those craving a rugged wilderness feel.
Great canyon for Grand Canyon standards. A lot of hiking for just 3 hours of technical but worth the hike and worth the experience.

Webbing was in pretty great condition throughout the slot. We replaced two anchors for the entrance raps. The "keeper" is full of material and seems unlikely to be a keeper any time soon. I can't imagine doing this canyon in two days. The exit down to Deer creek camp was long and tiring, especially with heavy overnight bags. Numerous swims throughout with often very slick mud. The water was murky, sometimes stinky and never very cold. One member wore a 2mm shorty and was only slightly chilly towards the end.

All anchors were in good shape. The keeper pothole wasn't keeping. It was cold and drizzly while we were in the slot, shifting to hail at one point. Although one member was okay in a 4/3, those of us in drysuits were happier. Time listed is from the rim to the river.

The water level was low-moderate. We started just above Snowshoe Falls and did the full canyon. Due to time considerations, we bypassed many of the shorter rappels. We built a deadman anchor with several large rocks in the main watercourse at Keekwulee Falls. From where we rigged, it took about 130' of rope to reach the bottom.

Despite the recent heatwave (105F+), a surprisingly large snowfield sits just upstream of R3 blocking the width of the canyon. It doesn't look safe to cross or skirt-around. We didn't check any higher, and started our decent from R3 (boulder midstream). The water emerging from under the snow was chilly (no shit). R4 (Keekwulee Falls) was kicking out a significant rooster tail. We went from a single bolt + tree combo on the DCR flow, all the way to the pool below the falls. It kept us out of the main rooster tail flow, but still in the fun flow that pours less aggressively off the lip. On the lower angle slopes below, passing under the main flow gave a powerful but fun hammering (entirely avoidable). From there we made the most of all the potential raps downstream. The raps are all quite short and technically tame, but the flow was still high enough to keep it fun. In lower flow they might be more tedious and warrant bypassing. There wasn't much sign of existing webbing, so we burnt through a lot re-rigging the raps. [starting from above R3 in beta] * R1 - Short, but steep with steps. Pool is deep on DCL side. * R2 - Keekwulee Falls, as described above. * R3 - Short, from obvious boulder DCL. * R4 - Short from DCR boulder set back a little. Awkward step halfway down. * R5 - Double slides from DCR boulder set far back. * R6 - Low angle series of cascades, from threaded boulders midstream. The Garcia gauge (12143400) was reporting 170cfs.

Upper 2 Rappels are not an option. Snow /avalanche flow is still 30 ft deep and impassable. Start at R3, off side trail to top of the falls. We had to hunt and built almost every anchor. Natural anchors are being swept away during hight water floods or webbing picked up by visitors, even from the trip I did 1 1/2 month ago. Water level is VERY low. Most pools at the bottom of the Rappels are only 4 ft deep and not jumpable.
Nice sunny day to enjoy an easy canyon. Flow was very low. Lots of hikers around the upper falls and crowds near the bottom. Parking lot fills up quickly.

FR 315 has been excavated and is now much less aggressive. Approach was slow-going and extremely brushy. Light flow. Fixed handline present at first anchor. Left our rope at second anchor to ascend out after completing the lower rappels.

Drive: No prob in a stock 4x4 Tacoma Hike: relentlessly both pretty (nice little pools and waterfalls all the way) and tedious (ever see enough catclaw and poison ivy to make cheatgrass patches a relief?) Nice to have decent water to purify all the way. The good stuff: Beautiful! Fun mix of waterfall raps and short swims. On both the longer drops, we couldn’t see the landing zones. So I sent people without third hands in case they had floating disconnects. Both landings are on rocky ground. Next time I’d rig beginners with VTs. Fixed rope at R1 still in decent shape.

Massive pools! I was surprised how cool this area is. You don't see this much water anywhere else in Central or Southern AZ. We hung out by the fern-filled grotto after R4 for a while. Really sad about the prospect of new mining at oak flat. Road can be a bit tricky and hike starts out fine down hackberry creek but is VERY slow going once in the main canyon. I jumped R1 after checking depth. R2 seems maybe jumpable but has more of a rock shelf to clear, so not for me. There's a handline installed at R1 so we only had to leave and ascend a rope at R2.

11 hours car to car using the Pueblo Canyon trail and the gully to reach the top. The trail that takes you south to the canyon once you top out is pretty easy to follow, but having the GPS track to follow would be helpful. Trail was overgrown with thick new mexico locust in one spot that was fairly painful - long sleeves and pant would help. Rebuilt many anchors - this canyon is rarely done and has many rappels - bring webbing and rings. Nice fall colors. There can be a few shallow pools in the first long hallway where you will get your feet wet.

A long day completed by doing the bottom-up approach loop. A high clearance vehicle is required. Hiked up to the waterfall in Pueblo Canyon and then south up a gully to Rim Trail 139 that was easy to find. This detour had us exiting in the dark at the end of the day. Webbing was all fairly new. Water was flowing after leaving the corridor. A light rain jacket was used but no wetsuit was required on and after the big rap which we had a rope pull issue. Lots of easy downclimbing and boulder hopping.

We did the shuttle. Started at 9:15 and didn't reach R1 until 10:45. The track on the map was heavily overgrown with thorny bushes. I would strongly suggest that anyone else approach directly from the direction of Aztec Peak to avoid crossing a number of overgrown drainages along the way. We weren't in any hurry, so we wound up walking out 30 minutes after dark. There was a black-tailed rattler a quarter-mile from the bottom, in spite of the cold overnight temps. Anchors were just adequate. They will need help shortly. The newer ones were 1/2 inch webbing.

We did the shuttle - dropped a car at Devi's Chasm Saturday night, camped near Parker Canyon, then drove to the upper terminus (by the ranch) on Sunday morning. Payson Ranger District says Workman Creek Road is closed...gate was open and road was in fine shape...not sure if they will close it soon. Trail 141 is fine...trail 139 only exists on paper and is full of locust. I agree with previous poster who said to go in from Aztec Peak...this too will be full of locust but is the shortest route so pick your poison. Approach sucks. Canyon rocks. Some small pockets of very avoidable shallow water. All rappels were in pretty good shape- # 2 and window to the world are even bolted. We fiddle sticked the 180 foot rap which worked well...feels like that pull could easily trap a conventional rappel set up. All in all great wilderness canyon that I would do again.

Beautiful, raw, and remote! As of this weekend, Cherry Creek Road is still open! However, there are plans to close this road in the future. Also keep in mind that in the rainy season, Cherry Creek will become impassable, however, water levels were low when we went! We did the bottom up approach, driving up Cherry Creek Road all the way up to the trailhead, where the approach for Pueblo and Devil's Tail begin. The drive up Cherry Creek Road begins as easy dirt, eventually has some small boulder fields to navigate, and finally a semi-steep uphill crawl with some loose rock up to the parking area. This can be done in a stock 4wd vehicle. From the pavement to TH, this drive takes on average 1.5hrs. Airing down your AT/MT tires is recommended, as this is a very remote trail and tire damage is possible! The approach took about 4hrs total, which starts up the Pueblo trail then cuts off into a drainage which you follow all the way up to the saddle, gaining 3000' of elevation. Multiple fields of very loose rock must be navigated along the way but overall very manageable and wasn't too strenuous. Once on top, following the KML track we made our way to the Ridge Trail, following that over to the drop in. The Ridge Trail is very easy hiking but disappears a few times. We took the easy shortcut through the flat woods on multiple occasions, meeting up with the trail along the way. Staying closer to the ridge's edge in the open forest, avoids the terrible Black Locust forest the the KML leads you through! The Fall colors were in full effect and gave a fantastic display!! Dropping into the crack is spectacular, once inside, surrounded by tall sheer walls, you get immersed into another world. The long rock hallway is amazing. There is a point where you can look up and the long shape of the hallway combined with an opening in the roof makes the shape of a "Devil's Tail" which is where I believe the canyon's name originated! Lots of fun downclimbs and rappels through this hallway which eventually spits you out into a lush gorge with interesting rock, beautiful features, and multiple large cliffy rappels with far reaching views of a primitive, old world landscape. Truly feels as though you are stepping back in time! Lots and lots of downclimbs through out this route, it is much longer than you may think! As you follow the drainage, water becomes more and more abundant, eventually turning into a flowing stream then as you near the end, a babbling brook where 2 drainages meet! We picked up a well used trail near the end DCR which led into a camping area near the road where we dropped our packs and began the 45min hike back up to where we parked the truck at the TH. All webbing looking pretty good! Fantastic day, we did the last quarter of the canyon/exit in the dark, plan on headlamps!

Geat adventure! We ascended devils chasm for our approach and spent 1 hour at the indian ruins. Our route had two 5th class sections to negotiate. We entered a chasm on the left side and that presented climbing challenges. If you stay in the main drainage you may be able to bypass these obstacles. The approach took 5 hours with the stop at the ruins and the canyon + exit took another 5. Approaching from Devils Chasm will likely save time over the shuttle and pueblo approach but could increase difficulty. We replaced a lot of anchors with black webbing. The canyon had some flow after the slot section. Rain jackets were useful, wetsuits would be overkill.

A very long day with a long approach and exit. Considering Waterslides is in the same area, I'm not sure I would do this one again. It's kind of a one and done. The ruins are definitely the best part. If the canyon had a shorter approach and exit I might give it a 3.5/5 stars but the effort necessary is why I give it a 2. I ended up replacing multiple piece of webbing so that anchors are now all in good condition.

This is really a fantastic adventure route with tons of unique features thoughout. We approached via Devil's Chasm, starting at 7:30am, and paid a visit to the spectacular ruin site on our way up. We eventually ended up in the same side drainage Eric and team found, facing a 5th class obstacle. We did not see an obvious path around this obstacle as the main drainage funneled us into this spot. We didnt see a break away where there'd be any other way to go, but perhaps it was much further back. Infact, it only became obvious that this was a side drainage once we were above the 5th class climbs and rejoined the main drainage. The first climb has a dead tree leaned over on to it. I was able to girth hitch a 4ft sling/biner around the tree up high to protect the climb. My partner belayed me up, I chose the outside line which has a slight over hang. The move was definitely challenging and would not be safe without protection. Took me a minute to defeat this crux, I was able to move the girthed sling up the tree to continue protecting as I climbed. Once up, I had my partner ascend the rope. There was another easier 5th class section following this that I was able to free climb and top rope belay my partner from a tree. Rejoining the drainage there were plenty of easier 4th class scrambles throughout, not terribly bushy. Autumn colors are coming in nicely at higher elevation. Approach took us 5.5hrs total to 1st rap. This canyon is really unique with it's tall narrow halls and intricate layered basalt rock throughout. There were a few spots early on that we got our feet wet, no real wades or swims. Lots of larger good quality rappels, especially in the lower half! Surprisingly, despite the last report, there was still plenty of webbing that needed replacing bad throughout, mostly due to abrasive cuts. Anchors should all be good for now. Technical section and exit took us 4.5hrs. Back at camp with plenty of daylight! I feel that this route has been made even better with the addition of the Devil's Chasm approach, adding a ruin site, removing the short shuttle and making it easy to start/end your day right at camp!

Onsight solo. Approached from Devil's Chasm. Chose the left gulley at the split while on approach and did a V1 boulder move to get up into the ramp. Totally not worth it imo. Above are more moves on very bad rock. Next time would just go to the right. Approach took me 4 hours. The canyon itself was pretty underwhelming. Considering a 4 hour approach and what I'll say is a 5 hour exit, the 1 hour of canyon is a huge letdown. The beginning slot section is pretty good but it's over quickly in about an hour. The remainder of the time is spent rock hopping through flooded boulder fields with the occasional rappel in between. Not my style but maybe for someone looking for more of a remote wilderness experience

We took just under 6 hours with a lot of extra work being done. This was the first time that anyone had run the canyon, set the anchors and established a recorded route. It definitely took a lot of time to do this. We feel that it is an easy 4-6 hour canyon now that the route is established.

On 11/15/01 a stock Subaru hatchback made it to the trailhead with no issues. Light flow at the 2 water crossings. After/during monsoons or heavy precipitation this road can be closed or impassable. At a very casual pace we 2 people completed this hike in 4 hours. Beautiful Fall colors, and trail was very easy to navigate with falling leaves and light flow. Running water nearby so pack light for this short yet strenuous adventure hike.

Quick morning jaunt before running Pueblo Canyon later in the day - took just under 2 hours moving at a swift pace. Trail was easy to navigate and there were two handlines in place at the 4th class obstacles. One of the best non-technical canyons in the state.

I recommend taking some pruners to cut back the thorns, it would be easy and make your and everyone elses life easier, and less bloody and ripped clothing. Also would be very good to take lopers and remove some of the worst bushwhack.

After setting up the shuttle, we began hiking from the junction in the road at approximately 830am. Following the gpx from BlueGnome, we navigated to the drop in without difficulty. All anchors looked to be good with the exception of the last one which was replaced with new webbing. The only potential problem was a rope pull issue on the penultimate rappel. The rock near the top going over the edge is rough with lots of friction and places to catch the rope. We did a test pull and could not get it to budge. Ultimately, we removed the block and anchored from the bottom. Doing this as a single rappel allowed us to get a better angle on the pull. If you were to break this up into two stages, the pull could be difficult as you would be forced to be close to the wall. After the final rappel, we bushwacked to the river, rafted to the AZ hot springs, and enjoyed some time there before hiking out the car. We arrived back at the car just as it got dark, around 7pm.

The anchors and webbing on the 4 necessary raps were in good condition. Rap 2 had a very hard pull due to the amount of flat and sloping rock at the top. The anchor could be extended so that there is less flat rock to pull over. Shortly after the last, long, two stage rap the canyon will start to fill up with very dense growth that can be extremely challenging to find a route through. The good news is that there are not a lot of cat's claw trees. We did our best to stay high on the canyon sides but there are times that you have no choice but to battle the overgrowth. The growth was so thick that we decided to climb out RDC a half mile before the river and got into Emerald Pools Canyon for our exit. We had planned on using this canyon to get back to our vehicle, avoiding the need for a pack raft and long shuttle. The exit canyon was a good option. Be prepared for a 800 foot, very steep climb at the end of the canyon. It is a little loose, but manageable.

It is a great adventure experience if you don't mind some strenuous bush walking. You are in the deep wilderness of the Black canyon. We combined this route with a Big Horn canyon exit. A real gem, if you have a right mindset

Frank Crane road was closed, lots of "no trespassing" signs. Had to go the long way around to get there, via Crystal Creek road. Took an hour of dirt roads each way, passable in a Honda Cr-V. Might have been fun if there was more water but this time of year it was just a trickle. Lots of bush whacking to rappel a mostly dry stream, not worth it. We skipped the last two rappels, tired of fighting through the thick undergrowth. Beautiful area at least. Two stars for the potential.

We set up a bunch of slung anchors and fiddlesticked a bit (all natural anchors). Beautiful day. The flow in the middle fork Snoqualmie was around 350 (accidentally said 650 before - honest mistake, sorry!) cfs, and flow in the canyon created some dangerous hydraulics in places. Flow down some of the falls was sufficient to kick you around hard. Rock near the creek was slippery from algae. Jumps noted in beta (edited 8/26/18) were all doable. Some had shallow water but no hard landings. We exited the canyon at the boulder garden above R14 and finished the canyon on 8/26/18 at significantly lower flow.

Did the canyon from R14 down to finish it off from our descent on 7/22/18. Flow was low enough that getting under the falls and moving through the flow was much more doable than on 7/22. Flow in the Middle Fork Snoqualmie was at 150 cfs.

Ran Dingford Creek on a rainy day with 11 people. We dropped in from the start and ran the full descent and found some existing cord/cordelettes at several drops. It took about 7 hours for us to complete. This is a wonderful canyon full of jumps and slides and beautiful water. It will surely become a classic!
Ran this again and established a couple more anchors. There were no hydraulics.

First descent of 2019. MF Snoqualmie gauge was at 306. Flow was slightly sporty. Nothing was too pushy; it was possible to stand at almost all of the pour overs, but a couple of the beginners needed frequent spotting. We did 8 rappels, a handful of small jumps and short slides. Some time was spent replacing anchor webbing. Half were natural anchors, half bolted. The route could be improved with a couple more bolt stations. One stuck rope was time consuming. 9 hours car-to-car.

Great group of aquatic swimmer/river rafting guides. Ran everything except the last 4 rappels due to higher flows significantly impact the access to the anchors station on the 4 bottom rappel;s. No new log jams or rock movements. All jumps and slide from last year are all viable. All 1/2 bolt/chains are in good shape and well placed. YOU must know where to go, because the beta provided is useless. This is my 16th trip down in the last 2 years and knowledge of rocks and downclimbs are critical. Trail up is great, and road in is slow and pothole and requires a SUV type vehicle clearance.

The Beta has been updated to reflect where/when is a slide, downclimb, rappel or jump etc. The Water wast 340 CFS and again we only did upper 3/4, but the lower is almost there. We went down the middle of dragon chute and Chunder dome with no problems except a lot of fun water hitting you.

Descended with the gauge reading about 150cfs. Still decent flow for "relatively low" conditions. Maybe not "sporty" but still plenty of flow to make it interesting. Just FYI for anyone on the newer side considering dropping in at 300 or 400+cfs. Although it sounds like flows may not be representative of the gauge? More importantly, this canyon has had a reputation for and continues to be talked about as a fun "waterpark." This is not only largely inaccurate but disingenuous and dangerous. I can attest from personal experience in 2019 going into it with an "everything is play time" attitude and broke my leg nearly at the top of the canyon on a minor drop. Just from what I've heard myself in 2020, there have been at least four minor injuries in this canyon. THIS IS NOT A WATERPARK. Many of the pools are NOT deep and wide. Boulders and ledges in many pools are numerous and frequent. There are slippery spots. Jumping or sliding in the wrong place or using poor form could easily lead to injury.
