(And all the more difficult to read at higher flows with more whitewater.) In addition, this canyon has downclimbing that may be difficult and intimidating for newer canyoneers. A slip could be disastrous, especially at higher flows. Please don't be lulled into a cavalier attitude in this canyon by those not warning of the risks or going in to only maximize fun.

First time down the canyon for the group. Hard to exactly follow the beta in places, but navigated without too much trouble in the end. I concur with other comments about "water park" being a misleading description - it seemed to have few straight-forward jumps but plenty of risky sections. One member of the group sprained their ankle jumping from the J2 high-option (over-shot the narrow landing by a few feet). After continuing down the canyon for a further 20 minutes it was apparent they needed to escape. We protected a few extra sections to help their descent, then used the halfway escape-point to escort them back to the trail, where they were able to return to the trailhead unassisted. It will be very interesting to see what this is like in different water levels.

Gauge was about 275 about a week after heavy rains. Seemed comparable to photos/videos from a higher gauge reading in early season. Significantly more interesting and challenging than mid summer conditions. I'd personally recommend wearing a hat/brim to go through the middle of the flow on the chunderdome, at least at these levels.

First decent. Both of us are Class V whitewater guides and love pushy water, which it was. Very slipper rock, PLEASE use felt booties, my 5-10 were slippery. Did the upper 3/4, and bailed on the last 4 raps, because there water flow was just waves leading into the next rappel station. All bolts are in great shape, no new debris in pools or new rock falls filling the pools. Beta is still good. I would not recommend doing this at any higher level that 400 CFS on the main fork. 2 comments below are accurate, this is not a Disney land water park, but more a short slipper walk/crawl to the next challenge water park. This is granite rock park, that is not known for forming deep pools due to the hardness of the rock, compared to sandstone. Assume your depth are shallow and alway lower non-jumpers to check depth. This is my 20th trip down this and knowing your routes greatly increases your enjoyment. If you are interested in doing this canyon, please contact me and I most likely will be free to join you.
Ran the full canyon. Did the wet routes on all options, no issues. Rock is still slippery. Nice canyon with afternoon sunshine
Ran whole route. Great water level for the wet lines (Tanner @ ~275cfs).

Ran it in the rain and chilly 60 degrees. Water level rose slightly during the day, made it funner. We lost a yellow back pack, with 300 ft rope in the final rappel, pool. Tossed it and it just went down. If found let me know.
Fast trip - 10.15am departure from trailhead, messing around in the final pool by 3.15pm. Great moderate fun flows & great low hassle day, despite the heat & smoke.

Still a little smoky, Verified a couple rappels can be slides also. Will update Beta. Ran the wet and dry routes with this big group based on each persons preferences. All pulls good. Recovered non floating back pack out of final pool. Great day. Going back in on the 17th

Back again. Cool & cloudy weather meant we opted to keep moving all day. Ended up completing the whole technical section in 3.5hrs; a total of 4.5hr overall. Gauge at 180cfs. Flows & pool depths definitely lower than earlier in the month, but no impact on the jumps & slides we usually do.

Fantastic day an quiet possibly the last of the season for Dingford. Middle Fork gauge was at 233. The water was on the high side of moderate, but did not cause any challenges, however, we did utilize hand lines using present bolts for two rappels. All the usual jumps and slides were good.

We only ran about 75% of Dingford. Middle fork snoqualmie gage at 574 cfs. This is still extremely high flow and you should only go with those who know the canyon intimately and leaders who are expers in white water canyoning environments (different than just white water rafting or any other sport involving turbulent water). Lots of avoidance, sketchy pool crossings, high risk of getting swept over if obstacles are not properly protected and lots of planning ahead. We avoided the Chunderdome by using the bolts DCR. Make sure to protect any pool crossing!

120cfs on the gauge, the lowest I've run it. Many pools felt shallow, but constrictions still had force to be weary of. Expected Chunderdome to be much milder, but it still had chunder to it. (Note, despite the gap in trip reports, many 10s of people have run the canyon since July).

All bolts fixed and good. All pools look clean, but always check. updated beta for couple new bolts put in last year. Ran the heart of dragon chute and chunderdome. Fun, fun. Great group of canyoneers.

Lost a 1/3 of the flow in one week. Less pushy, everything still good, but less surfing on some of the rappels. Was planning on doing mineral creek, but the road was closed, so did the turn around to here. Fun fun canyon. saw other groups in but they stayed just ahead.

Good run through a great canyon as always. Beta for R13 at the Toaster says not to jump but if you down climb through the tight sections in the flow (highly recommended in manageable conditions) our entire party was easily able to do a short canyon butt flop into the pool below R13 without issue and entirely bypass the rappel. Also recommend not underestimating the Chunderdome if utilizing the “in the flow” rap. It’s impossible to see your foot placements and the volume of water in the rappel line is not to be trifled with. There’s also several foot traps that 3 out of 4 of us found by stepping into them during the rappel. Could easily break an ankle/entrap someone in higher flow. Otherwise all bolts are good and the canyon is still a classic.
Great water park of a canyon. Super fun day, even if flow was a bit low. Chunderdome was quite mild.

Big water dropping quickly. This is not a waterpark, there are very specific routes and there should alway be someone in your party who has done the canyon before, because the beta can not be so exact. I am alway open if you need a person. Today, flow 550, all bolts good, but skipped the bottom 4 rappels, due to time and Thrill Vs risk. The waterslides were very pushy, trying to take you over the next lip. Two logs in Chunderdome, One completely sideways at the top, blocking wet route. Whoever removes them need to make sure they do not drop in and block the wet route channel. I reset the webbing up on DCL for dryish route.
The flow was somewhat spicy, especially in the chunderdome and the last rappel. No hydraulic dangers to worry about. The flow was about 270cfs at middle fork Tanner.

Great day in Dingford! Flow was still relatively high for this late in the season. Overall water conditions were moderate but brutally cold. Time includes lapping the big slide 5 times but bailing after the Chunderdome (at which we set up a guided rappel). By the time we finished the Chunderdome rappel we had a party member who was pre-hypothermic so we exited by scrambling up a chute DCR.

Finally to hit the famous Dingford! Nothing like a 20ft jump to start a canyon. Great canyon and a lot of fun, with a great group. Anchors were all solid. This canyon is well taken care of. I'll be back!
Amazing day in Dingford! All anchors are in good shape and there are no special concerns right now. Enjoy!

Great day down dingford. Tons of fun knowing all the slides and jumps in the canyon. Water flow was surprisingly not a lot for having rained a lot. Water was warmer than expected too (still quite cold)

All anchors were good, slides and jumps were more shallow than normal. Shallow technique is required even for jumps and slides that normally go. More moss than peak season, watch for slippery edges and climbs.

Ran at 160 cfs per comparison gauge, felt lower moderate, maybe mod low? Good splashy fun taking friends down. Some of the jumps and slides were noticeably more shallow than when I first ran it at 220 cfs a month prior.
Great day. All anchors are good. Normal jumps and slides are good too.
Tons of bushwacking, nothing interesting about this canyon. Not worth doing.

Updated KML track with a slightly shorter approach which leaves the forest road early and cuts across to the drainage, bypassing some of the dense brush at the start of the canyon. Replaced R1 webbing, and packed out some junk anchor material that had been washed further down canyon. There’s a rattlesnake in the narrows section. I was able to stem up and over it. Pools were deep, but avoided all except the final swim (ref. attached photo).
Very small narrow section, great for taking small kids from bottom up

No anchors were found, so possible first descent. Ascended steeps slopes west of the creek to reach a flat-ish area around 2900ft, then dropped into the creek. Nice-looking 45ft falls just above... next time we'll drop in a little higher up. Relatively low flow, although things still can get interesting when it's concentrated all in one area. R2 is the big pitch and is excellent: running right down a V-shaped groove. You have to drop into one pocket and push yourself through a veil of falling water to get out. The entire falls gradually turns DCR. Below, the canyon tightens up significantly with numerous back-to-back obstacles. Very reminiscent of nearby Clark Creek.

The pothole referenced in the printed beta is all but dry on the exit side and was quite the challenge to get out of solo. I had hard fixed all the drops above it to allow retreat in the event I couldn’t solve it, so when I did get through it (using two potshots and my pack) I then had to ascend two drops and pull three ropes before I could continue downcanyon. It’s probably still easy enough with an assist from a partner, but do be aware it’s currently as close to keeper mode as it likely ever gets. The first person out will need a boost and the last person left in it will need a handline.

Two hours using the river exit. Our group used a handline in one spot (15 feet from a tree) and rappelled the last drop, leaving an anchor behind - I downclimbed everything. Comfort with the downclimbs will vary from group to group. Two of the group used a handline to negotiate the last section of cliff band before the shore at the river on the exit, the other two hiked in the river through the deep mud.

Descended to river and climbed back out upcanyon. Left a rope as a handline wrapped on a rock for one of the drops in the middle, it was approximately 25'. With the setback of the rock my 60' rope was barely sufficient (double stranded)

Parked about 1.5 miles away from the TH due to snow patches covering the road so likely any group that goes after will be much faster if they park at the TH. Dropped in at 3800' but would recommend dropping in around 3400-3500' as there is a lot of creeking and nuisance rappels prior. Water is still snow melt, there are a few small waist deep pools.

Fun short little canyon with practically no approach and a quick exit, definitely worth doing if you have an hour or so to kill. Some easy stemming (which for most people will be optional, as it is also possible to walk along the bottom of the V-shaped slot) and chimneying down-climbs that aren't terribly difficult but could be intimidating to beginners; probably a good idea to bring a short rope or ~30 ft of webbing in case someone wants a handline. Exit up the slickrock prow seemed to require one slightly tricky move that is fairly easy but exposed, beginners or unconfident climbers may want a handline here too.

A nice short canyon with roadside access. With only one pitch at the start, only minutes from the road, you can pull your rope after you exit. If you use a tensionless hitch off one of the trees you'll want a 35m rope, otherwise the pitch is around 25m - though you can downclimb the last couple of metres. The canyon is quite narrow with a 'V' cross section pretty much the whole way. Some high stemming is required and you wouldn't want to fall. There were a few shallow pools, all easily avoidable except at the end which required a little more effort. The exit posed the biggest challenge for us. There had been 10cm of fresh snow overnight making travelling on the slickrock too dangerous. We ended up climbing the slot to the top before contouring around. This involved one tricky offwidth crack climb.
FD bias in a sense, verde river is great but the drainage is a total slog.

Ran this canyon after a group ran Dark and another ran Davis. So it is possible. We took and abbreviated rout to avoid the 40 minute creek walk and dropped us in almost to the top drop. We jump the first drop from both sides. The second rap is NOT deep enough to jump and set up and anchor around a very visible boulder. R3 was jumped from a ledge about halfway down by the majority of the group.

Checked out East after running Dark. We parked further up the road and bushwhacked directly east to the first drop. The exit at the Cispus river was running very high and very cold- silty glacial melt with rapids. It was easy to stick to the bank but use caution.

First visit after several recommendations to check this one out. Took the NorHex Route in and descended a narrow draw into the creek. Wow, what a canyon! Great bang for the buck. The last rappel into the narrows is spectacular. We found the exit route without much difficulty.

Exiting Dark Creek by a good hour, we decided to run East Canyon Creek just down the road. Rebuilt anchor at R2 (looks like it's underwater in high flow). Pools are amazing and lovely float out through the narrows. Less awesome: the bushwhack back out. Will update the beta on the best route. Ideally, the routes in & out should be flagged to standardize route development.

First time here and what a lovely creek! Good fun to play around in the pools, and it’s very pretty! The NorHex approach was great minus the final descent to the creek… though far better than a bunch of creek walking. We found R2 to be jumpable, though with a technical launch DCL. The pool below was at least 8’ deep… possibly 10’. It gets shallow quickly further away from the rock, so positioning someone where it’s shallow helps the jumper hit a safe target.

We should call this "The Big East". Of all the canyons in the area it had the most flow by a wide margin during the rendezvous. The big flow, big jumps, huge grotto, deep aquamarine pools, swimming channel, make this one of the most underrated canyons. The approach does suck. The exit is actually very easy now due to a pretty well defined use trail up and then open forest walking to the road.

Ayoooo this canyon was a BANGER! Sure the approach and exit aren't fantastic, but this one needs way more love than it gets. The canyon itself was spectacular and the flow was awesome, really glad we ran it. Each rappel was jumpable, but we touched bottom on the second pool. If I wasn't the first person down on the third rappel, to depth check, I would've jumped! That pool was so deep and I shorted the rope so the other members had fun plopping into the water.

Short but a lot of fun. Did the overland entrance which wasn't great, but I've done worse for less. First rappel went as a jump, second was a little too shallow, and the last was jumpable as well after rappelling down to the ledge.

Fantastic canyon with a lot of beauty and big wow-factor on that last 35-ft jump! Air temperature was around 50 degrees, not sure of the water temperature but it was cold. I had 11mm of neoprene in the chest area and 3-4 on the arms/legs. I was chilly in the water but okay.
Nice day out in a beautiful creek. Did 140' rappel on alternate entrance from tributary into East.
Splendid canyon. The 40ft jump from the ledge below R3 makes it a 5 star canyon for me. All anchors were in good condition

Great day in a new creek for me! We bush whacked in from NF-2322 and rappelled the tributary drop into the main creek, whihc was a fun warm up. There is a rebelay/second anchor on the ledge just below the start. Using this makes for a better line down to the main creek. The jump at R1 was great. We stayed out of the flow on the last two rappels. The walk down the Cispus was straightforward and the hike up the hill was steep.
Gorgeous canyon. This one rivals Davis in my opinion. Loved it. The hike out sucks, but it’s well worth it. I echo everything Trey said. Tributary rap all the way. Anchors were in good condition.

We did his Canyon as a back up to Adam’s. Adam’s was being Adams, which was highly unpredictable. We cut out some locals only rope that had webbing connected to it as if it was going to be an anchor. Did I just read the previous report comparing Davis to East Canyon? Wow. Thats wild.

If rappelling R2 the line you take will determine how clean your pull is, considering the quick link for the pinch anchor is both underneath the boulder providing the pinch and situated well far back from the edge. The beta says rappel to the left, but I was surprised how far to the left that had to be. Should’ve just jumped it but I was running solo. It was nice knowing from a previous party that the R3 jump was clear and deep. This jump is the only thing makes the hike out worth it.

Great sunny day in the canyon. We used the bridge approach and is was a scenic and floaty creek walk. All the anchors were good and the jumps went, although flow was too high for the in flow rappel on R2 so we used the bolt. The large punch bowl on R3 does form a recirc at this flow rotating back toward the falls along the DCL wall. A strong swim across toward DCR will put you in the outflow.
Great run after Dark Creek. All of us jumped the first rap, most of us jumped the second, and the final rappel was great! Floated down the Cispus to the exit.

A surprisingly nice outing in the Oregon Coast Range. Very aesthetically-pleasing quality waterfalls. Anchor options were a bit thin on several of the drops, but we made do. Water levels were low, but plenty of thermal protection was still required. Ice was present in several areas. Use caution in winter, particularly on the downclimbs.

Very fun day in the creek. Visibly more flow than in January, but not pushy. Last rap is the best; we climbed back up a faint trail DCR, across a high knoll with a stand of tall trees above the rap, then descend again on faint trail about 40' down to the rappel. Well worth the effort for a second go. Exit: for the final 1/4 mile we stayed in the creek, staying completely out of sight from the house to the west. Climb out of creek to highway was short and easy.

The approach took us through the valley of the shadow of death with bones and elk remains. More bones were found in the creek. The exit trail had lots of bear, cougar and elk poop. The creek itself was pretty good even with the low flow. The waterfalls are all scenic. The last rappel is the best.

Returning to East Zigzag. Not much noticeable change after the atmospheric river events of this winter, although seemed less slippery than last time. Some drops would be worth bolting if a better exit route could be found. Possibly the local loggers will oblige eventually. Saw clear bear prints in the mud on the hike out.

Canyon was as described, and we followed suit with ghosting techniques. There is an abundance of wasps and bees throughout the canyon. One of our group members suffered a half dozen stings. The approach is not as difficult as I thought, the canyon was 5-star, and the hike out is atrocious. Be prepared for a quarter mile of solid bush-whacking before finding a trail. Without having done it, I might recommend following the creek to the private land, then assending to the south ridge to find the trail.

Decent canyon. Good for the area, though I enjoy Fort Creek more. We spent time installing anchors during this trip and It may be more fun running the canyon without having to stop at each rappel for an extended period of time.

The hike in was not bad and in the fall there were no bees or wasps. Start early the jeep roads to the put in are very hot if it was the middle of the day. The canyon would be crazy in high water. The hike out if you no where your going is 10 minutes of real uphill bushwacking then not bad at all
Anchors in good condition. Added 1 bolt for a really slippery downclimb not suitable on the current flow. Between rappel 3 and 4.

Some interesting rock formations and waterfalls along with a stellar jump point into a pool were high points. Very low water levels despite recent rains. Lot of creek walking through debris. Not worth it in lower flow.

First descent of Eightmile. Some nice falls, but the exit is kinda long. Fairly high pay-to-play. Would be good to go earlier for better flow. Maybe April-May timeframe. This one may be a good early season creek.

Nice flow today, but we should have skipped the lower three falls. The creek walk to the lower sequence followed by the long creek walk out to Panther Creek is not worth it. It would be best to leave the creek via the exit after rap 2.

Super easy canyon. A little flowing water in upper section. One chest deep pool, one waist deep pool, a couple shin deep pools, but otherwise pretty easy to avoid water. Wetsuit only if get cold easily, otherwise through the pools pretty fast. Neoprene or wool socks. All anchors except first rap look like they have fairly new webbing. No pull issues. Scenic, deep canyon.

Parked at the exit and hiked up to the top. Encountered some hostile cows en route and climbed up a slope to give them time to clear the area. Other than that, a nice mellow canyon. Very colorful. Set an anchor on the drop into the deep pool, but suspicious one can climb up a gully DCL to an anchor higher up which allows one to bypass the pool entirely. Good fun -- I'd run it again.

Recent rains added to the adventure. Had a couple kids and a brand new person. Ended up doing seven rappels because of the water level. The waterfall rappels were fun. We avoided one swimmer before rap three by climbing up to the ledge and walking the slope back to the creek. (we were still staying dry at that point.)

A pretty straight forward and easy canyon. Beautiful scenery. The recent rains certainly added to the beauty and quality of the day! Flowing creak and waterfall rappels. Avoided a deep pool at the bottom of rap 3 by climbing up the wall to the left and walking off the slopped ledge. We were able to stay dry for the most part (got wet from the waterfall on rap 4 (or maybe 5??), was unavoidable). Ran this canyon with 2 kids and a beginner that had never been on a rope. Added a few additional rappels, 7 total, because of kids and water levels.

Did it as loop walking back on highway. Bypassed first rap going up on ledge LDC to avoid what looked like at least hip deep clear water. One narrow section with ankle deep water and pool after 2nd rappel is only place got wet. Pool after 2nd rappel only got one foot ankle deep wet with some skills and careful stepping. Otherwise, knee deep at most wet. All anchors looked good, did not replace any.
Shallow pools. Can run the whole canyon direct and not get your feet wet. Anchors in good shape. Dodged posse of feral cattle on the exit.

Did this on a beautiful and sunny day. The rappels are short and leave some to be desired but this was a great place to take newbies! We ran into some cattle on the way out but we gave them their space and they didn't seem to mind us.
Good canyon for first timers. We actually had 5 newbies in the group. Some pools but all avoidable. Hundreds of bees in many places

Parked by the exit and followed the kml path to the start (avoiding the road). Scenic canyon, even before the technical section. Pool at the base of R1 was maybe calf-deep. We avoided by going LDC. Between R2 and R3 is a section of walking, and we’d noticed the referenced bees. There’s a dead vulture at the first stage of R3. The second stage can easily be downclimbed when dry. Re-equalized the R4 anchor for a more LDC start as the first person down reported an unpleasant start RDC. Exit was quick and straightforward. Webbing was all in fine condition. Agree that this is a solid canyon for beginners.

The webbing at many of the anchors was crispy and not trustworthy. Flood(s) have buried webbing at a couple places making it impossible to fully inspect and difficult to replace. The next group through might appreciate a trowel as we had to be cautious of a loop of webbing that wanted to lift off a rock horn. Also recommend bringing a lot of webbing (40-50'). Found several anchors where EDKs were tied instead of water knots. One party member was stung by a bee.

I how done the canyon before the fire in the Pinals and when I just did it, the canyon had completely changed. Sand from the flooding has filled up the entire canyon and what used to be the longest rappel is now about 20 feet shorter. Where the rappel is shorter there is now one extra short rappel. It is still fun, but some of the cool features of the canyon are now buried under sand, like the tunnel that was used to bypass the giant pothole, which is no longer a pothole because of sand.

First 2 anchors are easily downclimbed or climbed around. The webbing on the mandatory raps looked good, as well as the deadman anchor on rap 4. (At least the 4th rap we did. The rap descriptions are a hot mess, and I can understand why… there’s lots of options and downclimbs and climb arounds, it’s hard to keep track.) Take a 100’ rope and a few feet of webbing and you’ll be grand. No bees this time, lots and lots of mud to negotiate. It was mostly avoidable, but not completely, so we got a little down and dirty. Hitchhiked the car shuttle with great luck. Great, easy, beginner-friendly canyon.
Most of the raps are downclimbable. Very dull.

A little bland, maybe, but it actually exceeded my low expectations. Several beautifully sculpted sections. Water levels were very low. I was able to avoid touching any water, but someone shorter than 5'11" may be forced into getting their feet wet at least once. Used existing anchors or downclimbed/bypassed everything. Fun little canyon for someone new to practice some easy downclimbs. 2 hours car-to-car, no shuttle.

R1 was clogged with fallen trees, I ghosted off a tree in the forest into the canyon and skipped R1. Use caution it looks like you could walk down to the river but there is a 30ft canyon wall that you cant see from the forest. There is one exit DCL at R2, past R2 your'e committed but you can see most of the way down the canyon from R2. I didn't like the anchor at R2 it was a fallen tree but there aren't many other good options. r3 and r4 are easy and as described in the original post. I didnt love this canyon, it was a lot of work and wasnt super fun. Lots of logs in the water/falls and no jump or slide options.
First descent of Elkhorn Creek. We had to hike 1mi in the snow as the upper section of the road was not plowed. Water extremely cold. Descended the big falls in four rappels. A keeper.
One pool at middle area rappel, but can stay dry with some bridging skills. No rope pull issues. Replaced cracking sun beaten webbing on last rappel with new webbing.

Big approach and lots of brush. Body armor recommended. Those willing to suffer a bit will be rewarded with sweeping views and multiple large, beautiful rappels. Pools were full, and under these conditions several were unavoidable (waist depth at most). R1 webbing is new. Used a releasable on R1, R2, and R6. Smooth pulls but was very purposeful with rigging selection and rope management. 2.5h approach, 3.5h descent, 1.5h exit. Video: https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=oqBaRczMCuI&t=20s

Approach is fairly clear, the bushwacking starts once you drop into the canyon. We replaced the webbing on R 2,3,5. On R4 first person took it for the team and meat guided the rest dry but it was futile, it was necessary to get wet on R5 ( waist deep ). Due to two stage, going around the corner configuration we fiddled R 3,5

Must have dropped my fitbit on R4 or R5. It would be nice if someone found it. I think R5, the rock that was jammed into the pinch seemed to be loose. Moved the webbing back to a real rock pinch. There was only one place to get wet, crotch deep. The last rap will need new webbing and a quick link.

Cost of admission: After an easy first mile along Heiro Trail, the inhike winds about 2000’ over about a mile through fairly open scrub. Then it relatively levels off on the high ground, meandering through slightly more open terrain and developing trail. The canyon itself was better than either of us expected. We knew there would be great views south, and were pleasantly impressed with a few short but cool slots. Scattered pools got up to about 4’ deep, and water trickled through most of the canyon, including all the drops. R1: Fresh purple webbing. R2: Black loop getting a bit crispy, backed it up with a new green one. Be advised: we hiked past R3 and R4 without noticing them. R5: Has features (2 stages, grass in middle) that seemed to fit the R3 beta, aside from reported heights. Good news is that its easy to see if thrown rope is adequate for first stage, and as long as you’re not alone, you can keep someone at anchor until sorting out second stage requirements. Couldn’t find the list Fitbit. Our rap count mixup became obvious when we next walked up on… R6. Can we talk about this anchor? Primary rig was a long, tan webbing slipknotted around a bush. A foot from the downhill end was a quicklink on an overhand that perfectly reaches the lip. So far so good. Not so good was the extra overhand at the tip of the downhill end. Using that would crossload the nearby loop with the quicklink. Now I wish I’d cut that bottom knot off. The bush seemed to hold us both fine. The “backup”… A stiff, bleached, but chubby length of static rope extended from a nice solid uphill boulder to the anchor bush, but was not connected to the webbing it was meant to back up(!!) We rethreaded the original webbing so it could anchor rappellers, not just the bush. Aso added a webbing sling from the boulder to the main webbing. Anyone know what’s up with the fixed aluminum ladder to a small cave/mine, visible LDC from R6? Outhike has enough catclaw to leave a memorable impression. We were right on the gpx.

Approach is long but isnt bad, brush was minimal and not annoying. Took us about 2.5hrs to get to 1st rap. We rappelled in from the far East side which made for a FABULOUS 210ft free hang rappel. This was the best part of the canyon by far; a very nice and memorable drop in. The rest of the canyon was just okay. There are a few shallow pools that we were able to avoid but near the end on the 2nd to last rap there were 2 pools that were unavoidable, not cold, knee deep. Replaced and fixed a few anchors. Our group installed a 10ft piece of dynamic rope on the final anchor to connect bush/rock together and extend pull to edge. I dont entirely agree with this and feel it should be bolted as the natural anchor choices are slim/poor. At the very least the rope should be swapped with webbing, in my opinion. The final rap isnt that exciting and is mostly a walk down. We nearly got our fiddlestick stuck in a crack after pulling and spent some time trying to recover it. The exit is very long, boring, but easy. Not much catclaw and minimal brush, we were prepared for a fight but found a pretty solid trail developing leading to the main hiking trail. Even in winter, this hike is very hot and in full sun. I wouldnt recommend the route for summer, would be a scorcher.

Found a few anchors along the way, so definitely not a first descent. Dropped in via the tributary with some nice warm up rappels. The upper falls was a joy to rappel. Loses some points per lots of walking between rappels.

Fun, cruiser canyon with a spectacular final rap into the colorado. There is a small landing area to inflate rafts and sort yourselves out before taking off down the river. Would love to repeat it when the weather is warmer and the days are longer. The paddle out is a lot longer than you think, start early.

The scramble to the top tier is quite fun…looks like there was a rope assist but has since been cut down. There are no anchors set so I ghosted everything. R1 In flow dcr, r2 in massive flow dcr, r3 is pretty much a dry rap cause you’d have to be psycho to drop in That flow. Upper falls end in pool that’s deeper than you think. Middle section lets you fall behind the pour over and rap behind the falls which is amazing, def the highlight of the series of 3 drops.

Great day at FCF. Big water. I don’t think the reference photos are close to accurate. Probably reversed in flow descriptions. Did the traditional way up so we could look at the flow. Water was a little brown. R1 could be better. Maybe a bolt DCR? R2 we used a traverse to the tree in the middle and went middle DCR. Went down 30 feet to a shelf and tossed a bolt in that is behind the veil (takes a little effort to get to the shelf) and out of site to the general public. Rapped to the bottom from there. We stayed out of the boiler hole as it looked wild and there’s a log in there. Put a bolt in the back of a boulder that’s in the middle of the creek and added webbing to keep things tidy. Fun day.
Easy stroll.

The roads start out graded then progressively get worse. Lots of sharp limestone rock notorious for slashing tires further on. Heavy duty offroad tires and airing down highly recommended. We car camped at the turn off where the approach starts. On a map, the last 4ish miles of high clearance driving trail shown no longer exist and must be hiked to get to the edge of the rim. Descending the rim you are eventually met with a lot of steep loose terrain. Use extreme caution here, one of our members took a tumble and almost went over the cliff. It’s pretty serious stuff.

Deep sand on approach. Wading through multiple cold pools, some with a thin layer of ice and others with neon green algae. One pothole was a brief swimmer with water several feet below the lip - we were able to climb out using a submerged ledge. Potential for true keepers to develop as floods move material in/out. Final rap lands in deep water and we descended directly into pre-inflated rafts. This time of year there is very little direct sun exposure on river for the 12mi exit paddle.

We encountered full potholes with warm-ish water. No wetsuits were used and we were all plenty comfortable. Canyon gets a lot of sun and the final rap into the river is awesome. All anchors were solid and the pull on the last rap was ezpz. Highly recommended and an excellent way to experience the magnificence of Glen Canyon and the Colorado River.
Potholes quite full, many overhead, really fun day out enjoyed the canyon a lot.

We did 2 rappels total. Fun downclimbs, and deep pools to bypass. The 2nd to last rappel was a good beginner downclimb. We took the ropes trail for a quicker exit. We also drove pretty far into the canyon which was nice, we left a shuttle at the ropes trail.

A fun little canyon! The zigzag chute sequence is terrific. Parked at the gate (being careful not to block it). Flow was relatively low, but still fun. To exit, we thrashed up the nose of the ridge right where Fir drops into Vance Creek. It worked, but was a dirty mess. Will look for a better route next time. On the way back, we realized that our parking location had the unintended consequence of luring multiple groups of hikers down the road past the gate. They were looking for the Vance Creek Bridge trailhead and, on seeing our parked cars, assumed we were parked at the trailhead. Ooops.

Another great little canyon in the Skykomish River Valley. Hangers on R2 are loose, but the bolts seem sound. We hiked out via the Vance Creek route. Vance is low this time of year and hiking along the gravel bars (crisscrossing from side to side occasionally) and taking the road out is far more pleasant than the bushwhack route we did last time.

Fun canyon with some really scenic rappels. A landslide has occurred at the base of the last rappel, which may provide a new exit, did not explore too far as there are also ground wasps making their home in the dirt. Definitely bring a change of clothes for the hike out if taking the long route.

Fir Creek just as pretty as always. All anchors in good condition. Flow was high enough that some of the hanging potholes were obscured from view, increasing the hazard of stepping/falling into them by mistake. The water was quite pushy at the top of the final rappel. Took the longer approach up Vance Creek for the first time and preferred it over the "shortcut" exit with the thorny bushwhack.

Great day in Fir! The cave sequence with the hanging pothole is beautiful. No notable challenges. All anchors in good condition. We bushwhacked and made it from the last rappel to our cars in 49 mins.

Fir was super fun and probably the best creek from the weekend. Too bad its so short. We paired it with Rock and had a fun day! I updated the shortcut exit. It only took 20 mins to climb out to the road.

Scouted the canyon ahead of a training day. Took the "2024 shortcut" exit. Bushy and strenuous, but creek-to-road was only 25mins at a slow pace. Returned the next day and took the Vance Creek exit. Gorgeous hike out along the river bed, and a good consistent gradient up the road. Hard to tell which exit to prefer - think it depends on time, weather & team.
The water in Fir is noticeably colder than Rock and Whitehorse.

Be mindful of Hornets throughout the canyon. Very short narrow section with tall walls were nice. Had some fun downclimbing R2 with partner capture. Exit lower portion thwarckong & mostly screw scramble mid way to top. Notice various fire rings along the keep trail on the exit. Might be more fun doing Canyon out & back with ascend R1. Comparison to nearby short canyonPHog: FW taller narrow section but very short, PH more pools and red slabs
A few avoidable pools. Did only one rappel, was as able to extend first rappel to continue down 12' section. Making for only one 100' rappel. Some fun stemming sections to avoid water. Scenic rappel.

Very straightforward canyon with one big rappel. There are a network of braided trails leading out. Basically climb the slope DCL wherever is easiest and keep going until you can get up through the rimrock band at the top. Camped up on top of the mesa for a great sunset.

A few shallow, avoidable pools and light trickle over R1. The travertine fall is quite beautiful and unique. Narrows section is short but very pretty. Downclimbed R2 LDC. Exit was fairly painless. Lots of flying critters out and about after the recent rains (wasps, moths, butterflies, grasshoppers, etc.). Underrated canyon!

Time is car to car for First Water, Hog, and Phog. 2h per canyon. FW was dry. Webbing at R1 in good shape. Anchored off a deadman for R2. Cleaned up some brush by the start of the exit. Done early morning which is not recommended as lighting is better mid or late in the day.
I think this canyon is better than it's ropewiki page would suggest. The travertine falls are beautifully sculpted. We had a fun little evening run.
Water seeping through the canyon. A little canyon with some character. A large owl flew right over us by the anchor station.

Brought a wetsuit not really knowing what to expect. Turned out being totally unnecessary. Canyon was almost completely dry with the exception of the narrow section towards the end. The pool was about chest deep. Set up a shuttle off the 88. Decent secluded hike, would definitely be better with some waterflow.

Likely a first descent of Fisher Creek as we found no other anchors or signs of passage. Had low expectations on water levels, but it turned out to be surprisingly good after the area received an inch of rain the night before. Creek was a little overgrown suggesting that Fisher runs low during the summer months. Spring and fall are likely the best. Lost count of the number of rappels, but the creek remains pretty action packed until after the final rappel sequence.

We had a great low-key adventure in Fisher Creek. When in low flow a3 conditions, this canyon is suitable for beginners led by competent leaders, but easily would push a4/a5 in moderate or higher flows. Given the low-flow conditions, we only needed to rap about 4 of the drops. Our group decided not to leapfrog, and we ended up rebuilding each of the 4 anchors where we rapped - we were not rushing. Based on the state of the webbing I don't think anyone had been in-canyon for at least 3 years. I hope our bush-whacking benefits someone. The shuttle makes for a good pay-to-play ratio, especially for dusting off the cobwebs at the beginning of a season. Things to note: -LAMAR should scout the rope pulls thoroughly- we were fine, but this could be a challenge for some given the vegetation. -Log jams were unstable in a few locations and rock was the typical amount of slick of the PNW. -The difficulty and risk of this canyon increases exponentially at moderate and higher flows. There is ample evidence that this canyon carries high flood flow in winter/spring. I also witnessed a potentially nasty sieve on DCR that could form at high flows. (I believe after the technical section?) -Entry and exit could use re-taping. Forgot my survey tape. Note that the loggers are using pink tape, so maybe bring a different color! -The canyon is mostly shaded and can be a little windy, so if going with a 4/3mm bring a splash jacket etc. -Lots of downclimbing!

Completed the route to camp at the mouth of Cranberry Canyon in 9 hours on day one, hiked to the rim in 8 hours on day 2. Found the slope of the Cranberry route to be quite steep, but fairly stable if you choose your route and footing wisely. The climb to the top of the redwall felt like about 30 feet, but only two sections that are really vertical for about 6-8 feet each. Holds are good, but some loose rocks along the way. Beautiful redwall narrows in the canyon, though they are somewhat short.

Easy approach with option to shuttle, easy exit. A fun nontechnical float day. Water was noticeably colder than other canyons in the area the same weekend. Bring extra flotation. Very slick creek walking with ankle biters. Excellent narrows and carved out walls. Some big jump potential. Brought a rope just in case but didn’t need it. Ran this the day after Rock Creek, made a good combo.

Solo trip to escape the forecasted 110F heat. Despite the baking sun, the water was still cold. Not sure this route should ever be done without one! As previously reported the creek-bed rocks are as slippery as hell, and just getting to the start of the canyon proper made me wish for some hiking poles! Given the lack of recent reports, I carried a short rope & harness, which remained in the bag all day. Once in the canyon there were no problematic obstacles to report. Some of the constrictions are pushy (but if I wasn't solo I'd probably call them "fun" instead). All of the rapids can be bypassed. I don't think any of them were jump-able (I lowered myself into some expecting to find a deep pool, but found thigh-deep water instead). However where the water runs slower & deeper lots of the canyon walls looked like great jumping spots. The large land-slide damn is still in place and can be passed easily DCL. I couldn't find the drainage marking the start of the suggested exit route, so bushwhacked directly uphill from the exit of the final pool. A little steep in places, but its not that long until the slope dramatically levels-off to a plateau, with only a short mellower climb up to the road from there. Taking the canyon cautiously, plus exit faff, and hiking the road, was 3.5 hrs total.

Approach was easy and short, water was flowing strong and cold. A mellow creek stomp up until the two rivers meet and it drops into the first narrows. A lot of slick scrambles around sets of rapids, but very doable. Water was very cold and I was grateful for my 6/5mm wetsuit. The narrows in this canyon are deep and spectacular and full of deep, crystals clear water. the crux of the trip was the rock and deadfall damn at the end. With the flow as high as it was the DCL down climb did not look like a wise option and I opted for a down climb through and over the deadfall which was its own adventure. Once over that it was an easy walk/float to the end with a great spot to do some jumps from the side right before the canyon totally widens out again. Any sign of an exit trail is totally gone since this canyon is rarely travelled and I opted for a straight up hill bushwhack to the road.

Flow was moderate, but water still cold. Rapids were easier to navigate around, but the down climb around the dam at the end was still sketchy, but more doable than in high flow. Unfortunately lost the rope and rope bag I'd brought along just in case under a rock in the first pool on the down climb, and decided it wasn't worth risking entering the hydraulic to retrieve it, and decided to go back later when the flow was lower and see it if it had reappeared. Let my mistake serve as a warning that although this canyon is nontechnical, the dam at the end creates some dangerous hydraulics that require caution.

Flows have increased due to recent rains but are still very manageable. Expect to get wet up to your neck and to get blasted with water unless you can very carefully rappel. It is probably worth bringing a wetsuit if temps are under 70 degrees. Rain jacket is a must.

Two rappels were set up with anchors. My team used a fiddlestick on a third rappel since there was no anchor set up there. The anchor at R2 had webbing set up on a tree that had a lot of risk to get to. A fall getting to that anchor could've been extremely bad. I was thinking that using a fiddlestick on a log for R2 would be easier, but I didn't try it. We were shivering pretty well after R1 and wishing we had wetsuits then. We were pretty cold and wet by the end of the whole thing, but managed without wetsuits.

We had a great time canyoneering Fort Creek. Approach hike was challenging but the trail was mostly well worn and obvious. Water was freezing with snow and ice throughout the canyon. Wetsuits were a necessity! Natural anchors were all in good condition. Some great rappels and downclimbing.

Great trip to do some Anchor work. First rappel now has 2 Bolts DCR. The sketchy eroded climb to the tree for rappel 2 is no longer necessary with 2 Bolts DCL just before the climb. Rappel 3 has a V thread Anchor is a large boulder DCL. Downclimb/rappel 4 has a single bolt DCL on backside of boulder. Downclimb/rappel 5 has a bolt DCR. Enjoy. Very slippery!

- ===CONDITIONS=== '''Anchors & Rappels:''' *All Anchors in good shape. *There are recently placed bolts on most anchors, some with two bolts, some with a single bolt. *There are two bolts in the drainage that are not well seated, though, and they protrude about a 1/4 inch causing movement on the hanger but not the bolt. *There is a supplemental anchor--rap 4.5--with webbing slung on a tree that was nice to have. *The last rappel has two stages, both of which have webbing slings on trees. The pull from the bottom is much better setting this up as two raps rather than a two-stage rappel. '''Water Exposure:''' *Two in our group wore 4/3 full wetsuits, with another wearing a thicker shorty--with long arms--and knee-high leg booties. All were comfortable but one person just had wool socks and their feet did get cold. *It is the end of August so the flow rate is probably either normal or low. This was our first time through the drainage so we didn't have anything to compare it to. *Being new to Class C canyons we felt the flow rate was comfortable. '''Weather:''' *Blues skies with a bit of haze from wildfire smoke. Temps in the 80s '''Approach:''' *There is a clear enough path to follow up the ridge--especially if one has the GPX track too. *Once on the top of the ridge, the descent down into the drainage is already starting to get bad. It is taking a lot of abuse and over time will get more sketchy. As canyoneers it would probably be wise to develop a pathway that stays on the ridge and intersects the drainage higher up to reduce erosion and trail damage; and also the minor, but still present, hazard of slipping when descending the current route. ===TRIP REPORT=== *'''Group Size:''' Group of 3 *'''Skill Level:''' Intermediate to Advanced *'''Total Time:''' 5 hours 15 minutes car to car We decided to see what everyone has been talking about. Being our first true class C descent--despite a lot of class A and B canyons, we decided this time of year with likely lower water flow was a good time to attempt this. We rigged releasable and were ready for any issues but none occurred. It was smooth sailing and a pleasant descent into a spectacularly beautiful spot right in our backyard. The Wall of Springs delighted us and verified this is a true local gem. The approach is steep, as was advertised, but was mostly straight forward and easy to follow; though the descent into the drainage from the top of the ridge was steep, slippery, and somewhat unpleasant. Traversing the drainage is very slippery, even with good shoes, which took more time, in general, than anticipated. Our quads and stabilization muscles did get put to work on this one. Being our first time through I don't have much to compare it with, but it seems now is a great time and flow rate for attempting this if it is on your list.
Full thing combined with upper. Time for full route. Took it at quite a casual pace

We descended Fort Creek 3C1 III in Alpine, Utah, during high-flow conditions and had an exhilarating adventure. The existing anchors were in good condition, and we had no issues with rigging or navigating the canyon. Some of the obstacles that are typically downclimbed were more dangerous because of the higher flow conditions, so we needed to be cautious. It took us five hours to complete the descent with our party of four.

Group of 4 descended Three Falls. Our approach was slow because one member of our team is still trying to get their lungs back after a recent bout of Covid. Once we topped out in elevation on the approach we made a low-effort attempt at the alternative West Ridge approach but didn't find much in the way of foot traffic and realized we would be bushwhacking so we quickly turned around and went down the standard Erosion Gully approach. We got a little off route but found it eventually. This part of the approach is still loose, steep, and slippery, but less overgrown--now that it is seeing a lot of use--but still requires careful footing. I still think it would be good for the community to establish a more sustainable approach route especially with how popular this has gotten. That is why we were going to attempt the alternative West Ridge approach. Water flow was moderate, which is typical for this time of year. I don't know the cfm but I wouldn't want to descend in flows much higher. Despite good footwear we all encountered a lot of slipping and sliding down the rappels (more so than I remember on last year's descent) but had no really safety issues. Anchors were mostly the mixed bag as described in the beta. Anchor issue we found but were not able to remedy: * R2 is supposed to be a 2-bolt anchor; but one bolt while, still in the rock, is missing the hanger. We found the hanger tied to a sling in a tree nearby. I looks like the nut came off the bolt and someone tied the hanger to a sling so it wouldn't wash away and could be replaced later. * R3 has an optional V-thread anchor LDC in the watercourse that is missing cordage--just an empty set of holes. * One of the single-bolt anchors lower in the canyon has a loose nut--but the bolt does not turn. It is very likely the nut on this bolt could come off taking the hanger with it. This would be catastrophic with a person on rappel. We were only able to hand tighten it. That was sufficient to get us down that drop but this really should be properly addressed.

A decent canyon nestled in the jungly woods that is pretty but very overhyped in my opinion… Approach is somewhat easy to follow but be aware there are lots of side/fail trails that go off to nowhere. The GPX on ropewiki is spot on up to the saddle. Pretty views, in and out of shade on this mostly sun exposed approach. We opted to peel off at the top to try and find the optional 120’ drop in cliff. While a tad bit bushwhacky in spots, there is somewhat of a trail getting cut in if you stay on the left side of the ridge, avoiding the worst of the brush (GPX not helpful here as it is just a straight line drawn.) We eventually got to near where the coordinates for the 120’ drop but found an easy walk down slope into the canyon instead… not sure where the drop could be but doesn’t seem necessary even when going this way. There was a nice spot to suit up on logs and rocks at the bottom in the creek. Approach took us 1.5hrs. The technical section is a pretty jungly scene with a creek flowing through it. Not very “canyoney”. Lots of careful slow and slippery creek walking. The rappels were not particularly exciting nor very vertical, more broken up, zigzaggy or low angle steppy. Almost every rap would be a downclimb if there wasnt a river flowing over it. Looking back at them made for pretty pictures though! The water was not freezing cold (like Stewart Falls which we did the day before). I took a chance on wearing my 4:3 and was very happy I did so. Very warm in the water and plenty of sunlight throughout the canyon. Flow was not too pushy except for a few spots and hardly any of it coming down heavy on your head. Very beginner friendly. After the last rappel (3 falls) there is what looks like a very established trail heading straight up the hill on DCL. Not sure what this is for, but it doesn’t seem to go anywhere good… stay in watercourse and exit at bridge!! Overall I found this canyon enjoyable but slightly underwhelming especially after doing Stewart Falls the day before. Took 2 of us 5 hours at a very leisurely pace
Did the canyon as a group of 3, took 4 hours car to car. All anchors are in good condition and in the locations described on ropewiki.

We hiked the ridge between Fort Creek and Fortknocker – 1.3 miles and 1,700 in elevation. Trail was primitive and steep. Then we bushwacked down to the creek above rap 1 and geared up. Most rappels had some webbing around trees or willows. We fiddled rap 3. Some rappels were optional and easily downclimbed. Lots of debris, trees, and deadfall in the creek to climb over, under and around. We connected to Fort Creek and proceeded down canyon. Time from car to car was 6 hours for the whole creek.
Water rating for just upper section of fort creek. Total time car to car for non shuttle route for upper and three falls

Canyon took 6 hours car to car. Replaced almost every anchor station. The canyon description doesn’t seem entirely accurate, there were more sections with ~50ft rappels than described. It is possible that at lower water you could down climb those rappels. At the current flow the granite slabs were too slick and steep to safely down climb. Those sections can be easily rapped or hiked around. Quality canyon but lower fork creek is significantly higher quality.

We were excited to get back out to the Fish Creek area now that Apache Trail is repaired. Very scenic area with great views, tall cliffs, and beautiful desert flora. The saguaros were particularly nice. As for the canyon, we saw five anchors total. All but one need to be replaced. The first one is short and possibly could be downclimbed. The second can be downclimbed on the right by chimneying down a skinny crack. The third might be able to be downclimbed with lots of friction, but it's about 20 feet, so we didn't risk it. The cave is at the base of the fourth one. Then there's one final one into an alcove from a small tree. Yes, we are guilty of leaving the long strand of purple webbing. Forgot scissors... doh! Tons of catclaw to bust through on the exit. Good luck!

From beta of previous group, (i believe wild paths) Slung Boulder on rap one is a down climb should have been removed, we didn't, please do Slung Boulder on rap two is a down climb should have been removed we didn't, please do Slung boulder on rap three remove...it is a down climb Blue eight mil cord on rap4 Is actually a 35 foot rappel or very spicy down climb DCL Purple webbing Still Remains on 35 foot Rappel number two (#5 from last group) because waste not want not until it needs changing Boulderthon downclimbathon until river Good day, excellent for very new beginners on maybe a meat anchor if not comfy on down climbs

Always a fun time when done at night! We left webbing at the first rappel in place but it needs to be swapped soon. We replaced most of the crunchy, sun faded webbing in the canyon so it should be good to go for this season. All bolts appeared to be in great shape. Pretty views at night.

4 hours includes walking 2.5 miles back to frenchman mountain trailhead from the canyon exit; someone seems to be living in the wash further down after you've exited the wash up to the mtb trails so be kind

important: there were about 100 bees nesting in the final "pothole" about 4 feet before the last rap. these were not there the week prior. they were totally unbothered by us, but we had a person in our group who is severely allergic which meant she had to cover up fully, stem across the mess, and execute perfectly. regress would have been possible out of the canyon but we would have been stumbling through choss in the dark and in the rain. made good enough choices. this was not our first choice canyon, but rain in the surrounding area kicked us out of our plans a & b. this canyon only drains the saddle and two peaks of frenchman and has good egress throughout.

I almost gave this canyon 5 stars. Sorry everyone. The last sequence is definitely 5-star worthy. Ran during uberfest 2017. The road to the dam is in pretty good condition - possibly even passable for a passenger vehicle - but pretty rough beyond that if you were thinking about making the shuttle. We had one individual in a farmer john wetsuit that I thought might be cold, and then we had another individual forget his wetsuit entirely. We all donated a few pieces of extra gear - a shorty wetsuit, 2mm socks, rain pant shells - and while he was cold and considered bailing halfway, he completed the canyon. I recommend a full wetsuit if you want to enjoy your trip. The canyon was in wonderful conditions. I consider it moderate to moderate-high flow. We used one 100' and 200' ropes each. It would be much more efficient to just go with two 200' ropes instead. Hydraulics were present but strong swimmers will not have much trouble.

Got some good intel from DE below & similar story. In fast flow for rappels that you have to commit to going into & will get hammered... which is part of the fun but seriously people need to know the techniques for being in C-Canyons. We had a large group of 11 so we put 4 ropes into play. Wore a 4/3 with rain shell & 5 mil neoprene socks & 2mil neoprene gloves, felt cold in the final narrow rapid sequence since it was shaded so put on an extra 3mil jacket , adidas hydrolace shoes felt warm & decent grip but still slippery, most people had Addidas, 5.10 or Bestard. the middle slog didn't seem that bad but did require squeezing under branches while creeking, found an alternative walk on a trail instead of swimming through reservoir. Again, getting hammered on waterfall rappels & setup are tricky on pool ledges so better have skilled people with C class experience to do this. In many rappels that push you sideways you have to avoid getting stuck in the narrow slot ( about hip width). A few overhangs as well while getting blasted by water. Did I mention getting blasted by water at heavy flow? Fun indeed so be careful & stay safe.

Above average flow but it wasn't too intense. This canyon was a blast. I especially enjoyed the slide on the last section of the upper narrows. Water is incredibly cold. I recommend at least a 4/3mm or better and a small group so that you can move quickly.
Fun canyon. Some of the drops were slide-able. Got a prius out there no problem. Saw a black bear on the hike in.

The damage from the fire last year is visible but no longer a problem. I imagine one or two more monsoon seasons to completely flush the canyon. There were 3 or 4 bolt hangers that could spin freely. I did not have the equipment necessary to tighten or repair them when I was in the canyon. At each location where there was a loose hanger, the other hanger at the anchor was solid still. The fixed rope section in the middle seemed questionable to me so I added a small blue sling with a rap ring on one of the fixed rope supports. I used it to rappel into a lower pool (45’)and slide down the remaining 30 or so feet. The temperature outside the canyon was 100 degrees and I still found myself wishing I had a light wetsuit. On the second to last rappel there was a rope stuck in a chock stone; I wrapped that rope around the stone to eliminate the pinch point but be aware that it’s there.
Water was a little pushy, but manageable. Great slides!
Great flow. In great condition. Fun, but not dangerous. Rope pull issues with boulder on rappel #8. Rope got stuck with biner behind boulder. Recommend last man double strain.
Moderate flow. All anchors look good. Perfect water conditions.
Always a fun trip. Built an underwater deadman in one of the last potholes for fun.
Beehive 400 feet after the last slide in the first section of Frye. Went around DLC for 30 feet then got back in the Waterhouse. Keep those eyes peeled and be safe.

Terrific little canyon with a great pay-off. Five rapid-fire rappels, followed by a short walking section, then five more rappels. Some of the anchors in the upper section require care to access. This canyon could be much more serious in high water. We set a rope for R5 but, in these conditions, it can be slid safely without a rope. Much fun was had. Opted to skip swimming the putrid cow reservoir at the bottom by taking the trail. Dodged the semi-hostile cattle.

Amazing day in S’mores!! Water was low but still running - easy and defined approach. Great rappels but water was too low for about half of the slides. Couple of the rappels had technical starts - ledges or getting around chalkstones. Pretty straightforward canyon! We didn’t know about the 8 mile gravel road approach that was rough on our van, so be aware!
Last rap is bone dry. No flow through the canyon.

Lazy canyon day! The canyon flow was in fun waterpark mode, rappels and slides were fast and slippery. Exicited for the snow to continue to melt to increase flow, Only gonna get better. As a reminder always check the big slide pool for debris before attemping to slide! Be safe and enjoy this wonderful canyon.

Approach hike from the reservoir took 45min to R1. Caution is required to access many anchors due to exposure and slippery, polished rock. Rebelays would likely be necessary in high flow. Slid the final tiered waterfall in the upper section (used fixed handline LDC to climb back up and repeat slide). Bottom pool was about waist depth but with a sandy bottom and with proper technique was safe for a slide under these conditions. Comfortable in 3/2mm wetsuit and rain jacket. Neoprene gloves were a luxury but not needed. Some webbing may need replacement soon. Beautiful canyon, particularly the narrows in the lower section.

Super fun canyon! Beautiful, easy to access, and an absolute must do! We drove the 4x4 trail up to the upper parking area and started there. Stock high clearance 4x4 can handle the trail with no problem. The hiking trail past the gate is obvious. We had another group ahead of us requiring us to wait quite a bit between raps. Once to the start of the middle section one of us bailed out, being too cold in his 3mm suit. (However I felt plenty comfy in my 4/3 suit) He hiked back up to the truck and drove it back down to the reservoir for us. The 2 of us continued on eventually coming across the fixed handline slide. My partner went down the slide, stopping at the end of the rope, then continuing to slide. Near the bottom he caught his foot on a small lip twisting it badly. We stopped for a bit, wrapped his ankle tight with webbing, then continued, slowly, him using me as a crutch. We debated trying exit the canyon but decided the steep hike out may do more harm so we continued. He side slid all the raps and we finished the canyon. After an X-ray, it was found that his fibula was broken just above his ankle. He has an estimated 12 week recovery time. Now, I IMPLORE YOU, please stop and think before doing any risky maneuvers such as sliding or jumping! What may seem like a straightforward, easy/fun jump or slide, can turn into a bad situation very quick! Just because others have gone before you and have been fine, doesn't necessarily mean you will be! Ask yourself: "Is it really worth the risk?" This could have all been avoided by simply rigging as a proper rappel. Lastly, even when all safety measures have been taken, always be prepared for the worst to happen. Never underestimate a canyon, no matter how "easy", accessible, or well traveled. My rating of this canyon is a general reflection of the canyon itself, not taking into account the negative experience we had half way through. As always, please enjoy with extreme care.

We had a solid day out. A little hotter and lower water level than we wanted but we are heading into the summer! Will probably schedule a month earlier next year. Was pretty happy to have my full wet suit but could have gotten away with thinner this time of year! We loved the final swim across the resevoir! Would run again.

A beautiful granite canyon, fun summer day in the water, perfect temps!! Flow still present but getting low. We did the 4x4 shuttle up to the top parking area. The night before we went through and installed a bolt at the lower section’s big low angle “slide” LDC. The fixed handline that was there was a dangerous eyesore and has been removed. We also added a bolt after the first pool on R10, breaking up R10 into 2 rappels rather than a 2 stage. Pull rope from the new R11 anchor station to avoid getting rope stuck in V notch. Night run took us around 6 hours with like 8 people and bolting. Morning run with 4 of us was a quick, efficient and fun 3 hours! Beta has been updated!

Fun day with sporty flow. The recirculating hydraulic after the big slide was in play. Water was checked for hazards before sliding was attempted. Flow was powerful and slightly challenging to escape from. 5 others in group opted to rappel instead and were provided a safety so as to no pendulem into hydraulic. Excited for the flow to increase in the coming months. Stay safe.

Fantastic day in near optimal conditions. Flow was pushy throughout with enough spice to keep us thinking about what we were doing. No special challenges to report. We did opt to bypass the chute at the end of the upper section as the hydraulic was quite active. Water levels were up from prior week but still within comfortable range for those familiar with this canyon. Expect higher flow in the coming weeks as there is still a lot of snow on Graham.
Amazing day! From fun whirlpools to manageable hydraulics to sporty pour overs! Highly recommend running this canyon right now if your comfortable in high flow (A6). Great day!

R6 now has a (badly placed, imo) single bolt LDC and the handline is gone. This bolt is too far back from the drop and on the wrong side to allow line-of-sight to those on rope. At this flow level we needed perhaps 220' to get down to a safe spot and retrieve the rope. We only had 200' and, in an error of judgement, we set it short and attempted to downclimb a section that was too slippery to be safe. One team member sustained a minor injury upon losing control and sliding to the bottom. The lower part was intense with heavy, very pushy flow and one recirculating hydraulic that wasn't too bad to escape because it was shallow enough to get your toes on the ground. Pretty spicy day but definitely amazing.

Waterflow has subsided a bit since earlier this month but is still quite sporty (maybe ~3 CFS) and we took a pounding at a few rappels. Also still a risk of being swept off pour-overs if you don't protect transitions between anchors. Sliced my thumb open traversing the handline at the bottom of the upper section (sorry about all the blood). As it turns out, canyoning is harder with only one good hand. Measured the first low-angle fall in the lower section (formerly had the long handline) and it's 115' from the single bolt canyon left.

Water temperatures 58ºF at the headwaters and rising to 63.7ºF at the last rappel. Slippery even with magic shoes on. We struggled to find the bolt for the big slide, which was LDC but set back a ways before reaching the slide area. Just a great day.

High flow, pushy when in the brunt of it, care taken with pour overs. Anchors are looking a bit sun faded but did not replace any today. Most fun run that we have had of s’mores over the last few years.

Waterflow was "lively" and challenging for those without much class C experience. One person on our team was flipped upside down by the force of the waterfall on her backpack at rappel 4. May want to send packs down separately. Take extra special care to make release from the rope quick and efficient at the bottom of rappels 3 and 4. We stayed on rope to access anchors that are situated next to pour offs before pulling the rope. Decided to skip the lower half and will come back another time.

Pretty high flow. There was still snow on the mountains above. We did both the upper and lower. 70-foot upper section waterfall lets you know you are insignificant. My head took a pounding, but still super fun. The webbing on a couple of stations in the lower section should probably be replaced soon.
Beautiful creek, so much life out there Running fairly low right now

We only did the upper section. My wife's first canyon with me. We also took our 3 daughters, she 8,10 and 12. I've been working with them rappelling for a while now but this was their first canyon! The did great and loved it! We'll be going again soon for another go of the upper section then another time for the whole thing. It's only an hour and 40 minutes from our house so pretty close! Had a great time!

Low flow, but still fun with many big slides still going. Chilly water, but refreshing. Snow patches visible on peak. Cleaned out 200ft of canyon prime stuck very weirdly in R10 boulder pinch, but also still connected to R9. Anchors in good condition. Road up to reservoir best suited to high clearance vehicle.

Flow is extremely low, barely a trickle now. Felt less comfortable about the slide at the bottom of the upper section given the depth. But the little slides do still have water, and it is still fun. Water no longer reaches near the edge of the little rock dam at the base of the last rappel.

Type 1 fun all day! Easy approach, rapid fire rappels, cool waterfalls. Still snow on top of Mt Graham. Comfortable all day in 4/3 wetsuit with top and bottom wool base layers underneath + 2mm neoprene socks. The bolt for R6 must be exceptionally well hidden - We couldn't find it so we down climbed this section.
Wall-to-wall fun. Flow high enough to be engaging, but not pushy. Just a couple very short swims. 5mm full suit was almost too warm.

Quite fun! Approach to the anchors for R1 is slick and somewhat intimidating for those less experienced with this type of rock/canyon. Like others before us, we could not find the anchors for R6. We rigged off of a small chockstone in the water flow just below to top of the first slide section. About halfway between R6 and R7 is a very healthy population of Poison Oak that is unavoidable if you are following the trail of use on DCL that bypasses heavy brush chocking the actual stream bottom.

Excellent half day canyon approximately equal distance from Portland and Seattle. Be aware of dirt bikes in the area. Easy entry, no shuttle required, rough exit up a steep hillside through overgrown slash cut debris. Very slick creek snot walking with many knee deep holes so watch your ankles. Lots of floating, small jump spots along the canyon. Handline recommended for final rap.

Ran Upper Fulton prior to an exploration of the lower part of the creek. New shortcut route is nice and drops you in just above the technical section. A new tree has fallen across the creek atop R3 which makes for a much better anchor in a non-exposed position. The rappel itself is slightly longer. Beta has been updated. Water was low, but still fun. We continued on down the creek to run Lower Fulton.

Halloween canyon! One week of rain and back to fun level (moderate to mod-low), but cold. Team member in drysuit was the happiest. Fun shortie outing in the Olympics. Replaced anchor at R2 as bacon is always better on the breakfast table. Used nearby Interrorem Cabin as basecamp.
Beautiful canyon, especially the amphitheater at R1.

A lovely canyon. The bolt on R1 is in good condition. We replaced the webbing on R2 and R3, and moved the R2 anchor to a different spot, to the root ball of a tree, to make it a cleaner pull. There are spots for some optional short jumps at the bottom of R1 and R2, check the depth as it is not very deep. The bottom of R2 could has an undercut at the far end of the pool, which could be a problem in higher flow. Great day overall.
Pretty solid canyon, light flow

Full Fulton was fun and totally worth running. We went a day early to scout the lower exit and work on the trail. We found a game trail and did a lot of cutting and cleaning up. The exit is steep and way less bushwacking than the original beta. I'll update the kml map. I wore a shorty wetsuit with a rain jacket and was fine with temp. Our time was 1.5 hours for upper Fulton and 4.5 hours middle, lower, and exit. Total was around 6 hours.

Great lil section, ran full Fulton, upper section was missing anchors, even found one log with webbing on it In The middle of the creek. I would not run just upper, best to make full day and do entire canyon! Rocks are quite slippery and dangerous. Rope pulls seem tricky by had no issues with rope retrieval.

An amazing winter canyon! Roadside, low elevation, easy approach/exit and with two big, fun waterfalls with great flow and rebelays giving fantastic lines through the flow. We were joking that this is 3 stars in summer and 5 stars in winter, and it's really true. Approach took us 25 minutes with minor routefinding and exit took us 15 minutes. A very enjoyable day!
Fun creek! Rocks and trees covered with thick layer of ice made approaches to anchors and descents challenging.

Having such a short approach and exit makes this a wonderful winter canyon. We couldn't find anchors at R1 or R4/P2 (lots of snow/ice) so we rigged retrievable. The ice and snow made the approach, exit, and climb up to R4 more difficult but made for a fun and character building experience. Would highly recommend!

Much different canyon when not frozen! Great flow right now…R2 has a rooster tail at the top in current flows. Exciting Raps all the way down. All anchors were is good working order…there is a fallen tree across R3 just before the water goes over the edge which is kind of a nuisance. If your in need of a spare tire there are about 50 at the bottom of the last rap. Pretty terrible. Beautiful canyon though!
Afternoon start during a rainy day made for some fun flow. All anchors good. The hike up from the bottom is steep up loose terrain for the firet bit, so be careful.

We only ran the lower section and spent much time at the bottom of R3 assisting with something unrelated. The retaining wall anchor was very loose and had to be tightened; all others were good. Unfortunately, the trash is starting to smell and is quite noticeable without higher waterflow flushing it out. The Rock Creek of the Gifford. :(

R3b (coming out of the culvert) is very spicy at this flow, honestly safer to downclimb behind the branchy cluster dcl before getting on rappel (take the rope with you, possibly even route around the cluster as a redirect) than trying to skirt in between the cluster and the torrent. R4b (bolts dcr) be mindful of the log halfway down the pitch in the middle the watercourse, stay dcl of it but don’t step between it and the rock, some chance of entrapment Skip the last drop, don’t even look, the sight of all those tires robs some of the magic of such a banger canyon. We took track of approach and exit, a map will be submitted. Otherwise the pitch lengths given in the beta are all longer than they are in actuality, but considering all the two pitch drops this is for the best. Get in there while it’s still flowing with spice, snow in the upper catchment won’t last long. Also, although I’m newer at canyoning and respect the work put in by the first descent team, in no world is this a class B slash A2 canyon. It’s best run in the off and early season. Off/early season flows can be expected to be A3 at a minimum. French V4 seems appropriate, whether it deserves an ACA technical rating of 4 is iffy, since reaching the rebelay stations (2b/3b/4b) from their respective 1st pitch anchors (2a/3a/4a) isn’t complicated, even in high flow. I’m open to a counter take and being corrected on it.

High flow day in Galena. We ran R2, which was fine, and R3, which was fairly punishing even after we used the redirect. We took a dirty dry line far DCR to avoid R4 because the flow and the log in the water course appeared dangerous. We left after that, skipping R5. The routes to the anchors are showing some wear and there might be some concern about erosion soon if this creek keeps getting run frequently.

Be very careful at rap 2. We continued past the anchor for rap 3, not noticing it, thinking it was the 150ft rappel. This left me about 50ft above the bottom on a very large shelf. I was too far and out of sight of my team to attempt to clearly communicate. I let them rappel down to me so I could thoroughly freak them out about how we were trapped there. The problem was easily solved, however, once they had brought me more rope. Tying another 200 footer to the rap side, we pulled the pull side bringing the block down to us to remove while simultaneously setting the EDK joining our ropes up near the anchor - then we just rappelled double strand from here. The real problem only occurred once on the ground. Skipping the real anchor for Rap 3 can apparently lead to rope pull issues. We rigged a simple 3:1 z-rig and freed our stuck rope with little effort, but the next party might not be so lucky. Water level was around moderate-low. No real C class challenges or swims. Our time is from Indian Garden campground back to IG campground. It can easily be done faster than 7hrs if staying in the canyon both nights, but hey, why hurry? I highly highly recommend spending at least one night at Indian Garden campground. I do not think its worth doing in a day.

Fun flow and great temperatures for the hike out. Our group met up with a group of 3 at the drop in so we combined teams. Anchors are in good condition, we downclimbed/partner assisted the first few drops. Our team wore 4/3 wetsuits and some of us had wind breakers.
Rim temp 65˚F Indian Garden temp 95˚F
