Started from top at 11am. Started canyon at 1:45pm. Completed canyon 4:45pm. Started hike out 6pm. Finished at top 9:30pm. Plenty of water sources, no need to pack more than 1 liter of water. No issues in canyon. Great conditions, water was awesome temps with no wetsuit. Hiked out in shade and moonlight, great temps. Earn it on hike out. Water low, but still very scenic and fun. Best way and time of year to do canyon.

Great canyon, but access is a monster. Downclimbed past the first anchor (didn't even see it) which threw off our rap count/beta a bit. Make sure you rig appropriately if you can't see the bottom. Fun but short canyon. Pushed on the hike out and made it up in 3 hours. 8 hours roundtrip.

2nd time on this route and it is top on my list for Arizona canyons. keeping group size down to 3 this time improved efficiency and cut down on time. the approach is awesome, but the exit can be a long haul if done in one day. kept all pulls on the right and double checked rigging before the chute and cascading waterfall. This proved to be beneficial in our case. a possible biner pinch on the pull was prevented, and no issues were had. A yellow grivel helmet was left atop the last rapp sequence down the cascading water fall. one member of our group made it car to car in 8 hours. we took our time and stopped at all 3 rest points on the way out and did it in 9 hours. 9

What a beautiful canyon. Anchors all in good shape. Special attention is required when rigging the R5 anchors as the rapides that end each of the fixed chains are quite large. My standard-sized carabiner could conceivably have passed through them if loaded along its long axis. Car to car time includes a descent of the east fork of Pipe Creek.

All anchors were in excellent condition. The flow was moderate due to recent snow melt. Temps that day were in the low 60s. Should not need a wetsuit soon unless you run cold. 08:00 am: Tiny Dancer stared down Bright Angel trail. 10:00 am: Informed Indian Garden Park Ranger of where Tiny Dancer was going. 10:30 am: Tiny Dancer arrived at Garden Creek Drop-in, suited up, and snacked on Lucky Charms. 12:00 pm: Passed a group of 4. Hey frens :) 01:30 pm: Tiny Dancer exited Garden Creek, took a 30 min break, ate some fried chicken & a cookie. 02:30 pm: Checked in at Indian Garden ranger station. 06:00 pm: Arrived back at car.

Most. gorgeous. canyon. ever. We got a permit for 2 nights at Indian Gardens so we were really able to take our time & enjoy everything. Hike down Bright Angel was icy in sections towards the top but perfect mid-70's (daytime) temps at the campground. Also, something like 30-35 mph wind gusts were predicted for Saturday afternoon at the airport (which had us a little concerned) but the winds were almost completely blocked in the canyon & even more so in GC. We wore 5/4 mm wetsuits & paddling jacket to help with evaporative cooling. The first downclimb was a challenge for me on the slick rock & awkward angles. Thank goodness for help from friends. All anchors were perfect and easy to access. We brought more rope then was necessary. Met a new friend in the canyon (hi friend!) who ran it with a 200-ft rope and 200-ft pull cord. Good to know for next time. Super super special place! Can't wait to go back.

Slept Friday on nearby Nat Forest land. Hounded down overpriced McBreakfasts on way through GC gate. Bright Angel Trailhead 0830. Suited up at dropin 1000. Used 2mm shorties, and were very comfortable. Prob would have been fine in just boardshorts. Wore felt sole tabis in the slot. Fantastic grip on algae, but still slippy (what isn’t?) on dry polished rock. Used a 200’ rope and 200’ pullcord. All anchors solid. Raps all relatively low angle, with easy starts. Clean, easy pulls. Flow perfect - not pushy. Garden Creek looked decent to purify/drink, but got a little less so near the end. We purified some from a small, clear other stream we crossed in the gully between Garden Creek and the trail. The long, steep outhike is the only reason this isn’t mad crowded, and could be a prob for beginners or kids. We hiked hard. Back at trailhead at 1745. It’s a long day, but we both were glad we daytripped it. Adding the pack weight of overnight gear to our pounds of wet canyon gear? No thanks!

3rd time doing this route, and the first time doing it as an overnight. Highly reccomend the overnight option. 1st drop is easiest downcl9mbed in the watercourse. 1st rapp anchor (knot chock) is a moderate downclimb. All anchors were great. Pull issues may occur while pulling the 2nd rapp of the multi stage. we pulled from high and back, and had a partner whip the rope to relieve friction caused by the water as it lays in the watercourse. Camping beta: Faxed the permit request a week on advance and got the permits in 2 days. Indian gardens has awnings at each site which was useful for hammock Camping. The food storage boxes at IG are big enough to hold gear, so you can lighten your load for the route. Packing light and breaking this up into an overnight made it much more enjoyable. Hiked out at 2 am to beat the heat. The second to last rapp is the banner photo chute rappel.

Time includes of descent of East Pipe Creek followed by Garden Creek. Approached via S Kaibab and exited via Bright Angel. Reached the start of Garden in about 5 hours. Descent of Garden took 3.5h moving at a moderate pace. No wetsuits. Wore a rain jacket with neoprene socks and was only slightly chilly towards the end. One swimmer, numerous wades. Chains at rebelay station are hard to miss. Devil’s Corkscrew was even more fun the second time around. Exit took about 4 hours. Some sheep kicked down a moderately-sized rock onto the trail that nearly struck Eric, so pay attention to your surroundings on the switchbacks out and don’t do anything that might provoke the local bighorn community.

Stellar canyon, after the downclimb it’s pretty shady about until 11:30. The first pitch anchor is good enough and the rest bomber! Got into the sun for the last few raps. Team of seven with 3 ropes and a pull cord. Finished and went to pipe creek beach before hiking out!

All wore wetsuits, 2 wore shorty and 4 wore full 3/2. None of us were either cold or to hot. CFS was higher with additional force than last October, but nothing drastic. This is an amazing fun Canyon! After finishing canyon we continued to Phantom Ranch and stayed in the cabin we reserved. Along with shower, steak dinner and breakfast this makes it a 5 star adventure. Oh, and the mules carried our ropes, wetsuits and harnesses back to the Rim. Day 2 we ascended S Kaibab Trail.

A very long trip to a short but beautiful desert paradise! We started our hike on Bright Angel Trail at 6pm, down to Indian Gardens Campground where we stayed the night. Took us 3hrs to get to the campground. From the campground we hiked 45min down to the approach in the late morning. The canyon had one sunny section but for the most part was shaded. Very pretty but the flow was very low, which made it much less exciting. Most raps are low angle walk downs. One rap in particular near the end was very neat and pretty being a shear fluted section. I didn't wear a wetsuit and was plenty comfy in the water. The technical section is very short and was over before we knew it. We hiked back up to Indian Gardens where we had stashed our stuff. I believe the from the exit to the campground took us about 2hrs at a moderate to slow pace. The sun was out, it was hot midday, but very bearable, lots of spots to rest in the shade. We chilled at the campground for about 30mins before heading back up Bright Angel at 4pm. By this time the sun had mostly hidden behind the canyon walls and we were in the shade for 95% of the exit hike. From the Indian Gardens to the top took us 3hrs & 20mins at a moderate pace with a few breaks. Total time was 12 hours which doesn't count stopping for the night at the campground. For the effort and time spent, I feel it would be more worthwhile to go back when there is significantly more flow, spend a bit more time down there and do multiple canyons in one trip. FYI, The water is turned off at both rest stops on the way to Indian Gardens as there was a pipe break, not sure how long until it is fixed. However, the spigot at Indian Gardens is on.

3.5 hr approach from bright angel. 3.5 hr canyon. 4 hr exit. Low flow. Wore shory wetsuits and neoprene socks and were all very comfortable. Anchors in good shape. Water is shut off at 1.5 mi and 3 mile rest stops, updates available at visitor center website. 1 to 2 liters per person was adequete. Finished the exit in the dark which is always nice.

TL;DR Absolutely beautiful canyon! Regular GC steep/long exit. Bring trekking poles. Canyon itself is amazing. It’s in th GC so the long & steep (fat elev loss/gain) entrance & exits are to be expected. Make sure all parties have experience hiking in these conditions. I personally think trekking poles are required, especially w a heavy pack. Start early & bring headlamps. The first two (mile 1.5 and mile 3) water stations on the way in/out were DRY (broken pipe was reported). The third station at Indian gardens has water and obviously you could filter the canyon flow if you wanted. For a pretty consistent (I consider myself fit) AZ hiker, I drank right around 3/3.5 liters for the entire canyon. We wore 2mm shorty’s and it was like the perfect temp on Sept 20th

We finally made the trip down to Garden Creek to celebrate Mandy's birthday a few days early! What an incredible experience. We have hiked the GC several times before but this was our first official Grand Canyoneering route. The trails were empty which made the hike in/out more enjoyable. We started at 6am and made it to the drop in point in just over 2 hours. The technical section was very enjoyable and the flow was perfect! The water is extremely cold this time of the year and there isn't much sun exposure if you start early like we did. We wore full wetsuits which was required in my opinion. We did experience a pretty significant rope pull issue at the re-belay station. It took us at least 30 minutes to remedy the situation. Morale of the story is, once you start pulling, don't stop. All anchors were in good shape and the beta is spot on. The hike out is difficult but that's to be expected. Our shoulders were hurting more than anything from the heavy packs / wet gear. We could have easily completed this route in >10 hours if we didn't experience the pull issue. We will definitely be back!

Beautiful perfect descent! The water and air temps were perfect for no wetsuit. We took our time and spent 2 nights at havasupai gardens (before and after canyon) and it made for a really enjoyable experience. I know people do this route in a day …. I don’t think I would lol! So much hiking!!! Highly recommend at least one night camping. Tips for the route: I studied pics and videos of the cascade sequence and felt really comfortable spotting the re-belay station. It would’ve been tough to spot had I not done this, but since I did it was easy. The first down climb sketched me out because I’m super short (5 ft). There is a Boulder w webbing and quick link above the first down climb on the top of the cliff DCL you can use to skip that first sketchy down climb if you want to. Also, we didn’t bring our stuff sacks and spent a lot of time untangling rope. Next time I will bring stuff sacks for all rope to avoid this issue. Overall, great canyon and incredible rappels and views. I will definitely repeat!

Water flow was about 50 cubic feet per second that day, nice fair weather on the hike in and hike out (about 60 degree on avg?) We were in water during the hottest time of day. Slight delay due to rope pull difficulties at first stage of 2 pitch rap and the rap after the 2 pitches, but no need to ascend. Had 3 of 200' ropes worked out fine. Beautiful canyon with gnarly exit with all the wet gears but manageable.

Time is starting in line at the backcountry office and a short overnight. We arrived at 715, the office opened at 8. We got permits to stay at indian gardens. We hiked down and set up our hammocks and stashed gear at the site. We continued down to the route which had good flow. After the canyon we hiked back up to indian gardens, ate dinner. And slept from 8 to 340 am. We woke up and left by 4 am and returned to our car at 7 am. I've done this route a few different ways in the past and as an overnight with an alpine start is the best. No wetsuits.
Time is for combo of pipe creek and garden creek. No wetsuits needed, all anchors good. Beautiful day.

At a leisurely canyon pace and silly hiccups, canyon was awesome. Bolts look good, water is cold but neoprene socks (3mm and 5mm) and gloves kept us mostly warm. Wouldn't go in bedrocks but I like being comfy, currently one short swim that ends in the sun.

Absolutely amazing day. The flow is much higher than I’ve seen it. One group member did the canyon a month prior and was astonished at the flow and depth of pools even only a month a part. Anchors all look to be in good shape. We wore shorty wetsuits with 5mm booties and were perfectly comfortable throughout the canyon. Highly recommend the overnight. Did this the first time in 24hours and definitely regretted it. Always one for the books. Enjoy!!

This is such an excellent canyon. Flow was moderate, which made it fun but not too challenging. A couple things to note: 1. Bolts have been added and the beta in Grand Canyoneering is no longer accurate. Rap #2 is now 140ft from bolts on DCR. There is a new 80ft rap at the end that bypasses the sketchy down climbing. 2. The rope pull for the first stage of the 400ft rap is heinous. It would actually be perfect for a fiddlestick, since the anchor is totally dry. 3. We did this in a day car-to-car in 14h and it was mega. Camp at least one night at Havasupai Gardens if you wanna keep it Type I fun.

We camped the night before at Havasupai Gardens and some of us camped a second night too, making for a luxurious trip! 4 hours in canyon at a leisurely pace, with some folks new or newer to aquatic canyons. This canyon is really something of an anomoly - a bolted, flowing canyon, solid rock, with an on trail approach and exit.. in the Grand Canyon!

Time is from top of the canyon to top of the canyon. Anchors are in decent shape but the route could definitely do with some more bolts near the top. There was one slide/jump throughout the canyon which was a nice treat. While the whole experience is cool, the canyon is fairly lackluster for such a high pay to play.

Time is from rim to rim. Shorted myself rope on the chute rappel and had to ascend to reset the rope. We ran the techincal section 2 times and it was totally worth it. 1 hour from drop in to the trail. All pulls went smoothe as butter. 🍄 on the hike out enahnced our experience. Enjoy.
R1 into the pool was a cold swimmer, on this date the other sections of the canyon did not require swimmers, nice slot section of canyon but wish it were longer
Light flow in the canyon after a storm the previous evening, only one swimmer, but water was very cold - we were happy to have full wet suits. Anchors in good condition.

Flowing water, but can easily stay dry finding way on dry ledges. No wetsuits. Never got wet, except maybe a little spray from big waterfall while on rappel. Only two real rappels, rest are downclimbs. Scenic but short canyon.
All anchors looked good. Did it in 3 raps, but could have done it in 2. Took a lot of time teaching and learning skills. Small trickle of water flowing through.
clear water, one swimmer

Extended R1 anchor with the existing webbing. The pool below this rappel was murky and I did not check depth, though water levels were very low and I'd wager it was less than waist deep. With some careful footwork I was able to avoid this pool staying along the wall RDC. Downclimbed both R2 and R4. Didn't notice an anchor by R4, though all other webbing was in good condition. The rabbit hole down to R3 was lots of fun, but this was the only noteworthy rappel. I may have actually enjoyed the hike out of this canyon more than the technical section... Definitely looking forward to returning when there's some good flow.

Canyon was in fun mode! No flow but very full pools of crystal clear, icey cold water. Fairly deep swimmer at bottom of R1 and some shallow pools at the bottom of the rest. The pools were very cold and, since it was windy, a rain jacket would have been nice, but it wasn't unbearable without one. Unecessary anchors built right next to 3 of the already existing anchors were removed.

2 raps. Flowing a bit but we didn't even get our feet wet. Swimmer pool was pretty easy to bypass by rapping to pocket RDC and climbing up and around from there. All webbing was good. We were moving at a very slow pace.
Light flow and clear pools throughout the canyon. Removed excess webbing from large but fractured and fragile arch high RDC near R1.
Driest and most algae that I have seen this one in a long time. Anchors are all good. No anchor currently after the rabbit hole rappel for the down climb.

3 rappels - Parking is convenient, arrive early if on the weekend - approach is poorly marked - you go all the way to the top, shoot for the saddle - avoid GPS track and go wide right to avoid tricky downclimb - follow watercourse to the start - water was flowing, it was truly beautiful, especially at the first rap where we stopped for a snack - donned wetsuits (not required in hindsight) - easy to avoid pools - final rappel is tricky to start, have to swing over the edge - exit is a bit tedious if water is present, it takes careful foot placement to avoid slipping and potentially getting hurt.
Beautiful little canyon with flow. Low effort for some memorable rappels. Anchors looked good. Quick exit. If you haven't done it.... Do it!

Back again but with low flow this time. Cool water. No wetsuits. Alage in parts mostly that can be avoided. No wetsuit needed. Bushwacking approach. Canyon never disappoints with the canyon views, rappels, and downclimbs. Find small entrance down into and under chockstone rappel. Easy exit with more to soak in. Team of 2. 3 hrs car to car. 1hr approach, 1.5 hr descent, 30 min exit.

Super dry, was able to avoid the two big pools after R1 and R2. Downclimbed R4. The rabbit hole at R3 was neat and certainly the most memorable rappel. There's a short but fun (optional) elevator downclimb on the approach, although it drops you into some very unfriendly plant territory. Will probably come back here next time there's some flow!
Small trickle of water, very clear pools, water was cool but not too cold. The warm day made the cold water nice. We replaced all the webbing as we went through..

Lots of water in the canyon, mostly avoidable. Trickle flow. First pool is about 5' deep, as verified by our guinea pig. Some avoided the water, others embraced it. Good time in the canyon. Webbing looks good.

While there were several neat pools available to rappel into and all anchors were in good shape, we managed to stay dry and only get the rope out once by downclimbing around everything except for R3. The rabbit hole on R3 is neat and that rappel is the only mandatory one when dry, but we greatly enjoyed the "elevator" downclimb on the approach and felt that it certainly added value to this largely scrambly canyon experience. Would certainly enjoy coming back when there is some flow.

All raps bolted with chains and in good shape. Dry, short, easy rap canyon that is great for beginners. Also great canyon to practice natural anchor technique as several options are available at each rap. GPS coordinates confirmed as accurate. There was some trash in canyon, namely several car tires (>10) someone is using as a dumping ground.
Nice little easy-access canyon. Good stop-over between LV and Zion.

It's a good "had some time to kill" canyon. Don't make it the only reason to drive to the Virgin River Gorge unless you have beginners with you. We did it since we were in the area doing Portal and Cherry and it was near our campsite. All anchors are bolted, probably because there are/were so many people hiking in there and they may destroy any other type of anchor. There was a lot of trash in there.
All the anchors were in good shape. There was one pool that looked chest deep that we were able to scramble around. Fun canyon if you’re in the area.
A few shallow, avoidable pools. Road was dry and in good condition but high clearance was helpful in a few places. Very scenic area.
Fun short canyon. Super short drive and good way to kill some time if camping in the gorge. Would be better to couple with nearby canyons.

First known descent of Gnat Creek. Super thick bushwhacking in. Some nice falls, but areas of the creek are filled with woody-debris. This creek needs a good hundred-year-flood to scour it out. There is one area where the creek curves around a tight bend that would be really fun if it were not full of old logs. Finding good places to anchor is challenging on the big drops -- you may end out needing to be way off to the side and rappelling a line out of the water. Water levels were surprisingly high for June given this creek does not seem to have much of a watershed.

Encountered one less rappel than canyoneering northwest suggested, lots of water and not too technical. was able to swim most of the .9 mile exit from the East Fork Humptulips River. Don't forget your National Forest pass.

Parked at the drop-in and descended Go-Forth. Only encountered three rappels. Final rappel into the Humptulips River is very pretty. Water then quadruples if not more. Flow prediction for this creek should really be the river as it's the crux. Was able to float / swim downstream 0.8mi. Had to hop out to circumnavigate some narrow bits. Shuttle would be nice to skip the 1.6mi hike back up the road, but it's not that bad. National Forest pass not required.

Hiked as an exit route after a descent of Motorcycle Canyon, and in my opinion, is only worth doing this way. Some nice, deep canyon walls and scenery, but loads of helicopter traffic, graffiti, trash and drunk people. Expect to wait in line at the vertical obstacles. I don't think I would ever soak in the hot springs in this canyon, who knows what bodily fluids are floating around in there...

A decent, worthwhile route, if in the area. River crossing was for the most part knee deep. I wore a drysuit just because, and stashed it on the other side. The approach starts up a very long rocky wash that seems to last forever. After an hour of rock hopping we got to the turn off and started to ascend the adjacent drainage. There were a few dryfalls to scramble up that were enjoyable but the final push was a slog with loose soil/rock in full sun. Would be terrible in the summer! Canyon is mostly wide, and starts with a few downclimbs before the first rappel. All drops are anchored by natural arches, rock cairns, or knot chocks. Some of the drops were fairly enjoyable going down short sculpted flutes of limestone. One of the larger ones had a neat little free hang. Canyon drops quickly and descent is fairly short. Some nice views throughout. The canyon profile is similar to some of the routes in the Black Canyon Range near Lake Mead. No water in canyon. All anchors are in good condition. Before we knew it we were back in the wash and after another hour of annoying sandy wash rock hopping, we arrived at the river. Overall, a decent route but a one and done for me.

Nice canyon but the long approach and exit takes away from its fun. We hiked up the ridge between the wash and the canyon and it was pretty easy. It puts you in after the first rappel. I did enjoy the fast pace of the rappels and many of them where very pretty. We did replace the webbing on several of the rappels with old, crusty, and faded webbing. Overall I dont know if it would do it again but did have a good time on it.

First known descent completed by Jay Wisocki, Cole Anderson, Mat Weeks and Erek Burek on October 19, 2019. Long approach and exit for short technical section. Beautiful sections towards last half of canyon with a number of fun downclimbs and pool bypasses. You are welcome Arizona.

No old webbing or anchors at any of the rappels. Setup all new bomber anchors. Water flow was amazing. Last water fall was incredible and beautiful, well worth the effort of the exit. Exit is long and steep, with no water source once leaving Post Creek. Thin 3mm-4mm wetsuit needed. Communication on first rappel of big falls is very difficult. Solid class C experience needed. You are welcome Arizona.

Absolutely beautiful canyon - water was flowing enough to fill a few pools to swimmer depth, but they can be climbed around with some exposure and a few tricky moves. No big annoying bypasses, though. Hiked from camp where the Tonto trail intersects Grapevine Creek down close to Colorado River and back in a hurried four hours, plan on more like six hours to enjoy it more. Heed the parks warnings about ravens and take your food or anything that could be mistaken food with you if hiking the canyon from a base camp - or leave it behind in a bomb-proof container.
Previously deep pools have all been filled in. All water was easily avoidable. Turned around at the final waterfall / chockstone obstacle, which is so close to the river you can taste it.

Sunny afternoon at the Great White Icicle. Approach was straightforward. Steep and rocky. All anchors were in good condition. Water level was low but rappelling was still fun. No wetsuits required. Light rain jacket and rain pants kept me mostly dry.
Steady stream of cold water, great views and some technically challenging rope and anchor work.

Quick romp. Very light flow, but typical for this time of year. Only water spray was on rap one, everything else is avoidable. Conditions are slippery, aside from this everything else was straight forward. All slings still in good shape.

We did an evening run on GWI. The approach is well-worn, but the tree debris from this winter's avalanches remains problematic. Descending GWI was straightforward, and the anchors and rigging were in good condition. Our feet and legs were wet from light spray and shallow water. It was a fun outing.

Top notch rappels and beautiful views make this a fun and repeatable route! We really enjoyed this one! Once across the river, there is a wide established hiking trail just through the brush. Depending where you cross, hiking up/down the trail a short ways to a spring flowing over the trail marks the turn off for the approach. Through the woods, the trail quickly gets over grown with lush (mostly friendly) greenery, but still passable and faintly visible. You can see the falls from one point on the trail to gauge flow. Trail quickly climbs up a very steep gully that is loose in spots mostly following a trickling creek. Very pretty and canopied on approach. First rappel is described as optional which I would say could be the case if it didn’t parallel down a flowing river and end at a monster cliff. Definitely recommend roping up here for safety… The chained anchor for R3 is easy to pass if not paying attention and is a semi hanging station. The big rappels are very vertical and very fun! The flow felt light, splashy, and enjoyable. Not pushy at any point, however, I believe the locals would call the flow “moderate” for these falls as it normally would be a trickle. We were happy without wetsuits, just rain jackets. Although, neoprene booties would have been nice! Full sun on descent! Our 225’ rope was just a tad short but manageable on R4 which we rigged off the bolts set back and high on the DCR wall. The forward DCR bolts on the ground could be a harder pull and slightly less enjoyable rap but 200 should reach from there I’d think. All the rappels are high quality and very enjoyable. The scenery and long reaching views are fabulous. The flow is beginner friendly. We took our time enjoying this one. 2 people 4.5hrs

Time includes anchor building, clearing brush from the watercourse, and exploring exit options. Suspect that most small groups will be in and out of the canyon in 2-3 hours max and that this route will be combined with other short options nearby. Originally set R1 RDC but due to loose rock adjusted LDC, hopefully for a better more free-hanging line. All webbing new. This one will likely be most worthwhile after rains.

Some light run-off made for a nice run. Combined R1 and R2 for a single 150’ rappel. Small pool at the base of R3. Some fun downclimbs. Added R4 to the route, though it’s certainly a bonus rap. Shuttled FR 189 the short distance back to the start. Webbing is in good condition. Combined with Orthrus Canyon. 6 hours total for both canyons.

Very fun little canyon! Rappels come instantly and in short succession. The geometric rock shapes in the canyon walls make for interesting and unique scenery. Lots to see in such a short canyon! A quick scramble up and you are back on a road. When you shuttle back up to the approach to retrieve the other vehicle, continue down the lesser used 4x4 trail to find a neat cliffside campsite with an incredible view!

FR 2703 is currently BLOCKED by trees about 0.75mi from the junction. Anticipate having to walk an extra hour. I'm not sure what conditions were like during the first descents, but there are a multitude of downed trees in the canyon. Makes going much more tedious and also detracts from the otherwise nice bedrock nature of the lower portion of the canyon. Also, entry higher has a bit of unpleasant bushwhacking. We only did 5 raps. Only the first (actually about 80', although it looks bigger) had an anchor which we replaced (it had been gnawed through). Don't wetsuit up until you get here. Retrievable anchors for the rest. Last two drops are much better on the opposite side of what is suggested IF the water is on the lower side.

There are now five rappels up to 40'. A wetsuit was not required. The Tuckup trail is nonexistent for the first 7-8 miles. To make your life easier, head down Tuckup canyon to the second drainage on the left (LDC) and climb up to the Tuckup route there. If you take the original route, you'll have to work your way through a cactus minefield.

From Tuckup Canyon Trailhead 11/23/23, Hades Knoll to just above the Conglomerate Arch in Main Tuckup on 11/24/25, hike back to trailhead on 11/25. Route just under 20 miles total. Hike to head of Hades Knoll, moderate pace with minimal breaks (lots of avoiding crypto on no trails) took 8 hours, 8:30a-4:30p. Hades Knoll arm took 8:30a approx. 5pm. 5 Rappels, two fiddlestick/handlines for my less experienced partner. All five rappels felt pretty mandatory for someone who is reasonably risk-averse. Maximum rappel we encountered was still 50 feet. Webbing was in surprisingly good condition for all anchors, but would not expect that to last long. Appeared that someone had gone through extremely recently based on two sets of footprints. Three Belly-deep wades, one knee-deep. Wetsuits not required, but we were chilled for a while after our wades. Followed description in "Grand Canyoneering" and spent time looking for the social trail on down-canyon-right before realizing that just because we could see the confluence, it didn't mean we were to the final walkaround yet.

From river trip, climb up obstacles in Tuckup, then at junction with Hades Knoll, go about 100 yards up Tuckup to an informal trail that bypasses some obstacles to get you into Hades Knoll, bypassing "rap #3". The canyon is nice but not amazing until after you get above "Rap #2". A brave person is needed to climb up rap #2. It's less than completely vertical and there are some pockets midway up. The canyon is BEAUTIFUL above that obstacle. Tall, curving polished Redwall Limestone with section of polished pink conglomerate. The beauty of this section is the reason to do this canyon.
The canyon was not run in this flow. This report was created for tracking water levels.

The canyon was fully run on July 26. The team took 2 other days to establish sections of the canyon. This is likely the most technical canyon in Washington currently as there are multiple traverse lines (with intermediate points) to avoid flow and hydraulics and requires careful footwork. In this flow, your team should be comfortable with these techniques and high water features like hydraulics. The canyon has very fun features but also very technical/serious sections that require respect. Other than those warnings, this canyon is very, very fun. It has a multitude of slides -- a feature not too common in the PNW. 5-star canyon.

Ran the entire canyon from where the road crosses. The big drop halfway from the road to where the classic R1 (Jump/ Slide) is was neat but I was fine doing it once as the rest of the canyon is much, much better. Be mindful of the rope as we did coreshot a rope on this rap. Stay in the flow to avoid this from happening. Half way from the big drop to the classic R1 is where the fun begins. Plenty of fun slides that could be added to the eventual run. That being said, the classic route is great. It’s like mineral meets Lost meets Davis with a side of Big. I know, sounds wild. The run is not for the faint of heart as the hydraulics are real and people need to be mindful of their teammates at all times. Also, check depths routinely. It’s easy to forget when you’re having so much fun.

Flow was slightly sporty, but we were able to walk into the problematic hydraulic (where the traverse line keeps you away) and stand in it - was chest deep and slightly pushy. If not prepared and you come off your feet it could churn you up a bit, esp. in higher flow, and be hard to stand back up. I like slides so thought it was great, even though it's not a committing canyon. There are two legit vertical slides plus another 25-foot low-angle slide that's noteworthy. Everything is fully bolted up now and ready.
Ran Hager for the 4th time. Water levels are lower. Jumps and slides are still In but noticeable touching bottom more but isn’t an issue yet. Great day.

NOTE: Google lies. You need to enter on NF48, not NR21, (the crossover road is gated from 21) We did Big, davis and Hager over 3 days. This canyon is amazing, best I have done in this state. S2 we repeated, jumped, and repeated, beautiful. The lower section is very technical and need solid people setting the ropes and traverse lines. The water flow maybe lower, but I thought it was perfect. We were able to get into the flow almost the entire time, and it was pushy. Several slide have solid reversals, and I did a couple unintentional back rolls when trying to get out. Baby throw bags are nice. The approach and exit are vague, so we flagged and tried to make a path in. Our flagged approach ended upstream 200yrd of S1, but it has a great changing area and their were several nice warm up Slides. We flagged the exit route, straight up the bank and right back to the car, huffing and puffing in our wetsuits for 20 min..

Still riding an amazing high from Hager. The most fun canyon I've done in the PNW, hands down. All fun and giggles until oh shit it's time to pay attention. The beta and trip reports do not overstate the serious technical challenges of this canyon. You need to be in there with strong leadership and a solid team. Hager was flowing a4+ and was noticeably higher than last Sunday. Hydraulics are not quite keepers, although some might hug you for a turn or two before they let you go. 😈 We did not enter the one below R10 which looked serious and like one to avoid. There is a decent social trail developing on the approach. It is easy to navigate yourself to the beginning of the canyon (5 mins). At the exit, follow a game trail between the creek and the clearcut until you see your cars parked at the hairpin, then scramble 10 yards up to your cars (15 mins). Someone didn't find these trails and flagged a different and less pleasant route on the approach and exit. We removed as much orange flagging as we could find. Major shoutout to the first descent team for finding and opening such an incredible canyon! Hager deserves 5 stars and is destined to become a PNW classic.

Ran Hager and Davis today. Quick updates. Log in R1 pool has been removed. Could be useful to check depth or find a new anchor to go in flow. Removed logs after S2 and pool just before the triple slides. Fun day. WATCH OUT FOR THE YELLOW JACKETS ON THE EXIT. There is a nest and they were active. We were fortunate.

Nice day on Hager. Oddly the flow was higher than it was a few weeks back at the end of July but not by a lot. Partner got very cold (3/4mm during a morning run) so we decided to exit after the three slides. There’s a pretty definitive trail now from that section back to the parking.

Hat's off to the first descent team who opened the canyon this year. Where is all that water coming from? The current map looks like one should drop in directly off the road, but that will put you at the top of R3. Looks like one should head up hill for a few hundred feet, then drop into the creek for the official start at S1. Realized our error and backtracked. We set up a short shuttle and pioneered a new exit route over to the lower switchback on FR 48. Lower exit added +1 rappel into a short dark slot (lots of fallen trees overhead) and then exiting out DCL up over the ridge. There appears to be some more interesting water features below our exit point. Easy hike out - mostly flat/downhill. At the lower switchback, there appears to be a flagged trail heading east that we didn't have time to explore... Does it go all the way to the creek? That might make a pretty marvelous exit.

A great day with a group of 7. Amazingly consistent flow since over a month prior. We ran R10 as a slide, with a teammate stationed at the bottom to help anyone who might have become stuck in the recirculating hydraulic. I would not recommend doing this slide in any higher flow than moderate. Doubled up several single bolts in the lower technical section. A great day overall. Done in time for an early dinner and drive home.

Amazing canyon with lots of great slides and fun rappels. Water level was a fun moderate flow but these desert Canyoneers would not want to run it any higher…lol. Props to the team that opened the canyon up and made that flowing hallway safe to run!! 😍💦👍

Another great day in Hager. Water levels pretty comparable to several weeks ago -- not much change. Hydraulics would still grab your feet, but pools were relatively easy to escape. Continued downcanyon for +1 rappel (50ft from log anchor), then exited at the cairn for short 5-10min walk to the lower switchback for a shuttle. If the canyon was extended a bit (couple small rappels, maybe another slide; might need a bolt or two?), there's a really nice walk-out DCL (complete with switchbacks!) just downstream of the tributary that comes in DCR. Easy walking through open forest over to the road.

Great day in Hager. Added the seasonal QuickDraw to the final sequence. Please leave it and we will get it when the season ends. Flows were very high. We avoided on potential strainer by exiting DCL. Very easy. Also avoided the 2nd traverse line sequence as getting to the station proved problematic due to flow + very slippery conditions. Again DCL for a few feet. All other jumps besides S6 were good to go.
Excellent conditions. Pushy slides but all hydraulics were easily escapable. We exited after the Upper Section so most of the group could catch a plane home.

Another fun day at Hager. All anchors are in great condition, all slides are in, but hydraulics while not dangerous were strong at times. We tried the alternate shuttle exit option after R15, and probably would just prefer to exit after R14 next time as the route finding was proved to be difficult and more time consuming than hiking back to the drop-in parking spot.
Fun day at Hager. Water was colder than Big or Davis, and definitely more challenging than them. So minor hydraulics in the canyon, nothing that caused worry. All anchors in good condition.

Hager is always fascinating for the sheer volume of water pumping through it. If anything, water levels seemed slightly higher in the last technical section. We set up a shuttle for the quick exit. Will look at flagging or improving the lower exit route next time, but it's a 5min hike out requiring minimal navigation. We also added a new anchor to R9. Sidebar note: a well-meaning member of our team inadvertently cleaned the redirect on R12. It is now taped to a tree with a sign just up the trail on the hike in. If you run the canyon, please return it to it's proper location. Thanks! (Do check conditions before you go as a new forest fire has broken out east of Packwood.)

Ran most of the canyon besides the last traverse and narrows. We were welcomed by 4 new logs which we promptly removed. Al we replaced two smaller stainless links with larger ones. We jumped or slid everything minus S6. Conditions were high and there wasn’t much to worry about with folks who have done the canyon more than 10x total. If you go after us please be ready to either tighten bolts or replace with a drill. We’ve never had to yank 4+ logs out on the season opener so who knows what happened downstream this past winter.

We descended Hager in its entirety. Thanks to the crew before us for removing the logs. The anchors in the final traverse and narrows were all in good shape, we tightened what we could. The redirect on R12 is still not there. The water in the final section was sporty and engaging.
Fun day. Nothing special to note. Fun was had.

Another great day in Hager. Took our time and enjoyed the slides and myriad clear pools. Found a dead deer floating a pool at the bottom of lower technical section. Exited via well-marked shuttle route. One mystery solved: the trail used for the exit (both directions) was built (likely illegally) by dirt bike riders.

Managed to get a run in behind the Rondy groups without seeing anyone. Just as fun as always but a bit on the cold side with a 3/4 wetsuit. There appears to now be two redirects about 10 feet apart on R12 DCL since the last time I ran this canyon, maybe not totally necessary but makes the rappel very easy with both. We dropped in above the beta drop in point and it has a few fun small slides. All bolts are good though and everything goes currently.

Low-Moderate flow. I removed a handline that was left between S2 and R1. It is behind a tree right at the entrance trail of the canyon (very easy to find). It is unacceptable to leave fixed equipment like this, one can go back after a run with ease and remove. Also - R12 has 2 redirects now. This adds minimal protection and should be removed, IMO.
Fun trip to see the troll. Anchors in good shape. Only issue was the smell and guts of a rotting deer carcass at the bottom of R14 - with guts scattered all the way to R15.
Fun canyon, fun team. The troll lives up to his reputation.

Flow was high but still a lot of fun in the upper section. It was a little warmer than expected in 5/4 wetsuits. A large cedar fell from above the anchor onto the R1 ledge and into the pothole. Unfortunately that tree is not going anywhere anytime soon. Added a note to beta. Quite a bit of debris in the outlet and above R2 as well. Next group in should bring a saw and continue the cleanup we started around the anchor. Exited at S8 due not having a big enough team to set safety. Walked down canyon right to R10 anchor. Getting to R10 anchor could need protection from being swept into keeper hydraulic. Looking down stream into the dancefloor it was rowdy but we would have continued with a bigger team.

A great day in the upper+middle section. High flow added frequent spice, but in a strong & experienced group it was very much enjoyed. I've improved the description of the R1 slide, and dialed-down the recent warning about it. Sliding R1 has been rarely done over rapping from the tree. The last report caused a bit of confusion as to which slide was blocked (people were worried it was the iconic entrance slide). Hopefully it makes sense to everyone now. All other slides and jumps go, albeit with extra precautions regarding whitewater hazards. The group were dressed for cold conditions, but found it to be surprisingly mild compared to Tule the day before. Exited after S10 as planned, to avoid the slower & colder lower section.
Full run from a little above S1 through R14. Flow was great. Anchors in good shape, both redirects present on R12, jumps and slides currently clean.

Super fun day in Hager. First time for all 3 of us. Did a couple slides an extra time, and took our time examining each problem. Nothing too difficult, but the hydraulics sent us spinning a few times. Not as cold as expected. We exited up the original non-shuttle route.
Replaced webbing/link on R1. Tightened bolt R7. Removed R8 single bolt, this bolt has been 1/2 way in/leveraging for the last 2 season. Webbing/link added DCL for more logical rap.
Beautiful canyon with a fun team, the first time through for several of us. We made improvements to a few anchors, including: - R2, moved anchor higher to make it easier to get on rappel - R8, ''

Brought some new friends to meet the troll. Pleasantly surprised there was decent flow today. Water felt particularly cold today. Hydraulic in S6/R7 pool was a touch grabby. Boil/keeper at R10 was still gnarly. The other major hydraulics in the canyon were benign. Appreciate the new webbing on R2 & R8 but kinda miss that janky old bolt.

Loved this narrow, intense little canyon! One team member was concerned about the look of the hydraulics and escaped to the road about halfway down. But 2 of us completed it, and the hydraulics we encountered were only mildly pushy. Close study of great beta on this site helped us handle everything just fine. AMAZING place!

A cold spell plus the driest January in 40 years has Hager running with surprisingly (and delightfully!) reasonable flow. Beautiful, fluffy snow blanketed the trees, logs and banks but we found only a little ice in the canyon. It was VERY cold! This would not be a good choice for anyone newer to winter canyoning. All the usual slides and jumps are clean and all anchors are in good condition with both deviations in place. Car-to-car time of 3.5 hours for the upper and lower canyon.

Water gauged Hager today. It was running fast but likely doable for an advanced team who had done it before. We did not descend the canyon but we did walk the rim and pull a sizable log out that would have likely become a strainer later in the season.

There is some new (relocated) wood in play in Hager. Scout ALL jumps and slides before committing, especially when on your first seasonal descent. Flow was high but manageable when not wearing ￼packs and having personal flotation. Our group had wetsuits and dry suits with PFDs and vests. Having a swiftwater mindset of rope management, flotation and downstream safety made the day much less stressful. 😎
Fun day! We took out after the triple pools and skipped the lower section - mostly out of laziness (conditions would have been fine) No new wood (or other hazards) to speak of compared to last year

Slides and jumps are all clean, anchors in good shape. High flow some slides will “washing machine” you before spitting you out! Removed several logs from the upper section that could have been problematic, large one moved into pool with others above R2. Exited after 3 slides due to time but checked redirects which are present.
Surprisingly moderate conditions in most of the canyon for this early in the season. Lower section running closer to mod-high.
Great day to have swift water training! Thanks to Sam and the crew! The only thing I have to report is the water is really really cold even on a 95 deg day!

This was the 2nd half of a Davis-Hager run. All slides/jumps in the beta are good. We did temporarily stick our rope on the log at R2 but recovered it. If not pulled quickly as the rope exits from the quicklink, the rope will drop into the log and get stuck. We recovered the cut rope of another teams at that same spot so take care on the pull. At R12, the 2nd deviation accessorry cord is starting to show wear. We didn't have any accessorry cord on us so the next team to come through should examine and consider replacing this. All other anchors are in good condition.

Time reflects upper, middle, and lower. First time through the canyon - spent a fair amount of time taking photos, depth checking pools, etc...beautiful canyon! Very cold water. We all had 5+ mm setups and were still a bit chilled at times (we also descended the canyon in the evening and air temps today were lower than average for this time of year).

Amazing day in Hager after some rain charged the creek up a little bit. Nothing scary, probably just more fun! All the slides and jumps are in good shape. Several of us slid the whole Dance Floor! Hopefully the beta for that will get updated soon.
We had a great time playing in the Hager water park. All the slides/jumps were in. The dance floor was so fun we climbed back up it to do it again since it wasn't slippery at all.
Great day running Hager for the first time. We were in 3/4mm suits and were feeling the cold despite it being a fairly warm day.

Water levels are still pretty low, but all the slides and jumps are still super fun. The hydraulics are all gentle and playful at this time, but that'll probably change pretty quickly when the rain comes in this week. Overall a great day if a little chilly as always in Hager. Don't forget that extra neoprene or rain jacket for this one.
Hager today was cold!! Bring neoprene caps and gloves. Hydraulics were in play.

Great day! Note: the flow was high but still a4. The upper section and exit have changed quite a bit. No log at the big opener slide, but it’s actually easier and more fun without the log. Debris has cleared from the pool and pothole below the opening slide, and R1 now goes as a slide! A very forceful slide, and only a few tested it, some of whom touched bottom lightly (its bedrock) so slide at your own risk. The low-angle bumpy slide at the end of the upper section is now a log downclimb. All slides were checked and clear for sliding/jumping except the narrow recirculating pool above the dance floor - the team used the traverse line to bypass that pool, so that has not been checked A large landslide at the exit makes the original exit impassable. Recommendation is to continue one more rappel and a few short drops, and take an exit canyon left that sustainably takes you to the established trail.

Baller full run of hager as last day of swift water training. Pools are huggy and spinny but easily escapable with adequate neoprene and decent technique. Half of us wore drysuits, the other half wore medium/thick wetsuits and splash tops. No one got *too* cold but all were grateful to the guides for increasing the pace from instruction/scenario mode to team/gtfo mode when we hit lower hager as chills were beginning to creep in. Amazing, in a sad way, to see the massive landslide now complicating the exit from lower. Sending confidant scramblers ahead to set a hand line is helpful.
Amazing as always! Did a quick run in the heat if the day which made the cold water feel great. Was interesting to see some of the changes with more slides and some filled in.
Amazing day in Hager! Drop slide instead of R1 is great but land flat.
Great day in hager. The big slide goes. 2nd to last drop also goes. Water was chilly but not bad. Ran into another team of 3 and merged forces.

Decent canyon with quick approach/exit. Canyon is very short with shallow carved walls. Doesnt have the epic grandiose vibe of some of the others nearby. Very much a “waterpark” style canyon with a few jumps and slides if you are into that kind of thing. Raps are all just okay, not very memorable.. 5/4 wetsuit was plenty warm. We took the early non shuttle exit as we were told it is much quicker than the longer creek walk/shuttle option. Trails are all nicely cut in, no bushwack

WARNING ON S3/R3 Person in our group struck wall DCL causing severe injury on R3/S3 of RW beta. While sliding R3/S3, second person down left leg struck a part of the DCL wall under the waterline with heavy impact. Remainder of group slid farther right (didn't know about injury until everyone already down). Wrapped up injured leg and evacuated through exit just after R4. Xrays on ER visit revealed broken leg from impact as anticipated (now in aircast and healing). I ran a few days later to reexamine R3/S3 more definitively and discovered DCL wall is not totally vertical/smooth and has some small deformations causing steps/ledges/obstructions under the waterline if a body part were making contact (especially at speed since second half of the slide is basically a drop before entering water). If sliding you MUST slide farther right near right edge of the watercourse. Note: prior beta suggests depth is only 6-7' deep but nobody else touched bottom (only impact site was DCL wall).

Excellent conditions, enough flow to be fun, but not overly pushy. New anchors are great. As noted in earlier trip report days earlier, be careful if sliding R3/S3 and stay right in watercourse to avoid contact with the DCL wall (especially under the waterline).

Fun, short, easy! We decided to ghost this, since alders on the channel boundaries are abundant and make for great fiddlestick anchors. The waterfalls are all low angle and easily rappelled in the water at lower flows. This canyon would make a very nice challenge with potential escapes throughout most of the canyon (if you're willing to bushwhack up a steep slope) during the fall or spring. Air temperature was decently warm, but the water is chilly. I started out in a shortie and changed into a full wetsuit midway through after getting a bit cold. The approach trail is very nicely built and makes this a very fast trip. Thanks to whoever did all the work to build up the Hall Creek and Zig Zag Trails! The first time I explored this area, they were just tiny boot paths.

What this creek lacks in technical interest, it makes up for in convenience and esthetics (at moderate-low levels anyhow - I think much lower and it would be pretty boring and quite slimy). This is a fantastic beginner canyon - only 3 rappels, one in the water course adds some fun. Also, the approach is a trail the entire way and you have service the whole time. A number of fun little slides too. That said, its still a technical canyon so beginners should not take that lightly.

Approach is pretty steep for the first half, without stopping to check the flow it took us 25 minutes to get to the top of Hall Creek falls. We walked for a few minutes up the creek to check it out. All anchors were in good condition. There is another rappel between #2 and #3 in the description, I'd call this #2.5. It may be downclimbable on the right, but given how slipper this canyon is, I would not recommend it. I think this is a 5 rappel creek. #4 anchor was difficult to find, all black against black rocks in the water, just FYI. We put a new ring on it. An anchor for rappel #4 was set up from a a tree on DCR rather than on the tree stump in mid-channel. The water is low, and you only get exposed up to your waist and hardly spend anytime in the water. This could be done with no or very thin wetsuit when the sun is right above the canyon. We used a 3/2 regular and 2mm shorty, started our descent at approximately 7pm. The water is very cold, but air temp was around 74 degrees.

Upper part of the creek is slippery as usual. Lower part has a couple rappels with nice flow, and is beautiful as always. All anchors in great condition, including a couple with nice new black webbing (thanks, Becca!)
Low flow. Very slick rock
Slippery as to be expected and flowing steadily. Replaced the webbing and quick link on the dry rappel for R5.

Descended the canyon to the river directly with ropes, ascending on the way out in 10.5 hours car to car. Used ropes for each fall but the last chockstone drop since I ran out of rope, the bypass around this last drop really is quite easy, though. Light, cold flow in Hance Creek - wading to knee deep only. Set all anchors and retrieved them on the way out - plenty of good material for anchor construction. Don't let the relatively short time make you think getting this route done in a day is easy - it isn't. I was moving quickly and efficiently and only took one break in the day - and with a party of one there are no bottlenecks at the vertical obstacles. Most groups would prefer two days to complete this trip - if doing so I really wouldn't recommend camping at the river as there is so little space to set up. There are a few really nice campsites right where you drop into the creek from the trail.

Flowing water in the upper third or so of Hance only. Hiked all the bypasses. The second (of three) is probably the most annoying. Checked out Page Springs and the remnants of a stone structure on Horseshoe Mesa on the return trip.

Extremely hot down in the canyon this time of year, as expected. Took the bypasses rather than going direct, and found them absolutely horrendous in every way imaginable. If I was to repeat this canyon (I likely won't) then I would absolutely take rope and build anchors for the several small nuisance drops. Given the effort required, I found Hance to have few, if any, redeeming qualities. Fortunately, Grandview trail is beautiful.

Hance Creek via non technical bypasses due to treating the day more as a trail run than a canyoneering day. Bypasses are steep and loose but well cairned and fairly straightforward All rappels except for the final one right before the river look to be newly bolted by someone else. The 6 ft polished chute described on the main page and "crack" downclimb next to chalkstone are also now bolted and had a brand new fixed rope, which really aren't very necessary. Strange that those got a bolt but the final rappel/bypass spot didn't. Great day and great route down to the secluded Sockdolager Rapids. The rappels would have been a lot of fun but the bypasses are also fun in their own way, if you are into that sort of off trail janky terrain.

Great day in the canyon—first known descent by rope. We measured the rappels and updated the beta. The route is physically demanding with a fair amount of boulder-hopping and scrambling, so be prepared for some effort. There's poison oak. After exiting the canyon, we camped by the river. The next morning, we made our way upriver back to Escondido Camp. Lots of poison oak along the river but that's unavoidable in Los Padres.

We hiked to the campground day one in about 4 hours (after driving 5 hours), hiked to the river and back day 2 in about 8-9 hours, hiked out day 3 in 4 hours. Though this itinerary does not leave much time for lounging around at falls or in pools, the hike to the river and back (16 miles) is one of the most scenic and memorable I have done. The narrows of Havasu Creek just before the confluence with the Colorado River can currently be waded up to about waist deep for almost its entire (short) length, one spot right before the river is deep and swift(probably very short swim), but shallow just before meeting the river and appeared easy to get out. We chose to just walk back up the narrows since we had already done the bypass route - also worth doing, easy and short.

Weather at this time (50-80ºf) was excellent. I did have to wear a hard shell at times for spray and wind, but I never felt like I was melting. Our team camped, during our time there we hiked down to the confluence and back. We attempted to sump-dive into the blue room. One of our party swam in, but didn't find any air and had to swim out (It's possible he didn't go far enough). The water was cold enough that it took your breath away for a few minutes, a 2-3mm full wetsuit would be ideal for this task if it didn't involve carrying one down the canyon. A guide from Arizona Outback Adventures very nicely came over and warned us that the water level may be too high inside the sump, and that someone had drowned trying to enter the blue room sump last year. Day 2, we had enough time to enjoy Beaver, and hike down to the confluence and back and return in daylight. Barely. We were moving pretty fast, but we did stop to play and take pictures appropriately. My time to exit from the far end of the campground to cars, with a 40lb pack, was under 4 hours. Some did it faster, some slower. I was the oldest member of the party at 38yo.

We attempted to find a more direct approach route, which took about 4 hours of fun but exposed Class 3 & 4 climbing and routefinding. Very beautiful rock and views. We wanted a sandtrap for one of the final 30' downclimbs, which was a little spicy.

A really fun and great canyon for pothole escapes! We used a combination of meat anchors, pack drags, pot shots, sandtrap, mechanical ascenders, and partner assists to escape the potholes. The majority of the potholes were dry. We packed wetsuits but only one person used a wetsuit (4/3) for one of the potholes (pothole where there is a knot chock). The water was only waist deep, a bit deeper at the exit lip, but it was still possible to use a partner assist to escape out. Make sure to go with folks who are experienced with pothole escape techniques.
Fun trip. Took us a while. Note that our water flow conditions where after a fairly dry spring.

Long route in sounded awful, so we pioneered a shortcut route in from the road on the north side of the canyon. Climbed down a ways, then did a dirty rappel into the creek. Slopes in the old clearcut are piles of loose choss. Heater Creek had enough water to be fun and some interesting geology. The rappels are rapidfire; the first and last are particularly enjoyable. (Beware foot entrapment hazard DCL at the bottom of R1.) All anchors are good to go. The final rappel exits into the lake where it was an amphibian fiesta. Never seen so many salamanders in one place before. Presumably enjoying the cooler water which was pouring into the lake. Enjoyable (and warm!) quarter-mile swim down the lake until we located a good place to scramble back up to the road. Encountered several boaters who were baffled to see folks swim by in wetsuits. Altogether, a fun outing!

Great little warm-up canyon. We ended up walking along the side of the river to the bridge instead of swimming across since it had rained heavily the night before and the flow was much higher than the day before (when we scouted it).

Started at the Armstrong Campground & thrashed up to the old road and across to suit up just before the drop into Helion. The beta is correct in that the upper drainage is a rainforest with some thrashing required even if staying the creek. It gets better as you descend. Excellent falls, all anchors in good shape. We cleaned a lot of older anchors and probably took 50ft of webbing out of the creek. Reaching the bottom we swam the river (ferry angle is your friend) and thrashed up to the road on the far side for an easy walk out. Seems Helion is best done when you can swim the river. Thrashing out along the shore looks like a pain and probably would have taken us another hour or so. More time for festive beverages at Fearless Brewing.

Replaced all webbing on rappels 1,3, and 4. Bypassed rappel 2 DCL before drop. One mandatory swim in pool, cold but in sun fast. Avoided first pools by climbing RDC and up. Cool canyon with huge walls and cool narrows. Would probably one of Arizona's most popular canyons if easier to access. Probably the two largest drops in Hellsgate Wilderness canyons. You are welcome, Arizona.

Beautiful smooth rock with a unique variety of colors punctuated by multiple deep pools and trickling water. A true back country oasis We drove in 2 days before to camp and run Acheron Canyon. We took the South trail from Young. These trails are rough and rocky. I believe a stock high clearance midsize 4x4 with aired down offroad tires would be fine. 4Lo and careful driving needed in a few spots. Both of our trucks (Frontier and 4runner) are lifted and did fine. Trails are too tight for a fullsize truck. Drove past the “car park” way-point all the way up to the cow tank up top and camped. Drive in took 1.5hrs on dirt. After Acheron we split ways and I camped at Hell’s Boundary approach. There is a great established campsite here! The canyon is full of color and the rock is semi sculpted and smooth. Lots of water and deep pools, many of which can be avoided with some careful finesse. 3 mandatory 20ft long deep swims, a few wades. Very cold but the sun was out making it bearable without wetsuit. These pools are picturesque and perfect to hangout in if the weather was warmer. I rebuilt all anchors and had to fine tune the beta. The big drops were of fair quality! Exit scrambling on a solid scree field was nice and engaging, great views. Eventually turned into an annoying bushwack to get up to “nice trail” which comes and goes, leading overland to the wash you approached in on. I found the exit to drag on at this point, took me a little over 2 hours. A solidly fun and beautiful route with some of the most colorful/unique rock Ive seen in AZ. Drove out via the North trail which was less rocky but not as interesting, still took 1.5hrs

canyon: Hell Hole Canyon (Aravaipa Canyon Wilderness Area)
comment: Hiked up into the narrows about 2mi to the springs and Hellhole Arch. Side trip while passing through Aravaipa Canyon.

The upper 1/4 mile river bed is very rough and full of logs and rotted wood and nothing to rappel. I would recommend bush wacking where you turn for the final big traverse when climbing up and work your way to the creek and start where the falls begin. Newer webbing in place and obvious. Nice grippy rock. Campground works but bathroom shut currently.

At the top of the trail turn right down an old logging road parallel to the road, NOT on the main road. However, I recommend starting the route at the switchback to the top of R3 and going down from there. Save about 250' of elevation gain, the bushwhacking and stream walking at the top, and only missing two minor drops. We downclimbed R1 and did SIX raps and we're very good DCers. So unless we missed something... Also, last rap is about 65' from the tree anchor in the prime center spot not 55'.

The trail up to the drop in (Henline + Ogle Mountain) is overgrown and difficult to follow in some parts (it will disappear and reappear unexpectedly so try to follow the GPS tracks as closely as possible). You should aim to drop in above Steve Falls. We used retrievable rappels for most falls except for Jerry and Henline Falls where the webbing was already there. The waterfall could have been a bit higher but it makes for a good beginner canyon/starter canyon for harder canyons in the area with more water flow.
Good flow. Did the whole canyon all the way to the Opal Creek confluence. Many anchors were missing or damaged, rebuild some and used releasable anchors for others.

Brutal approach hike. Defiantly a good place to have a GPS to make sure you don't miss any turns. After sweating it out and climbing to R1, the descent through the river was cool and beautiful. A 60m rope can be used, however like it says in the description, there is a 140' rappel that is easily down climbed DCL, and Henline Falls..... You have to climb DCR over slick rock to the rappel anchor which is right at the edge of the falls, then rappel to the end off your rope, then off the end (yes, off the end of your rope) dropping 2' to the wet rocks below. There is a good trail to the bottom of the falls and only do this if you have a spotter on the bottom. Defiantly spices up an already exciting and beautiful rappel. Thanks for the good Beta. Adam
Did a little bit of training with some newer canyoneers below Henline falls to Opal Creek. The fire closure is lifted and has left little noticeable impact on the area I saw.

Yellowjackets are out in force this year -- we were attacked on both entrance & exit. Creek itself had low water, but still fun rappelling the falls and floating in all the pools. Reached the bottom of the falls by a good hour, so continued with lower Henline for a full day.
Great outing in upper Henline, but last person discovered a core shot in the rope on the final rappel. Unclear what happened there; haven't had issues rappelling that drop before.

It looks like locals may visit some of the trail-side falls occasionally, but, otherwise, no signs of other visitors were encountered, so this may be a FD. We had low expectations, so were pleasantly surprised at what a nice little creek it turned out to be. Pretty much an ideal creek for beginners and almost worth a visit alone just to see the bedrock. Would have been more fun with some more water -- need to visit earlier in the year next time. A worthy day out.

No other anchors were found, so this was probably a first descent of Lower Hinkle. Found a nice trail leading from the road pretty much right to the top of R8. Quite a bit of woody debris in the lower canyon. Bushwhack out was relatively easy.

Returned to lower HT with a friend new to canyoning. All the anchors were in good shape, since we just paced them all last month. We exited above R13. A short bushwhack uphill DCL brings to you to a road grade and an easy trail to the top of the ridge. After that swack out via the same directions in the canyon description.
Totally dry except one tiny pool in the rock bed; Beautiful canyon with colorful rock, towering spires, and flowering cactus.

A few avoidable pools. Was able to do it with only 3 rappels, rest were downclimbable. Exit requires a little exposed climbing, more than other canyons in area. Worthwhile quality canyon, not a destination canyon. All anchors looked in good shape.

Fresh water trickling down canyon. Was nice. We enjoyed the series of little scramble-climbs exiting up the drainage. You can escape this canyon at any point. There's a steep, scree-filled crack LDC of the base of the big rap that allows fairly easy entrance or exit to the canyon at that point. Webbing at anchors looked good. Rope pulls easy.

A beautiful but short canyon with a noteworthy 2nd rap. Reminiscent of the views in Parker Canyon with respectably sized spires towering above you and smooth, colorful red and blue slick-rock along the canyon floor. The canyon was surprisingly lush this time of year, although with the exception of a few small, easily avoidable pools there was no water. All anchor webbing is in good condition, but when I return to this canyon I'll likely extend a few of the anchors beyond ledges to facilitate cleaner rigging. Turn-off for the ascent is immediately LDC of the final rap. Noted difficulty rating is due to the climbing required to exit the canyon, which I consider at least 4th class - maybe low 5th, depending on the line you choose. The last obstacle consists of a few staggered ledges with loose rock (watch those holds) and some exposure that may be discomforting for those without prior climbing experience.

Time is car to car for First Water, Hog, and Phog. Two hours per canyon. Shallow pools in Hog, all avoidable. CEM’d off a tree RDC at R1 to bypass a large, guided group. All webbing was in good condition. Fun climbing on exit, still plenty of loose rock.

On the exit is best to climb directly up the canyon rather than trying to climb out the side. The wall on the left when facing up canyon looks like the easiest way out but it is an extremely chossy climb.

Rained all morning prior to the 8A drop into Hog. Enough rain to create light flow in the canyon which subsided quickly after we began the descent. All anchors in good shape, and I set a new anchor to protect the short boulder problem in the exit drainage. This anchor can be used in combination with a progress capture or friction hitch system to provide a top belay for less experienced climbers. This same top belay technique can also be used on the final climb at the very end of the exit drainage- the team lead can take the chossy shelf traverse out, and rig a top belay off of a bolt located behind a large boulder upstream of the water course immediately after exiting the technical portion of the drainage. The bolt and hanger are painted brown and not immediately obvious.

Very pretty, fun, and super easy to access. The rappels are easy and fun throughout. There were alot of deep pools that required some finesse to climb around. Lots of beautiful rock! We replaced webbing at last anchor. The exit is super fun and engaging with lots of class 4 and some class 5 moves on crumbly rock required. Definitely not very beginner friendly. This is a really great route to combine with Parker or another shorty in the area. Definitely a must do!

Great canyon for beginners! There was a deep pool in between raps 2 and 3 that we had to go up and around and at the bottom of rap 3 there was a deep pool so again we went up and around and found a spot to easily down climb since no one wanted to get wet. Anchors all looked good except the webbing was a little sun worn on the last rap. Just to be extra safe we replaced it. A few spots where you have to scramble/climb on the way out that might be difficult for someone not used to that but it was nothing super crazy or too tall. Beautiful the whole way!
Knocked out the canyon in 1.5hrs. Anchors in good condition. Nice morning excursion with mostly unremarkable rappels but lovely views. Exit requires at least one person with basic climbing skills.
Cute little canyon. Anchors looked good. Trickle of water throughout. No wetsuits.
Great canyon! Good for lower temp B class canyons - full sun almost the whole way. Exceptional pay to play ratio
Great canyon, potholes are in keeper condition. Partner assists and anchor building skills needed.

Super fun canyon! The potholes are only partially full of water (usually waist deep or less). We used potshots to surmount the bigger potholes and partner assists, pack tosses for others. We used a sandtrap quite a bit in canyon and also the smooth operator. Currently it is possible to ghost the whole canyon and we didn't find any webbing. 3/2 wetsuit was good, air temp mid 80s.

What an amazing canyon! Water level: Deepest was chest level. Some other unavoidable parts were shin- to waist-deep. Wetsuit: One person had a well-fitting 4/3 and didn't complain about being cold. Another who normally runs chillier wore two layers of 3-4mm and was cold until putting on a rain jacket over everything. I was in a 7mm and got a little cold in the parts when it started drying up. Air temperature at the trailhead was in the high 40s/low 50s. Difficulty: Fairly straightforward potshots and sandtraps. Quite a few areas that we sandtrapped ended up being downclimbable with a catch. If done again, I would first meat back-up someone to scope it out for a downclimb to make it more time-efficient. The final rap is off of small vegetation; we fiddled it and looked for ways to increase rope friction over the rock.
Super fun romp through a canyon that can be entirely ghosted.
Time is car to car. Absolutely stunning canyon! Water was waist deep at the deepest spots. With temps in the mid 80s, we never put our wetsuits on.

A fun day! The first 1/4 mile of the approach trail was quite overgrown (and thorny!). Because of that, the trail was not at all obvious, but still discernible. After that it cleared up and was easy to follow. It was very helpful to have the map downloaded on gaia for finding it though. On R5, we used a 300' and a pull cord for the first pitch, and found when we pulled the rope that both ends of the 300' would have made it to the anchor for the 2nd pitch (with only about 5' to spare). So, we think that first pitch may be more like 145'. We also thought it might be fun to do the full waterfall in one rappel, and not pitch it out - it would keep you in the flow more. Might make for a terrible pull though. Plus it would necessitate a lot of rope. Overall it was a fun, esthetic creek.

Took a slightly lower line on the bushwhack to drop into the creek. Would have been fun with more water, but a nice mellow day. Creek is pretty action packed per not walking more than 5min between rappels.

Prospect in the area didn't pan out, so we fell back on Hopkins as a backup plan. Someone's done trail maintenance since the last time I was there. Approach trail far less brushy. Approach hike is pretty mellow, then you get a great payoff with rappel after rappel in the creek... including the big 250ft+ one that will keep you on your toes. Cowlitz ranger station appears to have posted some nasty-grams regarding all the pipes and other bricka-brac near the bottom... tho' all that junk has probably been there for years at this point.

Flow was high and very forceful. More like pushy a4. Creek crossing even got our attention in certain parts. I uploaded a screen shot of the current snowpack with this trip report for reference. It has been a dry week with warm temps, so the snow was melting quickly - highs in the mid-70's on the day of our trip.

Great conditions on Hopkins. Water was high but just low enough not to be too challenging. It was only pushy where the creek constricts. Felt like a totally different creek vs the dry summer conditions.

First time down it, in an experienced group who'd done it before. As last report, only pushy where the creek constricts. Elsewhere the flow eases off very quickly. First rap is probably the crux flow-wise, giving you little in the way of warm-up! Replaced webbing and quick links in various places, including on the multi-pitch (did it in 3 pitches). There's definitely fun to be had here in the next month or two before it dries out. (Set difficulty to "special challenges just due to the multipitch rigging). Watch out for ticks!

We did R5 in two pitches with a 200’ rope from the tree at the edge of the drop. Creek was very slippery which became tiresome with all the low-angle rappels. South-facing, open canyon gave us sun almost all canyon which was delightful on a cold day. All anchors in good condition.

Flow was not pushy and perfect for our group. Creek walking and rappels are super slippery!! We did R5 in 2 stages, 60 feet to the hanging anchor then 190 to the bottom. Hopkins is great, I love that all the rappels are close together without much creek walking. Run it before it runs out of water!

Fun day. Plan B. Noticed plenty of random and extra webbing. I guess this is a practice beginner canyon which is fine but let’s clean it up a bit. Noticed bright nylon cord holding up some black webbing.

Just about the right level for Hopkins. Plenty of wet stuff to choose your own adventure in, but nothing which had to be avoided. Zipped packs on R1 so people could enjoy the biggest stuff. Did R5 in two pitches, which provided a lovely ~200' rap line in the water. Still damn slippery in places.

First rap was the funnest and only one you could get an overhead dousing on the way down. R6 could be done down the gut in the flow from a small tree about 20 feet back from the other anchor. The other rappels were mostly out of the flow. An impressive old growth tree DCL just below R5.

Beware: R9's anchor was a wrap 2 pull 1 (to my memory) set high (eye height or higher) on a thigh-sized tree DCL. The first rappeler (avg weight) in low flow with proper rappel technique shook the tree back and forth a little bit. After he disconnected, I moved the webbing down to reduce leverage.
