I was the next one down so I did not get to watch the tree for movement but it supposedly was fine afterward. The lowered anchor made pulling difficult. If I returned to the canyon, I'd likely want to replace the anchor, maybe making it a traverse to a better natural anchor (both stronger and positioned easier to pull the rope down). I wouldn't be surprised if someone tries to move the webbing up for a better line, so please be aware (or build a better anchor), especially if you anticipate a larger load (or higher flow). R9 is not that steep but it is slippery, so it is easy to fall and increase forces on the anchor.

Super fun day in Hopkins despite tons of mosquitos. Team of 7 with 6 new to this creek. Great run that would have been even better with a bit higher flow. Some raps were very grippy while most were very slick.

Very fun but very wet and cold day out. We bypassed a few rappels. There was a bit more blowdown than last year in the canyon including a lot at the base of the final rappel. The big sequence is good and actually has 4 anchors going all the way down. Could stay out of the flow for the multipitch.

Steady rain was coming down all day, and we bypassed several rappels, mostly the ones where staying in the flow was mandatory and looked hazardous. The sequence down the main falls was quite spectacular and was the highlight of the trip. Anchors were in good shape.
Outstanding fun day in the high flow. Bypassed R1, did all other rappels.
Flow seems to have decreased a lot in the past week R1 was fun but pretty tame. All rappels were in, all anchors good.

There are ticks on the approach, a member of our group was bit twice. Terrain used to access R7 is somewhat exposed and eroded, suggest using traverse line in order to access the rappel anchor. There was also a fresh log jam at bottom of R9. Beta has been updated. Overall fun day. :)

Lower flow than expected, fun water level, good flavor but no spice. Replaced hanging rebelay anchor at R5. Repelled from the new traverse anchor at R7, better line to stay in flow and avoids the awkward start at the original anchor. R9 cleaned some downed wood out of the chute at the top of the drop but a handsaw would be helpful to finish the job. Spent some time clearing loose debris from the logjam and the bottom was f the rappel. We got of rope and pulled from the top of the logjam to avoid rope tangle in the logjam. Easy enough to down climb from the top of the jam.
Hopkins has great flow and is super slippery! I'll update the beta with items of note.
Great day out. Low flow and sunny. All anchors in good condition.

Flowing high, anchors all in great condition. Skipped R3 due to debris in the lower section of falls and in the pool making rope entrapment probability high. Lots of erosion and small tree branches in the creek. A few rock and tree strainers are present mid canyon. Easily avoidable however pull rope quickly to not get a rope stuck after being pulled down creek. Only encountered some rockfall on the big rappel at the 2nd station. Fun canyon in current water lvls.

12 hours rim to rim descending to the river and ascending back up the fixed ropes - though this is a difficult day that won't work for everyone. I ended up bringing four ropes: 130, 70, 60 and 40 and needed them all, plus a few slings for hand lines to help get back up a few of the downclimbs. I was solo and maybe being a bit extra cautious, but I felt like I needed all of them. I used the 40 foot rope (tied to the 130) to extend the anchor for the first rappel - which is a tricky one. Dead man anchors built in the wet gravel in the hallway before the drop just fell apart when tested, I ended up using a small pothole at the beginning of the hallway and filling it with rocks on top of the anchor to stabilize it. A longer rope of perhaps 200 feet would allow you to potentially find a better anchor at the top of the initial 20 foot drop into the hallway. The anchor for the second drop goes right through a chockstone overhang which can be very difficult (perhaps impossible) to overcome when ascending if you don't know a few tricks about aid climbing - use extreme caution. It may be possible to descend/ascend in a different spot to avoid this problem, but there wasn't anything obvious. After this drop is another of about 15 feet that I used a short rope to manage. The creek was flowing lightly (no wading, no pools), the bypass at the first drop is comparatively easy (though steep and loose) expect to do some short sections of exposed climbing along the way in other areas, though.

Fun day in Hose with the nice, clear water flow after recent rain. No problem crossing either Tortilla or Mesquite Creeks, Mesquite was flowing across the road, but not too deeply. Repaired/replaced 2 anchors, we only did two rappels. Like most Superstition Mountain canyons, Hose Canyon is a little bit more "pay" than "play", but the short technical section is quite nice when flowing.
Good flow made for a fun descent. Water was cold. Exit a lot of bushwacking.
Had a small flow through most of it. Pool at bottom of the 80' rap was belly deep. Anchors seemed fine. 5.5 hours for this and Prometheus, including walking up the road from Tortilla Flat.

Light flow. Three swimmers and some shallow wading. No wetsuit, and water was quite cold. Some very pretty narrows, and some of the clearest water I’ve seen in any canyon (due to recent rains). Some scrambling on the exit with great views.
Pools were full and canyon was lightly trickling with warm clean water from the thunderstorms last weekend.
Quick evening scouting of this drainage. Probably some of tallest canyon walls I have seen on the peninsula. Only encountered small drops/ logjams that were easy scrambles

Explore the most of the rest of the Hughes Creek. Lower section is fairly neat and worth doing, took about 1.25 hours from the car to get to. We explored to where marked on the map, took about 5hrs to get to that point (probably not worth repeating). Will probably bring going back to do the one rappels and maybe scout that other drainage that drops in

there is one pool to avoid 2/3rds of the way down one of the rappels - the pool can be avoided by stemming over it or carefully rappelling on the canyon left side of it. A little tricky either way. Only one other spot with shin-deep water that was mostly unavoidable. rebuilt two anchors that had tattered webbing. exit hike down the main drainage to get back to the road is not very fun - very brushy

More photography outing than a canyon run. After a surprisingly fun descent of the east fork, we followed the western tributary up to go see the "Big Idiot" waterfall on the west fork. We then thrashed up and out to a road just above the west fork. A good one for a rainy day in the coast range.

Returning to Idiot Creek after a couple of years; a nice one to do in the early season. Not as much water as last time, but still a fun day out with a trio of great waterfalls. Explored a new route out. Beta has been updated.
Surprisingly low flow for this time of year.

Easy approach. Three really fun raps. R3 is fantastic as noted. There was a bit of snow on the ground, but everyone stayed pretty warm (entire team in dry suits though). We came up a bit short on our rope for R3. Might be worth re-measuring this rap. Based on the amount of additional rope we had to use, we were guessing it could be off by about 30 or more feet. We exited DCL to the road. The hike before the road was pretty steep. Took us exactly 3 hours car to car. Pro tip, if you’re headed back towards Portland our team highly recommends stopping at the McMenamins Grand Lodge to use the soaking pool after!

This is a great rainy day creek. You have a direction choice on R3. Choose to go toward the water rather than to rap straight off behind the anchor. I edited the beta and added 25 feet to R3. I used a 180 foot rope and had 40 feet on the ground. I feel like 140 is a more accurate number.

Plan B for the coast range and we probably traded up. After an inch of rain the previous evening, Idiot was ripping with some care to keep your footing even when crossing the creek. Highest I've run the creek at, but turns out all the current anchors are good even at this level. Get a bit of a pummeling on R1, but in a good way; do not wear a pack. R2 allows you to slip down behind the falls, and R3 is always a joy to rappel. Thrashed out and nobody fell in the swamp this year.

The flow is very nice and only cold enough to induce an "ice cream" headache when you're directly in the flow. All three anchors were in good shape. Two of our team set a second anchor on R3 so they could go in the flow, which looked very doable and fun. It's a good time to be an idiot!

All anchors in good shape, water lvls are mild but still quite fun to rap in. Not pushy just enjoyable, the last rap if coming down on the DCL side is around 145, but due to water lvls I went right down the middle to catch the rooster tail and landing mid falls adds about 5 or 6 ft. The exit was steep and painful but relatively short. I would personally say in higher flow this canyon is way more extreme and good for thrill seeking folks, current lvls are just fun for all.
Great side hike loop w/Spencer Canyon. A little navigation required but fairly easy to check out AFTER completing Spencer then heading south. Carry extra water, dry and hot

Very fun and picturesque route. Fun rappels and neat features thoughout. We had to build alot of anchors. Deadmans and Cairn anchor in multiple places in water course, come with the skills and materials to rebuild them. No water in canyon. Got to the river in 4.5 hrs, there was a small area to air up rafts. Floated down to AZ Hotsprings and spent considerable time there. Really nice route for the area, a must do if out here.

Interesting as an entire route - a mostly easy approach (until the end), an ok canyon with a couple decent DCs, a paddle, and a hot spring. As ONLY a canyon route it's probably only 2 stars- mot very scenic or noteworthy. Anchors were well made and in good condition (2 recent descents). The 25' chute near the end was a nice DC. Wind was fairly low on the paddle and still was moderately tiring, took 1.5hrs. Our max rap was 90' (which should still be the case if NOT doing that final DC and instead doing it as a 2 stage rap. Bring a 100' rope and you should be good (200' not needed). I'll try to update beta.

Hiked from the Kanab confluence up Kwagunt Hollow. All potholes in Jumpup were dry, but there was steady and clear flow in the lower half of Kwagunt. Kwagunt has some nice camp spots if timing permits.

Very fun beautiful canyon similar to nearby Waterholes/Pockets but half the length, featuring some really great rappels and gorgeous sculpted rock! We hiked along the side of the canyon for a while looking to bypass the drops as beta suggests. We stayed out until we couldnt anymore and the canyon slotted up, unknowingly bypassing R1, we were faced with a large sandy ledge and no anchors. The previous “deadman/cairn” anchors were washed out so we used my sandtrap instead on one rappel. The other has a good sandy spot to use it but there is also an old tire wedged in a crack we fiddled off of, however, we almost stuck our rope on that one so sandtrap may be better. The pot holes were very shallow and of no consequence, I dont believe we even got in any of them. There was a few pools of water easily avoidable. All the rappels are fun and pretty and the technical section was short only about 3hrs or so. The bulk of the day was pack-rafting out which was very enjoyable! Would definitely come back! Beta has been updated!

Beautiful short canyon with some fun rappels and a great way to access the Colorado River! We did the same as last time, skipping R1, dropping into the canyon down a V notch and climbing around subsequent raps. I am going to adjust the beta to remove those drops and R1 as they are easily bypassed without missing anything. Arriving at the first big drop (now R1) where we used a sand trap last time, we found a single webbing loop popping out of the ground from what looked to be a buried deadman anchor. Gave it a soft tug, and backed it up with meat before sending our heaviest teammate first. As he rappelled, the webbing pulled straight out of the ground. Digging up the sand we found a large piece of concrete someone had buried. Upon further inspection it was clear the webbing had been cut and reburied, we suspect, by a spiteful local. I remember noticing a single set of footprints leading to/from the cliff edge… This should be a big warning to canyoneers, someone doesnt want us traveling through here and I feel going forward canyoneers should ONLY use a sandtrap on this drop and leave no trace. We added webbing to the tire rappel as the sand in this section is far too hard to dig up for a sandtrap. (halfway hardened to sandstone). We made it to the river in 4hrs. The Colorado was slammin’ today and we flew in our packrafts! Near the end the wind picked up and the paddle became more challenging, even so, this time it took us almost 4hr to paddle out.

Good canyon with a long exit. Big rappels from single bolts, and a sandtrap for r1, make this a not so inviting canyon. If anchors were improved, and a you took a boat shuttle instead of paddle it would be a solid 4 star for the area.

Very cool canyon with super easy access from Page. The amount of trash as a result though is disheartening and personally detracted from the overall experience. Sand trapping R1 is a fun aspect. The bolted anchors are sufficient but may need updated with increased traffic. Paddle is long, but fun. Time reflects substantial headwind on paddle out.

R1 is only like 35' if DCing the semi steep crack after, pool is about thigh deep, but can keep partners dry Can confirm longest rap at about 190', although appears uncertain Skill with DCing/steep slickrock and comfort with exposure is helpful for this canyon in a number of places Never encountered the wedged tire, but we bypassed going L and descended a broken ramp system Final sleing fed pool is at least waist deep No wetsuits used, but we were chilly and shivering at times from being wet We exited UPriver to the Ropes Trail. Total exit time was about 2.5hrs.

Pool below the final rap is around waist depth and unavoidable, but we inflated our boats and paddled across. A bit muddy throughout and some small patches of snow. Other pools were bypassed with a few exposed traverses and/or downclimbs. Toggle came in handy for a number of raps. Time reflects an exit via ropes trail.

Very cool human powered loop to do this canyon. Time reflects the canyon, paddle upstream, exit via ropes trail, and bike shuttle back to Page. Bike was stashed near beehives to provide a quick pedal back to our one vehicle in page. More logistically simple than multi vehicle shuttles from either rim or from Lee's. Anchors are in good shape and single bolts remain solid. May be worth backing up if traffic increases, although friction around bends and over edges reduces strain to existing anchors at most drops.

Canyon dry. A kangaroo rat lives deep in the slot and will run up and down between your legs. Bolts rusty but worked for us. Final rappel has a deep and sturdy deadman anchor. No cairns, trail faint or nonexistent on approach and exit. Bring lots of drinking water.
Two pits of horrible brownie-batter mud. No water. Would be more fun with water. Stunningly beautiful canyon!

Though the mileage is short for the routes in this area, don't make the mistake of thinking you'll get through them quickly. Kanab Zero itself took us 8 hours to descend to Kanab Creek and drop camp very shortly down creek. This is partly due to the high number of rappels, the time it takes to negotiate the off trail approach and exit routes and the fact that you'll be carrying as little gear (ropes) as possible to save on weight. We had to rappel once along the way (40 feet?)to get through the last cliff band along the Kanab route to get down to the slope that leads to the Esplanade. One brief swimmer where the book describes it - 4 of the group appreciated having wetsuits (3/2 full) one of us didn't wear one.
Water in potholes only. Replaced webbing at anchor stations.

As others have stated, do not underestimate the time it takes to get through this canyon. Long drops, less ropes, heavy packs and free hangs all eat up time. If you plan to camp up by Scottys, start at first light off the rim. Route was straight forward with the first drop off to the right. Anchor after the 210 3-stage difficult to spot, so a cairn was built. Pools stanky with dead sheep carcass. Enjoy!!

Temperatures this weekend were abnormally hot for this time of year (high of 100+ degrees). We started at sunrise, moved at a moderate pace, and reached the creek in 7h. Approach was steep and loose with consistent exposure. All webbing was weathered and/or damaged and was removed and replaced, except for R2 which was toggled from a small tree. Some pools around ankle depth that were all avoidable.

Todd’s description was useful in navigating the steep loose approach.. We took two 7.6 mm CE4Y rappel ropes, and two 1/8 inch Amsteel pull cords. This significantly reduced our weight and allowed us to save time leap frogging. Fortunately we had mild temperatures. All water was avoidable.

Hiked the section from Big Cove to Whispering Falls as an out and back from Hack. No water between Big Cove and Hack. Below Hack about 1.5 miles there is water in the creek bed for about a half mile, then dry until the Crack Baby pothole. The pothole is several feet lower than this time last year, but has water. Kanab Creek itself remains dry until about a mile and a half before Showerbath Spring, then flows well all the way to Whispering Falls, which was my turnaround this trip. Water conditions provided as a reference only. Call the park for current conditions before you go.
Saw sheep in the canyon! Many water crossings, neo socks recommended for fastest route

We hiked from the river to the Jumpup confluence. Before about 1/2 mile above Showerbath the creek had good flow, and large boulders made for required scrambling and slowed progress significantly. Above this point the creek bed was dry, but the going was much easier and required no scrambling. Taking a side trip to look at Whispering Falls was worth it.
We did this canyon on our way driving from LA to Lake Mead. The approach and descent are short and the canyon packs in enough rappels to keep it interesting!
Chilly day but not windy, approach hike in good condition, wore a wind jacket in the slot section was nice, R4 has been setup with 2 anchor options

The hike was very straight forward and the rappels easy. A very good canyon for beginners. We didn't have a high clearance car and mistakenly took Route T instead of Route S (see google map) and it was pretty difficult. (it was a rental car!). The weather was pretty good and not very cold until the last rappel, which was pretty windy.

This canyon has super clean and polished pink granite drops with easy walking and a few short downclimbs in between. Although short, the technical features are top notch. All the anchors are bolted with webbing, or a chain around a pinch point. It was getting quite warm by the time we stared hiking up at 10AM, and warmed up to 90F or more by the time we exited at 2PM. And yes, the access road is a rather rough, and you need decent vehicle ground clearance.

Roads a bit rough, but doable in 2WD if you take it slow. Developing trail leads almost all the way to the drop-in. Must be some other approach route as saw motorbike tracks in the gravel at the top of the canyon. All anchors in good shape. Removed worthless non-redundant anchor tied with EDK at R1. One of the downclimbs is now bolted -- nice for folks who don't feel like risking a twisted/broken ankle when sliding. Fun rappels. Good half-day outing out of LV.

All new webbing from prior group. Nice beginner pretty canyon. Added initial rappel into the canyon, as u first see the gravel creek bed on the ridge line, cut to the wall and you will see nice lime green webbing on driving. The road in from the west side is much rougher than turning in earlier from the east side. Still bumpy but no deep soft sand that way
Nice short canyon before heading to the airport. Re-rigged goofy last anchor rigged as American Death Triangle.

Great night canyon on thanksgiving eve! We started in the dark and used the GPS track from ropewiki to navigate the light use trail which worked well. The approach hike tooks us ~50 minutes and we had a total time of a little under 2.5 hours at a relaxed pace in comfortable temperatures.
Everything is in great condition. Canyon is dry. Fun as always.

Great day out and anchors are still in good condition. We had a low clearance car and drove most of the way to the trail head. We stopped about .25 before in a pull out near the power lines and the. Hiked in. It seemed the worst part of the road was beyond that (ruts, sand, steep in a section) and had no regrets about adding a little extra walk for the car safety. Watch out for a buried bees nest after R3. It's about 30 ft from the bottom of the rap on the way to the next rap. Easily avoided if you notice, but also could have easily stepped on the hives opening hole.
Approach was hot! Canyon is beautiful and petroglyphs at the mouth are really impressive.

We had 3 adults and 2 kids. The 3 adults are intermediate level. The kids are brand new in canyoneering. We've practiced in Calico Basin but this is their first actual canyon. They had fun! All anchors were good. Bees were minimal/non-issue.
Love this canyon! 2nd time doing it and it's just short and sweet. All waterfalls had new webbing recently installed. We did some top-rope climbing on 2 of the waterfalls. Great stuff!
Good anchors, all bolts or chains. Nice trail to hike up. We enjoyed the multitude of petroglyphs at the exit.
Third time doing this canyon. Love it. Conditions were great. Relatively easy approach and such a chill, cruisy, beautiful canyon. Great place to introduce someone to canyoneering.
all anchors in great condition; great intro to rappelling and canyons

It rained on and off that day. It was windy and calm, warm then cold. The water was barely a trickle in the canyon. The bolts and webbing left by previous canyoneers that we used that day were in good condition.
Canyon is in great shape, used this day to practice guided rappels. Note that the lower bolts used for guided rappels at the base of the 4th rappel have been removed.
Sunset canyon! We started the hike at ~6pm and finished by about 8pm, lovely choice to tack on after a day of work/climbing. All anchors were in good condition at the time we went through.

I took my wife here for her first time canyoneering and we had a great time. There was a trickle of water at the waterfalls which made the rock a bit slippery, but nothing too wet (and I think could've been avoided). All the anchors were in great condition. There was a cairn anchor at #5 (the 5', 5', 5'), which we used to rappel and that was a fun experience (it was our first time using a cairn anchor and it was bomber).
Super fun easy for new people canyon, this was my wife’s and I second time down and my sons first. Straightforward and a good time.
All anchors good, cool place.
All anchors in good condition. Bolts are solid. Webbing good.

All anchors in good condition. Safe, fun canyon, with easy approach. There are a considerable number of bees in the canyon and we encountered a swarm at a small chokepoint in the canyon. I only mention in case of allergies, but our group of 7 was unscathed.

The descent of Kingsnake took our team 1hr 40min. Some members of our team downclimbed every obstacle. We moved some loose rocks at the optional R2, which will make that an easier downclimb for most. No poison ivy noted this spring. This was nice to pair with a Bearwater descent. Kingsnake's several slot sections contained a good number of interesting downclimbs that were the right amount of challenge for most of the group. We were pleasantly surprised how easy it is to hike up the old road ridge trail between Bearwater and Kingsnake.

Mostly follows what is stated in Grand Canyoneering. 3 stage 175' rap is easy to manage. Didn't need wetsuits in 85 degree heat with low water present. Only 1 minor swim encountered, but this seems to be out of the ordinary.

Likely first descent of Koda. Weren't entirely sure what we were getting into, but turned out to have some quality falls. Would fun to do it again with more water. We dropped a shuttle car down by the campground, but ended out thrashing the 600ft back out and groveling up the headwall. Would have been safer to send the best climber out and rig a rope for others to climb. Headwall exit was the hardest part of the day. Nice half-day adventure.

Downclimbed/slid R1, removed old torn up webbing from R2 tree and ghosted it. R4 was a fun rappel. The downclimb groove is a cool little narrows. Followed the creek down to the confluence with Paradise then walked up creek and combined with a descent of Paradise Creek and the creek walk exit to Wind River Rd. About a 10 hour day car to car with lots of sitting in the creek to cool down.
Water was flowing. Hot on the hike out. Bring more than 2L of water. Really cool spot and great exercise.

One deep canyon-wide pool below the Boulder / La Barge confluence that was tricky to avoid, otherwise dry conditions. Thick brush in couple sections. Scrambled from the mouth of the canyon up to a social trail which led back to Boulder Canyon Trail.

Warm winter day so we elected to do the first drop into the swimmer. Not too bad. The next pool was very stagnant and covered in gross green film but we were able to skirt around it easily after dropping in. The pool at the final drop was also low enough to easily jump over.

Good little short canyon with easy approach and exit all on trail. Scenic canyon and interesting rappels. Fun boulder climbing and route finding at end. Replaced webbing on rappel #2. First pool was swimmer, nice to have wetsuit as we did canyon in evening as sun was setting. Second pool was should deep. Third pool was swimmer. Light flow in canyon. Overall a fun short canyon.

Time reflects La Milagrosa and Agua Caliente combo - car to car. Swimmers at the base of all rappels. Jumped the first two drops. Pools were full with a light trickle of flow. Added an optional rap/swim RDC (after R3) that can be accessed via a fun chimney downclimb in the watercourse. High winds today made for cold swims.

A nice canyon to knock out in an afternoon from Tucson with deep refreshing pools to cool off in after a hot approach. R2 can be downclimbed though this is nonobvious from above. R3 is seemingly the best possible location for a knot chock anchor and it felt pretty bomber.
Did this canyon very early in the morning. If you go before 6 you may need to park on Snyder Rd. A little bit of flow.
Rappels through falls to pools that were greater than 10 ft deep. Could jump the first two rappels. Had to swim at least two pools. Loads of frogs.

Approach has no shade so starting early in summer is a good idea. The granite is really smooth and slick compared to the sandstone I'm used to. Water level was too low to jump but I still had to swim a good stretch of the pool after R1 and the pool after R4. All anchors in good condition, the R3 knot chock anchor is really solid--3x redundant although not super well equalized. Water in the bottom pool kept me cool for the hike out. Great little bite sized canyon with easy approach and easier exit. Perfect for a summer morning.

Quick little canyon run. Water was deep and definitely chilly. We did it with rain jackets, but could probably put on a thin wetsuit if you wanted a more enjoyable experience. Added new webbing for redundancy on R3, but kept the old webbing as it’s still holding, just looking a little worn.
Scouted from above, 2nd swimmer has a thick green algae layer on top. Heads up for those thinking about this canyon and averse to swimming in algae.
First and third were swimmers, first three rappels have 2 bolt anchors, although the natural anchors look great

Christmas canyon. Had a couple hours in the morning, so only completed Ladder Canyon. Better than expected, and will return to combine with Micro. Ascending the rappel was lots of fun with some flow. Narrows were pretty. Webbing is new.

time includes Micro. Nice temps though I got a bit chilly after getting wet in Micro as the sun was behind the cliffs by that point. Was glad I placed edge protection, it's pretty sharp. I had issues getting over the lip as I only had one ascender (prusik for the foot). you can get one foot and an ok hand hold above the lip but it's definitely challenging. either bring a second ascender that you can attach above the lip or be ready to haul yourself up (shorter tether to upper ascender would be easier). short but pretty! webbing was fresh though I was prepared to just tie the rope directly around the pinch.
Fixed a rope at Ladder, and used as an exit after descending Tango and hiking along Fish Creek. Ladder had very light flow. Fish Creek was flowing rather high but was easy to navigate.

Combined Ladder Canyon and Micro Canyon for a short but sweet day in the Superstitions. Time reflects both canyons at a leisurely but steady pace. Replaced the anchor in Ladder Canyon. The narrows of Ladder Canyon is over fast but it's pretty. Ladder Canyon was dry and would be nicer with some water flowing. One piece of edge protection was enough, but I wouldn't want to do it without it.

I usually want more raps on a canyon route, but this plus Micro is pretty enough to be totally worth seeing. Fresh webbing at the single rap. Ascending is not really hard - feet on cliff and kinda low angle most of the way. Definitely protect your rope at the sharp edge - even alone, I was glad I did. Pools of lukewarm, clean water up to 4’ deep in Micro, which also contains a boulder obstacle that was slightly challenging but manageable alone and in wet hiking shoes (I’m not a great climber).
A very light trickle of water was present. The watercourse is an open wash and easy hiking up to the tributary used for Carbon access.

Ran the full LeBar, tho' we might have dropped in early. Followed the old overgrown road grade to the first stream / slide, then thrashed down a steep slope through classic Olympic forest to the creek. Reasonably nice platform just above the creek which allows you to jumpstart the day by literally jumping in. Great creek, great water conditions. Exited to the bridge, startling a number of campers in the area.

A fantastic 4th of July descent. Lurking on the old Canyoneering Northwest website, this canyon looked like it had some nice scenery. Did not disappoint. We explored both the upper and lower sections, which were not documented on the old website. While certainly low on the technical side, the canyon was quite beautiful and was well worth the trip. A great beginner canyon by midsummer.

Dropped in a the upper entrance this time. A trail has been cleared all the way there. On arrival, don't go down the steep dirt slope, but instead take trail to your left down through the trees. Flow was great today, but spicy in the narrow sections. Replaced missing anchor at R1. One short drop somewhere below was kicking off a horizontal recirculation which took some effort to pass through. Hangers on R5 are both loose and spin, but the bolts seem sound. Lovely to drift in the long cool corridors of LeBar when it's 100 degrees F back in town. A great day out!

Great day! 80 degrees outside made floating leisurely and wonderful. Flow is slightly pushy in a few places but generally friendly. We dropped in a bit lower than the main drop-in - on the hiking trail, we dropped down the 2nd tributary creek that the trail crosses. It was a very easy, non-brushy creek, and allows you to skip what I think are an annoying 30 minutes of slippery creek walking. Props to Jake & Logan for finding this new drop-in! (added to the beta)

We parked at the berm and followed the trail to the 2nd creek crossing. Lots of floating for then next mile. I almost think you can walk in from the car parking spot and save 40 minutes of creek walking and floating. This canyon has some of the best floating sections, but after 6 hrs we were ready to be done. Very, very pretty, but pack your lunch ( we didn't and got hungry). Massive log jams with some slippery downclimbs. Good beta. All pools should be checked. Most are only 6 ft deep, and have hidden rocks.

Dropped in at second drop-in point on the morning of July 4. Moderate flow and clear of debris until R1. R1 has a significant logjam (image attached, as seen from above R1). The original tat off the down log is no longer visible and the log seems to have shifted from the historical picture (Water guage image in beta is below R1). We felt that a rappel was possible off a natural anchor. The rappel would keep one out of flow in the upper section but the we didn't think it was possible to avoid the flow in the lower section. Be prepared to build your own anchor(s). We opted to not go further due the moderate flow, uncertainty of the rest of the water course and the skill/size of our team. Navigable for a intermediate team. We navigated back up the creek ~100 yard and escaped back up the hill slope to the parking lot start.

A well spent holiday afternoon. Water was moderate, cold and canyon was a stunning gem. Found someone had placed a bolt on the rock knob DCL right above the first rappel. Also the unlinked bolts for the final rappel seem to have been removed or torn out and only a single bolt is there (you can see the holes left from previous bolts) Had to cut a few feet off a rope on the first rappel when the flow dragged the end under rocks at the bottom while pulling the rope, but had brought plenty of rope to finish the canyon thankfully.

A great day out in this always beautiful creek. Our friends ran the canyon the day prior (7/2/23) and found that the log anchor on R1 had been washed away. They were prepared with a bolting kit and installed a single bolt DCL. Added a new R2 rap to the beta from a log jam after the Grotto rappel (it can be downclimbed but is inconvenient). Floods have taken out one of the bolts on the final rappel, leaving just a single. This bolt should be backed up again at some point, or potentially replaced with a V-thread as this section seems to see major flood events and anchors will continue to be damaged long term. If placing a bolt, please consider placing a removable bolt, such as a FIXE Triplex, so the holes can be re-used later if the bolts are damaged.

This canyon has some unique features that make it special. If you're looking for a canyon on the Olympic Peninsula, this is a good one to consider. Waterflow was a good level for beginners. All in all a great day out. Jake posted some pertinent details on anchors on his report.
Fun canyon. Water flow was forgiving and easy Little slide/shoot below deck project was fun

A cruiser river walk/float with annoying log jams which ends with 2 short but spectacular rappels in the flow we has today. Leader must do a flat jump to clear those boils and eddies. My partner got stuck in DCL eddy on last rap. Rescued with throw rope. Good times were had.

A great day in Lebar, we were happy to be in there on this very hot day. The canyon saw some major flooding this season with some new logjam debris to navigate. The grotto below R1 has a new gravel bar that was not there prior. The last rappel still has a single 12mm Triplex bolt, which is sufficient for now. One of the empty holes around that bolt will fit a 55mm Triplex 12mm sleeve bolt if anyone has one (now discontinued unfortunately). The eddy beneath the final pool required strong swimming ability. We had a throw rope ready in case someone got stuck (see attached photo)

Beautiful creek. Water didn't feel particularly cold on a hot day. Monster log jams. Cool jumps. The pool below the last rap requires a couple of strokes of strong swimming, or jumping out to clear the eddy. Overall, a great day, and nothing further to add from previous reports this year.

We had a great day in LeBar! I wanted to leave a trip report since I didn’t see any from later in the season — LeBar appears to run well into September and is quite lovely this time of year. Flow was friendly and the creek still had current to shepherd us through the long pools. We really enjoyed floating through the pools and taking in the scenery. By taking our time, we saw a lot of wildlife, including frogs, fish, a newt and CRAWDADS! It was so cool! We also had a curious dipper follow us down the canyon. All anchors are in good condition and the trap pool is a non-issue at this flow. 7.5 hours car-to-car using the standard approach.

We bushwhacked down on the steep slope about 20-30min to a decent tree by the cliff, and dry rappelled down to the creek about 30' (landing into a pool, so glad we suited up above), a few minutes walk to R1. The single bolt DCL at R1 was missing, only the screw was sticking out from the boulder (pic). So we had to improvise an alternative anchor ideas, and settled with body anchoring the first 3 people, and do a bottom belay for the last person. It doesn't sound bad, but we had to wrap the rope all around the round boulder DCL that barely had one questionable horn that was barely holding the rope from slipping up, so, it was dicy. The short rappel made it possible to do what we did. The creek walk with monster logjams after R1 and before R5 seem fluid every season, and the beta felt somewhat confusing because we didn't find the Diving Board definitively, and we found several The Deck Project type logjams. The creek walk before we got to R5 was solid 2+ hours with very proficient proceeding over all the logjams. Water was not super cold for the most part, but a lot of swimming and wading for sure. R5 bolt was there, but the screw in the boulder was moving very slightly, so we thought it probably needs to be re-bolted (or come up with a different anchor). We had a great time running this creek, but I thought it's not the best creek if one is nursing a knee or shoulder joint(s) because of the length of the creek walk and the intensity of the logjams (appx 25-30' high x 4 or 5 jams).
First entry, ever. Made it to the extremely narrow dark section after rappel 10, ran out of rope and turned back.

1.5h approach, 7h descent, 30min break at Hutch's Pool, and 2.5h exit to our shuttle at Gordon Hirabayashi TH. Multiple re-belays would be necessary to complete the canyon with <150' ropes. Removed and carried out 53' of old / damaged webbing and 30' of stuck rope that was left by a previous party who attempted to break up one of the large rappels anchoring from trees outside the watercourse (rope was tangled around an agave). Flow was heavier in Sabino than Lemmon, reaching moderate-high conditions after the confluence. Weather was perfect - ideal flow and time of year to run this canyon IMO.
water was a bit chilly but the canyon is physical with only one rappel so not a lot of sitting around and getting cold. fun and straightforward route. thanks Russell!

We seriously underestimated this canyon and learned the hard way this is not a place to bring a group of beginners without wetsuits who aren't comfterable with the cold or stemming(though I do not find a wetsuit necessary for myself). We wound up bailing at the top of the chamber rap. As advertised, the downclimbs are tricky. There are 3 serious downclimbs prior to the rap, the first of which we believe to be a rabbit hole that was recently filled in. We rapped this one since downclimbing the face in the flow seemed too sketchy. This canyon is surprisingly beautiful, the prettiest of the short Catalina canyons. Can't wait to go back and finish it!

We did 4 rappels and 0 jumps sadly. Flow is still a bit high which was cool for the big rappels but a tad intimidating. We started off as a party of 4 but two turned around as we had a beginner with us. The canyon section felt very short and probably took is 30 mins. While the boulder hopping was a hour and a half. We found one rabbit hole through some boulders that looks cool, otherwise very underwhelming.

No sign of previous anchors, so this was probably a first descent. Had rather low expectations, but the main sequence is good fun. A worthy 3-star creek when measured by the standards of the Oregon coast range. Gate was closed on Jones Creek Rd, so we hiked in via Kings Mountain past Cardiac Ridge, then out via Diamond Mill. Access top & bottom is great.

The canyon was spectacular and located very conveniently to SLC, but the approach for the long route is not to be underestimated. We overestimated the average skill of our group and what should have been a 5 hour adventure became a 12 hour epic. The beta describes the approach as "frustrating and miserable". Although I thoroughly enjoyed the entire route, some group members did not like the approach. After completing 2 sections of low 5th class free climbing with moderate exposure (some team members required a fixed line and ascenders for one section, which should have been a warning to turn back), we had a large rock fall from above our position and down towards a group of bystanders at the base of the falls 400+' below. At this point one group member became nervous and wanted to bail. However we determined there was a high risk of our team dislodging rocks if we descended the approach trail so we continued on. Due to a route finding mistake, setting fixed lines for several of the scrambles, and extensive coaching for less experienced team members the approach took over 6 hours. We had a rope become stuck on the first rappel which required ascending and rerigging, waisting another hour. At this point we were 2 hours overdue and had just begun the descent, so we decided to use webbing and quick links for all remaining drops to ensure a good rope pull. Hopefully someone will run the canyon again soon and can help remove that webbing. Sorry 😕. We were able to use one chokstone, but all other drops were lacking usable anchors and required building cairns. Additionally some team members were uncomfortable with the water flow (although fairly low) and required coaching, so progress through th canyon was slow. After expending the majority of our anchor materials and spending about 5 hours from the drop in, we finally arrived at the last rappel after 9pm. The bolts were present and in fair condition but our team was becoming tired, and rappelled very slowly and cautiously. By the time I finished the rap and pulled the rope it was completely dark and we bagged the ropes and exited to the cars by headlamp. Overall this was an incredible canyon that was less than a 20 min drive to access. However, please ensure all your team members are comfortable with exposed scrambling/climbing and rappelling through water flow (preferably verify this ahead of time in a less committing canyon). Don't make our mistakes!

The river down in the main canyon is raging right now with the heat wave melting snow fast. Upper waterfalls were a little heavy. We were happy in our farmer Jane wetsuits, rain jackets, and neoprene socks. Well, one of us did not bring neoprene sock and wished she had. Great day out. 7 rappels for us. Used the drainage approach. 👍 4.5 hours car to car. We never hit anything harder and than 4th class to a that route. You can hike around the falls to the first rappel anchor and enter the drainage to scramble up from there if you want to avoid the fairly sketchy approach hike. First time doing Lisa for both of us. Skipped one ripped bolt that was there to protect the hardest downclimb.

Great canyon for the proximity to the Salt Lake valley. We ascended directly up the canyon instead of going the bushwhacking route. I would highly recommend this route; the climbing was not super difficult and there was a pretty clear path to skirt around all of the steep areas. We took about 45 minutes to ascend all the way to the first rappel with our group of 8 (14 years old to 45 years old) and then took our time on the way down practicing some different techniques. Weather was great at around 72 degrees Fahrenheit. Started at 11:30 AM and we were exposed to the sun almost the whole time which helped with the cold water. You could stay out of the water on almost all of the rappels if desired. There were bolted anchors for all the rappels that we used, and the bolts were in good condition. We bypassed a couple of rappels and just downclimbed instead. There was one natural anchor with webbing around a rock but our whole group bypassed this rap.

Followed the KML and beta, did not pay much attention to the condition reports and that was my mistake. Approach was terrible, possibly fine with a smaller pack, but my oversized solo bag today constantly caught on scrub oak, areas of 4th class are not clean enough to provide confidence, and more scrub oak and deadfall. Judging from previous reports and my own thoughts descending, simply climbing up the drainage would be your best bet… though that points to the lack of intrigue I saw in this canyon. Approach took 2 hours and annoying effort. Descent was slow to the end, an extra set of unnecessary bolts is placed above the main falls, geared down too soon. Bolts are good, exit is brief.

Creek was running surprisingly high for mid-late August. Number of hydraulics were present, so we did most rappels out of the flow. Nice waterfalls and interesting rock formations -- but it's a long dull slog of rock-hopping between the upper falls and the bottom narrows. Had a somewhat low opinion of the creek until reaching the bottom section which is worth the price of admission. All drops in the narrows are less than vertical. Bushwhacked directly up the steep, yet mostly open forest slopes just downstream DCR of the confluence. Found a cave on the way out; there were bear footprints on the floor.

1 thing noteworthy was at the 12 foot ramp at the entrance to the narrows: A large rock (larger than a football) came off from below a tree on canyon wall DCR and crashed directly into and thru the line of the next rappel. We had already down climbed DCR of the ramp. The rock came from much higher above after our down climb. Maybe we loosened something just enough to cause it? But to our inspection it didn't seem that we had. When making that down climb take extra care AND when attempting to rappel out of the flow on that ledge DCR take extra extra care. That columnar basalt, especially in this canyon is wanting to come down.

Great run! Definitely could be rated much higher with the scenery in this canyon. Lush greens, bright red rocks, interesting rock formations, 1 beautiful rap and a very nice narrows section! Totally worth the trip, not sure why this place gets no love. It was relatively easy but its beauty is the draw here, not the challenge of how technical it is. I posted a vid for anyone interested is seeing what it looks like before making the trip out. 🤙 there is a large boulder above the R2, to rap in the flow, a set of linked bolts would be really nice!?

We ghosted the first rap off a young juniper DCL giving us about an 80' freehang. Second anchor was center canyon on a under a boulder pinch-point. Lots of off-roading traffic on the approach and exit.

Super cool mini route by Tortilla Flat that I actually enjoyed more than nearby Geronimos Ravine. Great bang for your buck and would be lots of fun with some flow. Rebuilt all anchors. You or someone in your party should be experienced in evaluating and establishing natural anchors. Some may prefer to build a cairn in lieu of the R2 natural arch - there are enough rocks nearby to construct one. Soft starts recommended for R2 and R3. Trail rope or wait to toss until landing zone is visible on R3 as there may be hikers below exploring Peter’s Canyon.

Long time canyoneer, but have neglected trip reports. Someone encouraged me to start posting trip reports to contribute to the community, 100% valid as RopeWiki is my primary site! I saw this pop-up and needed to bag two more canyons in 2021 to hit my 24 per year, this seemed like a shortish drive, easy approach, and exit for a great half-day in the Supes, and that it was! We started out, easy approach hike, almost all on the trail. The pinch point was perfectly fine. We had four total, so we went ahead and built a cairn anchor for rap #2, the arch seemed a little thin! Updating the canyon page with this. Even though we built it, please look and analyze anchors before using them. It was bomber for us, but water, nature and settling changes things! Final rap was off a boulder that is not fully stationary. Looked for a pinch point, chock or something else but could not anything substantial. The boulder moves, nervous the webbing will flip or move the rock, backed it up with a deadmans anchor but it didn't inspire us in the coarse sand. That boulder will change every storm as it is right in water course in a pothole with coarse sand. I'd always bring at least 30 ft of webbing in here specifically, just in case. These natural anchors could easily change, don't consider them guarantees. Great half day in the Supes, out at 9:30am from Tempe, back by 2:30pm.

All the anchors were solid, boulder on R3 is kinda wedged against the wall and buried in sand and backed up by the deadman and felt very strong and stable. I gave it "normal" rating because in current conditions it was easy but if they change you have to be able to built natural anchors.

Great option for a very quick, close-to-town slot! +1 on this being a better experience than Geronimo’s/Headdress next door. Trail quality is almost shockingly brush free. Approach and exit are quick and painless. All 3 raps have easy starts. Minimal, totally avoidable water was present in a small pool at bottom of R1. All anchors were in good shape - thanks to whoever built those solid R2 and R3 deadmen!
Replaced webbing on last rappel. Short canyon with nice final rappel. Easy approach and exit.
Light flow after heavy rains. Fun and fast.

Anchors in great shape despite recent rain. Added another webbing to R2 anchor. None of the anchors even creaked, and our group had rappellers up to about 250lbs. Must be more sediment, cause there was no water to contend with at bottom of drops (plenty in the creek below, easily avoided with careful crossing). Wildflowers were spectacular, and this early, the cheatgrass isn't dry enough to cause trouble yet. Go now!
Area was moist from recent rain. Quick approach and exit. Anchors in good shape. Decent canyon for beginners. Money shot on the final rappel.
All webbing in place and in decent condition, fun beginner canyon
Anchors set. Last drop anchor looks a bit iffy but was very solid when I got my rope somewhat stuck (poor rope management, pull shouldn't be difficult) and was yanking it.

Agree that this little series of drops is convenient and good bang for the buck if you are comfortable with assessing and rebuilding cairn anchors. We completed in two drops (combined R2 and R3 with a toggle, this took a bit of care on rappel) and replaced a faded loop of webbing during inspection of the R2 cairn anchor. All other webbing on the anchors was in good shape, though I do find the multiple strands of webbing on cairn anchors in this area to be pretty amusing. R3 looks to be nice geometry for sandtrapping if you're into that, but is unnecessary in it's current condition due to the boulder/cairn anchor available in that hanging pothole. On the not-so-great-side, I noticed a shiny new wedge bolt at R2 across the watercourse from the cairn anchor, went over to inspect it, and removed that bolt by hand with a few light wiggles. Not awesome. Unsure who placed it and if it was intended for use during flow?, but in addition to being unnecessary and poorly located IMO, it was clearly not installed properly. Stay safe out there and avoid blindly trusting bolts just because they pass a cursory visual inspection.

As always, fun route when time is short, but you still want a decent drop. Anchor webbing is good all the way through. It seems like the final cairn anchor keeps growing in height, and not in a really constructive way. The fairly massive base rock that the webbing is wrapped around didn’t move a bit, but some of the many rocks heaped on top of the high pile settled when our largest team member approached edge, (and the webbing angle correspondingly changed). Having a huge pile of small rocks on top just makes it seem scary when the superfluous top rocks settle or slide, and I doubt their weight adds meaningfully to the anchor’s stability.
Night run with visiting cousins who had energy to burn after a morning Christopher Creek run. The easy trail kept it simple even in the dark.

A great 2 day trip. Canyon never really gets narrow at any point but has some great views and long raps. There was one hole that had knee deep water. We spent the night at Zoroaster Camp and hiked out BA the next day.

Could be a good creek on a day with better conditions. Approach begins on an easy trail but disappears into burn area regrowth, lots of dead trees to climb over and hordes of thorny brush to thrash through. The falls themselves are impressive but Mt Adams has been pumping out heavily silted flow all season, you def want to avoid getting that sandy water in your eyes. Water level marked extreme for the final drop, the flow was not close to runnable and we popped over a ridge DCR to rap down a much friendlier leg.
Stopped and did the creek for fun. No sign of travel! The creek wasn't brushy and we had a good time. Certainly a seasonal drainage and not worth the time if its main canyon season

The most impressive part of this canyon is near the end of the route as the canyon walls narrow. Ferns drape over the edges of the narrow canyon walls and little streams pour over the edge making for an almost tropical looking environment - except the water is cold. There is a significant (easy) river walk between the first 2 raps and the beginning of the gorge area so look for another route to drop in right before the top of the gorge, if possible. If you decide to do the entire length, consider unzipping your wetsuit for the river walk. Combine Lost Creek with Parkett Creek for a full day of canyoning!

We found a route to cut the river walking in half. The water flow is intense, pushy, and adrenaline pumping when the canyon narrows for the last 3 drops. The 100' horizontal traverse looks intimidating but the water depth only reaches to about knee (or a little above) height. In this flow, the canyon should only be done by canyoneers comfortable in high water scenarios. Webbing was still in good condition.

Fantastic canyon. The road is blocked by a fallen tree to the drop in spot. The drop in is a steep and brushy hike/tumble downhill to the river. First rappel comes quick, then it's a long but wonderful hike on bedrock creek bed all the way to the amazing canyon. After part one of 2 rappels into the canyon, be repaired to stand chest deep in ice cold water setting up the second part off the snag lodged in between the canyon walls. Damn the last bit is beautiful!

I took a group of my friends for their first canyoneering trip through Lost. Absolutely one of my favorite canyons thus far. We had a blast and made pretty good time, too! Removed tattered webbing and cord and replaced it with new.
Canyon looked great. Little more wood than usual, otherwise everything runs the same as last season.

Seems to be some new woody debris in the upper tributary since I last ran it in 2015. The main stem is pretty much unchanged. Logjam is easier to rappel now. Always fun to visit the narrows. Combined with a visit to Pure Hate Gorge 30min away down FR 11. Time includes 2hr road hike.

Enjoyable romp. This would not be worth doing without the shuttle. We drove up the fire road (a bit scratchy on the truck) most of the way to the fallen tree pin. Great turn around spot, and with good map/GPS skills, found a relatively brush free entrance below R0. Flow was a not high, but was still playful enough be be a fun afternoon romp. The logjam below R3 is easiest to get around at DCR.

DCed R1. Be careful not to hurt the moss. R2 has a wedged log down about 50' (this rap is not 100'). Easy rope entrapment on the pull (found a piece of cut rope left behind here). Best to pull and bag and then carefully DC the chute to the small pool above the final drop. Anchor off logs for R3 or carefully shimmy down fallen log. At exit of little corridor DC/slide off rock into pool. Last "logjam" is pretty casual DCL

Hiked up the fireroad down the road from the exit (my usual approach to go straight to the confluence). The winter of 2020 left an extreme amount of deadfall trees on the fireroad. Freshly sawn trees showed that it is actively being cleared, but the deadfall continued in the forest down the slope and even cluttered up the confluence which used to be completely open. No significant deadfall downstream of the confluence however. Water was very low and several people downclimbed R2 (100') without a rope.

Walked up the hillside UCR to 44.5913568, -122.1071919. This put us right above the fun stuff. New webbing on R1,R2. Cut out old webbing in R3 and ghosted it. Fun day and be careful on the log pileup below R3 as it was unstable.
Lost is pretty, we did the full canyon. There was lots of creek walking and climbing over brush and logs. Highly recommend starting at the confluence. The full canyon is high pay to play.

We drove up the fire road as far as we could and then hiked the rest of the way in to the regular drop-in at the confluence. Cut about a mile off the driving until we couldn't reasonably move logs anymore. Lost is still in great condition with waterfalls trickling in from the sides of the canyon, but flow was down noticeably from the TR two weeks ago. There's no anchor for R3 so we toggled off the giant logs. The narrows are beautiful!

Lost is open for the season and in great shape. Water flow is slightly pushy in the slot section but wouldn't be bad except for a nearly hidden log standing vertically underwater in the waterfall. One member of the group slipped and suffered a signifanct foot entrapment requiring other team members to stabilize the victims head above the water while working to free the stuck foot. The same log then stuck the rope during the pull. Rope was abandoned and the group finished on a spare rope.
The shuttle routes are overgrown. We couldn't find the hike in approach. We hiked up the creek and did free solo climb up the technical section of the creek. Lost is pretty like always.

Parked the car on the upper classic road and hiked in rather than risk serious pinstriping. Road walk is easy. At the far end, cross the clearing and look for a bit of a trail that heads down through the brush to ultimately drop into the creek. Snacked on huckleberries along the way. Below R0, the creek is a bit of a mess, but gets better on reaching the confluence. Fun rappel into the chasm as always. Cleaned up the R3 station. Use caution on the downclimbs below. Hiked out through the narrows and headed for Quartzville Creek.
Removed fictitious 'shortcut route' from the map. There's a huge cliff wall over there.

We paired this with Quartzville for a full day in canyons! The new approach seems like the way to go! we got a prius almost all the way up. You'll leave with pin stripes so bring your beater car! All the anchors were in decent shape. Plan your pulls carefully, there are a few places you could pinch a rope, but we had no problems. There was still some water in the canyon but it was very beginner friendly.

Very cold and rainy day! Definitely should have worn something thicker or a dry suit, but the technical part is so short that it was uncomfortable but doable. Usual hazards still persist - log jam right above the third rappel and lots of logs in the third rap’s line, but all pulls were clean and the water was low enough to not be spooky. We brought one brand new person and another newbie, and it was a great day for everyone. Replaced the webbing on the third rap.

No other anchors were found, so we're guessing this was a first descent of Lousignont Creek. Creek was definitely in flood stage, bit pushy, and kicking off some terrific rooster tails on both drops. Surprisingly fun little creek. It was bucketing down rain all day (2" of rain forecast for the coast range), so we suited up immediately at the cars.

Fun short creek. Good flows made it fun without it would be rather boring. There needs to be a flood event to clear out the bottom of the 2nd drop. Fun short winter outing. Plenty of time for post canyon beer.

No anchors were found, so this was likely a first descent of Lower Fulton. We had somewhat low expectations dropping in -- and were pleasantly surprised when LF kicked off the day with a cool corkscrew rappel down underneath a giant chockstone on the first rappel. The canyon is committing, but has some fun obstacles, great pools, swimming, jumps, and water features. One of the best is a giant 35ft+ slide into a deep punchbowl. Need to find a better exit, but we'll be back next year to improve on the anchoring situation. Used the nearby Hamma Hamma Cabin as our basecamp for the weekend - awesome! :-)

Likely the first complete descent of Lower Fulton. It turns out we cut out a bit early last time and there's still some nice canyon to see below, including one more quality rappel into a short slot. We were rather surprised on exiting to find a very nice trail leading off downstream on the north side of the creek - clearly built and maintained by locals. It would be really interesting to know where this comes out. According to Jefferson County parcel maps, it's all private land ownership downstream with nary an easement to be seen. We explored the trail a little bit, but ultimately turned around and thrashed back out, staying on timber company land. (This exit is only mildly better than the original way we went out during the first descent in 2022.) Lower Fulton is a fantastic and fun creek up there with the other "greats" of the Olympic Peninsula, only brought low by a horrid exit. If the trail route out was viable for canyoneers (and a shuttle could be set up), that would be amazing.
Other info: Fulton clearly sees very high flow in the early season; some of the logs from last year were tossed around like matchsticks. The 30ft slide is in... although going down the first 10ft or so on a rope might be good to get past a rather bumpy section. The exit from the Dragon's Den was completely blocked with woody debris; we sent a scout down to check to see if we could get out... and had to knock out a woody block and remove a bunch of sticks so that we could squeeze through.

The canyon is the most beautiful I've done on the Olympic Peninsula so far with some amazing sections of narrows. Had to build a few anchors. Didn't read the beta before R5 and built a bad anchor on a small chockstone that led to some troubles dislodging our rope. Should have used the big tree far back from the rappel to avoid issues. We got off track on both approach and exit with lots of bushwhacking involved. Even with the tough bushwhacking would recommend it to anyone with good skills. Already looking forward to my next run-through of the canyon. Side note the forest road to the start of the canyon is in very rough condition we were lucky enough to have a friend drop us off and pick us up at the bottom of the FS road only adding about 20 minutes or so to the day. Would be tricky without car support to coordinate if your vehicle can't make it up the road

Moderate-low flow, mainly since near the end on the last rap it’s a little pushy. Would be epic if better access is acquired. Bolts and anchors were is good conditions (wasn’t looking for a bolt on R7 so didn’t check and missed if it was their or not). Some of the boulder problems were a little tricky. The Dragons den you can still fit thru, looks like you can’t from the top on DCR but if you look from DCL you can see the hole is large enough. Hint for the exit is it’s easier to crawl in a lot of places, wear pants

We all agreed that Lower Fulton is a very underrated canyon. It was our favorite in the Olympics and such a fantastic day! The narrows are beautiful - green and sculpted - and the rappels and jumps are really fun. The mega-slide into the punchbowl is in (and so fun!) and we also jumped R2 (25'), R5 (15') and R7 from the mid-pothole (20'). A lot of logs have shifted around since last season and all of the webbing in the watercourse was trashed. We cut, carried it out, and ghosted everything else except R1 where we installed a single bolt on the chockstone. The boulder garden is a fun scramble, and we found a rabbit hole in the middle that allowed us to avoid a nuisance rappel. The approach is easy and the exit was not so bad. We found game trails through the steepest sections and were able to take advantage of some overgrown logging roads in the upper section. We were back at the road in 49 minutes!

Full Fulton was fun and totally worth running. We went a day early to scout the lower exit and work on the trail. We found a game trail and did a lot of cutting and cleaning up. The exit is steep and way less bushwacking than the original beta. I'll update the kml map. I wore a shorty wetsuit with a rain jacket and was fine with temp. Our time was 1.5 hours for upper Fulton and 4.5 hours middle, lower, and exit. Total was around 6 hours.

First time back since the FD. Did the direct approach and ran just Lower Fulton. Jumped a lot more than my previous visit, and had a great time. Such a good value half day. The slide is in, as is everything else documented as a jump. R6 (Dragon's Den) has changed a fair bit. Rather than climbing up DCL and rapping from a tree as we did before - the roof of the cave is now open so you can rap directly in. However the narrow gap to exit it is blocked, so you have to climb up and over the top instead - not hard, just very much changed. The highlight was the jump at R7. With a small but committing jump out to the enormous root ball from which a ~30(?)ft step-off can be made. The area has a bunch of jump options. We tried to follow the updated exit, but frankly it felt very similar to before, albeit moderately easier due to more traffic through the thickest bushes. The first part of the dirt road is in bad condition and requires high clearance.

First time through this canyon for me, and wow! Beautiful carved rock, lots of jumps and maybe the best slide in Washington? All the anchors looked to be in good shape, but we only rapped the three mandatory ones and jumped or slid everything else. Hike out wasn’t as heinous as I expected except one small section of bulldozing through spider infested holly, but there does seem to be trails forming that appeared and disappeared throughout the hike out.
Flow similar to prior descent in late June. Exit path requires some attention as there are many game trails throughout. Requires careful navigation to avoid crossing into private land.
Beta updated to reflect changes in the Dragons Den. Fun day out in the canyon, interesting to see how this canyon changes year to year. A knife was lost, if found let me know!
One of the best slides in the PNW! Anchors are solid, rocks are slippery however the water in not very cold. Great run!

I cut out most webbing anchors on obvious jumps. Just meat anchors people down drops who don't want to jump, or use cem/toggle. One 10' drop is technical that we rappelled off the log. There is still an anchor on the last drop which is a technical-ish jump. Theres a bolt, hanger, quicklink, teblyon ring anchor dcr that goes as a slide. (It does look like a slide) Exit is wonderfully great to follow. Will hopefully update the exit track. There are ground wasps on approach and exit. For the approach to stay to the right of the track a bit once you start descending. On the exit, avoid the second steep lose section of the exit trail. I got stung once on the entrance and a teammate for 4 stings on the exit.
Pools at the bottom of every rappel. You can avoid them if you try too hard. Stinky. Lots of rock cairn anchors were poorly constructed, but we didn't do much to fix them. Great canyon at sunset.

Nice night in the canyon. Frogs were croaking their heads off and the water wasn't too hot or stinky. All rock cairns were completely rebuilt to be easily inspectable/replaceable. Provided there isn't too much rock fall you should be able to enjoy our work.

I was expecting to see evidence from my last trip where we created many excellent rock cairn anchors. However they were all blown out. There was evidence that someone had rebuilt one of the anchors, some rappels we had to build new ones. My rock chock was still there and it was the only evidence of my previous passing. We saw two owls, crabs, and a mouse. The water was full of algae and smelled gross. But reaching the ocean at the end of the canyon is really nice.

The mud between R1 and R2 is insane. Pool beneath R1 was surprisingly deep, it was up to my waist (I'm 6'2"). Beware of rockfall! There were 2 very large rockfalls while I was in the canyon, one a little too close for comfort. It would have been a pretty enjoyable canyon if there wasn't so much mud, or maybe if there was a little more flow. Saw an owl and some frogs. I did a total of 6 rappels, and will update the beta.

Started around 10:30, easy to follow the route to the drop in point. Was pretty discreet in packing all the gear and helmets in bags for the drop in and didn’t have much grief with access pre and post route. Webbing is pretty good; cairn anchors were rebuilt to support my 200lb self. Depending on comfort, the first and second raps might want to be replaced with a steel link or rap ring; the current rap ring is ~2-3mm 1.75” diameter welded steel ring. Smelly and unavoidably muddy so be prepared for some decent wear to your gear; bring your cheap stuff!

Fun little local route with a short approach and exit. Probably wouldn’t be worth it if it was bolted, but actually ended up being a great practice sesh for building natural cairn anchors. Not a good canyon for beginners for that reason. At least one person in the group should know how to build & inspect cairns, sequence group members, perform soft starts, etc. Due to recent rains, the bad smell in previous reports wasn’t noticeable. Pools were over waist high. Be very careful of falling rocks. Built new cairn anchors on R1-3. R2 ghosted a very marginal cairn anchor with a fiddlestick for the hell of it—don’t expect to find any webbing or hardware on that one. R3 had a set of rusty bolts on the ground LDC near the ledge that did not feel solid. I set up a new webbing anchor around a large boulder RDC instead. The knot chock on R4 was bomber. R5 is bolted with fresh hangers (chains were rusty but still in good shape).

Slimy. Very slimy. I smelled like a koi pond after I got out of there. Rebuilt most of the natural anchors in the canyon, as this year’s rain storms had washed out my previous anchors. R1 replaced the previous cairn anchor with a webbing anchor wrapped around two pieces of rebar DCL. Felt very solid. R2 & 3 have great cairn anchors now. R3 is bomber. Being the tallest rappel, I left a little extra tail on the webbing so it can be extended over to tie into the rusty bolts DCL as an extra backup for everyone but LAMAR. If you decide to use this feature, have the last person untie the backup before leaving so it doesn’t catch any debris during a future rain storm. R4 Warning: even with a thorough safety check and a soft start, the knot chock anchor on R4 blew out with me on rappel. SCARY feeling. Fuck knot chocks. Luckily was only 4 ft from the bottom….still caught a mouthful of pond water. Whoever runs this route next will need to rebuild that anchor. Would not recommend replacing it with another knot chalk, as the original was difficult to both access and inspect, and NONE of that rock feels solid (it’s basically all just hard, wet sand). Do not run this route without extra webbing and quicklinks. Be careful of loose falling rock!

Canyon was very muddy. There was no evidence of any previous anchors remaining in the canyon. Made very temporary anchors off of old rebar, slabs of concrete, and rock formations jutting out of the wall. Make sure to test everything before you rappel off of it since some of the rock is brittle and loose. The approach to the canyon is nonexistent and the hike back from the bottom is steep but very easy. Prior to the entrance, there is a cool long drainage tunnel to check out and if you hike half a mile up the beach the canyon dumps you out on you can see the SS Dominator shipwreck. Its an awesome canyon if you need to scratch an itch but don't want to drive all the way out to the mountains. Bring webbing and quicklinks if you go.

Took the “extended approach” by setting a car shuttle at the exit and starting the approach at the top of Agua Amarga Reserve. This adds about a ~1mile downhill hike through the upper section of non-technical canyon. It’s a nice walk if you’re looking to make a day out of the canyon. Somebody had run the canyon in the last few weeks ahead of us and left webbing on all but the last rap. There were pools at the bottom of every rappel. All pools were avoidable except for R1, which was roughly knee-thigh deep. Anchors aren’t really as described, but there are many possibilities with concrete slabs, rebar, and natural features. Note R3 as rigged from some concrete blocks DCR was too long for a 100ft rope with a CEM. Our group ran it on California Coastal Cleanup Day and helped pick up trash throughout the canyon and beach area. We chose to hike along the beach to the SS dominator shipwreck afterwards and would say it’s worth it if you’ve never seen it before. Time includes the extended approach, gearing up/down, and hiking to the shipwreck and back. Regarding the law enforcement incident in 2018 mentioned in the red tape, I researched the PV Estates Municipal code thoroughly and was not able to find anything that explicitly banned climbing, rappelling, or off trail hiking on the bluffs. You are required to obey all posted signage, but there is currently none. Much of the canyon is directly below a walking trail so it’s pretty difficult to “hide”. If you leave webbing in this canyon, please try to use a discreet color like sage, rust, or black so that it is not visible from the walking trail. All of the webbing we found was bright purple or red :( R0 is the only section that seems a bit questionable to enter. Canyon access in the dam area is fenced and the only entry is through the backside of the underground drainage tunnel. If you choose this approach, I recommend meat anchoring a hand line for R0 and helping the last person down the ~8ft dam. I would specifically not rig a rappel off the fence line to avoid creating future legal/access problems. R0 can be skipped entirely. It is possible to drop in right at the start of R1 from the North rim side near a large acacia tree in the drainage ditch.

Although there are some pretty gross algae pools that look to be about waist deep in the beginning, all of the pools can be avoided with some careful down climbing and rope-work. We started at R1 which is the toughest pool to keep out of and stay dry but overall not too challenging. Someone with good down climbing skills can rappel at the start and then stand on some good rocks just before the water RDC. After I down climbed, I set up a guided rappel to get my wife down to stay dry. I made the conservative choice to rig a 2 point anchor with webbing off the rebar and cord off a downed tree trunk to beef up R1 for the tensioned line and then cleaned up everything at the end of the trip. R2 is the next hardest pool to avoid but completely possible to walk around, just make sure not to slip on the muddy section right next to the pool. The rest of the canyon is pretty dry at the bottom but all the wraps were pretty slimy and slippery so extra care should be taken not to slip. Your ropes and shoes will also get full of mud and I certainly washed both of these right away after the canyon. This is definitely not the place to bring your brand new canyon newt. Down canyon we did see a tomato bush and colorful flowers growing which was pretty neat. The grand finale to the whole canyon was the beach exit into Lunada Bay which was a fantastic place to throw on the bathing suit and wash off any algae and canyon gunk. The beach exit and staying dry imo is what gives this canyon a passing grade. The 1 hour time includes the time in the canyon and the short hike back up (I paused the time we took for swimming which added another 1-2 hours). The anchors were pretty solid but just in case, I backed up every anchor for my wife. No anchors failed on us for the whole duration of the trip as expected. Wouldn’t recommend traveling out of your way to do this one but if you happen to be in the area it’s certainly worth a one time trip.
Very low flow, easily could be done in much much higher flow. Probable FD. Fun little creek/canyon
Fun low commitment winter canyon. Will update some of the beta soon.
Some nice moderate flow today, also not as cold as anticipated.

The first descent took us 10.5 hours. Likely a 3.5 rating canyon - more stars if the approach wasn't so difficult. The approach took us 4 hours but we realized we should have stayed on the crest of the mountain instead of navigating the very difficult terrain closer to the canyon walls. The canyon is very unique to what I've done. Almost all the rappels have curiously slanted and all have little "caves" behind then. Lots of rappels and lots of opportunities for natural anchors.
This scenic remote canyon was full of avoidable water, many down climbs, tons of bushwhacking, and did a few short rappels.

Good, cold flowing water - no significant water challenges. The trail was quite overgrown and easy to loose in just a few spots - this could be a seasonal problem. Beautiful fall colors. Others have reported difficulty with spiders in the lower canyon - we found none.

Loads of burn damage in the area, a few downed trees on the trail, evidence of major flooding in the canyon. Bolted the second rappel, rebuilt all other anchors since they had all been destroyed in the flooding. Most pools were fairly shallow, use caution if sliding or jumping. Used retrievable anchors for 3 of the drops. Canyon is still nice - again, no spiders.

High clearance required for access but under dry conditions 4x4 should not be needed. About 2h approach along a mostly well-defined trail. Lots of slide opportunities and short but tricky downclimbs. Only ended up doing 4 rappels. Many deep pools and a couple swimmers. Comfortable in a 3/2 wetsuit. Exited by following the drainage back to the car park which took about 45min and yes, there were many spiders. And yes, they wanted to cuddle.

Had a good run of it. Took us 2 hours 15 minutes for approach. Water was verrrrry low, which we figured for this time of year. We were happy to have wetsuits when in water, but definitely was overheated when hiking between water. Found some pools to chill in to bring down our temp. Would run earlier in the year for better conditions. The hike in is a slog as is the creek hiking. The fun/ ratio leaned more towards work for me on this one.
