I would run again but in tip top conditions, not worth it low. That being said, I'm am sure it's pretty rad in good conditions.

Biked up to the intersection between Marten and Taylor trails. Rainy and wet, so we moved kind of slow. Canyon was excellent, with a long bit of boulder hopping in the middle. A little slick in that boulder hopping part, but very sticky rock elsewhere. Pools were a bit shallow to jump or slide much at all, and we fiddlesticked an awful lot.
Phenomenal non-technical canyon on approach to panameta. interesting route from river camp to mouth of canyon. Look for cairns

The creek was running for the bottom 1 km. There was also nice flowing water a few hours upstream, just below the first chockstone bypass upclimb, but we didn't notice the water around the same time a year ago so it might not be reliable.

Six hours to go from Upset Rapid, up the three bottom ascents of 150-Mile, past the Ledge, hike up along the river, cross the river in packrafts, continue hiking the very hilly but well-defined trail on the south bank, eat lunch at the head of the slot, do the slot, scout the packraft launch in the rapid, and float back to Upset Rapid. Amazing slot! Not technical, very short, but stunningly beautiful. Slot water was much warmer than the river water. Worth the effort.

We crossed the Virgin River where it wasn't much more than knee deep. Cool weather and taking our time through the steepest part of the approach made the approach reasonable. Many anchors did not have webbing and we used releasable riggings, so those anchors still don't have webbing. There was a huge amount of wreckage on the approach, throughout the canyon, and on the opposite wall of the canyon, including destroyed 30mm rounds. We didn't find any intact rounds, but many still had their primers.

First off, there's pretty much no way this canyon is worth doing just to see a little bit of plane wreckage. I also would probably suggest contouring somewhere before the steeper cliffs at what I called R2 (actually my R1), where there is a side fork that can be DCed or rappeled. Appx 36.901552, -113.837017 This avoids approximately 300' of elevation gain that is the steepest and most annoying- besides the fact that you lose much of it going back down the canyon with no features. Note that this does miss seeing the debris (what little pieces I saw) scattered on the hillside on the way up. The rap off of the engine in canyon is what I'm guessing they are calling the 110' rap??? This can be done with about 75'of rope and then do a short 10' easy-moderate DC. Crossing the Virgin was from shin to knee deep depending on crossing point. Anchors looked mostly very good. I suggest starting at about 6am and most of the approach is in the shade.

Fun little canyon if you like torturous hikes and airplane wreckage. Went with a friend that works on A-10’s, so we spent about 2 hrs sorting through the wreckage. The hike up is a bear. Very steep and unrelenting. Dropping into the canyon was almost as bad. The canyon itself is very brushy. All the anchors were in good shape. Route finding was pretty easy. The canyon itself is pretty meh. If it weren’t for all the cool airplane parts it would not be worth doing.

The approach indeed require route finding, but it's not as terrible as people describe it to be. We went in with a scavenger hunt mentality and were picking up observing pieces of the air plane on approach and in canyon, and had lots of fun. All anchors were good, we didn't have to replace webbing. There was a lot of falling leaves and one member got itchy and later on ended up with hives. Still worth it.

Took a drive down to take a look at snow conditions. Drove to just under a mile from the parking lot and decided to call it as we started to do more shovelling than driving. Walked to the parking lot to get a lay of the land and was potholing ~12" in snow. Snapped some pictures and then turned around. Note: There is a fairly large hill ~.5 of a mile in that had 50/50 dirt and snow on the way in. If it was covered in snow it would be unlikely that a vehicle could get back up the hill without chains or a winch. Will hopefully return next month when snowy conditions have lessened.

Bridge crossing the Clackamas river and subsequent road is closed due to forest fire last year. We walked up the road about 1.5 miles and dropped into Memaloose. Water flow was way too high, we walked to the confluence and up S. Fork of the Clackamas but it was also running too high too. It ended up being an interesting hike.
seasonal fires have changed the landscape dramatically, however the canyon runs the same.

Approached bottom up. Crossed S Clack where the bridge used to be. Only a pipe remains. Lots of burned downed trees and sticker vines. Not a terrible approach. R1 was great!! The rest wasn't anything special. Water in memaloose is noticeably colder than S Clack. Exit back to S Clack was the same terrain as approach with downed trees and sticker vines add creek walking. Not terrible, not fun.
Hiked in from the convergence with Clackamas; crossed where the bridge used to be :( Fun raps with a fun belay from R1 to R2.

We approached via the Memaloose Bridge and walked up the paved road. Followed the map, there is a trail leading all the way down to the creek complete with hand lines. There is a new logjam at R1 but all the rest of the log jams have been washed out. I think its a mystery where all those hundreds of logs go because they aren't in the creek. I'll update the beta.

We dropped a shuttle car at the bend at Big Cliffs and then parked at Memaloose Bridge and walked up the 45 road. Took just over an hour and a half to walk up and then drop down the descent into the creek via the hand lines that are already on the trail down. The big log jam is still there above R1. It seems solid enough composed of very large logs. We threaded our way to the creek bed through the log jam from DCR. Replaced the webbing on R1. I consider this anchor marginal as it is only held onto the log by large protruding knot/broken limb. We backed it up with a meat anchor for the first two rappelers. More cautious canyoners may choose an anchor further back on the log jam, although the rope pull may be an issue then. R1 is a swimmer but you can stay mostly out of the flow until the last 10-15 feet where you will swing under the falls and into the main flow. R3 was a challenge for our team as we didn't like not being able to see around the corner as the channel turns right in this three tiered whitewater drop. The water was really moving through this narrow channel. The second tier will sweep you off your feet and spin you around at this level but if you stay in control you can scoot onto the ledge DCL of that tier and regain your feet. R3 anchor is DCL at the bottom tier on the horizontal ledge. We opted to zip-line all our packs on this one. If the water is flowing hard or you have less experienced swift water rappelers you may consider setting up a rebelay from R2 to R3 to keep from getting washed over the falls at R3. Plan on a 50-60 foot rappel line. The logs at the bottom of R4 are mostly out of play. There was one strainer log we had to negotiate but when we rappelled the main line down to the bank, then stayed on rope until we could step onto the strainer log then come off rope and drop into the pool downstream of the log and swim, we were all fine. Walked the old trail out to the Clackamas and swam across. Clackamas was running at 950cfs at the Three Lynx gauge and the swim was pretty easy in the slack water.

This short canyon packed a good punch for us. The log jam at R1 is extreme - It’s like a small city. We found a hole through it by climbing up and over and through the top on DCR, which allowed minimal interaction with the log jam. We replaced the webbing on R1, which is a log protruding out of the jam. We stayed out of the flow at first, but you’ll eventually end up in full flow toward the bottom. In retrospect, we probably should have put a wrap three on the log, but what we did worked well. The flow was pretty strong which made that rappel somewhat challenging. The R2 to R3 sequence was a little tricky and required a rebelay to R3 due to the flow level (about 60-65ft of rope). R3 is a great rappel line outside of the flow. The last rappel was pretty straightforward, it just required some awkward moving around some fallen logs. Overall a good day, but it was a bit more spicey than expected.

This was a fantastic October canyon with quite nice flow for this time of year — better than we expected! We combined Memaloose and South Fork Clackamas in 4.5 hours car-to-car. We ran Memaloose first and it took us 2 hours from the car through R3. We found a rad slide on R3. Rappel 80 or so feet to the final ledge, then get off rappel and follow the ledge to the waterfall. 20’ slide straight through the flow. :) All anchors in good condition.

Great day to run the creek. The big logjam is still holding up at R1. When that releases, the creek will be a mess because its holding lots of logs. Highly recommended to climb behind R3 and slide. Super fun!

there's a fairly easy class 5 move to get over a chockstone. the water is apparently spring-fed and was flowing with clean water when I was there. I used some mid-grade trickery to get over two pools but was stymied at the big one and it was chest-deep. time includes Ladder & Micro done at a leisurely, if not downright lackadaisical, pace. Fish creek was quite dry which made for easy walking on the sun-baked dirt.

Combined Ladder Canyon and Micro Canyon for a short but sweet day in the Superstitions. Time reflects both canyons at a leisurely but steady pace. Hiked up over the ridge to see Micro Canyon from the top down. Pretty cool little canyon, but be ready to wade through some water. I was able to avoid the first deep pool by shimmying along the wall DCR but the second big pool was unavoidable.

Combined with Ladder. I usually want more raps on a canyon route, but this plus Micro is pretty enough to be totally worth seeing. Fresh webbing at the single rap. Ascending is not really hard - feet on cliff and kinda low angle most of the way. Definitely protect your rope at the sharp edge - even alone, I was glad I did. Pools of lukewarm, clean water up to 4’ deep in Micro, which also contains a boulder obstacle that was slightly challenging but manageable alone and in wet hiking shoes (I’m not a great climber).

Did the big headwall entrance rappel and finished in the Escalante River. The canyon was completely dry except for the pothole with the arch in between the last two rappels, which was a swimmer, briefly. Most of us didn't bother putting on wetsuits for it. A deadman anchor was already present at the entrance rappel, and also at the rappel above the arch, although we used a sandtrap there instead which works great. A webbing anchor was already present on the arch for the final rappel. Road requires high clearance; the worst part is the last ~0.25 miles or so.

First pothole after big rappel is in keeper mode. Very difficult keep to get out of. No way to stand in pothole, except awkwardly at end. Very slippery and muddy to climb out of. Was able to get out using climbing grappling hook in crack on other side after 30-40 attempts. Pot shots not useful, crack is very far throw, and everything in front is downsloping and slippery into pothole. Bring your best tosser. Lower section was accomplished with sand trap. Water in first rap in lower section was deep swimmer. Good webbing at last rappel into Escalante River.
In keeper mode. Pretty dry. Pool with arch filled with sand to the edge of the arch.

Took an alternate entrance and thus bypassed R1 and the first potholes. Found the last pothole (after the sneak exit - before the final rappel to the river) in keeper mode, with the bottom of the arch that you are supposed to swim through 4' above water level - bring your creative problem solving skills!

Great day in MDH! We did the standard canyon first and breezed through it. Water was quite low and we only encountered 1 obstacle we could classify as a pothole (the arch pothole), and escape was trivial. A dead man and webbing around the arch are currently set for R2 and R3. No water in the rest of the canyon. Afterwards we played around in the pothole fork. Water was low and the first big pothole was in big keeper mode, requiring a 10-12 ft lift from ground under the water to top of rim. We didn't go down further than this. Time and rating does not include this messing around.
Swimmer at the bottom of R1 but easy to climb out. Did the full descent. Pothole with arch was pretty full and the arch was submerged. Partner assist or swimming under the arch work.

Randomly happened across this creek while looking for a campsite in the area. Came back the next day and ran the creek. Surprisingly fun with some beautiful rock formations and great flow. Somewhat akin to a mini-version of Fulton. Natural anchors are a bit sparse at several drops, so it may be wise to bring a bolt kit. Bolting would also let you run the flow on several drops, including R1 which is tight chute into a deep pool. Access is super easy -- a nice addition to creeks of the Wynoochee Valley area on the south side of the Olympics.

This canyon is a great short day. Five rapid fire rappels and jumps through a beautiful canyon. It's a shame it's so short. Placed bolts at R1 and R2. Will probably go back and develop a little further.

Parked at Three Forks near the Middle Fork ford. This is a good place to check the flow. Scattered reports suggested that the Middle Fork was largely non-technical, but given the old/vague trip reports I opted for a bottom-up approach - think I'd actually recommend going this way in any event for best views. Need to skirt around private land in the lower drainage to stay legal, but might recommend doing this anyway as sections of the Middle Fork are choked with vegetation. Staying on open higher slopes makes for easier hiking. Took about two hours to reach Guardian Pillars marking the entrance to the canyon. (Only one rattlesnake encountered en route. Watch out when hiking through tall vegetation!) Suited up and headed on in. Wetsuit was an excellent idea in May as the water is cold. Encountered a number of unavoidable waist-to-chest deep pools, although only a couple required swimming. Water levels were low enough to make progress upstream against the current and all rapids and mini-falls were easily bypassed. At the confluence with Pole Creek, explored upstream a ways until reaching a giant boulder/rockfall pile that appeared to bar further progress. Possibly ropes required... or Pole Creek would be a better descent route. Returned to the confluence and continued to junction with the next major tributary (largely dry) entering on the west side. Ahead the creek enters a cool grotto with a 15ft fall, bypassed climber's left. Continued around and S-bend in the creek and was able to exit up to a saddle, and from there up a large grassy gully leading to the rim of the mesa. This area is fantastic with spires all over the place - gaining views that you do not get down at creek level. Looped back across the mesa and down a road back to the parking area.

Total trip time ~3 hours. We found the approach hike to be very manageable, completing it in ~1 hour at a chill pace. We did 4 rappels (including the optional first rappel); the final rappel was set off a deadman anchor that required being very careful and should be checked (or use a sandtrap). There were a few avoidable pools of water throughout the canyon. Since we had two cars, we set the optional 5 min car shuttle which made the exit walk ~1 min. Be careful not to drop your rope on visitors to the canyon end!
Unbelievably beautiful canyon in Gulch area. Lot's of interesting DCs and V slots

Fast pace since this was our second canyon of the day. Approach was easy to follow (45min), descent was pretty clear as well (45min). We ended up doing 3 raps and a couple downclimbs. The first anchor was a Deadman, the second was a slung chocked rock and the third one was a Deadman with rocks on top. There was some avoidable water in pools, especially right after R2.

Fr 2466 really is very slow and steep - my Subaru Outback made it without terrible difficulty, but I'd rather not do it again. The steep sections are particularly concerning as I did occasionally spin out and have to pick out a new line on the large, loose rocks on the road. It took a little over an hour to drive the 6 miles to the trailhead. Wading to knee deep in the creek - violent thunderstorms adding some excitement. A few half-way decent campsites can be found along the way on the road for those looking to overnight in the area, but nothing great.

Time is car-to-car doing Bantam and Mini Hollow (MH) as a loop (up Bantam and down MH - this is recommended). Bantam was unremarkable, but I found MH to be interesting and enjoyed the final semi-deep narrows section. Pools of water throughout both canyons, was able to avoid with some stemming and a few full-length bridges. Still plenty of loose, crumbly rock in both canyons. Overall worthwhile for the unique slotted section in MH. 4x4 probably not necessary for the access wash unless saturated but high clearance could be helpful.

The last section under the bridge at the exit was a longish chilly swimmer, other pools in the canyon did not require getting wet, the day after the rain the creek was flowing fast, during times with rain the Apache Trail dirt road has some 8" deep crossings.
Dry and nice. Took a couple people on their first canyon and tied a practice rappel before the large drop

Took R1 bolts DCR. R2 off of bolts go into chest deep pool if rappelling through arch. Pool can be avoided by rappelling over arch instead of through, requires a little bit of climbing while on rope. Hangers on R2 could move, but bolts were solid. No required water crossings in Fish Creek.

Fun downclimbing problems before the main rap/rap sequence. Took the short R1 option RDC through a hole in the boulders. Very cool rappel. Went over the R2 arch to bypass pool. Pool below only looked about knee deep. Some standing water throughout from yesterday’s rain and minor trickle in areas. Very little water flowing through Fish Creek, stayed completely dry. Encountered one other party of three in canyon. All webbing in great shape. Ref. third photo for rockfall blocking the road.

Tons of fun downclimbing and crawling through tunnels. We did the two-stage through the rabbit-hole and then the 80 footer. One of us went through the arch and got wet, the other climbed over the arch and rapped down the other side, avoiding the water. There is a nice flat area from which to climb over the arch, it's really easier than it looks from the anchor station. Hangers were spinning on R2 but the bolts seemed solid so we went for it. Webbing at both stations was starting to get a bit crispy but should still last a while. A beautiful area!

Had a good time on this one. Would be fun with more rappels but we enjoy splitting up the one and going through the rabbit hole, then over the arch. Stopped for a bouldering session in one of the many caves.

We thoroughly enjoyed this route. Although there were only a few raps, they were unique and fun. The best part of this route was the approach. It was beautiful, engaging, and right up our alley. We could have spent hours in that outdoor play ground! We stopped for a while to do some fun boulder problems and to explore the various tunnels / down climbs. The bouldering has some serious potential and we found ourselves wishing we had a crash pad. I definitely recommend this route if you are in the area. We drove down from northern AZ on Sunday, did this route and spent the night camping near the trail head. We did Sierra the next day before heading back home. It was a great trip with great people!
Easy to avoid the pool by climbing over the arch while on rope for R2. Lots of fun downclimbing. Temps were perfect.

Lots of fun down climbing, primarily in the route finding. Fall colors in fish creek. Bushwhacking was thorny but easy enough to avoid most of it all. We did the canyon around 9am and I wish I hadn't brought my jacket for the bushwhacking. Road hiking in and out. Some people were shooting on the approach into zig zag nearby. Did the 140' rap and never saw water. Pools of clear creek water in fish creek was beautiful. Only did the one rappel.

While not a lot of bang for your buck as far as raps are concerned it's a pretty area and has some nice rabbit holes. A good winter option to take a beginner with no wetsuit required. Glad we made the trip out from Tucson. We walked the road out but there were lots of cars along the whole length of the exit, the road is now open past fish creek vista. Unfortunately this means no solitude in the creek.

No flow in the canyon; many pools but all were avoidable. Bypassed the one drop that would have been a swimmer - never got more than our feet wet. Rebuilt most of the anchors. Canyon would be nice with good flow. Easy to combine with Romero Canyon as a two day trip - we did Romero day one, camped above Romero pools (there are a few small camp sites left of the trail just past the pools) descended Montrose day two.
Swims at all raps.

Replaced webbing at R1 and R3. Canyon was initially underwhelming but quickly turned into an enjoyable and surprisingly aquatic descent with a number of jump opportunities and interesting geology. Lots of canyon frogs, and went for a pleasant swim with a giant desert centipede.

Quite an unremarkable canyon for the Catalinas, some sweet rock in sections but the pay to play ratio is a bit off for the lack of unique landscape. Still fun, but not a very aquatic descent. A slide or two will be fun, I wouln't skip those :) then again, they're short and slow, just an added bonus :) Pace could be quick 3 hours if without wetsuit. Thin wetsuit recommended in most temperatures in winter, swims are short and don't seem to warrant thicker neo.

Left rope at first rappel and retrieved after completing canyon. One wore shorty other 3mm wetsuit, both wore just wool socks, and felt great. Water temperature very pleasant. Set anchor at R3, R5, and R6. Downclimbed R4. Used fiddle stick on R7, R8, R9. Exit not very bushy to trail. Trail great to follow to road. Water flow low-moderate, never pushy.

Possible first descent? No anchors were encountered. Some very nice bedrock. Lower Langfield is more impressive than upper Langfield Falls -- sort of a smaller sibling of Little Niagara. Nice little jaunt.

Fun little canyon. I (regrettably) opted to skip the first main falls since there were tourists present, but the lower falls still made it worth a visit. I think I exited the water course a bit too early on the scramble out and never found the elk trail that is described in the write up.

Nice short little canyon. My partner and I rapped R1 several times getting practice in and out of the flow. As we are both beginners with just under ten canyons each this is a great place to build our skills and confidence on rappelling. There were several visitors at the falls and we continually asked if people wanted us to remove our rope so they could take unspoiled pictures of the waterfall but everyone we spoke with was more entertained watching us. Watch out for rockfall on R1 as the face is sedimentary rock with lots of exposed round rocks that work free very easily. The rest of the canyon was short and sweet. We rigged all rappels with retrievable anchors except the 50-foot rappel at the end. My arborists friction saver kept hanging up on pre-rig test pulls so I added a new webbing anchor on a large root DCR. The logs at the bottom were not a problem as long as rope length is correct, too much will definitely get tangled up at the bottom. There was a logging operation in the area of the original beta exit route, so we climbed straight (arduous climb) up as soon as possible DCR and then followed the canyon rim back to the top of R1.
Fun canyon, but there is a lot of walking. All the anchors were in good shape. A couple deer? skeletons can be seen while going through the canyon.
R1 has pretty red/brown/yellow on the walls, various sharp edges that require rope protection, quite a few people on the exit hike

Started at the Goldstrike TH and hiked the road to the canyon, back up to TH in 7.5 hours. Went down Goldstrike almost to the river. Constant helicopter traffic is a sore spot here, have to yell over it just to have a conversation. Notes in the beta refer to Goldstrike as a highlight of this route, I couldn't disagree more. Motorcycle has great narrows and really fun downclimbs, Goldstrike has deep canyon walls, but no real great narrows and is full of graffiti, trash and drunk people.

After meeting at a couple of spots in Vegas, we parked Jimmy's car at the new Goldstrike parking area and piled into the back of Steve's truck. We parked at the last junction leaving only a short walk to the drop-in. Rigging only one pitch at a time, the first two took some time to complete, but no one was in a rush allowing everyone to meet each other. A lengthy wash walk followed - the continuous helicopters quite annoying (maybe due to end of holidays?). The actual canyon was better than I remembered. Mira hurt her foot on the way out. This contributed to us not hiking down to the river and hot springs. Disappointing to see all the trash and graffiti in Gold Strike. Once back at the TH, Steve and Jimmy went to get the other car. I did this canyon last time I was in Vegas as part of a float trip down the Black Canyon.

did not have 4WD so hiked up for 1.5 hrs. First 2 rappels were great. lots of walking and down limbs were rough so we ended up with 7 rappels. LOT of walking for amount of rappels. will not repeat unless 2 solid 4WD vehicle.
Pretty easy canyon with an option to visit hot springs on the way back. Best season is winter
Leisurely day in Motorcycle. We walked the 1.5 miles instead of doing a car shuttle. This canyon was pretty enjoyable and was a nice contrast to it's trashed neighbor, Goldstrike.
Passed another group of 6. Jaunt to hot springs included in time.

Large group of 14 that included one first timer to rappelling and canyoneering. Experience level for the group included from beginner to intermediate and advanced. Mostly intermediate. A fun, social outing with all the seriousness of canyoneering. Total time includes a 30-45 minutes of snack break after coming out of the canyon, before starting the hike up. Did not go to the hot springs. All anchors are in good condition. All, but one, anchors were replaced by a group of 4 that started about 30 minutes ahead of us.

All anchors in great shape. Cairn anchors on some of the optional downclimbs are built to be easily inspectable. Webbing in good condition. Time reflects no shuttle vehicle, and includes a pretty lengthy soak at Gold Strike, which really made for an incredible combo.

The rappels were completed in 4 hours. We came up Telephone Canyon and took an additional 4 hours. The anchors are all in great shape. Some of the webbing must have been recent replaced with shiny new red color webbing. What used to be a downclimb or by-passable dry waterfall between R2 and R3 is now a rappel. We counted a total of 7 rappels (7 anchors = 7 rappels). Small puddles of water that were easily avoided. Coming up Telephone Canyon was nice and good, until the last part, just before getting to the top. In the map, towards the end, when making a right turn at the saddle, you are going up a class 3 ridge but then you have a class 4 (4+) traverse just below the cliff bands. Loose and steep. Long drops. Be very careful and test EVERY handhold and foothold. Those rocks become loose.

Beautiful canyon. All anchors and webbing are in great shape. We were worried about R5, but we added more rocks. We went to the hot springs around sunset. We hiked back in the dark with our headlamps. We had the hot springs to ourselves.
Took new people down here and went back up Goldstrike, R5 is still good, but may need replacing in a few months.

Great flow. Skipped R1&R2 because of crowds. Used the bypass rap on R3, however the hydraulics looked much friendlier from the bottom than they did at the top... would probably rap in the flow if I did this again. We replaced a few anchors, and they are in good shape for most raps. The confluence with the Duckabush was a very pleasant lunch spot. The hike out was steep and overgrown.
Ran the entire canyon and floated the Duckabush back to the bridge. All anchors looked good. Replaced R1 and another one down canyon.
R5: rap off the sling on the bouncy steel cable 😈. Rapped the side of the final falls to get a look, ascended back up and exited from there.

Follow the trail to the upper viewpoint with a bench. Follow use trails up behind the bench to gain the ridge. Follow the ridge up until it's relatively easy to traverse back over to the creek. If it looks steep you're in the wrong place. Large crowd of boy scouts cheered us on during the descent of the big falls. Below, creek very slippery in places. No hydraulics or dangerous water movements for us, but could be much more severe in high water. Found a much better exit route along an overgrown logging grade. Two minutes of bushwhacking, followed by an easy 5-10min walk out on the grade, then back up the road to the trailhead. Easy. Beta has been updated.

New cairn anchor had to be made. Some webbing and links replaced. New and loose wood appears to be everywhere. The exit is very bushwacky and GPS should be used as there is absolutely no trail most the way out. Flow was perfect. Check the anchor on cable at the last rap as it tears through webbing. Super fun.
Fun flow today. Hydraulics and siphons currently don’t need to be bypassed. Replaced about half of the webbing found in canyon.

Flow was low, no significant dangers or challenges posed by flow. We replaced webbing/quicklink at R4 and took out the cairn anchor that had been built nearby. Also found R6 had been moved to a tree DCL. The road grade hike out is overgrown, full of blow down and washed out in places. Would recommend the float out on the Duckabush option.

Lovely canyon. R1 and R5 are spectacular. We replaced webbing in several.places, and change the anchor in a couple of spots. Lots of logjam debris to navigate in the canyon. The strainer, siphon and hydraulics aren't of major concern in current conditions.
Fun day in Murhut. The water harzards aren't of major concern at the moment. Explored the upper section and found a few fun drops, beta has been added for those who are interested. A picture of UM_R2

Great day on Murhut, did the upper section as well and floated the duckabush as much as we could. Some strainers along the way to watch out for. Took a car all the way to the upper drop in point making it just a couple hundred feet down and you get started. Road needs a vehicle with decent clearance to get to the upper section and it's a bit grown over so make sure it's not a new car!
Fun chill day in Murhut. Upper and lower, with car shuttle

First descent on a dreary winter day where other options were limited. A few of us were testing out new drysuits (not so ideal with the all the devil's club), and we spent the day battling logjams and brush. Nevertheless, good times were had. At the bottom, we realized the keys for the lower shuttle vehicle were left in the upper shuttle vehicle. We started the 4 mile hike up the road to retrieve them, and fortunately hitched a ride in the back of a pickup truck with some locals. A day saved.
First Descent / Exploration. Had to set all anchors/webbing. Fantastic day in low 60's. Rain Jacket and swim trunks.

Today was substantially different from when the route was established. Utah's tremendous snowpack from the winter caused substantial avalanche damage to the overall environment, and the snowpack was still in place throughout the entire canyon. Anchors were torn up, we replaced anchors 1,3, and 5. Our team down climbed optimal rappel 4, and rappel 7 did not exist at the time so we walked around the complex snow problem. The flow was as high as this canyon will ever see, and the snow caves should be cautiously managed. This was as much as a canyoning excursion as it was an exercise in crevasse crossing.

Awesome day, gorgeous canyon! Make sure you’re competent with building basic anchors. Rappels 1-3 come fast. Rap 4 was bypassed and between 3-6 there was tons of avalanche debris to mitigate so slow moving. 5 dead mountain goats in canyon with other random decaying parts in the water - seems like a lot of animals try and access the water and fall to their deaths. Water flow was super beginner friendly this time of year. We were wearing 5mm wetsuits and got a little hot, but we’re thankful for them. Air temps when we started were in the 50s. Towards the end of the canyon the sun sets behind the mountains so the canyon gets darker faster and windy so they were nice to have.

Tons of cholla parts to avoid stepping on in the canyon - that was a bit of a distraction. Last time I did this hike was early on a weekday and was nice and quiet. This time we started at 9AM on a Sunday and there was quite a bit of dirt bike/atv traffic on the hike to/from the narrows. Almost no water at all.

Canyon was dry but semi-interesting and easily/quickly accessed from the Needle Rock parking area. These narrows should be classified as a 1A in my opinion as there isn't much in terms of technicality. Be very cautious of those in side-by-sides or dirt bikes during the approach. Plenty of opportunity to explore around the Verde afterwards or even turn this canyon into a longer loop hike. This section of the river has lots of pretty areas, and you can escape the crowds by heading further upstream.

Did not realize this was a listed route, we did the google earth gamble and found this little gem. We drove up the wash in a 4Runner and parked at the moth of the canyon. Met clear cool flowing water (small trickle) about half way up the canyon. Practiced stemming in the narrows. Not knowing what was here we were amazed by this cool little hike, worth doing if in the area!!

Fun morning exploring this little canyon with a beautiful view out to the Deschutes. It's so close to Maupin and seems to get some traffic based on what we found. You can approach easily by going left down the road until you pass the cattle guard then heard up and left. Go under the first rock outcrop then above all others. A trail is forming across the slope. The anchor at R1 is now a 2 inline bolt anchor. You can stay on rappel to get to the anchor for R2 but skipping that anchor and going straight down keeps you in the water course. This gives you some splashy fun in the winter and a cleaner line to avoid rock fall. All anchors were in good shape, though we did change the configuration of the quick links on R1 to make the pull easier. Beware of rockfall on R4. Everyone should be back almost to the road as we both kicked down a few rocks. I listed the flow as very low since it was little more than a trickle but for this canyon I think it was "ripping"!
Slight drizzle down first big drop, shallow standing pool between last two drops. Spent extra time installing additional anchors.

Descended late in the evening to avoid the heat and exited by moonlight. We were able to avoid the deep pools and only got wet below the knees. Keep an eye on bolts as some are loose. Although seemingly undocumented until now, this canyon has certainly seen quite a bit of traffic.

Some of the bolts are placed flat such that pulls are difficult due to friction. Especially R2. I actually had to ascend this rap to set the block on the other side to allow pulling this. I could have scrambled back up DCL (though everything is mega-chossy here) but was more fun to ascend. Pools were full, fairly fresh, and refreshingly cool.

A rugged and abrasive canyon in all senses of the word, with sharp and chossy rocks and no shortage of thorny bushes. Named Nikon Draw as I discovered R1 one day while taking landscape portraits with my Nikon, and then Nick and I returned later to do a descent. Not a true FD as we discovered bolts that were already placed before we ventured in, but the route was not yet published. All pools 80% full today, and water is fairly clean. All water is avoidable by creative stemming while rappelling, even with full pools. As the previous condition report noted, the rock is extremely abrasive and pull angles are often less than desirable, especially R2. Recommend releasable rigging such as a CEM to avoid damage to gear and challenging pull on R2, it worked beautifully. A nice downclimb completes the technical section of the canyon. Watch out for the downed wire about 100' from the exit coming off the telephone pole strung knee-height across the trail, it's a trip hazard especially at night.
Class C conditions. Amazing conditions. A couple swimmers. One member went without wetsuit and was cold, others had 5mm wetsuit and were perfect. Air temp was low 50s.

There were some pools full of algae that were all completely avoidable. It just short enough to be bearable this time of year with a very hot hike out and a shuttle back to the start. There were no issues with any rope pulls but we were careful setting the rappels for easier pulls. As per previous trip reports, a rope could easily get stuck, especially at R2.

Really neat little canyon with 5 bolted anchors and a single bolt for a downclimb st the end. Last deep pool was chest deep while the others encountered were waist or lower. Last pool can be easily bypassed RDC. A skilled downclimber could bypass a few rappels. Big bang for the buck.

First time, and I’ll be back. The short, easy-start, bolted raps, terrific country, and easy approach and exit make this a great place to intro visitors to canyoneering. Multiple pools, but we managed to get wet only to ankles by some minor maneuvering. Just right for a half-day, and would pair well with Loincloth if people have fun here and want a taller rap.

Best day ever in a beautiful canyon! Team of 4, advanced, intermediate, 8yr & 5yr. Most rappels into water are shaded so little to no alage. More water than a few weeks ago but temp warming up so alage in sun water. Most can be avoided. Special challenge to decide to use existing bolts or build own anchors. Be prepared to build natural anchors because bolts are loose, spin and in bad rock. Slow starts, no shock loading anchors if using bolts and backing up. Last rappel into water was a swimmer for first half. Possible to climb up and around DCR but steep and caution needed for kids. Overall great beginner canyon for kids that have required training and with an advanced leader. Quick approach. 3hr 30 min descent with a 8yr and 5yr. 30 min exit from canyon bottom. 4 hr car to car. 1 hr break at canyon bottom to enjoy the massive cliff walls, things swimming in the water and things in dried up beds that once swam. Had canyon all to ourselves. At exit one up to vehicle so all don't have to walk narrow road up. We've done before, did today and will be back!

Fun little canyon close to home, bolts are sketchy and in crappy places, if anyone wants to help me replace or add new ones let me know. All pools were avoidable. Once down and in main canyon walk down to the arm of canyon lake, makes a cool side adventure

Totally dry today. Fixed one strangely tied anchor, everything else was in good shape. As someone else previously mentioned, don't love the state of the bolts in this canyon. Use your best judgment on what you're comfortable with.

Time includes setting a fixed rope at Tufftatella, backtracking and running Nikon Draw, and then ascending out of Tufftatella at a fairly speedy pace. I'd recommend 2-3 pieces of rope protection for the fixed line on Tufftatella.

Good canyon for a 7yo boy's first trip. Agree with others that many of the shiny bolts are loose. It's not just hangars that spin. Nevertheless there are plenty of natural anchor opportunities. Time involved top roping a couple of people. Never more than calf deep water, but be warned, my shoes smelled terrible for the car ride home!

Very fun, fast canyon that we did to break up a road trip from NM to CA. The raps and all short but fun and the canyon views are beautiful. There were a few standing pools, the last of which (which would have been R5 off a single bolt) we avoided by walking around DCR as we were not spiritually ready to get up to our chests in water. The bolt issues others have noted are real. The bolts are not generally set up to be equalized here, and at least one on R3 was very loose (we used an anchor on a pinch between to rocks there). R1 had an easy pull if you block on the outside. R2 was the most problematic -- we stuck a rope due to the anchor geometry and friction on the rock, and climbed back up DCR. This one is probably best done with a fiddlestick or off a single bolt (for the second go, we double stranded off both bolts, but the pull was still hard). None of the other pulls were particularly challenging.

Great canyon for beginners. We casually worked our way through the canyon. After recent rain, pools of water were mostly calf to knee deep. For fun, we set up a guided rappel so that most could avoid a pool, which made for lots of laughs. Friendly exit. Great morning in the canyon.

Almost all bolts are spinners. Intentional rigging is required in a few places to counteract poor bolt placements and avoid rope retrieval issues. Tightened up a couple of the loose but non-spinner bolts and adjusted some of the webbing. Avoided all deep pools with some acrobatics.

Halloween freeze-fest! No anchors were found, so likely a first descent. Another nice short destination in the Wynoochee Valley with some beautiful pools and neat geology. Water levels were a bit high (and cold! air temp 34-degrees at the bridge) -- we suspect it was running in flood stage, so ended out rappelling along the edge or out of the flow on most of the drops. A couple of the drops can probably be jumped. Some strong current present in places and a few careful downclimbs. Anchors are a bit thin on the final drop... we'll be back to improve the anchor situation.

10 hours car to car exiting the 19 Mile Route. North Canyon is beautiful once you're down into the Supai. The most stunning part is the area near the pool and the chute accessible easily from the river. The single rap involves a steep and slippery down climb into a shallow pool (possible to keep your feet dry if you don't slip). Retreat from this point would be a challenge without a hand line but not impossible. The pool after the chute was the most amazing color green and roughly waist deep. We waded across with no cold water protection. We hiked at river level to the 19 Mile Route at low water and were able to stay dry to whole way. The exit is very loose and it's important people are conscious of being out of the fall zone as people above them advance upwards. There was some minor route finding required to get through the Coconino. A great route but the highlights can be easily seen as a short, non-technical hike on a river trip.

It was clearly Fall when we decided to run this canyon as the temperatures were colder and leaves were turning/falling. While the last waterfall is the main attraction, the gorge above Toketee is very beautiful. This river is dam controlled and the gauges were running at 87 CFS. All hydraulic at the bottom can be avoided but the force of the water going from obstacle to obstacle is surprisingly strong and fast! All rappels are outside of the flow and canyoneers should be comfortable swimming in high current. If you plan to enter after the rainy season, be prepared to bring a bolt gun.

Beta is mostly accurate. Saw no anchor for R1. Gave meat for my partner and jumped the center in the boil. Make sure to check depth and clear rock right of center. Appears a dead tree was used for R3. This has fallen and is no longer usable. We put in an anchor on a rock pinch left of center that will not be permanent. Would be advisable to just put in a bolt back around the left corner out of sight. This puts the rap on the easier/less white water left side- can jump the last 5-8' if you want to completely clear the whitewater. Bolt anchors in good condition for last raps. DC before R3 is very doable but delicate hugging the left corner. Exposure is minimal but consequences could still be high. Don't mess it up.

USGS put the flow at 96 cfs which i would say is the highest this canyon can be run. Any higher and you wouldn't be able to get out of the pool above the final repel before being swept over. Pretty sketh at 96. Not much to add from the other reports. The 3rd repel is off of a dead man's anchor on the right which drops you onto a ledge above the big hydraulic. From there you can pull the rope and jump past the recirculating white water. It would be nice to have a bolt on the left which is the better side. The nut holding the bolt hanger for the final repel had to be tightened as it was almost completely unthreaded. Very beautiful canyon with really good access.

After a long approach hike in (that we think was correct given the limited beta), we were surprised to find "not water" canyon full of water! Who would have guessed! Bee wary of yellow jackets, one member of the group was stung on the way in. The bottom of the first rap was full of slippery mud. The pothole mentioned in the beta was fine to escape using a partner assist and rope and careful spotting of the last person doing the ~12 foot hang drop. The second rap had a waist deep pool of water at the bottom. The first two people got wet rappelling, but we were able to do a modified guided rappel off a meet anchor to keep everyone else dry. There were then a series of downclimbs, some ending in pools that were knee or less deep. In the second section, we quickly encountered a difficult pothole escape after the first second-section rappel. A short downclimb/rap led to a chest deep pool with a impossibly slippery climb out. We made an attempt to use our potshots to get past this section, but it required a difficult ~40-50 ft throw that we had trouble with. The crack to throw the potshot looked like a great spot for the potshot to catch, but alas, it wasn't, and one was not enough to stick it. Eventually, we got one potshot over and sent someone back into the pool, but there was not enough stick from the potshot to work. Luckily, we hadn't pulled the rope from the first rap in the second section, so after an hour+ of struggling to get the potshot toss (and two people who had gotten chest deep in water with no wetsuit), we decided to punt the rest of the canyon and make an escape. By the time we got back up to where we had made the entrance hike, it was about 5pm and the sun was setting. This story is a great example of why everyone in the group should know how to ascend if needed! We reversed our entrance hike in the dark, following our GPS track. It was below freezing weather (but luckily there was no wind and we had enough warm layers to be comfortable, even with the group members who had gotten wet). We used two rappels to retrace our entrance hike climbs. The first was to get off of the top of the mesa. This had a tricky overhanging start, and one of the members of our group got a hand caught under the rope and pinched. The second exit hike rappel was to descend the first drainage we had initially climbed up the wall of to get up vertically. At this rappel, we noticed that our rope was frozen. Yikes! This second rap brought us back to the bottom of the side drainage off of the lower gulch trail. We followed this back about 2 miles (only bonking one helmet off a fallen tree hard enough to be knock to the ground) and arrived back at the car near 9pm to find it was ~27F with our shoelaces frozen. Not the aventure we were expecting, but we were safe and not bivvying in the canyon!
Water flow was perfect and not too cold; replaced a few anchors.

Water cold, wore 5mm wetsuit and rain jacket. Glad to have it. Replaced webbing on 3rd anchor with new webbing. Rope got stuck on 4th, last rap, due to webbing not being extended around rock corner and not keeping lines separated. Probably could have done canyon in 5 hours, if rope did not get stuck.

Water cold, 5mm wetsuit and rain jacket good. First rap stomach deep pool. Second rap swimmer. Slide swimmer. Third and four rap thigh deep. Extended webbing well past corner. Got rappel side stuck in crack LDC was able to get it out climbing up ledges RDC and flaking rope out. Last rappel loves rope.

Flow was perfect. Cold water, but comfortable in a 5mm wetsuit, rain jacket, 3mm neoprene socks, and some layers. Partner wore a 7mm and was warm. Some ice forming throughout the canyon, particularly at the base of the final rappel. Replaced all anchors. Took the creek/rock gully approach (Approach 2). Highly recommend - about 1.5h from parking to the start of the canyon, 1.5h in canyon. Was on track to finish route in under 5 hours, but stuck our rope on the final rap. Had rigged high off a tree, and was very purposeful managing ropes, but webbing was not extended far enough around corners and the friction was considerable. Took hours to free our ropes after attempting multiple strategies and finally having success with a 3:1 MA system rigged off a tree high RDC. Recommend extending webbing beyond the first major corner, though this may make it challenging to safely get on rappel in high flow conditions. Hiked out along the creek in the dark by headlamp. Despite these challenges, it was a very fun and beautiful canyon. Forest road was wet but manageable with high clearance and 4x4.
All anchors looks good. Did in Creek approach. Road to canyon is getting pretty rutted out.

We spent some time (approximately 1 hour) on route maintenance. All anchors looked great. Pull on the last rappel was very smoothe. Great 4 to 5 hour route with flow. The road in is muddy but a 4x4 shouldn't have any issues.

The road in was very muddy on the way in. 4x4 did fine, although I blew a shock on my way out. Flow was very high. The traverse bolt and bolted anchor station made for fun rigging and an easy pull. We opted to rap out of the flow and were happy with our decision. Stood at the falls on the bottom in the flow and could barely hold stance for longer than 3 seconds. Added a single loop anchor on a pinch rdc for the downclimb, it was short but flow was high and rock was slick. One of the best in the area with good flow, but can be challenging to access. We hiked up the watercourse and the scree gully.
out and back from river. Sketchy upclimb to enter canyon: watch for rockfall. Amazing from there on out. Water levels low but high potential of getting in pools without excellent climbing skills

All anchors had fresh webbing. Our 100' rope came up a little bit short on the first drop, but was close enough that we reached the ground including rope stretch. The middle of Olo is lovely, had flowing water, and would make a great camp spot if you can make the timing work out. The pools were waist deep and brief enough to make insulation unnecessary. The listed time is our transit time from Matkat back to the river.

All anchors in great condition. Pools were small or avoidable, with most being about knee deep. In the trip we did 150, Matkat, Panameta, and Olo in a 4 day trip, each day quite long except the day hiking out where we finished around 3pm. Brought 130' webbing and 18 rap rings. Ended up only needing to build one anchor at the first drop into Olo, using 30' of webbing. Everything else was good to go, though I expect that can change pretty quickly.

Scouted westwards along the rim and, after some serarching, found a good place to rappel down onto the giant rockpile west of "The Deep." Explored a bit westward, then backtracked and set an anchor to rappel into the bottom of "The Deep" which is super-dramatic. Scrambled out the eastern side through two small cave-like space. Even on a dry day, the bottom of the Hellhole was muddy & slippery. Found a memorial to a caver who died here in 2013. Signs of erosion everywhere; much of the terrain is not stable. Explored a bit eastward along the rift and eventually found a place to climb back out. This was not ideal, however, as the terrain was slippery and the rock quality was terrible (e.g. excavating footholds to get out). Once one person had escaped, it was easy to drop a rope to the rest of the team. Setting a rim rope ahead of time would have been a far better way to go. (There may be a better exit if one bushwhacks east along the foot of the wall to the far end, but we didn't explore fully in that direction. There definitely is a faint trail heading that way atop the rim, however.) Sage word of advice in the Hellhole: expect everything to be loose, uneven and slippery. Don't take unnecessary risks!

Quick, easy approach. Light trickle over R1 which is mostly free hanging. Watch for loose rock on R2. Smooth rope pulls. Cleared some brush (mainly catclaw) from the drainage. Exit is typical for the area. Lots of scree / loose rock. At the saddle, picked up game trails leading up to the old road. Webbing new. No signs of prior descent. Ran Griffin Canyon afterwards. 6 hours total for both canyons, including the drive along FR 189.
2 very beautiful very awesome semi free hanging rappels! Had a bit of a trickle gave us a shower on the way down. Quick and easy canyon. Do Griffin afterwards for a full day!

Outlaw needs more technical obstacles to make the pay to play worth it. The canyon action is rather minimal and the unremarkable canyon walking is lengthy. Inlaw or Tiger Wall would be better canyons if spending the effort to cross the Yampa. Conditions were fine, but a quality of 1 star seemed appropriate.

Ran the canyon in September 2024, weather conditions were perfect, the canyon was spectacular but not really technical. We did it instead of Scroggins due to time. The one Rap is gorgeous and the part of the canyon before is like a jungle canyon. Very green and untouched! Would love to go back when I try the other in the area. It’s just such a challenge to get to, but it may be worth it since it’s so remote.

We got Backcountry Weekend permits, and so did the approach from Brem horse camp, which was very reasonable. After a fairly dry spring the water was a trickle, but still enough to keep the potholes filled. Swimming was pleasant for a hot spring day.

After a rainy winter, the bolt at R1 was mostly pulled out and R2 was missing its hanger. We ran out of daylight and had to bail, and so didn't get to check out R3. We recommend the next group sets new bolts higher above the flow.

by: Ameister, hillbelfi, rodtel314, jennj, boof, epeairs, sqshemet
comment: Another wonderful backcountry weekend in Pacheco Falls! Time includes an entry from Kaiser Aetna road past Dowdy Ranch and two runs of the technical section by our whole group (a final third descent by a portion of our group is not recorded). Water was actually flowing and pools were much less grimy compared to the same weekend last year, making it really not a bad canyon! We replaced damaged bolts in positions higher above the watercourse.

Backcountry weekend this year was a cold, drizzly day! Comfy in a 4/3 with air temps in the 50s. Webbing degraded on R3 and replaced with a hopefully more permanent anchor. R2 definitely out of the flow, but looks like it may be difficult to access in very high flow. Nice, easy day. Hiking the peaks nearby was nice in the colder temps, but not exactly swimming hole weather.

Another wonderful backcountry weekend at Pacheco Falls. Light rain and weather in the 50s made full wetsuits good to have for most of our group. All anchors in good condition, no rebolting required after a mild winter. Adding to the canyon beta so I remember how to lead beginner trips here more effectively.
Checking out a new prospect. Decent creek, good for beginners. Would be fun with more flow. Probably better to hit this one in April or May for more water.
Descended Painted Canyon - Carleton to the Owyhee Reservoir as part of a backpack trip, returning via Honeycombs Canyon. Easy scrambling, beautiful scenery. Non-technical. More hike than canyon.

A bit of mild splashing on some rappels. Pools were never very cold and got downright comfy by the end, especially with all the sun. Replaced webbing on the first drop and cut out some redundant stuff on a couple of the others. Erek will probs write up some beta for the wiki. Big exit with the road walk at the end.

Scouting mission to update anchor stations for better rappel lines during swift water descents. Also planning on adding a few more to make the canyon more enjoyable and less destructive (example would be the last rappel 2 stage, when parties rappel over the boulder they swing into this cool cactus, which is almost dead now. The updated anchors will provide better rope pulls and better rappel lines for beginners and to more advanced swift water canyoneers alike. The now dead tree will also be removed which has caused rope entanglement issues in the past

Great productive trip! Several new anchors were added to enhance this amazing canyons potential the new anchors will help prevent hard pulls, help with rescue maneuvers if needed, and to prevent rope abrasion. Rappel number one is no longer 25 feet back and provides a great rappel line. Rappel 7 is another favorite update, located beside the large Boulder instead of the pinch point. This new 2 bolt station provides an amazing new line and buttery smoothy rope retrieval. Rappel 8 is from 1 bolt high DCR. My hopes were to make the angle highe in hopes of making the rub point over the boulder less severe and to prevent the swing into the cactus below (its almost dead) i will be returning to make rappel 8 even better in the following months. All anchors has been replaced with tan webbing and will hopefully be good until next July, with the exception of black webbing on rap 4 which was replaced last month. The dead tree was also removed to prevent rope entanglements. I hope people enjoy the new anchors. Stay safe out there! TJR

An incredibly fun descent down multiple pristine waterfalls one after another and lovely swims after each one! We left our vehicle at Sabino TH and our friend graciously offered to drive us up to the top! The hike in is long but fairly pleasant. About 5.5 miles, all on an obvious trail before turning off and a short bushwack down gully, KML is on point! The flow was perfect! We stopped to chill in one of the pools half way down for about an hour. Most pools are deep swimmers. I wore a 4/3 wetsuit and was plenty comfortable. This canyon flashes frequently this time of year and the webbing takes a beating, we could see evidence of this; extra webbing is worth carrying. The hike out is a long one but no terrible elevation gains. We got to the paved portion of Sabino Canyon Trail a little after 7pm. The Sabino Canyon shuttle had already stopped running at this point so we hiked the paved trail all the way back. Roughly 6 miles total for exit. Not sure when this shuttle stops running (I believe it varies by time of year) but one could save the last 3 miles of pavement hiking if you can snag it! We absolutely loved this trip. A wonderful canyon with perfect conditions! My SOG folding knife was left at either the gear up spot at start of descent or on the exit where we geared down. If it is found, please contact me, I would love to get it back!

Absolute Class C Excellence!! Hard pack snow on alot of the upper approach trail. Flow was lovely! Wore 4/3, wool base, and rain jacket. Hands were the only thing cold despite having 2mm gloves! We decided to change into dry clothes after the last rap and try to avoid water on the exit. This proved to be an annoying time suck. Would recommend leaving booties, wet pants or wetsuit on until arriving at the actual exit trail much further down the creek. We missed the tram and hiked the road back to parking lot (which takes 1.5hrs from TH, FYI) Approach and exit are much longer then you’d think! Plan for a long day!!

Started at 6:30 am at palisade trailhead. Entered the canyon at 9:30. Deep swimmers on almost every rappel. 2 people in the group had 7 mil wetsuits and 2 had dry suits. Water definitely woke you up. Good anchors and webbing at all rappels. Got out of the canyon and onto the trail at 5:00 pm. Back to the car at Gordon Hirabayashi Campground by 8:30 pm. Great day and highly recommend if your willing to pay the price the canyon requires.

Absolutely gorgeous day, Started in ice and snow in the high alpine, and ended with a warm/sunny desert exit, right by the Sabino riperian zone. Started at 7 AM, reached the drop-in at 9, Technical canyoning from 9:30-12:30, and chill exit hike. 9 hours car-to-car at a very leisurely pace, including the walk from the shuttle area, several lunch / snack breaks, and some anchor building. Water was icy-cold, and glad I had my full PNW setup. Left my Vlad at R1, so please look out for it =D. Replaced webbing/quicklink anchors at R4, R7, R8. Replaced incredibly sun-bleached / disintegrating webbing at R7 with 2 sets of stainless steel bolts. I realize that this method is very PNW style, so I won't be offended if webbing is added again XD Replaced R8 webbing with new y-hang anchor. Could be a 5-star canyon if it were multi-pitched-out for flow, and wet lines added. R3 anchor would be better as a traverse-line to an exposed anchor on the face of the wall. (can't easily hear whistle signals from the current anchor position, so I extended the anchor for the first person down. Annoying pull given the amount of friction on the rope.)

Skipped R5, wasn't even aware of a rappel point. R7 turned out to be much larger for us, probably 185'. I'm guessing, looking back at photos, the rappel could be shorter stopping at a higher ledge and downclimbing, though not feasible in higher flows. Either way, I'd bring the potential for a 200' rap. R8 is a traverse line single bolt to double bolt station. Long day, very fun but the exit was exhausting. Passed the AZT and hiked uphill from the drainage to reach the trail. Overall fun day, great canyon for the area, exit and approach typical of a catalina canyon.

Started at 815am at boyscount camp since road was closed seasonally Group was leisurely slow didn't stop for lunch etc Missed the shuttle by a long shot Car to car point A to B was 12hrs (17 miles) Then had to drive back up to retrieve car A at top 1 intermediate/advanced, 2 beginners Thermal protection: Person A thermals 5mm full rain suit - got slightly hypothermic but was able to warm up in sun after short time Person B 3/2 and 2/3 rain suit cold but managed Person C Thermals and dry suit .. very comfortable spent the most time in water to let other team mates warm up Flow was mild but worth it Removed old webbing R6 RDC bolts REPLACED webbing R6 LDC bolts Didn't expect all the boulder hopping after R6 to R7 which made us think rap/ anchor count was wrong and beta was written weird.. it's now fixed
Amazing canyon with cold swimmers and long approach from the river. Donkeys on the mesa!

All anchors in great condition. Several cold swims - we had 3/2 and 4/3 wetsuits, and were slightly chilly but mostly fine. Absolutely stunning canyon, and easier than expected based on the description in Grand Canyoneering. In the trip, we did 150, Matkat, Panameta, and Olo in a 4 day trip, each day quite long except the day hiking out where we finished around 3pm. Brought 130' webbing and 18 rap rings. Ended up only needing to build one anchor at the first drop into Olo, using 30' of webbing. Everything else was good to go, though I expect that can change pretty quickly.

A beautiful canyon with chilly pools (wish I had more than a thin shorty for running this on our cold snap day) and a spectacular approach via 150 mile+packraft+Matkatamebia. On our first day up Matkat, we intended to camp at the drop in for Panameta but were turned away at the giant chockstone in the zebra-striped rock layer. Grand Canyoneering suggests a partner assist here, but you can see in the photo below, floods have scored out the entire floor down to a deep rocky hole. It is now a scary ~5.8 or 5.9 free solo. We explored the nearby side canyon and found only a 60' dryfall and scorpions. The next day, unable to gain the bench, we returned for another look at the area. With a group member fool-hearty enough to try the move, we hoisted packs with a sort of reverse guided rappel around the crack and were on our way to into a city of skinny wild Burros! Unfortunately the Burros have decimated the ecosystem, plowing over all the plant life (and their funny calls though the night make it difficult to sleep), but make trails that rival official NPS maintained routes. My impression is they seem to be doing too well for themselves in a sort of sad Malthusian sense. The slide sequence at the begining was great fun! I've never played in such aquatic limestone before! Our rope was briefly stuck on the 3 pool sequence but pulled easily with a 3-to-1 system anchored off the bolt for the next rap (you may need > 100ft to pull this off). We felt compelled to back up one of the pinch points with a cairn and move the rock chock on the final rappel into a better crack. Took us 10 hours from the giant chockstone back to the Colorado, on a 4 day trip.

Just wanted to say that this is probably the coolest waterfall I have ever seen. Even though it isn't a technical canyon (well it is, just don't do it). I cannot stress how awesome this place it. It is a must do in the area!

Possible first descent of Panther as we found no other anchors or signs of previous visitors. The walk down the upper creek to Waterfall Alley is a bit long, would be nice if a shortcut could be found to drop in early. (It looks feasible, but that approach is not explored yet.) The upper series of waterfalls particularly nice and the creek stays pretty interesting all the way down to the end. We visited after a week or so of rainy weather in Oregon and flows on the creek were surprisingly high for June. This creek doesn't have that large of a watershed, so the other thought its that perhaps its spring-fed. Caution was required, particularly in the narrow areas on the creek, as the flow would suddenly get pushy on you. Several waterfalls were kicking off big rooster-tails of water. Altogether, a very pleasant descent. A keeper.

Had a fun day in the canyon. It was a hot day (close to 100 degrees in the valley) and we didn't need wet-suits. We used some rain jackets in the lower parts of the canyon, but we were very comfortable. One of our cars was a 2wd sedan and it was able to make the drive to the top.

Second visit to Panther. Water levels were about half of what they were in June 2020. New bolt station for the big rappel improves the anchor situation. We were able to downclimb much of the "trench" section in the lower creek; last time we bypassed per high water. An excellent early season creek in the Green Peter area!

Tried the bushwhack "shortcut" route in to see if it was any better. Steep in places, lots of detrius and slash from old clearcut operation made it harder. Took about an hour, so unconvinced it's an easier way in. Looks like Panther got hit hard by winter storms -- quite a bit of new woody debris and logs in the creek that weren't there last time. There's a new landslide near the bottom, where the creek vanishes briefly from view under the rocks. Other than that, a lovely day out. Lots of wildflowers. All anchors in decent shape.

I wanted to see this canyon before it starts to get more water in the fall. It was a trickle this time of year.We shuttled cars the gate was open and the road was in decent condition. The creek walk was long but wasn't hard, I don't think ill attempt the bush wack. We couldn't find the pinch for R2 we used a meat anchor. Anchors seemed to be in good shape!

Ran this with Matt and just wanted to add a couple of observations. Beta had not been updated before we entered the creek. New bolt station at R1 (2 unlinked glue ins center of creek well back from the edge) has now been updated. Use caution on this pull as your block will be sliding down the creek bed and ours got jammed on a crack or lip. Luckily this drop can be down climbed or unclimbed with little risk. R2-R3 beta confusing. There was a drop after R1 of about 15-20 feet that had no anchor (there was a giant boulder DCL, but we didn't see and anchor pinch) that we meat anchored. Then there is a drop with bolts DCL directly above R3 big drop that is easily down climbable DCR. Didn't know which drop was R2. Then there is the big drop at R3. From the two-bolt station DCL above the big drop it is only ~130'. We understood that to be the 180' drop from the beta and used our 300' rope with a pull cord extension. When I flaked out 180' there was a huge pile of excess rope at the bottom (not visible from the top) and the remaining 120 of the tail just about reached the bottom. Was the 180' in the beta from the drop just above the big drop? Why? Maybe in high flow that might need a rebelay instead of a down climb. The pull was very hard. Even with the pull cord extended we had to put three people pulling from quite a way downstream and have one person keep flipping the rappel line to reduce the friction. This anchor might need some work or Transient Releasable Rigging to recover rope. For R8 there are bolts now DCR. We had plenty of rope with a 50' rope for the rappel and pull. If you add bolts and change anchors to a canyon, please update the Beta.

3 Days from rim to rim. Starting from the beach of Papago Creek after descending through 75 Mile Creek the day before, the plan was to do East Fork but we had a strong team of fast hikers and it was quite early by time we reached the top of the East Fork so we opted to continue on to the West Fork. Some pretty rappels and a few narrow sections. We still had daylight left by time we approached camp so opted to explore the lower narrows. Most was down climbable but had to build a few deadman anchors to complete it to the beach leaving ropes fixed. We then ascended back up to remove our webbing and then hiked around back to the beach. We hiked out New Hance the 3rd day.

Approached from New Hance trail. Took us quite long to cross the ledge to access canyon start, 2nd member in group was not as comfortable with exposure. 3 hours to descend the canyon itself, including replacing webbing on several raps and building a couple Deadman's. Exited in the dark, not recommended as we missed the cairns to avoid the lower narrows and climbed out. Definitely recommend bringing webbing, we completed 10 raps in the canyon
Getting in and out is a bear, but a beautiful section of narrows. Nice to see, but not sure how often I'll be back.

Great creek. Creek walked up to the features on a very easy to walk creek bed. Quite possibly a rock hounds paradise. Large landslide up creek right just beyond the confluence of Proverbial. Scoped out the wide drops on the lower end of Proverbial. Neat but nothing worth running. Local have hand lines set up due to the rocks quality. Once around the landslide we continued up and around to the drop in. Very tight and fun creek. The big drop could be fun with some more water.
Took the creek route as it is mostly mellow and by the time the other tour is available via the gate opening, the creek wouldn’t be at a fun flow. This place is truly special.

Walked up creek after running Koda. Climbed up canyon left just before the base of R7 and then traversed to drop in above R1. The steep section is pretty open, the drop in is pretty brushy. Some of us did R1 as a slide, as always check the pool first. Rebuilt R3 cairn anchor that was gone. R4 single bolt is in a really bad spot for the pull. Rope slides across sharp edges on the wall and flicking the rope to release damages the rope. Climbed up and pulled from halfway, then downclimbed and shallow jumped into the pool. Recommend last person does the same or try adding another QL to the anchor which might help. Lower section with high walls is really pretty. R6 anchor high on tree DCL should probably be relocated and I wouldn't use it again. I recommend using a tree a little further back DCL which puts the rappeler in the flow, avoids damaging the bank, and improves the pull. Did the creek walk exit down to Wind River Road, it's long with lots of logs to crawl over/under. Was about a 10 hour day car to car combined with Koda. Watch your step, we came a cross a Pacific Giant Salamander, and a couple dozen frogs. The landslide spot is actually really cool, pretty easy going DCL. A couple hundred feet before the road there is a trail that goes off DCL, take it to a campsite and an easy road walk the rest of the way to Wind River Road...could park here instead of the turn out by Paradise Creek Campground.

Replaced webbing on 3rd rappel (2nd rap of two stage big drop) and on last rappel. Webbing on all other rappels looked good. No rope pull issues. Good water flow, but not overwhelming. Water was cold, in shade most of canyon, full wetsuit was nice to have. Couple anchors could have been better placed to stay in main water course. Did big rappel in two stages, staying in water flow for 2nd stage. Used piton in rock for 4th rappel directly after big rappel, instead of rappelling 2nd stage out of water flow and bypassing piton rappel (which looks like is more common approach to doing it). Did a couple jumps in lower part of canyon. Overall fun canyon.

Great water flow level. At least 4 pools to jump, all deep, max about 20'. One fun slide towards end. Did 7 rappels and did rappel #2 as two stage. All anchors looked good, replaced webbing on last rappel off of boulder pinch, RDC. Water temperature was great with 5mm wetsuit and rain jacket.

For some reason we were expecting a C canyon but there was not a slightest trickle, only few pools, chest deep at the most that could've been waded. Bringing and putting on the wetsuits turned out to be completely unnecessary. We changed webbings on all anchors. Last rap we downclimbed ( both down the watercourse and LDC ) and found washed webbing down canyon ( most likely from that rap )

A GOOD CANYON, Great for the Mazatals region! We waited until the first 80 degree day of sunshine in March and received low but fun flow. The approach was a bit exposed to the elements with little shade up a steep switchback trail through blooming wildflowers. We took a lot of breaks until we reached the saddle to head down. Once at the start of the canyon we filtered some water and had lunch. Pants and bushwhacking layers are beneficial once in the approach drainage. Waterflow was low and borderline B/C class, but very pleasant for us. Anchor LDC on multistage rappel ledge looked great. Would recommend setting the pull cord LDC to make the pull from this anchor more smoothe. One of us wore a Rain jacket and felt great, The other wore nothing for cold water protection and felt fine as well. We were glad we did not pack wet suits. Once we were finished with the technical section we geared down and realized we had about 1/3 of the total route left to boulder hop, downclimb, jump, and slide pools before leaving the drainage. Good canyon with some unique sections and easy but fun challenges. Would repeat with more flow for sure. Saw our first rattlesnake of the year while downclimbing on the exit.

A LOT of effort for a short section of technical canyon. The R2-R4 sequence is pretty, but it’s over quick. Unlikely to repeat this one and IMO nearby Oak Spring, Shake Tree, Barhardt, and Big Kahuna are all better bang for your buck. Lots of wildlife (hawks, owls, frogs, coatimundi). Replaced old crispy R3 webbing at the the unnecessary rebelay station, and added a rock pinch anchor RDC at R6 that follows the watercourse (accessed via rabbit hole downclimb between large chockstones). Downclimbed R7. Multiple deep wades and one mandatory swim. A few optional swims and small jump opportunities along the exit.

The approach is long and exposed with zero shade and lots of elevation gain, bring a water filter, you will likley drink all/most of it on the way up if done in the warmer months. the canyon itself is pretty, however its alot of work for a short section of technical goods and the exit is...alot longer than one would think. Overall a fun, strenuous day with lots of wildlife and sunburn...would consider doing again with more flow.

We replaced most anchors. Flow was moderate making this route one of the best in the area. Although the creek walking on the exit is long, it's very pretty and enjoyable with flow. Catclaw between the creek and the road was easy to avoid. I could see this route being an arduous day and not worth the effort if it wasn't flowing. Time it right to experience the true magic. With moderate flow we didn't have to do any swims or jumps on the exit, just some downclimbing. In the right conditions this one is as good as rock creek and shaketree and better than big kahuna and Barnhart IMO.

Time includes a descent of Park and Salome and exit up Workman / Hell's Hole Trail. Starting and ending from Reynolds TH, no shuttle. The rock in the watercourse of Park was EXTREMELY slick and this slowed our movement considerably. Replaced R3 and R5 anchors, and toggled from a boulder in the watercourse at R2. Park was full of boulder hopping in a mostly open drainage and was rather unremarkable until the lower granite narrows and final rap sequence. Salome flow was moderate high, and Workman was flowing high which made the exit swims and upclimbs more challenging.

I did this canyon years ago, when a friend told me 'It's flowing'... but it was just a trickle back then... used Ropewiki Waterfow to find out this canyon was running at moderate flow and run to do it. This canyon is definitely best experienced early in the season when it has good flow. Some people run it in Extreme flow, which is a bit crazy and not recommended unless you know the canyon well.

decent water flow day after night time rain, would avoid driving the car to the waypoint as it is rutted with boulders, it's only an extra 15minutes adder each way to walk from the camp area near the front. Nice swim sections, many areas open up with possibly early exit if needed or use as shortcut drop in. Exit is directly to the right up the wash after the final rappel. Lots of poison ivy up the exit with scrambling up rocks, it is a good workout & fun climbing , just don't expect a gentle trail waking up so keep track of everyone as up routes have many options around obstacles. A very fun canyon
Pretty.

Water is just a trickle throughout, but still pretty clear, all things considered. The pools at the bottom are really cold (went in the afternoon). Still has some fantastic slides, though. 3/2 wetsuit was pretty chilly make sure you know where the exit hike is; go to far and you'll get some nasty climbing among cacti.
No water flow at all; Pool water was cold!

No water flow. Very cold pools about chest deep. We brought wetsuits but did not use them. We took our time and tried to play the "let's stay dry" game using guided rappels and downclimbs when possible until we got to the deep pools.
Combined Flow of approx 10 CFS at gauge houses. No Jumps available as water was too aerated. 6 rappels necessary at this level- rope up for the slide.
Slide has deep water.
