Great day in Parker. Nothing unique to report. All former slides are still full with debris so don't jump or slide anything. On one of the rappels midway through the canyon I cleared a log that was blocking a rap in the water course. Optionally there is a rap up on canyon right from a ledge.
Water flow moderate low. Caution on slides, as water level in pools was lower than usual. Rappelled 2nd slide. Anchors all looked good.
Strong current with churning water in narrow channels. Not for novice or beginner.
Fantastic trip down a beautiful canyon. Water/air temps prefect, no wetsuit needed. Lots of annoying flies along the escape route, try to take breaks elsewhere.
Water is low but the pools are very clear and nice to cool off in! No wetsuits needed. All anchors in good shape. Saw a kudamundi on the exit hike out!

Great first trip through the beautiful Parker Canyon. Water levels were low compared to the flow shown in some of the description photos. The two others I was with were a bit cold in their 3mm wetsuits. I wore a full 5mm wetsuit and was comfortable. As the weather warms up, 3mm will likely be perfect. Webbing was all in good condition. Saw a mountain lion carcass at the rim after hiking out of the canyon.

A few shallow pools, but otherwise no water in the canyon at all. Parked just past the bridge and hiked the 4x4 road the rest of the way to the drop-in. Cairn anchor at R1 has some problems and should be rebuilt.

Canyon was exceptionally dry. No flow throughout except for a trickle of water over the last two raps. Saw a dead coatimundi around R2, several starved-looking mountain goats between R3 and R4, and the lion carcass up at the rim by the exit. Death and despair was the general ambiance.

Low flow but a great run. Definitely chilly without any neoprene, but manageable! Water temperature was much colder than the Jug which we did the day prior. Webbing was in good shape. Lots of wasps both in canyon and above by the rim exit. Took a fun class IV alternative on the right side of the drainage towards the top of the standard exit route.

Beautiful canyon! High walls, hoodoos, and spires with interesting rock shapes, and exquisite colors! Loved it!! We took my lifted Nissan Xterra Pro4x up to the exit, left our bags at the approach and hiked down the road to them. The trail to the approach has a short severely rutted section and a few large rocks to crawl over. This can be intimidating to an inexperienced driver but nothing that a skilled driver in a stock 4x4 cant handle. Fun rappels and a few small slides. No swims, water was mostly ankle to waist deep. 2 of us wore 7mm we were very comfortable, one wore a 3mm and he was cold but not dangerously so. Exit was a “stair stepper” with a bit of climbing but quick and fun, most of the rock is solid and was a joy to climb on! Would definitely go again! Such an awesome short beautiful canyon!

Water was 47.6ºF at the start of the canyon, warming to 50.1ºF by the end. Young AZ weather was 66/44ºF for the high and low. Thin wetsuits would have been totally fine; most of our time was spent out of the water. Waist deep pools typically or you'll go all the way under one time on the slide. Gage read 0.12 cfps and 1.3 ft high. These numbers represent little waterflow. Brushy road guaranteed to pinstripe your vehicle. Start time of 9:30 AM still gave good reflective light in the canyon. My best guess of reports of broken ankles at the slide is due to a slight right curve right at the bottom, but we found it inconsequential.
Nice to run Parker with some water. Saw a small herd of bighorn sheep mid-canyon. Beware poison oak in the canyon.
A few pools to go through. Replaced anchor on R1. All other anchors looked good.

No flow. One of us was able to avoid all pools but one, getting wet to mid-calf. This involved rappelling and climbing trickery, however. All anchors looked good. Water was clean and cold, wind was gusty. Nice temps overall though.
Great flow, not too cold. Air temps in low 50s.

Really fun and beautiful canyon! very interesting rock, lots of color, and super fun in flow! We drove a mostly stock 4runner all the way to the exit and hiked down. The road has a very rutted section that is no problem for a skilled driver with a stock high clearance 4x4. We wore 4/3 wetsuits and were very comfortable in the beginning, started to get a bit chilly at the end but still not uncomfortable. One of us had a 7mm suit and was hot in the beginning and hot while hiking between rappels. There is a decent slide after the 1st rappel that has no anchor installed. This looks daunting for new people and it is recommended to set up a meat anchor for 1st person to check depth, so that is what we did. Wouldn't hurt to have a bolt installed here... Exit is pretty and engaging, not too strenuous! 4.5hr at a leisurely pace. Flow was really great, lots of fun, definitely the best in the area for a class C canyon.
High water flow. Gauge was at 2.02 feet. Avoided slide after second rap, climbed over ledge RDC. Light snow on banks outside canyon flow.

Report amended 3/28/2023 after discussion of gauge levels. ** Parker Creek CFS measure is subject to high variance and not reliable. Recommend using gauge height for estimation of flow. ** Very high flow made for an exciting day. South Fork Parker Creek gauge height was 2.25 on 3/12. Churn in the chutes at start of tech section required partner assists. Opted to downclimb DCR of corkscrew to avoid exit boil. Footing difficult to maintain below larger raps. Water was cold but warm sun and low wind mitigated. Challenging but super fun run!

Flow has subsided significantly in the past couple weeks, but is still fun. We were all comfortable in light wetsuits. Rain jackets helped with the wind. Left a CRKT knife by the anchor for R3. I'll buy you a beer (or six) if you find and return it.
Extremely beautiful canyon. Flow was light, but still very fun. The big slide was running. Anchors were solid. Love this canyon.

Flow is a little low, but still enough to feel it and to provide a nice ride down the slides. This canyon has cool slides plus everything I love about Phog, but more of it. It is a nice time to go, We got away with no wetsuits but the exit isn't deadly hot yet.

The wife and I took two beginners through this canyon. Their rappels were slow but purposeful. This was our third time through. Roughly 1" of water flowing over the initial dam, with water flowing throughout the entirety of the canyon. This was the most beautiful we have experienced Parker. Waterfalls were flowing well at raps 4 and 5, but stayed out of water by rappeling to the side. Hike out was pleasant as it was mostly shaded. Started at 10:30 and ended at 4:30. This is one of my favorite canyons in AZ with awesome flow to boot.
Linked this canyon with The Jug, which made for a long day - Excellent canyon that I return to frequently

Conditions were great especially with the beginner (Courtney) we had in our group. This was her first time doing a canyon. The flow was decent. Not too extreme or too low. Temps outside were in the mid 50s and the water was cold even with a 5/4 wetsuit. As long as you’re moving quickly my wetsuit thickness was good however I was jealous of Tom Cross’s drysuit.

Water gage height was <1 (0.4) but water level was high. We added two anchors around boulders between rap 1 and 2 to bypass water chutes that are more like slides in lower conditions. We had a climber in our group who was also able to set up retrievable handlines over sketchy current parts. The waterfalls you could rappel and stay out of the current for the most part. All anchors were in excellent condition. Would not recommend beginners for this water level. this is our second time doing this canyon. And this time round we were a bit nervous with the flow.

Very High Flow dangerous for beginner and intermediate. Flow was strong and wide that not all raps could be avoided out of the direct flow. Team of 2, advanced. Used advanced techniques and class c canyon specific gear to mitigate dangerous raps and hydraulics. Some raps will push you under the heavy flow. Bolt placement at current levels puts you in direct flow. Last rap Very High Flow with current conditions. After out of water on last rap, massive rocks uphill make for a great view of the waterfall. Sunlight hits the skyscraping wall above the waterfall in the back during this time of year close to sunset. One of the massive rocks mentioned uptop was not there a bit ago and is new so use caution standing under that area as this falling boulder would be deadly. We enjoy taking our time and soaking up the canyon. 6 hours. 4/3mm wetsuit and 5mm wetsuit, 2.5mm vest, 3mm socks (5mm socks would be best), 3mm gloves. Would have stayed longer in the canyon but late start and cold water. The stretch before rap 4, plenty of spots to take a break and see the labyrinth of this canyon and hoodoos with massive vertical walls. 12:30pm start in the sun, then after R1 most of the canyon in the shade until half way where we caught the sun for just a bit with the way the canyon curves. Hiked out with light but no direct sun. Earlier start to be in the sun. Might park at exit next time but the exit once on road actually is mostly downhill and extremly beautiful walk back to 4x4 parking almost 1 mile or roughly 2 miles if you parked at the top. Stock high clearance 4x4 would have a hard time getting down this road to the 4x4 parking and there's no room to turn around. Water gauge for the day showed near zero CFS and height gauge which was not at all accurate for the Very High Flow.
Air temp was 73, water was nice for a 3/4 wetsuit, gauge said 1.34 at Parker creek above 288, no slime yet in main course of canyon, could see it forming in the sunny areas after last rappel.

5/31/24 - Our rope got stuck on rappel 5, tried for an hour and a half to free it (I did not include this time in the time for us to complete the canyon). We planned to return and run the canyon again with a different rope, but something came up and we were unable to. If you plan on running this canyon and can free my rope I would like to arrange some way for me to come and get it as it is practically brand new. Contact me at (951) 293-4263 if you are interested in retrieving my rope or have retrieved it. I will reward for your efforts. Canyon has very low water right now with some moderately deep pools, most are avoidable with some light scrambling and clever rappelling. The water was cold but not unbearable. The deepest water was after rappel 1 at about chest deep, but it is avoidable. The average water depth in most pools was knee deep. Watch out for aggressive bees on the approach as we parked our car in a pullout on the right about 400ft from the bridge and out of nowhere a small swarm of bees attacked us with a shoot first ask questions later mentality.

No flow. One mandatory thigh-depth pool below the second-to-last rap. Several other deep pools that were avoided. Damage from the the recent fire was present on the east side of the 288 but Parker itself was unaffected. Some graffiti along the 4x4 access road that needs removal.

4 hours at a leisurely pace. 1.44' on the height gauge equated to low-moderate flow. Very relaxed and friendly for anyone familiar with flowing water in canyons. Beautiful canyon when flowing. Straightforward and easy exit.

Two people in 3.75 hours with low/very low flow (online gauge was reading 1.2). We were driving a small SUV and parked near the klm point labeled start of 4x4 obstacles. Temps were in the 80s/90s and got up to upper 90s on the road hike out. We felt a little silly carrying wetsuits and never put them on and felt comfortable takin a leisurely pace to play and practice in the canyon. I thought the play to pay was great and enjoyed the beautiful geology and cute frogs that felt unique to this area / canyon!
Leading a group through a canyon was a fun way to spend a birthday. Hard to complain about wearing a T-Shirt and shorts!

Shuttled up to the campground and did a complete descent. Hitting it in spring is likely best for splashy water conditions, but water never more than about calf-deep. Canyon is over-bolted and many of them are in weird places out in the watercourse, although seemed in good shape. The lower canyon has a different flavor with big trees and ferns. A very pleasant early spring outing.

Very low water. Wet suits were worn but the creek could have been run in rain boots. The lower unlinked bolts had been linked via two quicklinks and a rap ring, which we removed. Please leave unlinked bolts unconnected!

BUTT SLIDE ALL DAY! This may not be the most "fun" canyon, but the access super easy. The creek walk down the the rappels is a little steep and slippery so watch your step. We were able to skip both R1 and R2 but it required some spotting and such. Also R1 Anchor station is super slippery. Once you get to R3 (the bolts in the moss) its but slide all day!! its one large rock you get to butt slide down. This time of year there was almost no water, but still enough for a long butt slide!! This is a great canyon to practice new techniques you want to master, test out new gear ect.. I would say its on the low risk scale, at about any point in the canyon you could walk into the forest if you had a problem. This makes it a great place to practice and learn! and fun for butt sliding! there arent any pools or any swimming, you could probably do this in your regular cloths and not get very wet.

One of the best canyons near the Detroit lake area! It is reminiscent to the canyons in the Sierras but with more green (and more slippery). The water is colder relative to the other canyons in the area and the last 2 raps are in a chilly spot so a wind breaker is advised. I wore a 4/3 and was starting to get cold at the end. If you have a whole day, combine Parkett and Lost - both look entirely different and are fantastic canyons! We jumped the all the slides so be sure to check the bottom if you do decide to jump or slide.

Parkett is truly a gem and a 5 star canyon in Oregon. It has jumps, slides, and beautiful waterfalls. We setup 2 lines where possible to get people down quicker. The water is Parkett is cold so a thick wetsuit is advised!The canyon ends before you know it and it should be combined with Lost Creek for a full day of fun!
Great flow, the Waterflow analysis was spot on. A nice shot canyon as a rest day after running Valhalla the previous day.
Discovered additional 20ft rappel 200ft downstream of exit narrows. Updated the beta. Water still quite cold; wetsuits a good plan.

I was warned there may be some interesting bolts. Looked like whatever was there must have been replaced by glue-ins, they were in good shape. Webbing also in great shape. Was a fun canyon to be sure, but the exit made me wonder how often I'll be back.

Fantastic short canyon with a spectacular 150' drop. Flow was moderate; sporting but not overwhelming. No special challenges. We retrieved a core shot dynamic climbing rope left by a previous group on the big drop. A good reminder to use static ropes and to creep the rope to avoid cutting the rope on sharp edges.

Probably best to give this one a few more weeks to dry out a bit. Very high flow complicates several rappels, greatly increases risk of foot entrapments and makes the slides very very technical by completely obscuring the terrain you will be sliding over.

Beginner was quite athletic, in hindsight it was a bit much for his first canyon. Note: My 15 year old son who is experienced, struggled with the high water flow due to difficult lifting rappel rope into the ATC (he is only 120 lbs). Last rappel anchors are dangerous close to the edge and in a cold, deep keeper. Have a plan before dropping into last rappel and do it one at a time. It would have been much easier if the flow were lower, I'd wait 3 weeks.

Ran the canyon with a group of 3. Conditions were moderate-high and went well. We added a 2 bolt station over R4 to enable future trips the ability to avoid the foot entrapment and the funky deadman’s. It is located DCL and there is a nice walkout to the station. Canyoneers will now land into a dry pothole and have the ability to have more control to the next bolt station.

Water was cold and definitely flowing. New bolts at rap 4 were super useful! Nice to pull and bag rope from the pothole. Access here is slippery (I slipped while rigging but was clipped in). I felt secure not getting swept over rap 5 but definitely had to manage rope carefully. Beta misses extra short rap at logjam to new improved exit.
Canyon was flowing very high. Bolts are in good condition and the last single bolt probably needs another bolts. The pool at the bottom of the 150 footer is filled with deadwood. Some floaters.
Amazing conditions with unexpected higher flow. Water was a bit chilly for a 4mm wetsuit, would have been ideal for a 6mm or drysuit.

Moderately pushy on big rap. Fairly cold water but never in it for extended periods and fairly sunny in the afternoon. Of note: I have only run this canyon once and have not checked jumps or slides extensively. However, I am generally considered to be very daring for such things and I thought the risk for most average canyoneers (or possibly more daring canyoneers) for most of the drops was unacceptably high- especially for the "quality" of the action. Most of the "bigger" jumps have small landing zones, awkward takeoffs, relatively shallow water (waist to chest), and/or a ledge that must be cleared. As for the slides: While not confirmed, it appeared the first slide had bumps and ledges along the way (especially lower) as well as the wall on the right that could easily catch a foot or tail bone. I'm not certain what the second one was- I think maybe the one with a submerged ledge that I thought was a better jump. Anyhow, call it lack of daring or being conservative but attempt these at your own risk... Also, for what it's worth, all of the shorter drops can be downclimbed with skill and some modest exposure by the skilled. But it's not recommended. Lastly, we estimated the 2 stage at 90'.
Flow was great, not pushy at all. Great day to run the canyon.

Water flow was a little saucy but completely safe - such a beautiful canyon. The canyon itself took about 3 hours with a lunch break, but the exit we took (very close to the alt exit but I guess not quite there) was also 3 hrs... so that took away from part of the fun.

The flow was moderate high based on the waterflow comparison but our consensus was that the canyon was running at a4/moderate with no serious swiftwater challenges. All anchors are in good condition. It's helpful to have extra length on the pull side for R3 and R5 to pull the rope from across the pool. The potential hydraulic in the pool between R4 and R5 was easy to navigate at this flow.

Flow was terrific today. This year is probably lower than the norm per the extreme heat wave Oregon experienced in June. Much time was spent lounging in pools and loitering. Replaced wonky anchor at R1... although the tree is dead and the anchor probably should be moved to a new location ere long. Took the short exit which requires continuing downstream and clambering over numerous log jams. Added a rock cairn DCR which is a good place to de-wetsuit. Continue down-canyon along the creek for ~50ft from the cairn and exit directly up the steep slope.

Parked the car at the bottom and walked up to the trailhead. The water in this creek is cold! The person on the team with a 3mm wetsuit was uncomfortable for sure. All the raps are really fun, especially the longest one! We skipped the last two short raps and down climbed instead. Thanks for the obvious cairn to know when to leave the creek. The uphill thrash at the end sucks. Use your GPS. If you just go uphill you won't end up at your car (ask me how I know). :(
Parkett is awesome! Water is cold!!
Chill day. Water was cold. I wore a 5mm and had small periods of being chilly. For a group of 2 it wasn’t a problem. Could be if you take more than 4.

Terrific fun in Parkett! Did we open it for the year? Had forgotten how good the big rappel is. Replaced several anchors which, oddly, were missing. They are all in good condition. All jumps are in at current water levels. Watch a large log floating in the hanging pool on the two-tier rappel. Having it come down on a rappeller would be no-bueno. Beware yellowjackets on the exit.

Parkett was stunning and a real treat to run on our way back from the PNW rondy! The flow on the big rappel was nothing short of pure fun, but it was difficult to hear the whistle blasts from up top. Our only grievance was being stung 8 times by yellow jackets on the exit! When you find the rock cairn, you'll see an obvious slope on the left side of the embankment, typically used to climb out. Unfortunately there's a huge wasp nest there, that we didn't see, and my partner got lit up. We found an alternate path up by walking on the fallen trees to the right of it, but you've been warned!
Saw some bees, ran. Ran the canyon. Very chill. Ran into a group in front of us.

The waterfalls weren't too pushy but definitely still a fun amount of flow. I was a little cold in my 5mm wetsuit, wish I had a hood. I don't enjoy the approach or exit from this canyon, but it's definitely worth it!

Flow was definitely moderate. Waterfalls were just pushy enough to be really fun! We got a late start, so there was very little sunlight in the canyon making for a cold run. For the big drop (Parkett Falls) we rapped on a 200' rope and tied 2 100' ropes on for the pull. We couldn't pull the ropes, they absolutely wouldn't budge. We ended up cutting as much of the pull side as we could since we needed some rope for remaining 2 rappels. We went back the next day with the intention of retrieving our stuck gear. But we found the Forest Service gate to NF-11 closed and locked. The Forest Service put up signs on the gate stating that the road is closed due to the Pyramid and Slate fires. We ran into a Forest Service Ranger there at the gate who confirmed that even though Parkett isn't inside the closure area, access via NF-11 is prohibited. We will reward anyone who runs Parkett and retrieves our ropes and gear. Bonus reward for getting pictures of whatever prevented the pull. Find me on FB at https://www.facebook.com/djkessler

We had a team of four, this canyon was fun this time of year, the water was just right for a beginner (me) but also had more water flow that some of the other canyons close bye. (lost, Panther ect) We added some quick links to R4 to reduce the chance of a stuck rope with an 8 block "euro style". I read about the exit and expected worse. There is very little bush wacking if you use the exit thats in the beta past the cairn. It is very steep but mostly open forest with some downed trees, it reminded me of elk hunting as a kid. I plan on doing this canyon many more times! The pay to play feels worth it. I was wearing a 6/5mm and felt comfy all day(I get cold easily). Off too quartzville for a romp to end the day!

Replaced missing anchor on R1. It appears some well-meaning party is continuing to remove the anchor here from year to year and rappelling DRT or ghosting the drop. Rappelling DRT is not good for the tree; one of the trees here is already showing signs of friction damage. Ghosting can be used in some circumstances, although retrievable systems are generally more complicated/risky to use. Many are static in nature which is non-ideal in aquatic canyons. This drop is not visited by hikers (non-canyoneers) on any regular basis, so there's no good reason to be removing the fixed anchor here. Added an extra single bolt to the second drop in the canyon to provide options in high flow: rappelling or to protect the approach for a jump. Allows for many different options R4: removed the quick link from lower glue-in bolt. The quick link on the upper bolt /might/ help avoid getting ropes stuck, but the lower one was just creating more rope drag and making the pull harder. Am unconvinced having any quick links on the bolts is a good idea. When the anchor is underwater during flood stage the quicklinks will be thrashed around wildly 24/7 until the water levels drop... which can result in damage to the bolts. Please do not add any more material to the anchor.
Parkett is great as ever. Flows were on the low side but still fun. All the jumps are in and anchors are in good shape.

The R1 tree didn’t have webbing so we used a friction saver. All other anchors were in good condition. Bit of a longer creek walk in and steeper bushwhack out than we’d hoped for but all in all great time. Parkett falls is amazing in mod to mod high, can only imagine what it would’ve been like 3 weeks ago. Thanks to the large mazamas group who also ran it for letting us jump in ahead of them. Combining it with Quartzville afterwards made for a banger day.

Very long and challenging day. With no beta available (before we posted ours), there was little information to prepare us for a very long day. Recommend good high ankle hiking shoes (as you’ll have plenty of opportunities to roll them), long pants and sleeves to navigate the poison ivy in the lower portion of the canyon, and plenty of water, electrolytes, and food for a very long day.

Kids were 2-7 in age. We backpacked the littlest one through about half of the slot and almost all of the approach and exit. The kids did great but the entrance to the wall was trickier than anticipated. We brought one harness for getting the kids up the wall. The wall was more intimidating than the videos make it look. We belayed them up on a meat uneventfully, and they all climbed up pretty easily without needing it too much. We brought a 8' big bird, but next time I would bring a 30'+ rope. Keeping a harness on the 2yo was nice because we could pick her up like a puppy over obstacles at times. All the kids did great and loved playing peek-a-boo (scare) with the grown ups. My 2yo kept wanting to play in the exceedingly fine sand instead of travel through the canyon. Plan that in next time if you expect to keep moving. The canyon is a really fun sandbox with exceedingly beautiful sand for young children. She would plop down at some of the narrowest sections, and we were mindful of avoiding creating a bottleneck. The area has a grid of trails everywhere. It is easy to follow carins and end up off-track. We got off track and added maybe a mile to our trip when traveling between peek-a-boo and spooky. The road is terribly washboarded. Letting air out of the tires helped. Found a good campsite about half-way to spooky gulch trailhead that was remote. Time is car to car, including peek-a-boo and spooky gulch (6.69 hours). For our kids, they got tired when starting spooky but dug deep and did it. It had rained a few days prior, but the road was dry and the canyon only had a few muddy spots that could easily be walked around.

Delayed somewhat by enthusiastic culvert construction going on along the Molalla river road. Falls were terrific, but the creek loses a lot of points per the thick strenuous bushwhacking required in order to visit them. With no forest canopy above, the new undergrowth is unchecked with vines, fallen trees, woody debris, and loose rocks slowing your progress. Beware occasional random leg-eating holes likely caused by a tree's roots burning completely out of the ground.

Combined with Middle and Lower Tortilla Creek, starting at Tortilla TH and ending at Tortilla Flat. Very little water this time of year. Some large boulder fields to navigate, but nothing requiring rope.

Very pretty red canyon, short but fun. The 2 raps of 150 & 70 are mostly vertical with overhang sections, the pools are avoidable by various climbing options. The exit is to the left just before the canyon opens up to the large views. The exit has many vertical sections of climbing with exposure. Good route finding, climbing & skills are needed for the exit
A few pools that are easily avoidable. All anchors in good condition.

Nice warm day, would have been fun to do some slides with a wetsuit. First rap goes through the water course, so we got a little bit wet. Nice short canyon! Super easy approach, and not a bad exit either.

Water was trickling on us for the big rap. the flow was fresh and pretty. Webbing was super fresh. Didn't find the pull on the 2nd rap at all tricky. Just give yourself plenty of extra pull so you can walk out a bit before pulling.

A scenic canyon with a noteworthy first rappel. Webbing in good shape. Pulls went smooth. Pools were all avoidable though there was a light trickle over the first rap. A hummingbird paid us a visit for a while, and plenty of other flying creatures (including wasps) were out and about. Exit felt straightforward and was not difficult in terms of the climbing/scrambling. Moved at a leisurely pace.

Time is car to car for First Water, Hog, and Phog. Two hours per canyon. Some shallow, avoidable pools in Phog. About a dozen wasps were congregating around what little water was left at the base of R2. Brushy in the last section of the exit - pants recommended.
A much nicer exit than Hog. I actually thought this canyon was prettier also.
Such a pretty canyon. Lots of pools with recent rains, all avoidable but we got soaked on the first rap. Solid anchors. This one would be good for newbies and beginners.

Beautiful canyon, super quick and easy approach and just a couple rappels but they’re both really fun. We replaced the webbing on the second rap. There were some pools but they could all easily be avoided. A couple people in our group opted to walk through one of them, it was about waist deep. Saw lots of frogs and a baby rattlesnake. The exit is steep and a little exposed but not crazy moves required.

Little flow from recent rains. Lightly used trail made approach easy. Fun and scenic packraft was nice. Did it in 2 rappels. Remeasured rappel #1 to be about 90' and rappel #2 to be about 230'. All webbing on anchors looked good. First rappel has loose rocks on start, caution to people below.

Started 6am, snow and ice at start. Exit palisade trail at 2.3 miles. Head down stream. Slick down climb canyon right. Continue through boulders down canyon. First rappel removed old webbing replaced with purple webbing, off dead tree LDC Rappel 65' Continue down canyon, boulders and down climbs Big Rappel 150' from living pine tree LDC. Replaced webbing with purple. Removal of gear beneficial at this point. Lots of boulders, slick down climbs. Temporary webbing set on a dead man for large chalkstone maneuvering use for hand line. Continue bouldering /down, climbing down stream. Stream takes a hard right heading west. Palisade Trail crosses stream hop out here. Exit left follow east fork to Sabino canyon trail. Made tram exit.

Time is for establishing the route, building anchors, and pioneering the exit. We attempted the route a week prior and had to bail and return due to conditions. The flow on this run was moderate and felt safe. High flow the week prior would have been difficult to negotiate. Anchors new and in good condition.

Short and sweet, this route offered a unique narrows section cutting through the blocky stone. Flow will provide the best value, however during our initial investigation at higher flow, we did notice a hydraulic forming below one of the downclimbs. Once finished, take the opportunity to soak in the beauty of isolation canyon before exiting.

2.5 star. I don't shortleeve every canyon, but when i do, its apparently on the worst ones. The two red kml descent sections are really pretty and sweet. Had trouble finding the first bolt, beta update reflected. Downclimbed the noted dowbclimbs in the beta, also was able to avoid the only potentially unavoidable pool thanks to traversing to a fixed line mid pool. Without that, I'd have turned around or swum. The exit was pretty brushy. Not terrible, but not my favorite point in the day. No catclaw, but many manzanita and scrub oak.

Decent quick escape from growing valley heat, but no real repeat value, at least not in this no-flow state. Decently pretty, with scattered deep pools of water that are avoidable with some mostly class 3-4 action (very brief low class 5 bit to clear the big pool near handline). Single bolts seem solid in this hard volcanic rock. Staying with the mostly settled talus where possible kept the exit reasonable. Manzanita higher up lays on some pinstripes, without becoming vicious.

Truly amazing slot. Unquestionably one of the best in the world of canyoneering. Only Upper West Canyon can compare in beauty. Completely dry, though in spring probably has several small pools up to waste deep.

Pinto Creek would be a nice outing for a family outing. It's very pretty and peaceful. From a canyoneers perspective, I would not recommend it as it's basically walking through a stream (with spiders!) and the walls never really close in or become sculpted.

Water levels were low (a few calf deep sections) but we enjoyed this trip. Wore water shoes and walked through the creek most the way. Continued hiking through the wash after the canyon opened up before turning around at about the 3 mile mark. Didn't see anybody else out there.

This is a spectacular canyon with an easy approach. This was my first time in true quicksand where I sunk waste deep in one step. There are pockets of quicksand in the water course that make for an entertaining hike.

9.5 hours car to car from Bright Angel trailhead. We used Bright Angel trail for both ingress and egress so we wouldn't have to fuss around with shuttle bus rides - this looks like it may add .5 - 1 miles to the trip. Pipe Creek was flowing very lightly, no deep wades or swims. Replaced/repaired a few anchors, all in good shape now.
Car to car time includes a descent of Garden Creek after exiting Pipe.
3 hr approach. 3 hr route. 5 hr exit. all anchors in good shape. 1st anchor backed up and is less than 100. pack light and enjoy.

Time reflects a descent of East Pipe and Garden Creek. 2.5h approach to Pipe Creek via S. Kaibab starting at Pipe Creek Vista (0.75mi from TH). Flow was low and pools at middle and base of R1 were knee depth at most. Wore neoprene socks but no wetsuit. R1 webbing is new and extended to lip for a <100’ rappel and smooth pull. Small cave along R2 is still pristine - tread carefully. Midway through canyon I became warm and removed my windbreaker.
Time includes descent from bright angel trail to pipe creek, then descent of garden creek and out the bright angel. Pleasantly surprised by rappels in pipe creek, beautiful mossy caves.

Time was in canyon. We did garden creek right after in a back to back day. While the canyons was not the best it was nice to to it as well as garden creek on a single day. Anchors should definitely be replaced by the next team. There were a few aluminum rap rings that should definitely be changed out.

Sweet canyon! Nice introductory high stemming movement. Useful to bring a sand trap and potshot. But - I can't recommend this one in the heat. Would be delightful in 60-70 degree weather... not high 80s like our run through it. We ended up topping off on pothole water. We enthusiastically dunked into a few thigh-high potholes, without wetsuits.

River crossing was about knee deep at most at about 140 cfs. Good use trail up to saddle. Light brush on top to first drop in. Completed doing only 3 raps, first down climb had webbing, but can be easily downclimbed. Exit can be a little brushy, exit RDC for least brush. All raps had existing webbing and looked good.

Salt River flow was around 150 CFS, we just waited across in our hiking clothes, and got wet up to about 4 feet. Water was warm enough that this was not an issue. Approach hike up through the arch was fun, with braided trails, not too hard to follow. Moderate cat claw in the canyon and on the exit hike. Found no anchor at first repel, so we built a nice deadman right at the edge. Easily inspectable. Olive drab Milbeck webbing, with a Tebylon half ring. We didn’t have enough webbing left to get around the.R2 massive boulder. But the existing webbing was severely bleached. We ghosted this, leaving the existing webbing as a better than nothing alternative, although it should be replaced. It will require about 20 feet.R3 purple webbing was in good shape. Aside from R2, a downline could be performed at R1 by veering DCL, and at R3 by using a hand line attached to the anchor that we left in place. Overall, not a real compelling canyon, but a fun half day in the local area.

Our group agreed that this was a Zion quality canyon. It features narrow winding passage ways, down climbs, and large drops! The rock is sharp here so protect the rope when you can. The anchors for the first few drops were completely destroyed by the most recent flash flood and we had to rebuild rock stacks or replace webbing. This canyon has bolts for the drops where the rope can easily get jammed. Awesome canyon…except the shallow wading across the frigid Virgin River was cruel on our feet (the water level reached just below the knees).

The river crossing was only slightly deeper than knee-deep. Our group was thankful for clouds and a light sprinkling on the approach -- even with the cool weather, the steep approach would be brutal in full sunlight. Many drops did not have anchors and we often used retrievable riggings, so these drops still do not have anchors on them.
Rebuilt a few anchors. Lots of slings with no quick links so we fixed that.

River was cold to cross. Just above knee deep. Approach is not fun. I should probably knock my rating down a star just for the approach (but I didn't). Anchors were in varying conditions - some needing rebuilt, others not too shabby. Despite it being a bit overrated imo, the canyon photographed extremely well. Perhaps because of it being mostly in shadow.

All anchors were in place and looked fine except one, one deadman anchor was replaced as when we pulled on it, it came out of the ground. A majority of the anchors as of this report were tree anchors or natural anchors, some rappels have chains in place to make it easier for pulls. Be prepared to make anchors (lots of webbing and links) just in case as it may be days or weeks before someone passes through the canyon and this canyon has a lot of potential for flash floods to take out all the natural anchors. Start this trip early as it can take all day depending on your speed and level of experience in the group. You will cross the Virgin River from the parking area so be prepared for that and make sure the water flow levels are safe before heading for this canyon. The approach isn't difficult, but it is steep. It took us maybe 2 1/2 hours. The GPS coordinated path leads you up a wash, then on a ridge to Cherry, and then on a wash again until you have to go up a steep vertical all the way up to the ridge. From there you path across a bowl with a lot of steep rock screes along the way. Finally you hit the ridge above the bowl where you can descend into the canyon. If you want a "shorter route" but is still fairly steep, you can go directly into the bowl (right side of the ridge where Cherry starts) and cross / climb it instead. We ended up counting 20 rappels, some other reports of people online say you can down climb some of them, we didn't see an obvious way how to do that, but those crews may be more advanced in skill. We were in the canyon for close to 10 hours with a varied in experience group of 8 and we had several ropes to help us leap frog so my opinion is this canyon leans more on the longer side. The second long rappel is a beast of a pull (for us it was rappel 8) from where the anchor is. This anchor is on a sturdy tree that is right up against the wall of the canyon, it then stretches out onto a jagged ramp for about ten feet before descending into a 100 foot wall. That adds for a lot of friction. We ended up putting two people on the pull chord with full weight before it would budge. Consider bringing a smooth operator or fiddle stick to make it easier to undo your rigging above and provide as little friction as possible on the pull. The rappels are fairly straight forward though a few have chains that are far out over the edge and do not have a good way to anchor yourself in with a safety before reaching out to get the chains. I'd suggest having two people tether to the person who reaching for the chains for the most safety as possible, or someone can add a bolt closer to the safe part of the rappels so they can leash themselves to that while they reach for the chains. Several of the short rappels have face smashers so prepare your crew for those techniques if they don't know how to get around those. This isn't the most beginner friendly canyon, but is a great one for those looking to practice there skills in a less technical canyon who is looking for a long day in the late fall to early spring. If you have a beginner crew, definitely look at doing Cherry.

Snow/icy on the top of the ridge on the hike in, but other than that it was dry. There is a trail now instead of climbing up the drainage that takes you to the top of Cherry. We replaced a couple of deadman anchors due to crunchy webbing. Definitely does not rival Zion, but worth doing if you are in the area. Of the two—Portal or Cherry—I liked Cherry better.
All anchors in good shape. Lower section is rather oven-like this time of year. Last rap in Portal proper had a tarantula hanging out halfway down it :)

The early start was nice because we were in shade for most of the approach. There is a use trail on the ridge east of the drainage which the kml currently shows, we only discovered it partway up so not sure where it started but I'll attempt to estimate it on a new track. It's better than staying in the drainage. We did 16 raps (including bonus) and used a handline a couple of times. We didn't replace any webbing though some in the upper section were starting to get a bit crispy. The beta is pretty bad. Might take a stab at improving it but it's so hard to erase these layers of beta accretion (seems especially bad in the Vegas area)

All anchors and webbings were good. Took us 11 hours because I was a ditz and followed the descend line as approach, oops. That probably costed us half an hour. The approach was gnarly but able to see sort of a faint used trail. The canyon has same flavor as Cherry except the narrow canyon part is longer, and the approach is way longer. River crossing was generally easy in fall, about knee deep in the deepest part.

A decent, worthwhile, full day route if in the area. River crossing was for the most part knee deep. I wore a drysuit just because, and stashed it on the other side. Approach took us 2.5hrs and was not overly challenging. The canyon has some pretty unique and interesting sections with a few small picturesque spots and pretty views, interspaced between long stretches of uninteresting terrain. The big rappels were nice enough but not overly exciting. Lots of nuisance drops throughout. Lots of downclimbing. No water in canyon. The slots have some nice features but are nowhere close to Zion quality and should not be compared as such. I'd say this canyon more closely resembles the routes near Lake Meade in the Black Canyon Range but without the big river and packraft exit. Most anchors are cairn anchors except for the 2 big ones which are bolted. All webbing in good condition. I found 4 ticks crawling on me throughout the day as did some of my team so keep an eye out for them... This route would be an absolute scorcher in the summer, wouldn't recommend it then. We were blessed with cloudy cool weather this day. All in all it was a good day out with good folks. This is a decent canyon overall, and I suspect, the best in the area. However, it is a one and done for me.

Back through the Portal! Now that I live much closer, this run is much more justifiable! Though my last report still stands firm as my opinion, I can definitely appreciate the layers and intricacies in this canyon. We took off our pants, and brought a change of shoes/towel for the river. River was thigh deep. Approach took us 3hrs, descent 4hrs. Webbing all still decent, none replaced. Big rap and last rap are bolted. Unlinked bolts/chains on big rap are not easy to spot, keep in mind the low tree anchor at the big rap is just a short access rappel to get to the chains DCR. I almost passed them. This route still contains ticks, we found a few on us…

Very short canyon with some interesting geology. Water was very low, little to no flow with deep pools. We didn’t set any rappels and downclimbed all obstacles with some partner assisted downclimbing.

A short diversion outside of Port Angeles; was passing through and stopped by to check it out. Would probably be more fun with more water, and the new (?) homeless camp (lots of kitchen knives lying around; totally bizarre) atop the falls takes away from the allure. Use caution as the central pothole is deep and may need a partner assist to escape. Brought a rope but ended out downclimbing everything.
Got rained out of our planned canyons so headed over here for some pothole practice. Good fun even though it was a bit cold and sprinkling!

Descended forested slopes a short distance to drop in via a convenient draw. Progression downstream surprisingly easy. Flow was low, but some quality rappels. Re-rigged the last two rappels to run the flow and rappel into the Dragon's Chamber which was super fun. Think we went past the recommended exit point and ended up having to do a bit more thrashing to get out of the creek.
Fun creek! A couple of the rappels could use bolts to run the flow. Probably best run in June-July timeframe for more water. Could be a good weekend combo with Tule nearby.

Hiked the entirety of the route in 4.5 hours. Nice, easy route, really good, deep narrows right at the end of the canyon - you could actually see almost all of the good stuff by hiking up from the bottom if backpacking in Aravaipa Creek. Rebuilt one anchor - totally dry.

Time includes primo and a trip to into hellhole up Aravaipa. A nice canyon, though it takes a long time to get out there. The rabbithole was especially cool and may be downclimbed, only R2 was mandatory if you are a comfortable enough down climber. It was quite hot outside the canyon, I wouldn't do it any later in the year.

This was a cool and unexpected slot canyon. I'm glad we did it, but not sure I would do it again. We did the canyon as the start to a 2 day backpacking trip. No shuttle, with all our backpacking gear went up the hill, then proceeded down to the canyon. The downhill part of the approach was what made me question ever doing this canyon again. It's was loose rock down a steep slope. Once you start down, you're not going to be able to go back up. We were rewarded with a unique changing slot canyon. It was very pretty and feature multiple down climbs that were fun. The 3 raps were all in the last quarter mile and close together. We put new anchors on the first two, the 3rd seemed ok for now but will need to be replaced soon. By the way Bear Creek road is drivable in any SUV to the beginning of 5026C where there is a fire pit that would be a good place for a shuttle vehicle. Aravaipa Road has some water crossings between bear creek trail head and aravaipa trail head, that would be fine with any vehicle with 4 wheel drive, depending on conditions 4 wheel drive may not be necessary.

I pulled the rope after the first drop because the beta said only one drop. Fortunately there were plenty of anchor spots and anyway ended up digging up webbing while I was trying to clean up a pinch point. Put an anchor on a pinch point at the 1st drop. Pool was belly deep at the bottom of the 2nd drop and unavoidable unless you're a really good climber.

Scenic approach. About 1h to the start of the drainage. Not too brushy. Removed / replaced R1 webbing and re-located anchor for cleaner pull. Left R2 webbing be as it was still in decent condition. Pool at the base of R2 was about stomach-depth at the midpoint. All other water was avoidable. Majority of the canyon is nontechnical, but pretty.

Great little canyon, 2 rappels hard starts but nothing crazy, setup a Zipline for the 2nd rap , one of our team dropped a figure 8 in the belly high water and couldn't retrieve, then a bunch of 2ater pocket obstacles 2 couldn't be avoided and the last was a bridging challenge that only 1/3 of us managed to conquer Would be great with hose canyon and run both in one day

This amazing little creek is a perfect place to chill and enjoy the views with out the hard work or getting in or out...1 rappel can be done but in good conditions can be jumped from multiple spots ranging from 17ft up to 40ft if you climb up DCL. To jump there is a gap to cover a few feet out but pretty easy to accomplish. Once in the pool the 2nd jump is only about 3-4 ft into another beautiful deep pool with awesome underwater rock features and fish! Water levels were perfect for practicing rappels or working on water entry from different heights. Exploring past this area up creek to the Washougal falls is def worth the time, there are a few other deep blue/green pools to get in and the views are really nice.
Great little swimming/jumping area. There were 5 other people there swimming plus our 3. Water is cold, we wished we brought more than shorty wetsuits.

Approached the canyon from Cherry Creek Rd (FR 203) by taking the Pueblo Canyon trail up to the gully on the track, then scrambling up the gully/drainage to the top in 3.5 hours total from the alternate parking after Devils Chasm trailhead. Anchors in good shape in the entry side drainage, no water. No anchors present for the two drops in the main drainage of Pueblo; the first we anchored from a good tree on canyon left that keeps you in the light flow from the spring (fun hip slide), the big waterfall rappel (no more than 100 feet to the trail) we anchored from a big rock horn right at the top of the drop. Ruins were awesome - decided to skip the lower canyon and take the trail back, from the falls to the spot where you leave the trail on the way up the gully on the approach is quite thin/overgrown in spots and can be difficult to follow. The road is in good shape up to Ellison Ranch, then decent shape up to Devils Chasm, though narrow and bumpy. Not long after Devils Chasm a large boulder partially blocks the road - some short, tough vehicles can squeeze past it, many will not want to try. If you have any doubt about passing the boulder, park at either the Devils Chasm trailhead or at a fairly large pullout at a spur road on the right shortly thereafter and hike the rest of the way. The road is quite narrow in this area and turning around can be difficult.

Absolutely INCREDIBLE trip!!! Drove out Friday night and camped a few hundred feet from Pueblo Canyon TH. Cherry Creek RD is not challenging. The boulder in the middle of the trail people mention is not an issue to simply drive around in a midsize 4x4, and I suspect that even a full-size truck may fit with some careful maneuvering. The rocky areas on trail are not really 2wd friendly but a stock, high clearance 4x4 can handle them fine with low range and delicate throttle. Same goes with the rocky climb up the spur trail, to access Pueblo Canyon TH. In current conditions, there is no reason one couldn't drive straight up to the TH in a stock 4x4. (airing down with off-road tires definitely recommended) We did the bottom up approach and completed the full upper/lower route. The long scramble up the gully wasn't near as bad as we were expecting. Bushwhacking was overall very minimal, and there are faint remnants of trail to follow throughout. Once on top, hiking around first drainage, through the woods, and over to start of canyon was a breeze. This approach took us nearly 3hrs total at a moderate pace, Right away from the first drop in you can see that this is a special canyon. High walls, very cool geography. Has an ancient, untouched, old-world feel through out. Many of the rappels were in quick succession. Once reaching the water course we found a pleasant flow and great opportunity to filter water. First waterfall rappel (R7) is out of water course and easy to stay dry. However, the next one (R8) was not and we definitely got wet. However, we were plenty comfortable without wetsuits. The ruins were a real treat! Huge cliff site, and quite extensive! The largest, most well preserved collection I've seen to date. Absolutely incredible! We continued down the lower section, some downclimbs were in flow and impossible to stay dry. "The Great Hallway" was really cool! Tall slot walls, lots of unavoidable ankle to knee deep pools; cold but far from unbearable. Once out of the hall way section the water continues flowing down a picturesque, layered rock landscape. We eventually made our way our to Cherry Creek Rd. Having only one vehicle we did not shuttle and hiked roughly 45mins down Cherry Creek Rd to the spur trail and back up to our campsite/vehicle. Loved every bit of it! A fantastic adventure! One of my all time favorites! Beta has been updated. Please, enjoy responsibly!

Amazing trip down Pueblo Canyon. Drove up to the Pueblo Canyon TH. This drive requires high clearance. Started the hike at 7am. Made it to the top of Upper Pueblo around 10am. No discernible trail to the top, be ready to route-find and bushwack your way up. The entry gully into upper is able to be slid down, we didn't need to rappel in. Over the course of both upper and lower we chose to downclimb several rappels from the beta. We only replaced one anchor at the end of the lower canyon. Everything else was either in acceptable condition, skipped, or ghosted. The ruins exceeded all of my expectations, which were high to begin with. Water was present throughout upper and lower, and it was mostly avoidable. There were a few sections of unavoidable water up to thigh-deep.

Remote, Wild, and Amazing! This route was a great adventure starting from Devils Chasm TH. The road is passable to the Pueblo canyon trailhead and we could have easily shaved 2 hours of hiking off, but the road was nice and the views were fantastic, so whatever. 8 miles starting and ending at Devils Chasm. Cool surprises all along this route. On 11/14/21 a stock Subaru hatchback made it to the Devils Chasm Th without issues. Most Anchors were replaced by NClegg with bomber cairn anchors! Looking forward to doing this route again in the spring. Thanks Fd team for this canyon. Thanks Nick for updating all of the beta for the lower portion, Building all of the anchors, going first, and for keeping us alive. Zero pull issues. Water avoidable or minimal. Bring extra socks. Highly Reccomend. Bring webbing, but enjoy those downclimbs!

Time for loop starting at Pueblo Canyon TH was about 5.5 hours (canyon only - not including a hike along the ruin spur). Two back-to-back unavoidable knee deep pools in the long hallway after R5. All rappels can be done outside of the watercourse in low flow conditions.

Incredible canyon! We completed both upper and lower sections in one day. See my condition report on the other Pueblo Canyon page that details the full route. In my opinion, for the time and effort it takes to get to the TH, it is definitely worth completing the full route, and very doable in a single day.

So pretty. Did it in 6 raps. Moved the anchor at r2 to a pinch point in a secret rabbit hole chamber accessed RDC. Cairn is gone! One of the later cairns we moved to a pinch LDC. Whoever set this canyon up was really into cairns ;) Time includes checking out amazing ruins. That road in sucks.

Took the direct approach. Some spots may require a handline/spotting. The water was cold and we either had dry suits or 7 mm. We were fortunate enough to get a ride back up the hill - try running the canyon on a Friday and time your exit around the time people get off work to avoid the hike back out.

The water was not running & an employee told us that it had not rained for about a month. There are some showers coming tonight that might add some water??? There are 3 chilly pools that we had to go into about chest high & a few warm knee/ankle ones. It was warm outside so we decided not to use the wetsuit even though we carried them with us. We took the longer safe route up the wash instead of the climbing exposed route. The bushes & thorns were manageable. There are a few exposed ledges & climbs on this route but our group seemed fine & might try the fin route next time. Lots of walking on it so the trail is noticeable.

It had rained heavily in sheets the day before, which was of the heaviest rainfalls for the area in a given year. But here was no flow. Stagnant smelly water. 3 mandatory simmers. 2nd swimmer was particularly putrid and smelled like an animal had died in it. 8 mandatory rappels. Longest rappel 100ft. At 5 or 6 anchors there were some good fairly easy down climbs. The Fin approach was a bonus. Flakey, cumbly choss made the otherwise simple climb dangerous in spots. Class 5 exposure on the Fin. No baby Big Horn siting today.

The drainage of Punchbowl Canyon is not a creek and given that this is Arizona (i.e. dry) it is a bit unreasonable to expect water flow in this canyon except in rare conditions. The high quality ratings reflect the beauty of both the approach/exit hike and the exceptional (for the area) quality of the narrows. Water was not stagnant or smelly on our visit, nor was it cold enough to warrant the use of wetsuits (for most). Scrambling on the approach is no more than class 3.
via the super fun fin route. not flowing at all. several pools still are swimmers. brought wetsuits but could likely do with shorties or without if sunny. one pool had large quantity of algae.

Light, fun flow in the canyon after recent snow and rain - water was plenty cold. Parking area before the gate is getting more popular...getting there early might make getting a spot in the small parking area easier.
No flow. Still a few mandatory swimmer pools. No wetsuits. Water was cool, but refreshing with the heat and hike out.

Nice flow in canyon. Pools super full. Some of us jumped rappel #3 and #6. Water was nice, not super cold. Anchors all looked good, but a few may need replacement in the next few months. Removed a lot of old bad webbing not in use. One bolt on last rap was a little loose.

We were lucky enough to catch a ride to and from the parking area BY THE SAME GUY in a white F150 both on his way to and from work. His name is Joe and he works at the dam, super nice guy! For the approach we took the longer route up the drainage which spread out the elevation gain nicely. We also encountered a large heard of big horn sheep on the way up! Enjoy the incredible views of one of Canyon Lake's water ways as you look back! Quite a few pools, most were swimmers, cold but plenty bearable in a 4:3 wetsuit. Some pools were gross and stagnant with algae forming a thick film over the water. Updated a few anchors. Final rappel down into the "punchbowl" was fantastic. It puts you right back on the pavement where you can disrobe and hopefully solicit a ride from one of the workers driving up the road below.

Approached and exited along the Salt River via boat. Took the fin ascent route which was very engaging. Lots of exposed climbing on chossy rock. Views are some of the best in the Supes. Nice flow from recent rains with several swimmers and a few opportunities for jumps. Fresh rainwater was not too cold, was comfortable in a 5mm wetsuit + rain jacket. Others had less substantial neoprene and managed, but reported becoming cold about midway through the canyon. Webbing at the final anchor should be replaced soon, all other webbing was in fine condition. A member of our party sprained her ankle on one of the more challenging downclimbs with three rappels left to go. Fortunately our boat was docked near the base of the punchbowl and it was smooth sailing back to our vehicles.

Absolutely GORGEOUS! Deep clean pools with fresh rainwater runoff, every rappel was flowing!! We were lucky enough to get a ride to and from to the approach gully by Mr. Jeff Bennett, one of the workers replacing the power line poles near the dam! The approach up the gully is easy but a but thorny! It was very windy up top but not bad once in the canyon. I wore a 4/3 suit and was comfy! Alot of the webbing is starting to get worn and should be replaced soon. We replaced the webbing on the last rap. Beautiful gentle flow throughout canyon, super fresh and clean! Wonderful conditions!

Water in upper section was really cold. Air temp about 65 F. Lots of new webbing, did not have to replace any anchors. Be mindful of rope placement as some anchors are setup near cracks and possible rope pull issues. Was trickle flow, all pools were clean and clear.

Parked at the top behind the gate in our Prius (road in is in great shape). We were not passed by any vehicles while on the road that leads down to the river. 80 degrees to start, but peak temp of 93. Approach is easy to understand where you need to go, but bring GPS to be sure (we took approach two due to climbing abilities of group members). Steep and full of non-scratching bushes. Music and engine noise from those on the river was prominent throughout the entire canyon. This took away from most of the ambiance we seek out in canyons. Definitely a pretty canyon, but it doesn't offset the difficulty in getting there. 13 raps total, some so short it's annoying they can't be downclimbed by all in our group. Final rappel is beautiful. The trek back up to the car was in the shade, so while long, it wasn't too bad. Pools were full of algae and the temperatures were refreshing.

A top notch super fun class C canyon when flowing! A totally gorgeous gem full of high quality waterfall rappels! We hiked the road from gate to approach in 45mins. We took the fin approach, climbing on wet class 4 terrain. Must be careful with steps and hold. Lots of loose crumbly rock. I did like this engaging approach, much less bush to wack and more spice! This approach took us 1hr & 45min to get to the canyon. It had rained quite a bit a off and on for a few days prior and heavily the night before, into the morning! Water was clean and flowing moderately. Very fun, solid flow for our beginner to experience for the first time without it being dangerous. By far the most flow Ive seen on this route!! Multiple pools were full out swims. I wore a 4/3 and was on the chilly side of comfortable but not bad! Most webbing intact and in good shape. Replaced 2 anchors. Once down we hiked a 1/4 of the way up the road before a worker graciously picked us up in his truck to take us the rest of the way up. Just in time as a big evening storm started to roll in!! This route is top notch and super fun!! All the rappels are quality and the scenery is splendid! A must do after the rain!

Took the gully approach and it took us about 3 hours to reach r1. Stuck a rope on r3 and was able to climb back up, but it did add about 30 minutes to our time. We walked the road both ways and camped up top at a nearby campsite. Flow is very low compared to a week ago. We enjoyed the perfect Temps and clear water.

Team of 2. We enjoy taking our time and enjoying every part of the canyon. 10am late start. 1 hr slow hike to bottom. Fin took fin approach 1 hr to top. Fin approach easier than expected for us with climbing experience and ok with huge drop off at the top with narrow sections. Take your time. Use caution. At the top of the fin we stayed for a while to soak in amazing 360 views. 1 hr from top of fin to cross over to R1 with only two of us and full packs. Day started off hot but turned cold after sunset. Caught the canyon with good flow and spraying waterfalls! Clear pools. Cold water but with wetsuits fine even at night. I used 4/3mm wetsuit with 2.5mm vest added later. 3mm gloves and 3mm socks. She runs colder and wore 5/4 wetsuit and 2.5mm vest later, 3mm gloves and 3mm socks. Replaced webbing at R3 and extended it for easier rope pull. Sun bleached webbing on a lot of raps so come prepared. R5 120' is off one bolt very far back with tons of webbing all tied making a super long piece with lots of rapides, so lot of points of possible failure with so many knots. Next time I'd look at repacing R5 with one super long piece of webbing. R5 replaced rusted rapide that was in watercourse. Come prepared with rapides too. One rap hanging far over huge pinch boulder made for an intersting start. Locked off to use two hands to climb over boulder and hang before starting the rappel. R8 anchor in watercourse with deep pools so have to check anchor underwater. After R8, at least 3-4 more raps before R9 off bolt. One of the raps after R8, couldn't find an anchor in the dark for a 15' rap but eventually looked RDC, looking up on rock face. Stuck rope on last rappel. Did it in pitch black night. Fiddled last rappel and rope fell on shelf rock half way down. Able to climb LUC to retrieve. 75 mins to hike out. Incredible canyon all to ourselves!

Took gully to join with fin approach on the ledges. Bushwhacking was horrible in there. Had trouble finding the fin approach up there and probably got off route and wasted a ton of time in a chossy chimney (below the base of the cairn climb/traverse which was nothing after this). In hindsight, I suspect you probably should keep a little right of the line in the GPX file to find a way to the base of the cairn climb. Had 2 deep mandatory swimmers and a lot of wades. chilly

The main decision points for my group were (a) which approach route to take and (b) which thickness of neoprene to bring. Two days prior to this trip, about half an inch of rain fell across the Phoenix metro, so we thought that there would be more water in Punchbowl to enjoy. We took the non-fins approach route, and the gpx track provided on this site was fairly accurate, and there was some amount of bushwhacking up the gully and approaching the first rappel, but the middle section of the hike wasn't bad. The approach hike took three hours 15 minutes including the 2.5 mile private road section. Members of the team took 4/3mm and 5mm wetsuits, and were slightly cold on a 75 degree day. Due to recent rain, there were several pools deep enough to swim (8-10 total) and several more pools from knee to waist deep. We bypassed a few pools that were choked with green algae. The water was quite cold so the mid-wetsuit thickness was a good choice, and mid-day the canyon was bathed in sunshine for a warm-up. Many of the rappels considered to be downclimbs have anchors with installed webbing and QLs. The return hike on the road (2.75 miles) took less than one hour.

Heat between 80 - 100F on an unseasonably hot day for March but we decided to go for it anyways. We got a later start than maybe we should have for such a hot day (~8:45am), but dealt with the heat best we could; I would recommend an earlier start if attempting on a hot day given the length and sun exposure of the approach. The approach is pretty real and a fun part of problem solving and adventure! It took us about 1 hour to get down the road, and then we soaked our shirts in the creek water before starting the ascent. We opt'ed for the fin approach via the elevator, and then picked up the northern approach marked with an orange line on the klm rather than staying high since it seemed to provide more shade. The canyon was class B with several refreshing swimmers. We brought wetsuits but never put them on, and the pools had a fair amount of algae. Finished the road walk during sunset. Total approach time was about 1 hr for the road walk + 4 hr up the fin in the heat to r1, 4hr of the descent, exit 1.5 hours up the road; total time ~10.5-11 hours. We were curious about the boat entry mentioned. How long does it take to kayak from the marina on Canyon Lake or would you need a powerboat to make the boat approach?

Fine canyon. Lots of Fun. We rebuilt most of the anchors. Slow going down the creek for sure - classic rock and log hopping(falling)! We down climbed the first waterfall There is some loose rock at the top of the last big falls rappel under the big at the top. Nimbleness is required. This is mostly a free hang.

Hiked in from Fish Creek to view the waterfall (9 miles RT). Water flow was very low compared to previous pictures (see file history of main cover image for comparison). The waterfall is very scenic but I would not recommend doing the entire canyon with the lower water levels.
We did not run this but passed it on the way to Whale. Road is clear to the top parking. Water looked quite low- probably not worth doing.

Decent canyon with two scenic rappels. Occasional shallow pools were easy to avoid. Canyon doesn't get much traffic -- all anchors replaced. Added anchor for one ~ 30' rappel (optional downclimb) prior to the documented two. Late start led to a return hike down Peters Canyon in the dark. More pay than play on this one.

We entered via the described shortcut, but it's still a good hour of hiking along the creek before you reach the gorge proper. It'd be good to find another way in to get right into the good stuff. Amazing set of narrows, pools, falls, and rock formations. Be aware: R2 is a chained rappel. We pulled our rope down prematurely before realizing there was another 5ft drop just ahead. Do NOT jump this second drop -- there are all manner of hidden rocks and boulders underneath the surface. We ran Lost Creek the same day; earlier in the morning. Its about 30min away on FR 11.

I'm sorry... this did nothing for me. It was beautiful, but the rappels were short and awkward. Only a few jumps. No slides worth mentioning. Really thought there were going to be more slides. Maybe in higher water? Basically it was a long river walk with a few "good parts" but nothing that was really amazing. We did the alt-exit, go up the drainage a little bit and then crawl onto the shelf - then up the side of the hill... CAKE!
This canyon is legit. Its one of my favorites around. We had a blast
FFD (first fishing descent) completed 8/17/19. Great narrows, jumps, and rock features. Fish were biting.

We were all blown away by the completely unexpected fun in this canyon. After doing the Parkett/Lost combination for 5+ years, had never been down this stretch and it was absolutely incredible -- so much fun jumping and swimming in the pools. The water on the sunny Aug day was mod-low, and absolutely freezing cold. 5mm wetsuit recommended. Everyone in the group went without a pack except for 2 people in the group carrying group gear. Not one part of the trip could be complained about -- 5 min approach and exit. One of the best bangs for your buck you could ever hope for in a canyon.

Great day out in Quartzville Creek, water was cold but flow was very manageable for a pair of beginners. Followed beta with no issues, R1 bolt in good condition and R2 webbing anchor rigged by a previous group was in good condition.

Quartzville is really fun at this level with lots of jumps and enough current to give you a nice push through the big pools. Our favorite was the diving board off a giant log jam into a deep pool. All anchors in great condition.
Pioneered a new route that drops you in right above the entrance to the Gorge. Probably cuts out an hour of creek-walking from the original entrance.

Quartzville is fun as always. We had a romp with much swimming. More folks from Portland should make an effort to come down and run this one. The new bolt at R2b (now R3) is a nice addition. It looks to have been bent by flood events, but is still usable and makes the canyon a little safer.
Sidebar commentary: the anchors are primarily glue-in bolts, but, oddly, are linked with chains which is unnecessary in PNW canyons. It'd be better to keep the bolts unlinked as Quartzville clearly sees massive flooding during the winter and tosses old-growth logs around like matchsticks. Any material linking the bolts is liable to catch flood debris. The force of the water leveraged against the bolts can weaken, damage, or even destroy the anchors. We left the bolts as-is, but it'd be much better to remove any connective material. Ropes can be threaded directly through the eye of both bolts and blocked. (In the Desert SW, one should absolutely not do this per the issue of dirty ropes damaging the bolts when the rope is pulled. This is not really an issue in the PNW. Alternatively, a quick link could be left on each bolt if really concerned about damage to the glue-ins.) It's good to keep an absolute minimum of material on bolts when they're situated in the flood zone.

This canyon is no very technical as far as rope work goes, but its still lots of fun! Easy exit and approach(we did the shorter approach) lots of fun jumps and swims! This canyon is great this time of year when panther and big alder don't have much flow. We paired this with lost creek and had a great day out in canyons! The anchors were in great shape. Just a reminder there is a huge boulder in the water on R3. it looks so easy to slide/jump but the hidden rock in the water would for sure get you.

Wonderful day. We entered at the 'mid-point', not the uppermost entrance. First rap was easy. Second / third raps looked harder than what I wanted to take three first-timers through. Flow was 70cfs at the Galena gage, for a point of reference. I would have run it with a more experienced crew. However, we found a fairly easy portage on river right. From the top of the 2nd rap, scramble easy blocks and then steep forest slope for a bit. Then traverse, headed downstream. At this location (44.58529, -122.22971) you will find a steep grassy/mossy gully that will get you back down to the creek. There is a large log you can rap from, or you can just slide / climb down. PERFECT work-around. Rest of the creek was a blast.

What an amazingly scenic canyons….very easy peasy relaxing way to calm down after running Parkette. I would not say this is a great primary destination but great 2nd canyon for a full day. Anchors were in good shape and water lvls are great for a few jumps. This place is a photographers dream, easy approach and even easier exit.

Great run today! Beware this canyon is MUCH lower than this same time previous years. Multiple stops that’s have been solid “senders” when jumping were much more shallow this year. This canyon has tons of playful jumps that are not mandatory but check your depth first. We had a blast, we used this as our training canyon to practice sled rescue and rescuing others so we took our sweet time. Then enjoyed the jumping after!

A reasonable day in ROC. We did Spooky, The Beast, and ROC in a day. Time is for all 3. It was a group that was somewhat new to desert canyons so going was quite slow. We skipped the lower ~100 ft of ROC as one of our party fell ~8 ft from ~30 ft off the deck to ~20 ft and sprained an ankle. The canyon is quite challenging if you try to stay low. You will often progress ~50 ft down the canyon just to find that you have to backtrack all of that in order to climb up and make progress. We found the movement in ROC to be harder than The Beast. Part of this may have been that we were more tired after a long day. Bring lots of water obviously. We brought ~2 liters and our party wanted more for sure.

Its clear no one has been here in years. Found two pieces of very old webbing. We only did the upper canyon - to the base of Old Spur Falls. We did it in 4 rappels and a few downclimbs. Above the technical section, there is a LOT of wood. Once you get into the technical section, the canyon is quite beautiful. Water is incredibly low throughout the state right now, and in low conditions, the canyon is a3 - definitely not a4. Benign with relatively short rappels... but also with some good exposure to get to some of the rappels. All in all a fun day!
Really fun at this flow! All but R5 anchors were in good shape. We did not replace R5 - just downclimbed dry canyon left

We ran upper and lower. Lower is a much different beast - very deep canyon walls with much higher flow. The flow seemed to be about double what it was in upper. The 250ft waterfall is one of the tallest vertical falls in WA and features a severe roostertail that blasts horizontally out about 2/3 down. The 2nd tier of the big fall rappel has a huge curtain to duck under and a behind the veil rappel. The downclimbing from the last rappel to the hike out is some of the best in WA. I would give the upper 3-star and the lower 5-star.

A day of extremes. Overcast skies, very slippery canyon, and many blow-downs detracted from the Upper section. But my god! The lower section is amazing. A huge multistage waterfall. High flow made it look incredible, with double rooster trails and general aquatic mayhem. Despite the chaos it was straight-forward to rappel. We did it in two pitches (skipping R12). We also doubled-up the R13 single bolt to make a great exposed stance. The down-climbing below the big falls was terrible - hideously slimy and very slow progress. Then we exited DCR by scrambling up to the ridge line and returning back to the vehicles at the mid-point exit. This too was unpleasant, with loose terrain followed by steep clear-cut bushwhacking. Despite both these enormous detractors the multistage still made it worth it. I'm still convinced there's a better way to exit by climbing to the rim and then dropping to the power-station and walking out the gated road. I think I'd prefer 2 miles of flat road to climbing clear-cut, but hard to say by going off maps alone. (This is different from the original exit idea of crossing the river).

Overcast skies and temps in low 60s. Canyon was very slippery and the big log in R3 still remains unfortunately. New bolts were added at R5 to replace the pinch point webbing anchor that requires annual replacement. All other anchors were in good condition The lower section is super scenic with some rebelays and great exposure. New anchors were added at the hanging pool below the big drop so the canyon can be run with 60m ropes. Exiting the big falls was not fun but totally worth running the lower section. It was EXTREMELY slippery and a lot of down logs to manage that are new. We used the same scramble up exit as last year to the shuttle mid-point exit Great day with a solid crew!
Giant rap rings on most raps. We fiddlesticked off them as to not spend a rapide. Rap 2 has a sketch bolt in some cracked rock. Pretty good for a short day!
Well marked exit. Webbing in decent shape. Bolts on the 2nd rappel.

Really fun Canyon. We are beginners, and a little hesitant to try Death Valley canyons because we were cautioned by more experienced canyoneers that DV canyons require a lot of skill with anchor building and route finding. We were fortunate that some more experienced canyoneers were in the area to do a more advanced canyon and offered to run Rainbow with us. We were very happy they went through with us, but we could definitely have done this Canyon on our own. There were anchors in excellent condition at all 3 raps. The first was a rock anchor, the second bolted, and the third was a cairn anchor. New webbing and rapides at all 3. The exit was a little strenuous, but straight-forward and not that long. Definitely a great, quick Canyon anyone from beginner to experienced could enjoy!
Keeper pot is pretty deep, don't go in there. Kairn anchor tested well with 220 lbs
