Quick, fun canyon. All the anchors looked very fresh including the bolts on R2. Previous TR is very correct, avoid the keeper pot at the bottom of R2, it's a biggun. R3 anchor is 2 separate cairns equalized to a piece of rope. Exit was more work than anticipated, a bit steep and very loose. A Navy rescue heli flew into the canyon as I was looking for the exit. Loud.

Nice canyon. We replaced some of the webbing which was trash. Please be careful about the keeper pothole. If you go into the pothole, you need pothole escape gear to get out. Otherwise, there is a pretty exposed traverse canyon right. Not for beginners or those for without climbing skills, at least one person needs to be able to cross it and set up a handline for the others.

First anchor was different than in 2019. A slung chockstone instead of the slung boulder previously. Last rappel we fiddlesticked so bring webbing and quick link for the rappel. We found the old anchor material at the bottom of the last rappel. Likely washed over in historic rain event. Hike out was harder than I remembered.
There is a very large dry pot hole at the end. You can skirt around the edge carefully. The exit has some loose rock that you should also be careful about. Fun easy exit day canyon.
We took our time introducing new folks to the sport. Very good intro canyon with cool geology. Lots of loose rocks on the exit scramble.
The giant pothole at the bottom of R2 was completely filled in with gravel and rocks. Amazing change!
The giant pothole at the bottom of R2 is completely filled with gravel and rocks. It's gone! Amazing!

Nice day in a beautiful canyon; honestly never seen such colorful rock layers before. We replaced the webbing on R1 and R3, and left no webbing on the R2 unlinked bolts. R1 is not (currently) longer than 100ft; we were able to complete the rap with a doubled 200ft rope.
The pothole at bottom of R2 is still filled in.
Brand new webbing on all anchors. Although we ghosted the whole canyon using sand traps partner captures and spicey down climbs.
Had some nice flow.

We hiked from the parking lot and when we made it down to the creek we found it easier to walk through the creek in some sections, then trying to bushwhack around. The approach was, at least for us , a certified bushwhack. It’s possible that we didn’t take the best way, but it was a little difficult to find a non-prickly way through and we were not necessarily dressed for the occasion. We ended up doing about 2 extra rappels to protect what I would consider very spicy down climbs. There are four bolted rappels and they were all less than 100’ to the next anchor station with the exception of the last rap which is about 150’. If the next party wants to use webbing at the bolted rappels then you will NEED to replace all the webbing on all the stations. We also found on the third rappel there is a pretty aggressive Pinch point when pulling our rope back instead of yanking on it, we were able to scramble around and free it.

Grey 60m rope left at 150ft rappel! After all these years I finally pulled a rope with a knot still in... If found please contact me @ 928-848-9196 I'll throw you some cash for bringing it out! Won't be a bit until I can go retrieve it. Webbing has been replaced, bolts look good.
One or two cold swimmer potholes as of this trip.

9 hours from a camp site at the confluence of Jumpup and Kanab Creek at a quick pace by hiking up Jumpup and Indian Hollow. Most anchors in good shape, not much water in the canyon, but one very cold swimmer. The pool at the last rappel was totally dry - this pool is sometimes used to replenish group water supplies. Multiple awkward starts to rappels - as always in Grand Canyon. Dirt roads were in good shape - only the last 3 miles or so are quite bumpy and slow. Camping may not be allowed at the parking spot described in the guide book since it is within a quarter mile of a wildlife watering hole.

We were expecting a lot of water as it is early in the season but, it since it was a very dry year, there was very less flow. It appeared that by first week of July, the flow might even die leading to stagnant pools. The last rappel was from 2 bolts. When we reached there, we saw that the webbing had snapped and had shock loaded the the other bolt (the one in the front). The hanger in the first bolt was flattened out. I believe either a big rock fell on it or that last person who rapped on it was very lucky!!! Will upload photos in few days.
Please note road closure on M-1. The canyon can still be accessed by following M-2 though it takes additional ~30 minutes of driving on dirt roads.

A fun day out with friends not taking no for an answer from the rain and snow. An easy day and a surprisingly pretty canyon. Early start and early conclusion meant more time for beers at the end of the day. Good times.

We descended this on a cold and rainy spring day with a winter storm advisory in the mountains above. This creek exceeded our expectations (which were low) as it's quite pretty with lovely rappels. Easy access, easy exit, and straightforward rappels would make it a great descent for beginners or those just getting into canyoning (ideally on a warmer day!)
Fun early season shortie creek. Good for beginners or when other creeks are running high. Would have been more fun with a bit more water. Found a big pile of old webbing someone left at R1. :(
All anchors in good shape, flow was low but this is a very chill scenic run. The last two raps are really fun and make the canyon worthwhile! Would love to go back and redo in higher flow!

Rainy day in canyon, the bushwhack in is surprisingly mellow and short lived, the canyon is a short but nice one, rappels are scenic and easy, although the pull on R2 almost stuck our rope and took some work. First two quick links are very rusted, webbing on the last rappel is only half inch. Canyon could use with some updates to the anchors.

Nice flowing water, full, clear pools. Rebuilt all anchors but one. The easy approach/exit trails help speed the trip up, the 4 miles in the canyon slow things down. The technical section is quite nice, loads of slow boulder hopping between the upper technical section and the falls at the end. Day 1 took about 10 hours to camp, hike out on day 2 was about 4 hours.

We followed the Road Trip Ryan directions, approaching off of old sheffield road. There was one unavoidable pool of water at the bottom of the only rappel. We had a ton of fun rigging a guided rappel off of a meet anchor (aka the first rappeler) and seeing if we could keep everyone else dry! We didn't quite finish the loop after a few of the group members struggled with one of the climbing sections where it got quite narrow. We hiked out in the beautiful sunset.

Our first visit to Red Breaks. Did the canyoneering route from the drill hole to the main fork, then hiked back to the main fork instead of reascending the big west fork. Great scenery and a fun easy day. Rappel was well-rigged off a bush LDC. There was knee-deep muddy water at the bottom of the rappel. Dry otherwise.

This canyon has 1 rappel, that you need gear for (I’m not sure who said it’s down-climbable- it is not.) and the canyon itself is SO TIGHT that you (me, a 5’7” 130lb woman with no b**bs and no butt..) haveto take your pack off to get through 90% of this canyon. If I did it without gear (not sure how I would w the rap), it MIGHT have been enjoyable. But with gear, it was not. Not enough technicality to be called a canyoneers canyon. And the rap makes it so gear is required. Exit was all sand and then up-climbing? With packs? No thanks. Hard pass. The hype around this canyon is insane.

On Sunday, May 10, we hiked to Upper Red Breaks (3A), descending the east fork before climbing up the west fork. It turned out to be a surprisingly beautiful canyon and a fantastic way to wrap up our weekend trip. The approach was straightforward thanks to the many cairns along this popular hiking trail. We reached the hilltop marked on the map in about 20 minutes and arrived at the canyon entrance roughly an hour into the hike, and 1.7 miles. Upper Red Breaks delivered fantastic, sustained deep narrows with several fun obstacles, including a unique cave-like feature. There were also absolutely beautiful skylight views shining through the canyon’s twists and turns, while the long, winding corridors seemed to stretch on forever. There was only one mandatory rappel in the east fork of Upper Red Breaks, although a meat anchor and some partner captures could have been used alternatively. Climbing up the west fork was especially fun, with larger and more challenging obstacles that kept things interesting. The west fork was also noticeably more scenic than the east fork and ended up being one of the prettiest canyons we explored all weekend in Escalante. We found no water in either fork and had no issues navigating the canyon. Car-to-car, our group of five finished in 6 hours. #conditions

Approached via a ridgeline that runs parallel with the canyon. Brush was manageable and mostly forgiving. Some residual ice coating the walls of each rappel which also made for some interesting downclimbs. Very light flow, no unavoidable pools. Anchors / anchor material all new.
This is on private property and the cops were called on us. The owner doesn't like the risk of people climb/rappelling on his land

Our party used Rider as the exit for a short through trip that began with Soap Creek. The exit we took is best described as a sneak, exiting the Rider drainage about halfway along its length and requiring a scramble up a long slide to reach a gap in the canyon wall. This version bypasses the rappel. Some of the short (<10ft) upclimbs required creativity and partner assists to navigate.

Sections of the approach road were washed out with a few stretches of deep sand. Removed a piece of a barbed wire fence that was washed into the road by floods. Lots of mud in canyon that was mostly avoidable, and the narrows held waist and chest depth pools. Removed some fried webbing at the rappel. Packed out some trash that we found throughout the drainage. Sneak / hiker’s exit was loose and steep. Either we botched the lower section (large scree field) or rockfall has wiped out what may have used to be a trail, but eventually we picked up the path and then it was smooth sailing to the car park at the rim (though exposed and hot as hell). 1h approach hike, 2.5h descent, 2h exit. At least 1h each way for the access roads.

Fun canyon, however we failed to complete the entire canyon. Our party did only the upper section, which included some nice narrows and the single rappel. We then took the trailhead exit which cut out the lower section of narrows (or so we have been told). The upper canyon was mostly dry and pools were avoidable. However the clean pools contained a dead bat which was a bummer. Additionally there was some thick mud that one of our party members wardrobe fell victim to. Good times! I gave the canyon a 2 star because I am a stickler, however I believe my party gave the canyon an over all 3 stars. Who knows, maybe the lower section is just that good...

Webbing is good but I would recommend a fiddlestick due to the pull friction and choss. Camped at house rock rapid and walked the shorline, staying low. We exited stright up the drainage, finding the most used trail.

This route is not done very often, plan on building anchors and problem solving. The canyon itself is mediocre - nice enough, but others in the area are better - good if you're looking to do something different and you've done the others in the region. Always interesting hiking up through the hot springs with all the weirdos and naked people.

Canyon is completely dry, all pools but one filled with gravel. Last remaining pool is chest deep and bypassable. We packrafted downstream to Willow beach. A Subaru Forester had no problems on the access road but we got a 2WD Tundra stuck in the deep sand and took over an hour of expert fiddling to get it out.

Four hours (taking our time) car to car to complete the route from the end of the gravel road and paddling up river to Cranes Nest Wash to hike out. The paddle up river is easy given the slow pace of the Colorado River in the Black Canyon area and could probably be hiked in case of a nasty headwind, though it might not be very fun. Zero water in the canyon, rebuilt one damaged anchor, longest rappel was around 35 feet, but probably smart to have at least 50 feet of rope. I'm not sure I agree that the road is really passenger car friendly, and there are really no good places to park along the way if you need to stop. I personally don't think ascending back up this canyon is a great option, given how undercut 2 of the drops were and that one of the anchors is a dead man anchor. The up river packraft is easy, short and fun and the day is already plenty short so you won't be saving any time or effort.

First time through ringpin. Definitely not much going on in this canyon, but nonetheless its pretty with some twisty narrows and makes for a good excuse to packraft down to Willow. All webbing and rapides in good condition for the 3 rappels, no bigger than 30 feet.
3 rappels all anchors were good. Drive in was quite slushy. We had AWD but not HCV so had to walk 2 extra miles to and back. Still made it 3.5 hours car to car. Great for half day activity.

Did this trip with Mum and Emily. Approached via Wave Cave and then traversed over to the canyon after gaining the ridge. This took a lot longer than taking the trail, as the slope was not Emily-friendly. We almost turned around and hiked back down, but ended up continuing to the top of the 'canyon' - which is really more of an abseil route. The area at the top is pretty cool with a giant balancing rock. 4 pitches here. the pool was low and easily avoidable. The 75m pitch posed the biggest challenge as we didn't bring enough rope. We managed to retrieve it by using joining prussic cords, harnesses, caribiners, a backpack into a 10m length... it got dark whilst doing this and we walked out with a headlamp/phones.

We took the long route and went past Wave cave on on the approach. Be ready for plenty of Boulder hopping and brush. If you have a penchant for misery you'll enjoy the bushwhacking from rap 3 to rap 4. After leaving Charney Springs Trail stay in the water course until you gain the top. The track available on ropewiki lychee through a bunch of brush and holder hopping unnecessarily. The Trail is relatively well marked with cairns. Watch out for the massive prickly pear on rap 3.

All anchors looked good. Setup zipline over only pool on rap 2 to keep most dry. Shin deep water in pool on rap 2. Can avoid most major bushwacking between rap 3 and 4 by traversing right and not dropping into drainage too soon. Somewhat sharp edge on rap 3, rope manage.

The big last rap was fun and the supes has a lot of beautiful views, but the bushwhacking between the last two raps and on the exit before reaching the Carney Springs Trail was brutal! Left with a lot of battle wounds. Large cactus after the first drop on Rap 3, took some team work to avoid. We took the long approach, visiting the Wave Cave first. Lots of boulder hopping and route finding throughout.

Two hour approach, two hour canyon, one hour exit. Ghosted first two raps. Webbing good on last two rappels. Last rappel used 240' rope had at least 10' to spare. Dry. Smal amount of water on second rap, but easy to avoid. Lot of brush on exit.

Carney Springs Trail is steep and exposed/hot during all times of the year, recommend an early start. Decent routefinding skills required towards the end of the approach. Downclimbed R1, and somehow managed to traverse RDC at the base of R2 and just barely jump the pool. There was a slight trickle running over a few raps. Removed some of the prickly pear at R4. The brush between R4 and R5 is still rough in sections, though I found most of it to be quite forgiving. A few bees at the base of the final rap. They were friendly but slightly agitated when 460’ of rope came crashing down on top of them. Exited LDC on a nice hiking trail.
Light trickle of water at last rappel. Second rap pool was shin deep. Replaced webbing at first and last rappel.

Perfect, albeit a tad warm, day to go up Carney into the Roost and then down the technical exit. One person in our group did R1 while the other two replaced the missing anchor on R2 even though it can be downclimbed. R3 and R4 webbing was decent but will need replacement soon. That prickly pear on R4 is getting quite large so definitely trail the rope or drop your rope from just above. The bushwack to the last rap was a chore as always. R5 had a cluster of two faded and stiff 1 inch webbing and a faded and stiff 1/2 inch webbing--a total mess. The anchor webbing was replaced and extended to facilitate a good pull and if using a fiddle stick the fiddle stick would not be rubbing on rock. We easily could have cut our day by an hour but the weather was nice with a breeze so we took our time.

Surprisingly fun route!c, absolutely worth running. The only real Superstition thrash it has is part of that stretch between R4 and R5. The rappels are fun, the views are great, and the roost area up top is unique and striking. We took our time and had a great day. Anchors are all relatively fresh, solidly positioned, with clean pull lines. We cut and removed redundant old tat from R2 and R5, and moved the older, frozen, but functional R5 7mm li k to the very new (date helpfully written on it) webbing. Removed the newer, too-large link, cause it was so big I could pass my Attache and Am’d through it. Other R5 notes: The long webbing is great for pull angle (or, in our case, toggling). But as with anytime there’s a long nylon webbing, it becomes dynamic enough to cause rope sawing at the edge(even though the edge is not especially sharp). Consider edge protection, rope creeping, and to minimizing bounce while on rappel.

Brought visiting cousins. Good fit for people new to canyoneering, but willing to hike and bushwhack. R2 pool now near dry, easily avoided by all. Waterfall also dry. Beautiful weather! Removed the kinda crispy chartreuse extension strap at the big rap. With that gone webbing stretches and rope saws the edge much less. With the single remaining loop, my 230ish rope just touched ground when set with a toggle, and the toggle is easily set out in open space.

Look, I would have loved to do a canyon new to me, but with different fit but new family in town, not enough wetsuits for all, and limited time, the Roost was a perfect fit. Having a second experienced team member to be FPD and fireman the rest makes this a great canyon for newbs, despite the tall final drop. All dry, save a few puddles along Carney, above the Roost. We stayed DCR after R4, almost alll the way to R5, which seemed to minimize whackage. R5 webbing (not old - dated mid Oct 2025), had become surprisingly stiff and mildly bleached, enough to tip us into replacing it. R5 webbing is much more exposed to sun than the other anchors.

All the anchors were clean and only showed minor wear. The pool of water at R2 was easy to avoid. The upper portion of the canyon was draped in fog. Lessons learned: check your rope, we grabed our 200' rope not our 300' rope. We were able to tie ropes together to dessend R5 but it was not as simple as it should have been.

No other signs of anchors were found, so this was likely a first descent of the Alt-Rock tributary of Rock Creek. Ski Jump Falls must be super impressive in higher water. This tributary drops right in above the main technical section of Rock Creek and is an easier approach than the original one described for Rock Creek.

Descended Alt-Rock and dropped into the main technical section. Flow low in Alt-Rock, moderate in main Rock. All anchors replaced - would be lovely to find a way to stay in the flow on pitch 2 of Ski Jump Falls. Water in main Rock is much higher, but easy to find lines out of the flow. Don't let the 2mi hike out scare you, much of the hiking is on gravel bars and bedrock areas. Takes about 2hrs to reach the PCT from the bottom of the last rappel.

Descended Alt-Rock to main Rock. The massive ramp of Ski Jump Falls is pretty unique and Rock itself would get run all the time if access was easier. The 2mi creek-walk out is relatively easy with numerous gravel bars and areas of bedrock, plus a few obstacles to keep things interesting. Follow the path of least resistance to the PCT footbridge. Ran into a PCT trail crew at the lower trailhead who fed us snacks. One tick found.

Canyoneers on ice! This is one of the most slippery creeks I've visited in the Pacific NW. Even canyon-specific footwear comes up short here. Dropped into the creek above the bridge and headed down through the most interesting section. Probably one of the best slides near Portland on the terraces. The big drop was kicking off some serious flow. Would be fun to set an anchor to run the flow although it should be unobtrusive as this area is clearly visited by locals. Creek-walking down to the lake was a bit tedious; we had to stop and dunk in the pools occasionally to cool off. Found a much better exit along an old track that climbs back up to the powerline. Short stint of bushwhacking, but really not that bad. Fun half-day adventure.

First descent of Indie-Rock. Would be more fun with more water - probably would be more enjoyable earlier in the season - although Rock Creek will also be higher for the hike out. Interesting rock features near the fin at the bottom.

A great day in this gem of a canyon hidden in plain sight. It's unlikely anyone had been down this canyon in quite some time. Short, sweet, great bang for your buck. The truck overturned in the upper creek appeared to be fairly new. Called and reported it to the Mason County Sheriff's office.
If you find the green scooter for a good jump photo, plz rebuild the rock stack for future use 😀

Great conditions and an unexpectedly beautiful creek! (almost mod-high conditions... couldn't decide) Plenty of non-technical jumps (but a depth-checker is a must because if you jump in the wrong spot, it would be bad), a couple of short bumpy slides, and a stunning hanging pothole. Rock creek on a sunny day - highly recommend! The exit is a calf-burner and a bit dirty, but well worth it.

If you like jumps and friendly whitewater this is a great one. All but the final rap can be jumped, into deep pools ranging from 10’, 15’, 25’ and 30’. A couple friendly champagne rooms to play in. Three short bumpy slides are better to avoid tearing the wetsuit. If the final drop is bolted DCL to go down the massive 80’ rooster tail, this would be one of the best rappels in the region.

Excellent day. Lovely jumps with impressive but non-challenging water. The R3 pool looked a lot more hydraulicy that it was, no problems exiting. Surprised to find new DCL bolts on R5 that provided a lot more options, and we stayed in the flow. Superb finish. The hike out made me almost deduct a star, but it's actually shorter than I'd imagined and quickly forgotten, so overall the route gets a solid 4*.

Water has certainly dropped, but I was impressed at the flow that remained. The jumps all still went, though much lower and they'll need to be shallow-jumped. The twin jets at the beginning were still a fun feature, and the final sequence still offered pushy flow in the middle for those that wanted it. This is such a high quality canyon IMO, despite being short. This is my third time in it this season, and though conditions have changed, it's great to see that it's got at least a solid 5-6 week season, even without rain.

Always a great half-day canyon! Flow was lower than expected… the first little jump goes, the canyon left jump to the big pothole goes, the shorter jump at the end of the 3-tier sequence is filled in and too shallow. Be sure to set the rope long enough to get down. One of the big logs in the pool at the bottom of the big falls is gone. Amazing. Where could it be?? The ancient looking log is still there, though it has started to tip in a different direction. The enormous biting bugs on the exit were very unpleasant.

Great day in the canyon- the water is clear and refreshing. The 110’ rap looks saucy from the top, but it can be negotiated on the right, or take the pothole route. All anchors were in decent condition. Taking time to set rope length is still a good idea as the pools are still fairly deep. The flipped car is still there. Bring a trash bag to help keep the turnout at the parking area clean. We dispersed an improvised fire ring that presented a hazard to people trying to use the turnout.

Terrific half-day canyon in the Skykomish River Valley. Spent some time playing and jumping into the pools. Single bolt at R3 is not well placed for ease of getting on rappel; would be nice to improve the anchor situation on this one. Rappelled down the wet side of R4 to the rebelay for an exquisite final rappel. Hats off to the installation team. Route out is not entirely obvious; I should have brought some marking tape. Would be good to standardize an exit route to combat erosion.
Great day at Rock creek. Anchors were in good condition. Water was pretty cold, so take enough neoprene. All jumps went. The exit was pretty bushwhacky, thankfully short lived.

Found the canyon flowing moderate and had a great time. All the jumps looked to be clear of debris/obstacles, all the anchors were in good condition, although we did not check the webbing on the DCR option for the last rappel into the mosquito pool or the anchors for the semi-hanging rebelay since we opted to make the DCL rappel in a single shot. Pull was very clean on this and didn’t have any trouble with the rope. The bolt on R3 is solid, but the hanger is a little loose and someone might want to snug it up with a wrench next time through. There was quite a bit of trash in the canyon from people treating it like a dump and might be a good idea to put together a team/group to do a clean up some time. Water is very cold.

A great day out with a group of 7 for the 4th of July weekend! Water running at moderate flow. Added a single bolt DCL after R3 to avoid having to make the shallow jump, use human anchors in the limited space after R3, or having to do R3 in two stages (which leads to an entanglement hazard in the pool). The "Mosquito Pool" pothole is becoming stagnant and the water level is dropping to keeper levels. Recommend taking the multipitch route DCL into the final pool.

Bit depressing to find the parking area full of trash left by previous slobs with no respect for the environment. We picked up a little, but the next team should please bring a garbage bag; hopefully we can all do a little to help keep the area clean. (Our team elected not to experiment by ingesting random drugs found on the ground in the parking area.) Canyon itself was in fine form & fun as always. Couple bolts could use tightening, but all anchors in good shape. Very slippery!

Scenic and fun canyon! Waterflow was a good level for beginners. Jumps were rather shallow as pools had filled with more gravel compared to prior years (according to those who had been there before). Exit is a bit of a thrash (Grade 1, BW2 on the Cascade Brush and Bushwhack Rating System), but it's not too long. Although you suit up at the parking, some might prefer to pack a thin long-sleeve shirt for the exit (instead of wearing the wetsuit top).

Took mosquito pool route, pothole escape is easy-moderate difficulty using climbing. Messed around with some pack toss for a few minutes, poor geometry and or not enough weight (only used one bag). Exit is punchy
Great early season day out. Wasn't sure if the route was going to be low enough to be runnable but it was! A lot of features look scary but they were easy to avoid or go through.

A great day in Rock Creek on a hot day. Moderate water flow. Not much has changed from 2023. Some pools still on the shallower side with some gravel fill-in. Be aware the lip at the edge of the pool on R3. A teammate hit their knee coming off rope into this pool and required a few stitches but was ok.

Great little canyon. Super fun with some unique features. We didn’t do any jumps. Jumping seems doable in certain spots, but there are quite a few shallow areas at the current water levels. My only complaint about the canyon is that I wish it was longer. We didn’t see the sign in the beta and ended up walking a little too far on the exit, but were still able to find a relatively straightforward path out. You could easily run this twice or combine with another nearby canyon. Anchors and webbing were all in good shape.

There’s more trash in this canyon than any other one I’ve seen in Washington. It seems folks just dump their litter into the creek from the roadside. 😭 Next time I’ll bring an extra dry bag/trash bag. All anchors are in good condition. Exit trail is coming in quite nicely, we could follow the social trail all the way from the creek to the road.

I wish I would have read the condition report about the trash. We should have brought trash bags. Fun creek, protect your rope on the edge of the bridge. The water was surprising strong on the chute of R5 till it hits the end where it makes the rooster tail. We rigged it and ran it all in one shot. Fun rap!

Good shoulder-season day in Rock Creek. Canyon features themselves were really fun and water was chilly for those wearing 4/3, but comfy for 5/4. Unfortunate amount of trash along DCR past the overturned car where someone is dumping trash over the side of the road. It makes it down to the water which is gross. Had I known the in-canyon trash situation I would have taken a big pack and three trash bags to triple bag the gross-ness out of the canyon so people don’t have to see it- it detracts from the experience in a big way. If some canyon angel does clean up, bring nitrile gloves and work gloves for your protection. The exit had a lot of blown down trees- someone has been cutting the blowdown which is great, but there were three cans of half-used motor oil (prob for the saw) laying sideways next to the stream and associated trash. C’mon man. I don’t know if it was part of our community or not- we removed it. If you’re going to the effort to hike in a saw, hike out the accessories too.

Rock creek was in fun mode, although a new log obstacle has appeared at R3 (R2 if you jump/downclimb the optional R1 which I’d recommend at this flow). I was able to push aside the one log primarily impeding the rappel, but the other one still presents a strainer hazard. The DCL option for the final rappel is in extra spicy mode, opted for the mosquito pool rappel option which is lovely this time of year.
The webbing around the log at R2 is now replaced by a beautiful pair of unlinked bolts. Jumped all the canyon except R5.
Beautiful sunny day in canyon. Anchors in great condition. Jumps are in.

Rock Creek was ripping today! One team member who has run Rock early season over multiple years said this was the highest they have seen it. For those who had only run the canyon at normal flows, the feedback was that it felt like an entirely different canyon. It is COLD and even with a 5mm and a splash jacket you could feel it. No issues descending, precautions were taken (throw ropes, staging teammates, etc) to ensure that swiftwater hazards were managed. This type of management is required to run the canyon at this cfs. Some jumps but minimal compared to what one could do in lower flow. This was because 1) fun wet lines and 2) a combination of slippery conditions and high flow/swiftwater that frequently made rappelling the better option. J1 seems to get more shallow every year, so definitely depth check and spot for people. R3 was a delightful sequence that involved rappelling a few feet, setting your rappel device friction to low, kicking off the back wall and quickly dropping on rope to pass the violent boil. Team went the mosquito pool route for the last rap sequence as the alternative would have been hazardous to our health. It was truly a wonderful Lagoon! With that being said: Bolts/anchors generally in ok condition!
Water has already down to moderate flow. Conditions are perfect right now for this canyon. No issues. Appreciate the new bolts!

Great lil run, cool jumps and really fun last rappel with sweet rooster tail! Rapped in from the bridge to start. Lots of garbage thrown in from the road above. There is even a ford truck with lots of tread left on the tires. Downside is very slippery rocks and the exit isn’t the greatest.

No other anchors were found, so possibly a first descent. Nice to finally visit after hiking by this minor trib for years and being curious about it. Lots of loose rock and signs of tree damage in the lower creek; something major clearly came down last winter. While Post-Rock will never be one of the "greats", it made for an entertaining outing.
Beautiful creek -- feels more like a California creek than the Pacific NW.

Dropped in via the "Alt-Rock tributary". Will have to add beta as the approach is different, but encountered a previously unknown (to us) 250ft high multi-tier giant skateboard ramp falls. Alt-Rock enters the main fork of Rock Creek between R3 and R4, so we hit the best part of the main Rock Creek. Flow on main Rock is great currently -- fun to rappel all the falls. Needed to be a little careful in places where the flow chutes up, but not that bad. This would be a Grade-A creek were it not for the long approach and, seemingly, even longer exit. Recommend being prepared for a long day out. Our time is likely a little longer than the norm per exploratory approach (first descent?) on Alt-Rock. We found a giant seven-point elk antler at the bottom of Kala-Kala Falls.

First descent of Indie-Rock. Won't be one of the all-time greats, but an entertaining day out with a surprising amount of bedrock ramps that go on and on. Indie-Falls and adjacent spire was pretty cool. Anchored high up on the right to run the flow. Beginner-friendly. Long walk out on Rock Creek.

What can I say, the falls and geology of this canyon are amazing. Such a diverse range of rock - it really is beautiful. The approach and exit take away from it significantly. Some REALLY steep stretches of the ATV trail on the hike in and then the miles of river walking is less than awesome. Doubt I will run this again, but it was a good experience.
Descended Alt-Rock. Very low water on Alt-Rock, moderate-low on main stem.

Great day descending Rock Creek from Alt Rock. That means we skipped the first three rappels. Water was moderate-high so we stayed adjacent/on the edge of the flow. This led to fun rappels with some impressive close interactions with the main flow (like tucking under the horsetail on R7). The exit is a dream for a 2 mile creek walk with lots of bed rock and gravel bars, plus a few fun deep spots to cool off in.

Exploratory outing into this part of Rock Creek. Wished we'd brought wetsuits to rappel the falls. Plunge-pools below Tsolo look like a fun place to play. Great cataracts, side waterfalls, beautiful sculpted bedrock. Total gem of a (non-technical) creek.

canyon: Rock Creek (Upper North Fork, South Cascades)
comment: Explored the middle tributary and found +3 falls. Water in the tributary was fine, but tripled on entering the main fork. Still lots of snow in the upper drainage.

canyon: Rock Creek (Upper North Fork, South Cascades)
comment: Explored the east fork. Unfortunately only +1 waterfall. Looks like the west tributary is the best bet for running NFRC... or drop directly in from the PCT. The falls on the main line are worth it, but the cost to play may be a little high for some.

canyon: Rock Creek (Upper North Fork, South Cascades)
comment: Very low water, just a trickle at most of the falls. Deepest pool was about waist deep, but most were far shallower. Walked through a bee hive on the east side of the creek on the stretch between r3 and r4 and got stung on my legs, arms, and neck. :(

canyon: Rock Creek (Upper North Fork, South Cascades)
comment: Flow was surprisingly low for mid-winter, but much fun was had. N.F. Rock seems to be a good one in the early season with a nice south facing drainage. Temperature was 38 degrees F on returning to the car. Practiced a few rigging techniques just for the heck of it.

canyon: Rock Creek (Upper North Fork, South Cascades)
comment: I ran the lower section below the PCT. It's been almost a decade since I last did this and I forgot how much bushwhacking was involved. In my opinion it is not worth the effort. I only rapped R5 and R7 and was able to down climb or bypass everything else. All raps were ghosted and I removed some old webbing along the way.

Replaced webbing on most anchors. Low flow. One of us wore shorty and other 3mm wetsuit, which was perfect. Exited before last rappel, which was good choice, very easy to connect to trail. Exit not bad at all.

Trail up is overgrown but mostly with soft plants, nothing too stabby (yet). Water was warm and clean, quite a delight. Upper section, anchors were mostly still useable from March. Lower section we replaced most. Don't listen to the report from March of this year, the lower section is definitely worth doing and arguably more beautiful than the upper. Most drops would be very uncomfortable to downclimb or annoying to bypass. They really are better as rappels and we've changed the beta accordingly. We exited down the drainage which was a lot of boulder-hopping and creek walking but cheaper and logistically more straightforward than bringing a supplemental blood supply, in the author's opinion. One member of the party wore Walmart shoes which he does not recommend for future visitors to this canyon. This one is really gorgeous.

Fun and scenic canyon with a long approach and even longer exit! We did the full route both upper and lower. I couldnt imagine skipping the lower section, was very pretty. The 170’ rap in the lower section was our favorite rappel and definitely the highlight of the route! Approach is entirely on a very obvious trail. Most rappels were fun enough, even with the low flow, but not spectacular. The water was pleasant, clean, and not very cold. Comfy in 4/3 suit. Flow increases a bit in the lower section. We stuck our rope on the 2 stage rabbit hole rappel in the lower section. Could not pull it at all, had to ascend and recover it. Pull angle on this anchor is very bad, consider rigging off tree further back. I ended up downclimbing the first stage DCR and ghosting off a log chock in water course on the 2nd stage. Exit definitely took us a few hours, all rock hopping down a generally clean creek. Came out in the dark. Great camping at the trail head. Totally a worthwhile canyon! One and done for me though!

Stayed completely dry in upper section pretty easily. Lower section got thigh deep clean spring water pool. Rappelled lower 170' from overhanging ledge LDC instead of down main waterflow to avoid getting completely wet. Alt rappel was about 170' with very cool free hang for a long way. Exited down drainage, first half is slower going with a lot of route finding and downclimbing. Second half much faster, mostly just creek walking.
We did the lower section in 6 hrs 15 min car to car. Really great flow. Good canyon.
Lower section is spectacular with significant flow. Polished slides, undulating falls, and interesting features.

Full route. The inhike along well-maintained trail is beguilingly quick, and we made good time up the steady incline. Rappels were fun, many (soon lost count), but not quite stunning. The 170 is the most memorable. Replaced one seriously bleached anchor. The rest were in decent shape, and included an impressive variety - one on a submerged dead log, and one a massive (prob 40-50’)webbing chain off multiple chockstones. This one is low angle and could eat rope at the chockstone level, but this was avoided by running the both strands above the overlying rock layer. On the rock topic - very cool geology here, with granite and gneiss intrusions through compressed blue-green and burgundy shale layers. We had low flow, getting no more than thigh deep through the many pools by just a little deep water avoidance effort. Map says 2.7 miles outhike, but all the meandering, classic Mazzy creek dead-veg false floors, and scattered minor downclimbs make it feel and take much longer. Night caught us with at least a mile still to go, making me was really glad I’d brought a headlamp. Also grateful for half moonlight, even though it gave me a “Moonshadow” earworm. I hate that song, and spent a memorable hour or so fighting to replace it with “Red Hill Mining Town”, while picking across baby-head river rocks and trying not to get my eyes stabbed out by oak branches. Reaching the intersection of streamed and road, we dropped packs and jogged up to my truck, which I met like an old friend as if it had driven itself there to take us home. Overall, nice tech section, but the exit puts this one firmly in the one-n-done box for me.

Approach: The hike is short and dry. The approach scramble from the base of the falls is well worn and obvious to follow. Once at the apex you scramble down to the watercourse and, at this point, it becomes very loose and it is easy to knock sizeable rocks down on one another--so take care on this section. Anchors: The is a single bolt on the LDC, but there is also a tree slung with webbing RDC. The webbing is in good shape and was set up as a proper wrap 2, pull 1. The second rap is LDC and consists of two bolts on a chain. It is solid and in good condition. (The first rap can be skipped if you want to down climb to the second anchor) Flow: The water level is very low--disappointingly low if you're looking for a class C adventure--as it is slightly above a trickle. We brought wetsuits (mostly hoping to try out some new ones) but didn't even put them on. We got a little wet by staying in the watercourse but we were kind of trying to get wet at that point. The moss is slick and wet but there is bare rock in places that have good traction. In some of the wet mossy sections wasps are congregating but there moved aside as we came through.

A surprisingly nice mini-creek for the Oregon coastal range where the creeks tend to be full of woody debris. Might visit a little earlier in the season next time for some more water. No other anchors were found, so likely a first descent.

Fun shortie we paired with adjacent Rogers Ladder. I wouldn't run either of them individually and updated the map to show the link route which was basically a (game)trail the whole way. Replaced a couple anchors, but otherwise all in good shape. Took about 4.5 hours car to car, not counting the shuttle and we stopped for a lunch break before dropping into Rogers Ladder.

Fun shortie we paired with adjacent Rogers NW. I wouldn't run either of them individually and updated the Rogers NW map to show the link route which was basically a (game)trail the whole way. Replaced a couple anchors, but otherwise all in good shape. Took about 4.5 hours car to car, not counting the shuttle and we stopped for a lunch break before dropping into Rogers Ladder.
Light flow. Avoidable pools. Replaced webbing at both anchors. Minor brush on approach and some cat claw after R2, but overall not too bad.

Light flow due to heavy rains the day before. Replaced webbing on first rappel. Lots of catclaw brush after last rappel. Pools never got more than shin deep. Did Yankee and Romero in 8 hours from Fish Creek overlooking parking.

Bit brushy but not too bad. The boulder described in the beta looked washed out. When we got there the R1 anchor had already been moved to a bush LDC. The next team through may want to bring extra anchor materials to back up the bush. Not sure if the new anchor location will create issues with the pull as it is not as direct, but we had no issues using a fiddlestick. Replaced webbing and quick link at R2. Extended out the webbing for an easy pull and to keep it out of the pool.

Parked at the bottom of Fish Creek and hiked the wash for approach/exit. Approach is straightforward, not too brushy, and once you find the game trails up to the saddle it's easygoing. Both rappels are great! Exit includes quite a few unfriendly plants, but that part is over quick and then you're back in Fish Creek. 3 hours car to car.

No real flow, many of the pools were dry. We were bummed how dry it was until we did the one unavoidable swim and experienced how incredibly cold the water was, then we were glad. One short swim - anchors in good shape. Still a pretty canyon even without flow, much better with flow.
Swims at first rap.

Did it in 4 raps. For the "big" rap sequence, we rapped down to the sandy 2nd stage, where we found nice fresh webbing on a cairn anchor in the pool to get us down the next drop. We downclimbed the final drop. webbing was all good, though we set an anchor at the final drop because it just seemed too slippery and dangerous to downclimb in the flow. No sun in the canyon but water was not too cold from the recent rains.

Replaced all webbing and rebuilt all anchors. Cairn was washed away at the back-to-back rap sequence. Established a pinch / rock chock anchor about 90' down from R2 on a small 2-3 person shelf. Did another 2-stage <100' rap from here (R3), and downclimbed the last few drops in this sequence with ease. Water was fairly cold in the deeper pools but we were very comfortable in 4/3 neoprene. Air temps were nearly 90 degrees. Any hotter and I'd consider this one out of season due to the long and exposed approach.

Brutally hot approach for November. Unfortunately it was dry/a trickle despite a high reading from the Sabino creek gauge last time it was taking data (it's been offline for a few days). Still, the R2/R3 sequence has pretty rock.

Very cold water, but with the rappel only spraying you, short sections of waist deep wading with one mandatory short swim. We approached from root creek trail, and scrambled / bushwhacked on canyon right once the bottom of the falls were visible.

We used the approach all the way from the top described in the beta, since we wanted to get a hike in anyway. The views do not disappoint! The canyon is fun, could be really fun in high flow. We only had a trickle. The drainage is very localized, and only goes up to 6200' or so, so when the snow line is higher than that, expect very low flow.

Trip probably would've gone faster had I not been out of shape, and Kurt Climbed the peak once we got to the end of the trail. Then of course this anchor issue as described below. Had an issue with the pull on r6 as there is a horn undernearth the water dcl that I had to ascend and unstick the rope. r2 - tree is broken, need wrench to remove chain unless tree has completely split thru. used tree just past the water fall dcl we slung it for a good pull. exposure getting to small tree, suggest using tree further back to get to the smaller tree. That then sort of eliminates r1. Aren't a lot of trees in the canyon dcr, there was a horn that seemed usable but it had issues with all the trees and debris in front for a pull. Suggest putting a bolt somewhere dcl high above the water line. R1 chain is usable, R3/4/5/6 all in decent conditions bolts and chains good. We added a small sling after r6 for a hand line or a rap because its quite slippery.

4 day trip with 4 people. Standard canyoneering gear with 2x 220ft ropes and 300' pull cord. Used 35' of webbing to replace anchors. The roads from through Havasupai land are closed and requires additional hiking from the nearest parking area. Still a 30 mile dirt road drive to get there. Day 0: Hiked to rim of Canyon in afternoon. Day 1 Hiked down Bass canyon trail and stashed water at the trail junction for the return trip. After heading west from the junction the trail quickly becomes more rugged before descending into the canyon. Early in the canyon there is an optional 80' rap that can bypass the "ledge of death". Later we came across a dropoff with two large swimmers at the bottom. There is a route to the left that bypasses these with a marked trail and a class 3 down climb. There were a few more pools that were easily avoided before reaching royal arch. Camped near the arch with flowing water source. Day 2: Immediately down canyon of Royal arch is the rap 1. There is a rabbit hole RDC that leads to a boulder that was used for an anchor for the 170' rap. Rap 2 135' with tree anchor LDC. Rap 2 is accessed by a social trail LDC that is overgrown with brush. Short hike down to optional rap 3 20' potentially down climbable or alternate rabbit hole down climb LDC. Rap 4 RDC short loose down climb to tree anchor with 20' rap. Immediately after is a 10' downclimb LDC that is accessed by climbing over a chock stone. Can set handline on chock stone to help non-climbers. The downclimb leads to a shelf that leads to a series of social trails and cairn marked slopes that can be followed LDC to avoid mandatory swimmers and descend into Elves Chasm.

A rugged Grand Canyoneering backpacking adventure! Day 1: We drove up and met in Tusayan. We consolidated gear and people into our 2 4x4 vehicles and left the other in the woods near the highway. Drive on forest roads took us 1.5hrs to get to the abandoned Ranger Station where we car camped at the start of Point Huitzil. Roads were not terrible only 2 spots that high clearance 4x4 was helpful. Was nice to air down the tires though! Trails super narrow last half, wouldn’t recommend taking a fullsize truck, my Frontier barely fit! Day 2: We hiked in via Point Huitzil trail. The trail starts out meandering through sparse woods/fields. After 45min-1hr you arrive at the edge of the Grand Canyon. Following steep cairned scree trails down and traversing along, down climbing cracks (one of which was 30’ and a very fun big downclimb) and other obstacles, we eventually made it to the esplanade. We found the ropewiki track to be slightly off. Follow the cairns and you should be good. The esplanade continues mostly flat for awhile. Slick rock water course, traversing around drop offs. Eventually you get to some large drops that are easily bypassed by a traverse trail high DCR. In hindsight it would have been fun to stay in the watercourse at this point and rig the larger drops but no anchors were present and we wanted to save the webbing for the next day’s descent. (This would have eaten up alot of time too.) After rejoining the water course the canyon walls narrow in slightly and the long rock hopping walk broken up by many downclimbs eventually brings you to a flowing spring with pools of water, easily traversed around. Shortly after, just around a corner, the arch can be seen. Atlast our campsite! With some slower hikers in our group, we moved at a leisurely pace, making it to the arch in 9hrs. A few of us decided to climb to the top of the arch and rappel the 150’ freehang for fun! Day 3: We descended Royal Arch Canyon. First rappel is only a few hundred feet away from the arch. The canyon is decent, the 2 big raps were enjoyable but much of the canyon is bypassed by traversing the sides and crossing over portions of the watercourse. Still very pretty but I wish we had brought wetsuits so we could have stayed in the watercourse. The water course drops looked fun and no anchors were present. They were all flowing and all dropped into swimming pools. The final drop at Elves Chasm looked particularly neat. I was able to traverse a ledge and rappel into the chasm without getting wet then ascend out without swimming. The descent took us 2 hours and we spent an additional 2hrs enjoying the Chasm before exiting. Hike out took quite some time. We eventually encountered the infamous 20’ climb. The climb is rated at 5.8 and there is an accessory cord rigged to an anchor up top with tied loops to clip into that protects the first climber. Once one of us got up we dropped our own rope and provided an active top rope belay for the rest of us. We probably spent an hour here. Eventually the trail parallels Royal Arch canyon and we could see our campsite 400’ below us. We hiked the trail until the wash was only about 200’ below us and 2 of us decided to rig an impromptu rappel rather than hike all the way around. Sure enough, we read the topo lines right and my 222’ rope made it with 10ft to spare. Everyone else decided to take the long way around. Made it back to camp in a leisurely 8hrs. Day 4: The exit. We decided to split into 2 groups on the hike out. 3 of us were able to make it back up to my truck in 6 hrs at a fairly brisk pace. Quite the contrast to our 9hr approach!! Overall, a very fun trip out! Alot of hiking, much of which I found fairly uninteresting, punctuated by a few really neat parts and good memories! I enjoyed the trip, but the pay to play ratio is a bit out of balance for me personally.

Beautiful canyon, south bass exit is great but the tonto trail was not my favorite. Huitzal is a great approach. Royal arch is slightly farther down than the campsite point on the kml. I believe in Todd's guide, he mistakes a mention of the tonto trail for the escalante trail. The bypass follows the joining of the drainage following the escalante trail, which is easy to see on the right and I could not imagine going LDC. Ryan's beta is great and up to date!

Huitzil route was utilized for the approach and exit. Many beautiful riparian sections in the canyon and cold spring fed water. R1 was in good shape and webbing was replaced at the R2 watercourse anchor. There was no rope fixed to protect the 5th class exit climb near the river. My partner and I are both decent climbers, and the solo was still scary as hell. An unprotected fall here would possibly be fatal given the small starting ledge and several hundred feet of exposure below, and belay with a thin, static canyon rope did not inspire confidence. Very challenging route overall, particularly in the heat of May.
Very boring

First descent of SMC and lower falls. 5 hours car to car. We did a sneak approach on the logging road (47.54994, -121.70274) that brings you to SMC falls (47.55396, -121.70104), making for an easy bike then 30 min hike approach. Fresh downfall in canyon otherwise it was a great day in moderate flow. Plenty of anchor options at each rap and likely an escape from canyon at each rap, and an escape at the bridge, so this would be a great canyon for an early season and/or rainy day close to Seattle.

A worthwhile trip. Some beta describes a "4th class" approach but that shouldn't be the case. Our team skirted that section of the rock band to the right and found a very easy walk up. Canyon was generally dry/low water except we got wet briefly in 3 spots, no special thermal protection needed on a mild day. The bottom of rap 2 held a large pool chest deep in the center, but only waist deep if skirting left. At first I was very :( to see the water cause I'm a baby about such things and thought it would be super cold, but it was no big deal. Second spot of water was in the chamber at the bottom of the little down climb about mid-shin deep. This water was colder than the first, but not very deep. Third spot was mid shin at the bottom of the stem-to-elevator downclimb problem. Other than these spots, it was dry and not necessary for any thermal protection.
Time is car to car. The hike was beautiful, with lots of flowers. Highs in the mid 80s, no need for wetsuit. We enjoyed the narrow, dark section. Not too muddy.
Replaced or repaired almost every anchor - this canyon doesn't seem to see much visitation. One swimmer in the lower narrows, but water wasn't terribly cold.

First time doing this section for our group. Was involved in the SAR mission for an injured canyoneer here a year ago, so played it cautiously and really took our time. Did it as an out and back from Salmon River TH. Accessed river via drainage between Goat Creek and Kinzel Creek. Rappelled 5 times; replaced 3 anchors with new webbing and SS rapides: 1) Vanishing Falls; 2) rapid below Vanishing; 3) Final Falls. Noted a couple log strainers, but all near rapids and easily avoided. Water was pushy in a couple spots. Exited canyon by returning to trail 650m past Final Falls. Temperature was 5-10°F warmer than forecast for Welches.

Great day out checking out the Salmon River Canyon. Was all psyched up for a long, arduous day, and it didn't happen. There wasn't even any bushwhacking! Gorgeous canyon and beautiful falls. Not super technical, but visitors need to have their swiftwater game on. We skipped the early exit and floated a little over a mile downstream to exit up the bank right onto the trail. Beta has been updated. Edit: I would deem flow "moderate" by the standards of the Salmon River. Some pushy current in places, but the water features/hazards were challenging but relatively easy for an experienced team trained in swiftwater skills. That said, it is a real river and packs considerably more water than many other canyon destinations around Portland. Likely best to run on a 100-degree day in late summer. For reference, the Sandy River water gauge reached a high on this day of 270cfs.

I was on Kevin’s crew but wanted to share my own review, as my experience and perspective were a bit different. We started hiking at 6:30 a.m. based on an old trip report that estimated 15 hours. I believe we were changed and getting into the water around 9:00 a.m. The water was freezing—even on a hot day, it made my hands ache. I wore a 4/3 farmer john, a 4mm jacket, and an impact vest, and I still felt cold at times. By noon, the temperature had climbed over 90°F, and sitting in the sun felt amazing. I will start later next time and still plan on a hot day. Due to the high flow (15cfs salmon river at gov camp), we downclimbed around many of the falls. While we floated a lot of the route, we were extremely cautious—there was a lot of wood, many undercut boulders, and strong, pushy water throughout. This is not the kind of gentle creek most of us are used to—this is river canyoneering. Frustration Falls is pretty spectacular. We went down canyon right (DCR), and I’m glad we took the path we did—I would absolutely do it the same way again. I went first since I’m a strong swimmer. My original plan was to swim across the pool, through the whitewater, and exit downstream. That plan fell apart quickly. The current was extremely strong and pushed directly into the undercut. I adjusted my line—approached the edge next to the undercut, pushed off, and swam hard across. I didn’t get far before being pinned against the rock. Fortunately, it was fairly easy to push across the face of the undercut. I stuck my legs inside and couldn’t feel anything—it’s deep. I was very glad to have extra flotation that day. If you attempt this, ensure your entire team has proper flotation and carry at least a 100-foot throw rope ready to deploy. The final waterfall was also stunning, and at this flow, the exit path downstream was easy. We climbed out as close as possible to the trail, and it was actually marked with pink flagging tape. At this flow, R3 and R4 were the only rappels where we were truly in the creek. The rest were bypassed or done dry to avoid swift water hazards.

Soloed the Salmonberry for the second time. Love this hike although it is starting to get pretty grown over in the lower half. A group that I passed were doing minor trimming of the brush along the trail. Weather was perfect and I camped just after the final tunnel, across from the water tower on a mossy hill along the shore of the river. Hiking time was 5hrs the first day and <2hrs the second day, but I was moving fast. I did the shuttle with my dirtbike, leaving my truck at the bottom. I covered the dirtbike with a tarp in the woods at the start so it wouldn't be easily seen.

Arranged to be dropped off and picked up by a friend to avoid leaving a car at either end. Fascinating to see all of the old railroad infrastructure. At least one tunnel is collapsing, but it can be bypassed by hiking around the end of the ridge. Camped on a gravel bar above the river. Not a trip for your average hiker.

The route seems much more overgrown than last year about the same time. With minimal use, the Salmonberry is in danger of becoming a lost trail. Best sections: east end to Wolf Creek, Belding Road area in the middle, and the west end. Large tree fell and hit the Wolf Creek trestle, doing some damage. Encountered an adult cougar on the north side west of Enright. Bounded out from behind a thicket about 15ft away and went tearing away down the tracks.

The GPX data on ropewiki is flawless. Get it. Flow level perfect. Higher would be tougher, lower would be less fun. Lots of snakes. Watch your step, wear thick pants or at least thick socks. Endless bushwhacking. You absolutely need long pants, long shirt, gloves, face mask, eye protection. From the confluence up Workman's Creek is a nightmare. Slow, difficult progress. The 5th class climb out is tough and filled with cactus. Impossible with a heavy pack. After free climbing it, I set an anchor at the top and rappelled back down to ascend with packs and my partner. There is no viable camping in the canyon. We bivyed in an uncomfortably tiny spot an inch above the water in Workman's creek, above the tough climb out. Two large scorpions outside our tent. The trail up from Hell's Hole is highly overgrown for the first two miles. Lots of sharp plants and tarantula wasps. Would never attempt this canyon in a day. We camped, with absolutely minimal gear, which I would recommend. This is a hard, difficult and long canyon, with 15+ hours of hard effort of boulder hopping, slippery rocks, bushwhacking and route finding for two hours of the most amazing canyon in Arizona. Worth it.

Two of our group wore wetsuits, two did not. Chilly but doable without wetsuit. Time is one day, car to car loop from Reynolds Creek TH. Definitely doable in long day by moderately fast hikers. Water filter was useful to have. Approach trail had very little bush, except near Park Creek junction near start. Considerable amount of poison ivy in canyon, but easily avoidable. Water flow was low, but pools full. Class 5 climb can be avoided by going about 100-200 feet back down canyon from Class 5 climb to much easier climb out up canyon right. After climb out, find lightly used trail to remain out of creek for remainder. Exit trail has some over growth, but overall pretty good. Lots of creek walking and hiking for very short section of unique and beautiful section of canyon.

Tackled in one long, incredibly arduous day. The rock in the watercourse was unbelievably slick (particularly in Park) which slowed our progress considerably. Replaced or rebuilt all but one anchor. Flow in Salome made the descent moderately more challenging, but manageable. Flow in Workman was HIGH and we were barely able to upclimb some of the obstacles or swim up canyon. To bypass the most formidable Workman waterfall we picked a 40' 4th class line RUC. We ended up doing the entirety of the 6 mile Hell's Hole Trail exit in the dark, and it was heavily overgrown in sections, full of spiders, and just overall a really terrible time. The "grotto" and granite narrows are certainly quite remarkable, but I wouldn't recommend this route as a day trip to my worst enemy.

it rained the night before, water flowing at a non-C level. Everyone had wetsuits with several longish swimming sections, but chilly waiting on the one and only rappel for some. Did some rope creep for abrasion on rappel. Road in good condition day after heavy rain, not recommended but saw a Prius at the parking lot, one water crossing for the drive in.

Nice clean flow, minor baby hydraulics easy to jump in & swim through. Having a wetsuit is good so you can play in the water more since this is a water park, very comfy in a 4/3 suit with 5mil neoprene socks, lots of grip hydrolace, stopped in middle to soak in sun on the rocks for snacks, put on rain jacket at anchor while waiting 30min for group in front (due to clouds with slight breeze), spent some time setting up our own hand line to the traverse as the old line is worn out needing replacement, there is webbing for a handline to the lower ledge for the jump. Water reference point: water at drive in at first creek crossing was license plate high so lower sedans will have issue which it did last year at the same time. The report that the road crossing Salome is a low 2" for the enjoyable flow in the canyon.

Ran during uberfest 2017. Great day for the canyon. Weather was excellent at 85F, making my 4/3 wetsuit very comfortable (although I do wear 7mm neo socks and hydro pros). Some got chilly at the rap station. I found the flow to be higher than this time last year, and while I could've used more - it would've made it difficult for some of the older, slower people in the group who already were struggling to keep up. Unfortunately, one individual broke his fibula on a 6ft slide. He attempted it the first time and informed the group it was very shallow; however, he inexplicably did it again and sustained the injury. We assisted him through the rest of the canyon without much too much difficulty. A few member of the group were Wilderness First Responder certified (including the injured party), and they made an excellent splint. He was able to walk out almost on his own. At the creek crossing we had another problem. A minivan that had made it across in the morning shut off as it was almost to the other side of the creek. No idea why. It did not have trouble physically making it across, but I also have no understanding of these newfangled all electronic pieces of shit cars. Perhaps the air intake is super low and it sucked a bunch of water in. Seems like a fluke.
4wd not required, easy dirt road all the way.

Not much flow, but enough that the water is pristine and clear, there are a lot of nice slides, and the 25ft is a great jump or rap/drop. If you're not swimming, though, the water was mostly under our knees The traverse line across the ledge (see Chris Brennan's beta on the main Salome Jug page) to the jump is still there...in triplicate. The rigging here is very messy; there are also a number of long, knotted hardware-store ropes, like people were trying to handline down the chute. In summary, gorgeous, but not challenging or technical. 3/2 or 4/3 wetsuit was fine.

We descended this canyon at night to take advantage of clear skies, a full moon, and some night photography. It was fantastic done this way. (Otherwise with the low water it would have been rather unsatisfying).
Very Low flow. Slime in some of the pools. The hand line has been replaced with a thin metal chain.

Removed the chain between stations and hiked it out. It was un-welded link, twisted for tension, and if anyone truly slipped and hung on, the chain would fail. chain was also 20' too long. I left the rope at the other station as a jump aid. All anchors are in good condition.

Water was not flowing and the algae had bloomed in 50% of pools. Swimming was still required but not especially enjoyable. Somebody had left a bright green rope tied to the rap station; the rope was a boating rope, specifically for towing people on tubes... it was sun faded and precariously hitched. I removed the rope and packed it out. Whoever set it up was fortunate their improvised knot didn’t pull.

The road coming in had about 8" of water across 100 yards of a wash. We crossed it in a vehicle with medium clearance. We brought mountain bikes and biked in for the first two miles of the trail. Definitely recommend it! Water was nice a cold and clear, and it made for a great day!
Water was very gross and cold. Wet suit strongly recommend. Water was not flowing but many large pools still remained.

Terrific little canyon. Flow and company were great. There was much enthusiasm and jumping into pools. Doesn't have to be super technical to be fun. Access is very straightforward... even with a sign indicating the exit.

Took a smaller group May 6, the bigger one May 18. Water low and getting slimy, especially by the 18th. I wouldn’t go any more this year until a decent rain clears it out. Beware: between our two trips, some oxygen thief defecated right next to the waterfall anchor, fouling the usual awesomeness of the waterfall rappel. Another reason to let the slot have a flash flood before you go.

Water temperature was very comfortable. Had a full wetsuit but could definitely have went without it. Fixed lines in place at the jump. Many large wolf spiders throughout the canyon and a rattlesnake along the exit trail. Time is car-to-car for the Jug only. We combined with Phog which we ran earlier in the day.

Fun and very beautiful little canyon. Even with only 1 rap IT IS WORTH CHECKING OUT! Lots of swimmers throughout. High, sculpted, picturesque slot canyon walls! The only rap is near the end and goes into a very large deep pool that is fun to float and hangout in. We brought some beers and chilled here! Definitely a gem! Approach and exit took about 1hour each way and is easy but very boring down an obvious hiking trail. There is even a sign in the creek telling you where to exit!

Great flow, great company, great time. Lots of 4 inch spiders and wasps. Drove a sedan to the trailhead from both directions. Water crossing from the west was passable and maybe a foot deep. Crossing from the east was a wet concrete slab. Water was a great temperature, never needed wool socks, neoprene socks, or wetsuit gloves, though others there reported the water being really cold. Measured 58.2°F. Drank 1L of water.

There was clear water in the pools but quite low flow at the rappel. Nearly had enough bottles by the end of the route to build a raft. We removed a couple sections of tattered handling and cleaned most of the mess at the bolts.
The anchors at the jump location are getting a bit worn and starting to spin. The traverse line/rope looks like it's also in need of replacing.

The water level is still good enough to make the slides possible and the jumps are in good shape. The webbing at the rappel is about in need of replacing. The water temp was great. I wore a 2 mil top and the rest of the group just ran in shorts and tees. Had a bit of a mishap and sacrificed a shoe to the canyon gods in exchange for returning my foot to me. Got lucky.

Perfect conditions right now, with warm air and clear, flowing, but not too pushy water. Cold enough to make my skinny friend shiver by halfway through the slot - very thin/easily chilled people will be happier with a little neoprene on, but we and everyone we saw were in shorts/tees. Anchor webbing was fuzzy, not quite frayed. Someone fixed a 10mm real (not Home Depot) rope at the rap. We pulled it all up, found it in good shape, and used it. Looked like actual technical rope was also set for the handline to jumpoff. Found an iphone at the bottom of the big pool and was happily able to return it to its owner. Gonna start calling it Davy Jones' Locker. I've found enough stuff down there over the years to encourage everyone to zip up and tie down everything before you jump or rap - Apple Watches love to snap free and sink.

Water was balmy; no wetsuits or wetsocks necessary; not even wanted by most of the group. There is one down-climb that a canyoneer will easily solve but is heady to the uninitiated. One person got their foot and knee wedged in the crack of the waterfall. Much better to stem over the water. Recommend using the jump station to top belay beginners because the waterflow makes extraction tricky. Watched someone outside of our party slip on the traverse onto their butt unharnessed. It is slick when wet. Replaced webbing at the waterfall and cleaned out accumulated tat. There are three solid bolts at that station, one glue-in and two expansions. The hangers of the expansion bolts are bent out of shape and should be replaced. It is unfortunate that webbing has to be used. After crossing the traverse line, there is a ~15' section of rope to reach the jump point. It is core shot and should be replaced. Maybe 15 cars were there today. water was still clear.

It's the perfect time to go. We were comfortable in workout clothes. We rappelled down, but the small group after us had no trouble making the jump. The anchor at the top is a bit loose, moderate swimming skills needed at the end.

The water level looked low at start, but it was flowing well throughout the canyon - Stopped to have fun with niece on two of the 'water slides' on the descent - Second time through here, opted to skip bringing ropes this time - all 5 of us jumped off of the rock ledge near the rappel - very exhilarating - very popular, no issue getting to or from the canyon

Low flow was meh. But it's still amazing and jumps are still in. Chilly if spending a lot of time in the water. Definitely worth jumping the one rap but the approach is indeed spooky. A proper fixed handline would help that a ton. Right now it's just a mess of webbing to protect individuals.
getting algae but still a tiny bit of flow. dry culvert on the east. west side road to A-cross is blocked off, you have to go up to Bar-X and miss the culvert check there.

We went with a large group from university of Arizona started on trail at about 9:45. The flow is still plenty to have fun and kept the pools pretty clean later on in the canyon. The water got a bit colder, outside temperature was in the 90s, but we would occasionally get some overcast. I would say to bring a wetsuit if it’s anything else than a hot sunny day. All the jump and raps look good and can still use the slide with some help. We ended up using the high flow anchors for as it was some people‘s first time.
Glad to have full wetsuits on for this one. The air temp was great for hiking in/out. Canyon was pretty. Anchors were in good shape.

Low flow, yet still higher than anticipated given extremely sub-par snowpack. Algae blooms are already in full force and if record heat conditions persist the creek is likely to soon be quite swampy. Bolts at the anchor station are in questionable condition but traverse line itself is in decent shape. Carried out some junk rope and hardware that was left in peculiar location anchored from a healthy sycamore. Only encountered two other groups today, which is remarkable given it was a Saturday and weather was pleasant. Water is still cold and full 4/3s were sufficient.

Usual “everyone’s cold by rappel time, but warm soon as we start hiking out” cycle we usually have this time of year when hiking in swim trunks, no wetsuits. Shorties would’ve been more comfortable. Lots of algae, but water still flowing a little, and slides still slideable! Longer than usual time, cause a big group of mostly brand new canyoneers just takes a minute, even with a twin releasable rig. And cause I had our team send up some harnesses to lower 4 guys from nontechnical groups around us who were extremely anxious about the traverse and jump. Rope salad at the station. Glue-in still looking good. Older hangers are flattened. 11mm pink traverse rope is too slack, and granny-knotted to the glue-in. Orange 10mm rope only reaches about halfway down the waterfall crack. Removed about 20’ of yellow 9mm wedged midway down the falls. Found an iPhone (Jessy as owner is all I can tell on it. Anyone? Also an Apple Watch. Please find me if you can help me return them.
This is a canyon that can be run during Spring. R2 in higher flows should be rappelled without a pack on and the rope likely will pinch. The last pothole was not dangerous and easily escapable.

This TR is for the first descent of the East Fork of Sardine Creek. The East fork has significantly more interesting drops than the Main Fork. The rappel sequence is mighty fun as it features 4 drops back to back and the canyon makes several turns. Water levels were perfect for the intermediate canyoneer.
No need to worry about where you park (at least not much), just pull off when it's convenient.

We ran a group through the East fork. There wasn't much water, even with a small overnight rain. Because some of the group was new to rappelling, we "rappelled" two drops at the start of the canyon: a slide on canyon left and immediately ~25 ft off bolts canyon left. Not quite as incredible as my first time through, but still a great way to spend a Saturday! I wonder how much longer that dark green webbing will be in such great shape... Rumor has it the webbing was placed two or three seasons ago—still looks new today! The East fork portion took 2 hours, then the rest of the canyon (including lunch) took the other 2.5.

Descended the lower part of the main fork. Channel completely dry from where we dropped in, but picked up further down. Must be flowing underground / under rocks for a long section. Maps show more waterfalls farther up the main fork.

We did the main fork. It was a lot of fun. We only did 3 rappels, R2, R4, and R6. We easily went around the other ones. I would say neoprene socks are close to a necessity at this time in the year. we didn't have them and we froze our feet off.

First descent of the upper main fork. This is hands-down the best section of creek in the Sardine complex. Lots of waterfalls in quick succession and beautiful bedrock features. Found a cow skull tied to a tree at the exit which made the whole experience that much nicer. :-) Con: the upper section takes more effort to reach. Would have been nice if the flow was higher... Sardine is probably solidly "in" somewhere in June timeframe. The upper and lower sections could be combined for a full day with a fast-moving experienced team. Took us 1.5hrs to hike up to the top. Arguably hiking beats a shuttle on time.
Little bit of a walk without a shuttle but it's pretty easy. Lots of multi-tier drops make for a fun day. The creek was one of the slickest I've encountered.
Very low water. Not very exciting. Many different combinations of rappelling and downclimbing possible depending on water level and skill. 5:50 for both Upper and Lower.

Gorgeous river canyon. Flow was pushy in places, but not that bad. Most of the rapids can be bypassed on one side or another. Lower canyon was sublime. The Olympic's answer to Oneonta Gorge in Oregon, except with zero tourists. Ran Middle Fork Big Creek and the Big Creek Narrows earlier the same day.
A nice chill canyon in the Olympics, although a bit of clambering around tree debris.

A light trickle over the falls and beautiful frozen river between R1 and R2. Downclimbs and general navigation more challenging due to ice. All webbing is new. Anchors place you directly in the watercourse. R2 is IMO one of the best raps in the Anchas. Time is car-to-car (not including driving time on shuttle road).
Fun canyon with a really incredible 2nd rappel conditions were icy and trickling at the time of descent.

Some lightly flowing water over R2 and a couple ankle deep pools. Quite lush from recent rains. Fixed ropes and ascended back out. Placed a few pieces of edge pro along R1 but only one piece used for the lip at R2. It rained for a bit on the exit and we took shelter underneath a roof at the base of the R1. Hiked out by headlamp and witnessed an incredible lightning show taking place in the distance.

I guess it was a thing to do back when I was young and felt like rappelling every pour-off in the Anchas for no particular reason. These drainages have seen a decent bit of exploration over the last couple of decades. Most of them we never found worth repeating, particularly with classics like Parker close by.
