The rock quality is generally poor. You can climb in and out of the drainages between most of the drops or jug a fixed line if you need to. Definitely not worth hiking out Cherry Creek or setting a shuttle.

This is a really great mini route that holds alot of value if you like ascending or want ascending practice. While short, the features within are really picturesque and the rappels are really nice, especially the last one! Approach is fairly quick. We rigged both drops as fixed lines. 1st drop webbing on tree needs replacing, we rigged directly to the tree instead. 2nd drop we rigged directly to the 2 bolted hangers using alpine caving techniques (2 lockers and an equalized fusion knot). If planning to pull your rope, bring webbing to rig an equalized anchor as there is no webbing rigged here... 2nd drop is a really nice nearly instant free hang!! Huge bang for your buck if you are in the area!

We completed Scotty's Hollow as part of a three day trip in the Kanab Creek area: day one Kanab Point route, down Kanab Zero. Day two Whispering Falls via the sneak route, then up Kanab Creek and up Scotty's Hollow to above the chockstone upclimbs. Day three finish hiking up Scotty's and take the Scotty's route to the rim in between 4 and 5.5 hours collectively for the group. One anchor was present at on of the higher, farther upcanyon chockstone climbs. Some deep wading, one or two short swims. The climbs take some teamwork.
Egress from Kanab 0/Whispering Falls Canyon trip. Gorgeous canyon with many nuisance upclimbs.

Beautiful canyon. Up-climbs were challenging with heavy packs. Waist depth pools, comfortable water temperature. We started up the canyon from Kanab Creek on Sun evening, filtered as much water as we could carry at the final spring, camped above the confluence of the north and south forks, and exited up the remainder of the south fork Mon morning (starting at sunrise to beat the extreme heat). From our camp to the rim was only 2.6 miles, but the off-trail exit route was steep and rugged and took us three hours. Over that course of time one member of the group drank 5L of water, yet by the end of the hike was still showing early signs of heat exhaustion.
Did Secret + Packraft 3m + Arizona Hot Spring. Cloudy and a bit cold on the river, was good to soak in the hot springs afterwords.
nice warm 75F day, took a variation that started with R1 about 2 minutes from the parking spot, took a long sit down at the river

great half day trip from the Kingman area. we did the alternate R1 literally 2 minutes north from the parking area, pulled the rope, fixed the other 2 raps for ascent and exited up the old dirt road back to the car. side note on alternate R1...a competent climber can downclimb the first section to another set of bolts DCL. From the second set of bolts to the ground is ~60'. All anchors are bolted and in good condition. I would highly recommend fixing ropes and ascending back out. The exit from further down the river seems like a lot of work and will add a good bit of time to your trip. Our time was at a very casual pace, observing wildlife and hanging out at the river for a while. this canyon can be done car 2 car in 2-3 hours via ascending the ropes. There wasnt any water in the hot springs but a dip in the river was perfect on a 97 degree day. The canyon was full of life..many lizards, bighorns and chuckwallas. Overall a fun day in the desert.

Followed the wiki KML exit route by paddling a short distance from the mouth of Secret to Palm Tree Springs and then hiking back over into the canyon via a well traveled and well marked path. Secret itself had some interesting geology and a trickling warm spring after the last rappel.
Team of 7, 5 of which had never ascended before. Perfect day to go out and teach a new crew the basics of ascending.
Time includes a descent of Secret and packraft to AZ hot springs. Very few people along the river and surprisingly low traffic at the hot springs.

Several pools, most avoidable with climbing and crawling along shelves. Some deep, knee+ depth sand just prior to the rappel. Large pool at the base of the rappel was avoidable, but another further down canyon would have been a mandatory deep wade or possibly a swim. Ascended out. One notably tricky up-climb where some may require a partner assist.

Route makes a great option for an easy overnight along the river. Easy walking down the drainage to the point where the canyon narrows, we downclimbed through the rabbit hole and rappelled the rest of this downclimb as we had overnight packs. There is a large limestone arch that works well to handline or rappel this feature. The actual drop has two bolts on DCR, we linked both of these with knots for ascension on day two. The pool below this drop can be bypassed with a narrow shimmy on DCL. From there navigate the boulders to the beach. There are great camping options along that whole beach, rafters may occupy the camp on the downstream end but it seems like good options exist above. Ascent out was aided by rigging a 3:1 to raise overnight packs instead of ascending with them. Finish your adventure with desert sweet sauce wings from Cliff Dwellers Lodge down the road.

Second descent? This canyon was a backup plan that turned out to be a gem. Excellent conditions, though very smoky due to the Creek Fire. Water is cold (outflow from Shadow Lake) and flow is solid - an experienced team could run it in higher - but not by too much, as several sections get narrow. We had to rebuild all anchors. Traces of shredded webbing (from the 2016 first descent?) remained at two. The final mandatory rappel was quite pushy. Awesome day and team!

4.5 hours car to car. Hiked out through the hills of the other drainages which is a pain - would be better to hike down Teakettle Wash, then hike up to the head of the south fork to descend, then check out north fork out and back.

We were inspired by some recent posts to go do some class C canyons in Arizona. I thought Death Valley is all avid canyoneers can do in winter, but no, February is actually prime time for Arizona class C! I did not know that before! We used Ropewiki's Waterflow Analysis the day before to find out this canyon was flowing Moderate-High... we choose Shake Tree over Parralel Play because of that!

Flow was good. Water temp wasn't too bad in a 5/3 with a rain jacket. Look out for rope pull issues. Built the anchor for the big rappel in the watercourse as a cairn, make sure you check it before you commit.

Great flow after all the recent rain and snow. No hydraulics or other major water obstacles to worry about, water was plenty cold. Most of us wore 2mm shorty wetsuits under either 3/2 or 4/3 full suits and were mostly comfortable. Rebuilt a few anchors including the one for the big drop which is a deadman anchor that should always be inspected carefully since it is in the watercourse and will shift over time in the flow. Super fun!

First time through the canyon. Lots and lots of bushwacking. Group before use had a rope get stuck on the rap after the long rap. In full wetsuits we still got pretty cold. There was still snow at the top of the mountain. 10 rappels, all flowing. Due to our late 10:30AM start, we had to hike out in the dark. Fantastic trip.

Warmer than the usual spring flow water. Low, but fun enough to get in flow without getting overly blasted. No swimmers, everything can be walked across. All anchors and webbing in good shape. No rope pull issues.
Great water flow conditions. Pretty big flow conditions. Replaced worn out webbing at rappel #6. All other anchor webbing looked good. Water was cold.

Do not use coordinates from rope wiki, it took us up the crappy jeep road, turned around. long rocky hike up with bush wacking down. long rappel could be done in one 165 ft. pull good. Three spots had bad pull features and last person had to convert to double line rappel. One spot had webbing on upstream side of 8 ft boulder. Hike up is long and brutal with 4 mile walk out. Rappels not worth the effort. Overcast and windy, it would be brutal if sunny. This canyon is fed by a very small spring

Absolutely fantastic!!! We took the 4x4 sneak. Which took 35mins. Lifted Xterra and lifted 4runner, both on 33” MT tires, aired down, had no issues. Easy 4x4 trail with the last hill scramble being a chill moderate where 4lo was helpful. Nothing a stock 4wd with good tires couldn't handle. Canyon was excellent flow and an ABSOLUTE TREAT! Super scenic, beautiful high walls adorned with plant life. Rappels come quick one after another, the big ones were especially fantastic! Plenty of opportunities for slides and easy stemming downclimbs over the swift water. We cleaned up brush, replaced and adjusted anchors. Had no rope pull issues. Technical section took 2.5hrs I wore a 7mm and was plenty warm. My partners were in a 5mm and a 4/3 and were both comfortable. We took an alternate exit an followed the creek a ways bush whacking as we went. We exited just south of where our vehicles were parked. This proved to be challenging, steep, and super loose with lots of cacti/agave in the way. However it sounds like the other exit is the same way and we enjoyed spending a bit more time in the creek going the way we did. Overall we HAD A BLAST! Such a fun canyon! Big waterfall raps, super scenic, lots to play on! Dont listen to the naysayers, this is definitely a must do! (Yes its better than Christopher Creek)

Low flow made for fun and comfy conditions. Not too cold with thin wetsuits and rain jackets. The few downclimbs were fun. This canyon was fun and memorable. All anchors looked great. No issues on rope pulls. "Standard" approach seemed like just that= Standard for an AZ canyon like this. The rapps (with water) are definitely worth it. Would come back for moderate flow after checking out the rest of the options in the area.

Warm day but the water was still pretty cold. Anchors are either in great condition (the longer raps) or getting a bit borderline (though always redundant). We didn't replace any webbing but could use some replacement before too long. The exit was a bit annoying but we avoided some bushwhacking by heading straight north out of the exit drainage once the track turns west.

Would be good to establish a standard route in and out of the drainage. Ropewiki maps do not appear to offer the best approach once you leave the trail. After the final rappel, we continued downstream a short distance, then exited via an easy trail DCL. We headed directly up slopes to the north to eventually intersect the sneak approach, then west back up to the main trail. This approach is steep, but appears to avoid a lot of brush.

Canyon was almost entirely dry, as suspected, but several clean and refreshing pools up to waist depth. Approach and exit hike from Barnhardt TH was exposed and hot this time of year. We all drank about 3 liters of water. Replaced R2 webbing and removed junk webbing from a couple anchors. 1.5h approach, 2.5h descent, 2.5h exit. KML has been revised.
Replaced webbing on R3. Only a few pools with max depth about stomach high. Took time learning and practicing skills.
Turn my swag on

Great canyon! Perfect bit of flow to make for an enjoyable time! This time we approached via Y Bar Trail from Barnhardt TH. Hiking was easy but this trail feels like it takes forever. Having done both approaches/exits, Id say 4x4 approach is definitely quicker but more bushwhacking through creek on exit and probably a more obnoxious(but shorter) scramble up and out. Wore 4/3 wetsuit with wool base layer and rain jacket. Was plenty comfortable. Scramble up exit drainage was easy, lots of large boulders to climb on and soil at the end is firm. Total 7.5hrs Kevin Iosue lost his green beanie on the trail, if anyone finds it, reach out! His mom knitted it for him…

Beautiful conditions in Shake Tree. Hiked through some light mud and snowmelt that was contributing to the moderate flow. Merino wool base layer with a 4/3, spray jacket, and 3mm neoprene socks was enough to be comfortable.

Perfect slot for a winter day! One of those otherworldly spots it’s difficult to believe is this close to the Valley. Between the water, the big hardwood trees, and unusual plant life, it’s visually interesting. Add a rapid stack of flowing water rappels, and it’s pretty awesome. We struggled with rope pull on the 165. Thought about hauling from the next anchor with a pig rig, but opted to ascend instead. So, up the big falls… where I found nothing more than a minor rope twist. In a couple other spots, we learned to respect the class C, as water found ways to force lengths of neglected rope into cracks in the blocky rock typical here. We did the 4x4 route east enough in a stock Tacoma. Inhike is easy. Outhike kinda evil, starting with relentless crumbly deadwood along the creek, and ending with a short but steep and kinda prickly climb to the parking spot.

Great canyon! Some really cool rappels, made spectacular with flowing water. Well worth the drive from flagstaff. Water felt good with about 5mm of neoprene and Merino base layers for both of us. Based on other comments about rope pull issues we used a fiddle stick on the big rap and used a CEM on the rap towards the end that goes around the corner. This was smooth and easy in both cases.

Mellow, straightforward descent with light flow throughout. We took the approach from Barnhardt TH. Overshot the entry by 1/4 mile and backtracked in. Water temps were comfortable with light wetsuits. All rappels flowing nicely - mod low based on comparison photos. No challenges, just great scenery. The exit hike was a steep slog, but not nearly as unpleasant as I remembered.

Started at Barnhardt TH. Super mellow, gradual approach.2hours exact. Feels like the trail just KEEPS GOING. Straightforward rappels. All webbing good. Water felt amazing with light 4/3 suit. There's brand new bolts along the sandstone wall in-between raps 2&3. Curious who is developing there. CTC 8.5hrs

This canyon is pleasant and has some nice raps, but I was not blown away by it. With 3 snakes and some cool water beetles I'd say wildlife was the highlight of the trip. We didn't need to pull out our wetsuits when we did it in late April.

Prius to TH parking - approach feels long - kml to easily spot left off of trail and down into canyon - some edges near anchors to keep an eye on - mainly walk-down rappels - a few deep pools felt great in the summer, no wetsuits - We all had a blast, and the third rappel is the best of the bunch.

6 hours car to car. 1.5 hour approach. 3 hour canyon. 1.5 hour exit. All anchors looked fine. We did not see the bolts mentioned in past report, but we also weren't looking. Flow was good and really fun. We wore combos of thin wetsuits and vest. one member wore a 7mil and rain jacket and was super comfy. If water is flowing on in the drainages on the approach, you can be certain shaketree will have some flow.

A top notch route for the area, with these conditions!! Air was warm on approach and exit. I wore a 4/3 with cave suit in canyon and was comfy. All anchors good. Flow was perfect!! The big drop feels huge and if rappelling DCR you get dumped on!! 7.5hrs

The canyon was flowing moderately, pretty cold, nothing deeper than waist deep. 4 people, all in 3 mm full wetsuits with rain gear over and we were all comfortable in spite of the air temp being less than 60 that day. 8 hours car to car at a very leisurely pace. We used a 200' rope and pullcord. no pull issues at all. We got off early on rap 8 and did the downclimb to avoid what looked like could be a tricky pull. The hike out is crazy steep and annoying, but we were able to avoid most bushwhacking. Some of the webbing is getting a little crispy, may need to be changed out soon, some looked brand new. Absolutely worthy canyon!

Slow pace, good day, wish I went without wetsuit, extra weight was not my favorite. Put it on just before the big rap and took off after R7. Sweet and cool canyon, flow is low enough to allow some algae pools to develop. Too many mosquitos in the area for my liking, another rain would pick this one up to be a fun day. Exit kml is spot on and advisable.
We took our time practicing building different types of anchors. This canyon is better off for little kids - explore the other nearby options if you are looking for a more challenging/scenic canyon.

Sheepbone was ascended with a climber to inspect the various rope/webbing before the rest of the group went up. The hand lines that are there have some wear which have various knots to isolate the damage. For the first climb & various others we used on own rope to ascend once the climber got up instead of the hand line.
Saw a rock with legs! = desert tortoise on approach. Otherwise, the climb up was more fun than the descent.

All the ropes in the approach are in good shape. It was a little bit hot as we started a bit late but once in the canyon the shade provide relief from the sun. We did not have any issues with the ropes section with the exception that some of the climbs required multiple attempts to get up the walls. The canyon anchors are all natural with a few cairn and tree anchors. The last rappel is about 60ft and the cairn anchor is safe but could be built better to allow for the webbing check to be faster. This is an entertaining and fun canyon.
Fun scrambles on the way up. Fixed lines are in good condition. One is a bit tricky, I used rock shoes and brought my brother up on a rope. Anchors on the way down are in good condition.
Great day. One of my favorite Canyon approaches. Most of the anchor Webbing are fairly new (1" black). A couple drops with Green Webbing that's still ok but should be replaced soon.
Fun upclimbs! Fixed ropes were in good condition

Super fun and engaging route requiring relatively little effort to access. We drove our 4runner all the way down the dirt approach trail almost right up to the first climb. The narrows is fairly pretty and the climbs are easy and fun to do. All climbs have fixed ropes installed and are in good shape. Once past all the climbs it is easy to miss the scramble out of the drainage to the ridge line, keep an eye on the GPS file! The rappels on the descent were fun enough, each being straight forward, down clean walls. Many of which are off well built cairn anchors that always require full inspection. All webbing seemed fairly new! Very nice views on the ridge line and on the descent. Some neat rock formations throughout! We did not have a 2nd vehicle to shuttle with, so we cut over cross country, down the wash, following the powerlines around the base of the mountains, picking up another access trail, then over until we picked up the trail we drove in on. While there are certainly prettier canyons over in the Redrock area, that is roughly 1hr away. So I give this canyon 4 stars for ease of access and huge bang for your buck on a lovely half day adventure!

Rebuilt one cairn anchor at the drop-in to Quarry. Somehow ran into another group at the final rap sequence despite our large team and relaxed pace. Sheepbone was more scenic than expected and the climbs were a lot of fun. Handlines were in fine condition.

Had a great half day with a couple of newbies in the group: it was just the right difficulty level for them. Fixed lines in Sheepbone are all in good condition. R1 into Quarry has red webbing that is probably nearing end of life; we decided not to replace it and then regretted it later. The rest of the anchors were in great shape, many with fresh black webbing.

Another great run of this super-fun canyon combo. Sheepbone - All handlines seemed fairly new and in good condition, even though most of them are not needed at all. You can easily free-climb most of the falls. Quarry - All anchors were in good shape and fairly new. We did some top-rope climbing on 2 of the waterfalls. Bonus - On the way out, we went into the water tower, climbed up the ladder inside and rappelled of the top. Such Fun!

Pretty standard S/Q. All but the last fixed lines in Sheepbone were good. We removed a core shot from the uppermost fixed line, but some of the butterfly knots have worn through the sheath. It’s time to replace, but we didn’t bring any. We used the alternate entrance into Quarry. We replaced the first sling. On the second stage of this rappel, we toggled the tree. The webbing here was a little faded and we only used it to clip our tethers while rigging below our toggle. (Aside: I preferred the standard entrance.) The rest of the webbing was fine, though we CEM/toggled everything for practice and we did skip one anchor completely because our pull cord made it easy to do so. The last cairn wasn’t DV-style inspectable and we ended it up rebuilding it completely due to biggest rock being loose. Unfortunately, we also didn’t build it inspectable (sorry).
Not a major canyon destination, but a fun shortie creek.

Fun trip. Probable first run through as we didn’t see any anchors. Great for winter/ spring trips when the snow is cutting off other cool places. Be mindful of the flaky basalt on the big drop as it is incredibly hazardous.

Nice little creek, though as with other smaller creeks the creek walking borders on bushwhacking. The big drop was really nice, gives you a good shower even in lower flows. We used the anchor high DCL, puts you right in the flow the whole way down. We removed a very marginal anchor at R3, webbing slung on a dead, downed tree right at the pourover. Be very aware of rockfall, last person down dislodged a significant sized boulder halfway down the pitch.

Four hours for the north fork route. The first drop in the northern fork seems to be commonly bypassed on the right (there was no anchor, bypass is easy), after that there are a few downclimbs that some may wish to use a rope to negotiate - they are not tall, just steep - before reaching the rappel down the chute. You can also scramble around a bit to find easier ways around the steepest parts. The northern fork really only consists of this one significant drop before reaching the main fork of the canyon, by which point you have bypassed some of the best parts of the main canyon. That one first drop is quite nice, though - and certainly worth doing at least once if you have already done the main canyon and are looking for something new.

Time includes both Foxtrot and Sierra canyons, approaching and exiting via a drainage that begins at Fish Creek Vista. This is a much quicker alternative then hiking miles of closed road. A few deep pools. Completed 4 rappels and downclimbed the remaining obstacles. Replaced lots of webbing, but bring extra as this route is certainly seeing less traffic these days.
Great conditions. Pools were full with light flow over all rappels. Two swimmers. Downclimbed R4 and R7. Comfortable in 5mm neoprene + rain jacket.

Pools were quite low and easy to avoid with the exception of the one at the base of R2 which we again skipped by going off of a slung boulder well out of the watercourse LDC. Found the plaque to BL just below this pool. All webbing was in good condition. We downclimbed the final rap, exposed but plenty of holds. Weather was perfect.

6.5 hours car to car. Frog leaped with (2) 100' ropes. All anchors looked good, did not traverse high on shelves to bypass water. Avoided all pools except for one (short but cold) swimmer. Good canyon with interesting geology and fun dowclimbs. Exit hike was not as bad as some had thought. The approach and exit drainage as well as the hike out along the creek made for an overall great outing. THANKS FOR THE CURRENT UPDATES.

This canyon was an absolute blast! The approach in was long but worth it. We started at 10 am and got back to the parking area at 4:30 pm. We were moving fast but stopped frequently to enjoy the views and the amazing geology. When we made it up to the saddle, we took a short break for lunch. Another party of 4 was heading up behind us and stopped to chat for a few minutes. We continued on in front of them and frog leaped through each rap. It was fast paced and worked out well. We were able to avoid all pools by doing a few spicy traverses / boulder problems except for one deep swimmer. The water was cold but I'm glad we didn't put on the wet suits. If water was flowing or the remainder of the pools were full, I would have had it on for sure. The raps were fun but not some of the best we've done. By the time we made it out of the technical section, we were way a head of the other party. The exit wasn't as bad as we thought it would be. Overall, we had a great two days in the Sups!

An absolute GEM of a canyon!! A must do! We hiked the road down to see the landslide and continued to the bridge where we rappelled off the bridge (nice free hang) into the creek. The creek walk was “pleasant” the approach up to the saddle was fine with nice views. However, once you drop into the canyon, after the 1st rap, you get SLAMMED with incredible scenery! Bubbling stone walls forming a slot with undercuts and a gently flowing creek with various pools down the center. Incredibly picturesque! We were able to avoid the deepest of the pools and the few we did walk through were only knee deep and not at all cold. The raps are short but fun enough for what they are. We exited up the drainage in KML. Check for ticks, we found 2 crawling on us. All in all, a fantastic day!

Huge monsoonal activity just west of us but we only had a few drops. We were keeping an eye on escapes and ledges that would be out of flood range all the way down. There are plenty with only brief sections of truly committed narrows. Was overcast and cool all day, just as I was hoping. One swimmer and some shallow wades. Nothing too nasty so I guess it had been freshened up recently. A lot of the webbing is looking faded but still supple so we didn't replace anything. won't last too much longer in that sun, though.
Bypassed many of the pools. One mandatory pool about thigh deep. Fish Creek was flowing.

Years back, I ran the north variant to get that taller rappel. I had no idea until this trip how much fun I'd missed. The raps are short, but the scenery is fantastic all the way. We replaced some crispy webbing, and moved main R3 to a higher pinch that makes the rappel start much easier. Other webbing was mostly fresh, except for a soft but maybe bleached loop around a small tree (in turn attached to a fresh, long piece of black webbing). Full swim in cool water below R2. Otherwise, mostly avoidable water in the slot. Then we didn't care anyway and waded up to calf deep in Fish Creek all the way back to the exit.

Perfect winter canyon for a mixed group. With road reopened, parking from the pullout 3/4 mile north of the bridge is a much easier hike than coming down from the viewpoint up top. Moved R2 anchor to a big tree, with a long webbing over the central boulder, making a less awkward rap line. For fun and practice, first down R3 swam the hole, then z-rigged a zipline strand to a big boulder to get the rest down dry. Worked great. Replaced frayed R6. New webbing has a courtesy point for easy rap starts, and a longer one for LAPAR and an easy pull.
Replaced R5 webbing, all others look sweet

Great canyon! Very low water conditions today, but this would be gorgeous with some flow. Skipped R3 and climbed down a crack DCL that brings you directly to the R4 anchor tree. I couldn't find the slung boulder mentioned in the beta. R3 would be knee to waist deep at least if you couldn't avoid it. Parked at the bottom of bottom of Fish Creek and walked the wash for approach/exit. 4.5 hours car to car.

Unlike what the Ropewiki beta says, the road was indeed open to the fish creek parking. This is a gorgeous approach. (4.5 hrs. car to car, not 4.) Onrope GPX was a fantastic track. More water than we expected - The Fish creek approach had water between ankle and shin deep. There was a trickle of flowing water the whole way in-canyon, and the water was icy-cold (1 deep swim, 4 chest deep). We brought wetsuits just in case, and needed them after our first dip into a pool left us shivering. Skipped R1 and R(?) (downclimb) We replaced webbing on 2 anchors that had torn / bleached spots. The large boulder webbing will need to be replaced soon. Suggest future teams bring plenty of replacement webbing. Suggest extending webbing to make for easier pulls. 3 of the anchors were missing quicklinks, so we added them. Really creative chalkstone-redirect anchor that looked weird at first, but was solid. Stunningly gorgeous greenery, desert blooms, and flowing clear water in-canyon. We got lucky with the conditions.
*Beta has been updated to reflect re-opening of the Apache Trail*

One swimmer and a few wades. All webbing in good shape. It had rained the prior day so the rock was still wet and choss was more excessive than normal. A fair amount of bee activity around the final couple rappels but they seemed mostly unbothered. Time reflects a casual pace.
great access from river. water levels were low but COLD. Dead RTC where we put wetsuits on!

As advertised. I could see one pool becoming challenging to escape if the water drops low enough and the walls remain slippery. We descended and pack rafted to RM30 on the 1st day and were back to the car in around 4 hours on the second day racing the rain.
We did the loop starting from the river. All pools were full, with ice encrusted on the topmost pool, but with drysuits we stayed comfy.

A lot about this route is big. Big drive. Big hikes and elevation shifts in and out. Massive views! Relatively big cost, for a canyon (for two of us: $40 for the recreation.gov camping permit, and $30 for tribal lands access). Current kml track is more a guess than actual trail. The latter can be tricky to stay on, but boulder hopping along the floor works too. Technically section is pretty but short, with 4-5 bolted raps, all with easy pull lines. Frosty pools, some overgrown with a strange rod-shaped surface algae I’ve never seen anywhere else. We floated to and camped at Fence Fault so gear could dry out overnight. Outhike is a grind, but the vastness and geology (we ran across fossils and flint) keep it interesting.

A great, short canyon to do from a rafting trip. Very high reward for low effort! If you're a rafter and a canyoneer and only do one canyon on a Grand trip, this should be it! We left half our rafts at Shinumo Wash, floated about a mile to Fence Fault. There's an old, unmaintained trail to the top of the canyon. Our group was mixed in what we wore for cold water protection: one person in none, several in farmer Johns, most in full wetsuits, and one in a dry suit. Weather was warm, and we were all fine, with the person in no wetsuit being a little cool. The canyon is beautiful, with smooth light-colored walls and rappels, pools of water.

Anchors were solid. A few slides, several swims. Was a bit cold by the end in a full 3/2 as air temps were moderate and it was fairly windy. Full 4/3 (or drysuit) would have been more ideal. Very slick rock. Accessed and descended the canyon on day one, explored the broader South Canyon area via packraft and hiked out on day two.

put in 4 anchors to safely descend the canyon. There is 2 dry rappels that we did to access the canyon. First one is cord off a tree down about 105 ft. Second is webbing off a tree, 120ft. This takes you to a tree above the first big waterfall. ~200ft. after that, cord off a tree DCL ~220ft. On the last rappel you will probably be unable to hear any whistles from your rappeler.

Low elevation, easy access, short day and great seasonal flow makes Skookum a lovely winter objective. The tall waterfalls are so fun and the flow is dispersed. At moderate flow, you could pick a line straight through the flow or a line to keep your head dry if you wanted to. As the creek is so open, it felt warmer than other canyons in similar conditions. 35F at the trailhead and we weren’t cold in the canyon. FR-7315 is gated from December 15 to May 1, so we parked at Sun Top Sno Park which added 1.75mi and 900’ of elevation gain to the approach. 3.5 hours car-to-car.

Fun day! Flow was great but the water was extremely cold. Most of us tried to stay dry on R3, but R4 is great fun in the dispersed flow. I managed to accidentally leave a closed system at the anchor, so one teammate got to ascend the final pitch. That is a cold, wet ascent, 0% of which is dry. Not recommended 😂 The 4h car to car includes the ascending shenanigans.

Awesome pay-to-play ratio for this one. Flow was fun but not challenging. Very cold water (I wore fleece underlayers, 6/3 mm of neoprene with a hood, a rain jacket and neoprene gloves, and I was chilly after the last rap but generally a good temperature) Beautiful last rap!

If you find my VT prussick by R2 please let me know. Just a few feet above anchor on flat spot. 814-592-4211 thanks! Great canyon with drive up, 4 back to back raps, and quick exit. Recommend goretex hood over helmet for r4. Got brainfreezed

FR7315 is still closed, so we parked at the gate and hiked 40min up to the usual upper parking spot. Down-climbed the rock cliff (described as R1, but couldn't find existing webbing). Then used the existing webbing DCL for R2 (adding a missing quicklink). Anchors still good in R3 & R4. The log has gone from the river crossing, instead we had to do a ferry swim, with some rope work to get the packs across. Such a lovely route, even with the extra hike in. General thoughts: * check out a potentially better approach of crossing the stream high up, and following the ridge down to above R3. * bolting the DCL wall at R3 to allow for a more-wet line, keeping the route interesting in lower flow.

No signs of anchors or other presence were found, so this was probably a first descent. Pretty thrashy in sections on the way in and out, but the creek itself was excellent. Snag Creek Falls turned out to be pretty straightforward with a tree really nicely situated for the final drop. Suspect flow is on the low side for this time of year per a really dry spring. Not sure I'll be back, but nice to click one off the list I'd been curious about. Saw two wood rats on the hike out.

Returning to Snarky for more documentation and to scout an approach from the bottom, which, unfortunately, turned out to be a seemingly endless thrash through the salmonberry. Two thumbs down. Seems like the best access to SB is to shuttle up to the top and descend through mostly open forest. The upper creek is a little brushy, but has some nice rappels. SB saves the best for last, however. Added a single bolt to improve the anchor situation on the final tier. Should be fairly unobtrusive/invisible from below. A fun day out!

Great trip! The weather was spectacular - sunny and almost 80F. The water flow was just enough to make it fun, but we did have to do a fair amount of stream walking rather than floating. The log jam mentioned in the canyoneeringnorthwest.com beta (somewhere between Fall Into The Wall and Franklin Falls) was no longer present. It was National Trails Day, and Franklin Falls was packed with spectators. We continued down canyon and exited at a popular swimming hole after Denny Camp Falls.

This is a great canyon for those looking to get into canyoneering. This canyon is more like a waterpark than a canyon and the lower section is my favorite. Always check the depth of a pool before jumping. Water flow was moderate-low and should not be a problem for beginners.

Fun day in the sun! We did not alter any anchors, as all were as (now) described in the Descent beta. The drop previously described as a slide looks like a very dangerous slide, even at low flow, and anchor options abound. The beta has been altered to reflect this. I can't even remember how many times we jumped, but all the jumps were incredible. The water was cold enough to where our hands were getting somewhat numb after swimming a lot. Gloves would have been nice. We took my Dad down his first canyon, and he had a great time. No difficult raps, but a few tricky downclimbs.

Agree with Tiffanie's note below: it's more of a waterpark than a canyon. Lots of short swims and jumpable pools. Nothing technical. Much is more of a creek-walk than a canyon, so if you're looking for a deep dark slot this isn't a good choice. Great place to take canyoneers who haven't tried Class C conditions before.

Did just the top section - Fall into the Wall to the base of Franklin Falls. Most pools had a fair bit more whitewater than lower water. The hydraulics are no big deal, but the foam made it impossible to see into pools. As a team of 2, we didn't find it made sense to check the depth in enough detail to do most of the jumps. Anyway, most of the places we usually jump from had enough flow to make them pretty sketch to get out to. Some of the downclimbs were more challenging than usual, but still not that bad. We used the upper bolts to more safely access the lower bolts at the lip of Franklin Falls, but water was not flowing through the pool near the lip. Shortly after the pool, water was flowing about 1-4" deep (pulsing) over the ledge leading to the lower bolts. It was slippery and the flow was pushy when it pulsed, so we felt good using the upper bolts. Franklin was easily the best part of the canyon at this flow, whereas the jumps are usually the highlight at lower flows. We bailed after the falls.

Great beginner canyon with Franklin falls always making me worry about beginners as they go over the edge. NOTE. The road is closed from the upper side due to construction till mid october. From the bottom, drive up and through the road closure signs at the franklin falls lower, lower parking lot. Parked car at upper lower franklin falls turn out and shuttled up to turn out above the franklin falls past all the switchbacks. Parked and walked uphill half mile to jump in spot. Not bad, just different.

Garcia gauge on SF Snoqualmie was reading 80cfs. This is a great and fun level for most canyoneers. We descended at 6pm and finished by 8pm by hiking to the car after franklin falls. Until right before the falls, everything is either a jump or slide. Full wetsuit is recommended as you spend a lot of time up to your neck or chest in the cold south fork water. We could have finished it faster, but made a stop to re-do the Franklin Falls anchors. Note that the handline from the pool above to Franklin Falls anchor is gone, you must use the one bolt anchor to rappel yourself to the anchor. While it may seem to be an couple of easy steps to get to the anchor point, a slip would certainly mean falling down the falls.
Ran the lower half. Perfect water level. All of the anchors are in good shape.

Downstream gauge (12143400) was reporting ~60cfps. Great fun! At the second feature I did the 20ft jump (didn't touch the bottom [6ft, 210lb]), other two in the group did the 18' slide. As noted before, no hand rail above the falls. Returned to road from Franklin Falls.

This canyon is best at super low flow - none of the raps get you all that wet anyway. The pools are all clear, the jumps are super fun (and sessionable) and even with the smoke, it was as pretty as ever. Walked the wagon road trail and the road back to my car. FYI, parking on the left side of the road next to the exit is closed right now, and the road is one way (uphill only) - makes the drive in slower if you're used to getting off at the Alpental exit.
Ideal flow. All the jump ledges are traversable, and the spray from Franklin is primo. Get after it! Removed some tat from the anchor at the top of Franklin. Otherwise, anchors are all good.
Post work evening trip. 77cfs on the Garcia gauge. Good conditions for the jumps. Rigged line between R2 & R3. Somehow core-shot the rope on Franklin however!
Great water levels. Could use a bolt installed on the initial traverse to Franklin, we meat anchored our way across. We picked up some trash in the river. Good time, great group
Fantastic trip through South Fork Snoqualmie on a hot afternoon. Flow was 52 cfs on the Garcia gauge. The big slide is VERY fun at this flow. All the usual jumps are in.
A very fun thing to do on a very hot day. Very chill, and a relatively swimmy canyon. Some beta says bail after Franklin Falls but I think you miss the prettiest part of the canyon if you do this.
48cfs. Ran whole length, usual jumps & slides all in on upper & lower section. Orange glow from the smoke just added to the already beautiful route. Lower section really does add to the overall trip.

Full canyon. 45-50 CFS. Great canyon! A very adventurous one - no doubt! Not much webbing in place, so come prepared with quick links and webbing. Lots of slides and jumps. Water was a bit chilly at times with 3mm gloves. Truly amazing views and scenery in the canyon! Didn’t expect that amount of creek walking though. Several miles of Creek walking. Some more technical rappels, Some slightly exposed or risky climbs to get to the rappels. Took about 6–7 hours to do the full canyon.

Water is lower but the pools are still deep and cold. R1 anchor was removed, we opted to just walk around rather than set-up a new one there. Franklin Falls anchor has redundant large rappel rings, two highly rusted and does create a significant drag for the rope if you run it through them for the pull down. Test before use, or remove and replace them completely.

Ran full length. 78F air temp, but the water was particularly cold. Low flow (25cfs), however all significant jumps still went fine. Found black fig8 in one of the early pools. Cut rusted quicklinks off Franklin bolts and replaced with fresh stainless steel ones. Canyon was looking very beautiful. A lot more trees in the canyon this year, with a dam formed in the lower section where the large jump is (you might want to now rap down the damn if not jumping).

Such a fun, playful canyon! All the usual jumps and slides are in. Flow was 74 cfs and there were no special challenges. We recommend the traverse lines to approach Franklin Falls as the spray was making the rocks slippery at this flow. All anchors in good condition. 3.5 hours car-to-car from Fall in the Wall to the Franklin Falls Trailhead.
Really fun, easy canyon to run in a short time. Only ran it up to top of Franklin Falls and then exited back to cars.

An unseasonably cold and dry period meant South Fork was a winter wonderland full of snow and ice. 😍 Flow was 90 cfs and had been fairly low for the previous couple weeks. We ran the upper section and then hiked back up to our cars. There was knee- to thigh-deep postholing involved!
Fun day is SF. We bypassed most of the creek walk as in high flow + slippery it was annoying. We did a lot of the jumps at the start but did not do the slide. It should be depth checked.

Late afternoon cool down. Alice Creek gauge was at 104cfs. Slides/jumps etc all go. Little pushy in some sections of rapids, but nice current to float in reduces the creek walking. Careful footwork needed on some of the middle section jumps. Franklin anchors are good. Didn't run lower.
Even on a rainy weekday you may have an audience at Franklin
Low water made for a lot of slow creek walking. Many of the slides and jumos were very shallow and had to be done carefully or skipped. The last rappel was still worth the price of admission!
This creek is a very pretty creek walk with a dry last rappel into an amphitheater with 50 people watching. There is so much better stuff to do than this one.
Very nice rest day canyon. The mud was only a little bit in some places, easily avoided.

Water flow through the upper part and technical portion. Webbing was good on the first 3 raps. at the last rap we rigged off of the relatively fresh webbing RDC and as the first person was going down they dislodged a big chunk of the undersurface of the overhang! We called it off and went LDC, replaced crispy webbing there and rapped the less exciting but safer route down that side (mostly ledges, with ~25 ft of free hang). I feel like the time estimate on this page doesn't include the walk to the river.

Scenic but quite muddy. Under current conditions, a rain jacket is advisable on R3. R4 is dry, but has an interesting overhung lip to pass on rappel. Completion time reflects north fork parking to river only - we exited via Rider the next day.

Lovely route. Used last rapp rdc, anchor looked solid, but some loose rock did come down with last man down. Fun overhanging rapp, but stay clear of rock fall, or use anchor LDC. Just under 7 hours included a trip to the river and 30 minute lunch break. Although we did not have to rig any anchors, we feel the time estimate is reasonable and accurate. The return up the north fork was fantastic and we thoroughly enjoyed bouldering breaks along the way. One steep section on the return has a rope ladder rigged and a spare static rope for emergencies. Thought this was a fun and awesome touch. Overall an amazing canyon, scenic, and fun!

a mild amount of mud. R2 was a bit tricky to find but not too bad, just look for the little hole above the slot. R4: the large boulder LDC is perfect and clean...no need to even consider going right. Return up the south fork was mostly easy with the occasional tricky climb and not something I'd want to do in the dark.

Replaced webbing at R2 and R3. Bypassed most the narrows on a shelf canyon right as they included several seemingly deep, uninviting pools of mud. Used boulder anchor canyon left for R4 which had a large but shallow mud pool at the base. It was avoided by swinging onto a ledge below the undercut section. Scrambling up the South Fork was fun but exposed to the heat - which this time of year on this particular weekend was rather unpleasant.

Light flow throughout the canyon, replaced anchor for R1.. One very cold swimmer, chose to wear a wetsuit through the narrows and didn’t regret it. Lots of slippery mud, but nothing overly deep to sink into.
One cold swimmer, but out and in to Sun fast. Setup cairn anchor into pool after R2. Muddy. Fun scramble out on a few places.

Soap Creek with Flow! Well.. a trickle. A small stream throughout the whole canyon, a couple deep wades through the narrows section. Temps were great and sun on the way out felt great. Bypassed first pour-off from bridge. From there, pinchpoint below giant chockstone boulder. Sandtrapped the next short drop to avoid a blind plunge into the waist deep pool. Stemmed the next to use the deadman anchor out of the narrows. Used DCL anchor for final rappel. Exit was pleasant and engaging, with a few fun boulder problems. Water in lower canyon tastes terrible even after filtration, bring plenty.

Parked the car at the exit and walked on the road to the canyon. Went by easily. Soap is still flowing! Each of the rappels were out of the watercourse and we had no problems avoiding the pools. At the second rappel we noticed the flow beginning to increase below us indicating the ice further up canyon was melting. This didn’t become an issue. At the second rappel through the boulder chalks, there was about 60’ of fresh black webbing set up to bypass the watercourse. We repurposed the webbing to rebuild the second rappel and stayed in the canyon. At this time we still weren’t sure if the next pool could be avoided. Ended up being able to thanks to the rappel off the boulder DCL. We used the anchor DCL at the last rap as well and meandered to the river and back. The hike out was pleasant too! All in all a quality day out.

Pools potentially avoidable, good day out. Be competent, stay safe. Left a clear Nalgene with an orange cap somewhere in the technical section, orange cord with green carabiner attached, if you find it and feel like returning, I would appreciate it.
Fun day out. Water can be mostly avoided. Didn't need wetsuits. Super amazing geology, salt and a fun rabbit hole rappel

There was a little water flowing which made things very muddy. There were a few places up to knee deep. R2, we ended up using an anchor on canyon left. The canyon right anchor was on a couple of wedged rocks that I barely touched and they moved. One fell. I removed the webbing. On R4, there was no webbing on canyon right, but we were able to ghost off a boulder using a toggle. The exit was definitely tricky especially with how muddy our shoes were. Beautiful area!

Started at 1030hrs and out at 1630. Webbing looked great at all rappels. Shoes got quite muddy (sunny day after 2 days of sprinkling rain) but not bad. On R2, we used an anchor LDC with long webbing that extended under a boulder, slow transition to keep from swinging left. We continued on R2 down past the first ledge and had a fine rope pull. After going down the watercourse slightly (and being unable to avoid stepping in a calf-deep pool), found what I believe is the actual R2 above us, that descends through a hole in the boulders above. IE there seems to be two options for R2, the first being LDC that might get you a bit wet and a second option further downcanyon that completely avoids the water. For R4, we did not find webbing LDC, so we rappeled off webbing we found attached to two chockstones beneath a boulder RCD. Seemed to be different than the official anchor description that urges avoidance of RDC route. Muddy down Soap Creek North fork to confluence. Exit up Soap Creek South Fork had some exciting bits.

Great day in soap creek, other than one little hiccup. Group of 2, took us 10 hours, we didn't have a shuttle, and proceeded all the way to the Colorado river and played in the water for about an hour, which added close to 5 miles. All the anchors were good except the last one. I should have gone with my gut. I found an anchor LDC and RDC for the last rapelle. We went with the LDC based on the Beta, despite me questioning it. I thought, maybe I was missing something, the right one looked good, the left one looked like it was going to be a sketchy rapelle. When I went to rap off the left one, the ground gave out underneath me while I was transitioning the edge. It caused me to drop and pendulum pretty bad. If I could go back I would cut that anchor off so no one else uses it. There was a trickle of water in the beginning of the creek, which ended 10 minutes after the first rap and then a little bit of mud on a couple of the raps. Great boulder challenges climbing up and out soap creek trail.

Good natural anchors, lots of creek walking on bedrock, climbing through boulder gardens and big log jams, easy approach, exit from the creek is steep but short.. Final drop is tricky and long, bigger water would make this especially challenging. Tallest drop determined to be ~225’, using a 240’ rope

What a perfect day on the creek - warm fall weather, 75 and sunny. The water was icy cold, but I stayed comfortable in my shorty and rain jacket. We had no idea when the creek was last run. The existing anchors were in terrible shape, except for one: a length of webbing wrapped around a tree. It looked new, apart from a small, rusty quick link. We replaced all the anchors and even added a fresh quick link to the tree webbing. It's a mystery how that original webbing survived, or how anyone could have navigated this creek and only replaced that one anchor. At Crackin Falls, we set up an anchor on a low-slung log, running a long loop of webbing over another questionable log. We were concerned about the rope catch due to all the cracks and crevices waiting to snag. We pulled the rope from the pool above the final 50-foot drop. The anchor's high position provided a great pull. For the last 50 feet, we installed a new anchor in the pool for a safe rappel. *** Agree with the other trip report!! Final drop is tricky and long, bigger water would make this especially challenging. Creek walking between the upper section and lower section wasn't bad but not as easy as the beta reads. There was some bedrock and a lot of slippery rock walking. Sometimes you could cheat into the forest for easy walking. The exit creek walk after the boulder downclimbing was brutal. It was more of a half mile climbing through a logjam than a creek walk. It was work! I have some ideas about an easier exit that I'm working on and will update the beta as I learn more. This will be a 4 star creek with a better exit.

Did not wear wetsuits, which was a big mistake. This canyon has unavoidable pools and long swims all the time, as well as Tonto Creek. Would definitely recommend wetsuit at least 3mm maybe even 5mm. Did upper narrows, which were nice but probably not worth adding on to an already long day. One in group was able to stay dry in upper, other did a long swim. Upper is slow going if trying to find ways to stay out of pools. Completed canyon doing just 2 rappels, 100' and 25'. 5 hours approach including upper narrows, 1 hour technical section to Tonto Creek. 3 hours exit. Gun Creek gauge was 3.16 cfs.

Did 5 rappels with a 200ft rope: 60ft, 20ft, 20ft, 50ft, 70ft. Found 3 anchors intact but replaced webbing on the final rappel. Lots of pools and a little flow but was able to avoid getting wet above the ankles.

We got through with a 145’ rope. Toggled R1 to keep the pull clean. Raps are short, but the geology and little slit part are fun. Definitely would be more interesting with some flow (it was almost completely dry).

Let me say this, it'd be damn near impossible to get wet anywhere above the calf for even the shortest canyoneer. Unless you lay down, that'd do it. Ankle deep water, nobody here who says they avoided water had to work very hard, though keeping your feet dry when the canyon holds water would involve bypassing the rappels. Speaking of, most all raps are bypassable but not necessarily downclimbable. Bring webbing and hardware, as things seem to wash out for the lower rappels (i.e. absolutely no chance you downclimb R3, but feasible bypass high LDC). Very fun technical section with an easy approach, the exit gets tiring with a continued need to routefind and search feasible downclimbgs in the wide, boulder ridden drainage. If you want more rappels, there are drops for them too. If you are prepared for the long downclimb exit and want to avoid water, give this canyon a go!
Some ice in pools.

UPS route both directions. Only two of the pools had appreciable (freezing!) water. The last downclimb is annoying without a partner assist. I'd call this canyon mediocre, but it's straightforward access to an amazing section of the river. Car to car time includes a side trip to Stanton's Cave/Vasey's Paradise and washing mud out of many articles of clothing on the beach.

Shallow pools near the start of the narrows were frozen over. Other pools were under waist depth, not frozen, and full of slime. The first and last chockstone climbs were the most challenging and I would not have wanted to attempt either without a partner to assist. I suspect some sand has shifted around in recent years. Vaseys was frozen over. We combined Bedrock and South over the course of two days and exited via the UPS route.
Warm air temperatures and cool water, so thin, but full wetsuit was perfect. Canyon was nice, but not great. After doing Silver Grotto the day before, this felt like a let-down.
Water flow was extremely high and we did not run the canyon (Memaloose or South Fork of the Clackamas). River crossing was not difficult.

Tell your friends to stop swimming here. Log bridge is now unsafe DO NOT CROSS take route up drainage Canyon Right. Also stop leaving your underwear up top. Trees all burnt out for last rap. Don't count on it being run anytime soon. Set fixed lines and ascend out.

Crossed the Clackamas. It is walkable at this point just above the rocky beach. The bush push to the old road grade is a little wild. Once on the ild road it isn’t too bad. The wooden car bridge is now gone at the confluence with Memaloose Creek. Tunnels are about the same. Off trailing is a bit more difficult as the ground is very loose. Worse than any other time I’ve been back there. First time was before the Box 36 Fire. We went to rescue a rope and ended up not being able to pull the ropes we used after freeing the left rope. Will go back and extend the bolted anchor to ensure a better pull. A case of beer or a few nice coffees to anyone who grabs them before we go back but highly unlikely. The biggest issue with the current state of this canyon is the rather large log that spans pool 2. It’s a strainer at any higher flow. Be wise and make sure you can negotiate it before continuing.

Chest deep water at the Clackamas crossing. Followed an emerging use-path to the old road grade along the west side of the creek which takes you to the tunnels. Several bats in the longest one; a headlamp is a good plan. Great bedrock and warm water conditions. Expect a lot of trees to come down and fall into the watercourse over the next 3-5 years. Fun day out -- thanks for leading, Brian!

The water is warm and low for this time of year. There are some new log jams from burned trees in the creek, we will be experiencing effects of the fire for many years to come. We removed a large floating strainer log from the bottom of R4.

After much searching, tapping, and discussion, we agreed on location for the DCL anchor. I rappelled DCL along the edge of flow testing areas to cross into the middle of the flow. The flow DCL is very strong and I had trouble finding a place to cross. I would descend and test and descend and test. I ended up above the rooster tail and didn't want to enter it. I chose to drop my pack at this location (about 150 feet up) even though I didn't want to, I hate dropping my pack from that high. I crossed it quickly and was fine. The middle is showery due to rocks above breaking up the flow. I'm going to try to cross at the pour over next time and avoid the strongest flow DCL. We did have a guy run the entire flow DCL through the rooster tail with his pack on. He said it wasn't that bad but I don't believe him because it was strong. I updated the beta and will continue to do so as I learn more about it.

New wet line on R3 is so much fun! 🤩 It’s technical and requires good footwork and control in heavy flow on rappel. Not many drops like this one in the PNW - it definitely makes the canyon special. R1 webbing is a bit worn (we jumped it) but otherwise anchors are in good condition. We combined Memaloose and South Fork Clackamas and it took us 4.5 hours car-to-car. We saw 5 salmon spawning in the river after the last rappel! It was a great day 😊

We lost a florescent orange gear perspective rope bag on R3. It was empty and was tossed off the top above the pour over. We never found it at the bottom. We looked all around the bottom pool and even searched the sides. I guess the floatation was removed but I still thought it would have some type of neutral buoyance. The wet line on R3 is spicy. I tried to cross the flow at the top but swung back DCL. This might need a redirect if its possible. It will be a project for next year.

The water is swimmably warm which was surprising! Flow was lighter than normal. We installed some temporary pusle bolts on R3 and ran directly down the middle of the flow. It was a great line! I'm thinking you might be able to get that line from the DCL anchor but am hesitant because I don't want to pendulum into he heavy flow line DCL. I want to try another temp anchor location DCL but 10 feet behind the current anchor. It will be a good line but I'm not sure about the pull. To be continued...

Just the ticket after a dry dusty backpack along the PCT. Dropped a car at Cascadia State Park and hiked the road (~40min). Dropped in and floated most of the way back. Tomco Falls and one unnamed rapid required a bypass DCR. Ropes & harness unnecessary. Helmet probably a good idea per slippery rocks underfoot. Lots of water-worn rock and miniature arches... a pretty fascinating place. This canyon is probably quite dangerous in high water conditions.

Another fun trip down the Santiam. No technical obstacles, but most of the fun here is exploring and checking things out along the way. There are nice sections where you can drift on your back looking up at the canyon walls. Tomco Falls was kicking off some current, so we spent some time at the bottom practicing swiftwater skills. There was much jumping.

What a great trip! Our first canyon and well worth it. The water was a bit chilly for the first swim... walked about .8miles before having to jump in... this was probably the longest swim, approx .2mi per our watch. After that was mostly walking/climbing and occasional dips. I swam more than my wife because my shoes didn't feel secure enough to scramble on some of the rocks. We tried to stay out of the water as much as possible. She loved the slot, never knowing our river was like that anywhere, and loving being able to be in that.
Third time down the Santiam. This is a terrific creek to run on a hot summer day. There was much jumping and lounging in pools. Practiced some swiftwater skills around Tomco Falls.

We pack rafted this at about 95 CFS. Much better as a float than a canyoneering route. I'd recommend pack rafts if the flow is close to 100 CFS or higher. Beautiful canyon, but not sure I would have enjoyed it as much if I was walking/swimming the whole thing.

Lots of cholla on approach. Some fun downclimbs and nice rap at the end. No water. Setup new webbing anchor at optional 15' downclimb, but easily downclimbed. Replaced webbing on 1st real rappel (just before big rappel) on rock pinch. Webbing at big rappel looked a little warn, but still okay, probably needs replacing soon. Big rappel is setup on good rock punch, but should be plenty of material to build deadman, if desired. Remeasured big rappel to be 150-160'. You are welcome Arizona.
big rap is much fun but not much else going on with this canyon. webbing was fine for last rap. it's anchored off of the big chockstone at the top of it. walked through the campground unmolested.

Lots of unforgiving brush on the approach and exit. Some nice views. Took the shorter approach to the lower section of canyon. Couldn’t imagine thrashing through any more brush to add on one more rappel. Built a cairn anchor at the first rap since available chockstones were loosening and the tiny hopseed bush/tree that someone had previously anchored off was taking quite a beating. Replaced webbing at big rappel as well and removed all junk material. Won’t be repeating this one. I suppose if you hate yourself a little bit then you might enjoy it.

We kayaked from the marina to end of the tributary a short boulder hop from the start of the ascent. Bush whacking wasn’t nearly as bad as the comments led us to believe. We took the short approach to the sketchy chockstone rappel. As previously noted, the chockstone did not instill confidence. No evidence of a previous anchor existed, so we (over) built a new cairn anchor. The second rappel does indeed have a tricky start, but nothing overly challenging. The existing webbing is in decent condition despite its color faded from the elements. The exit is where you’ll be glad to have long pants and sleeves, as the abundance of angry bush blockades will spite you for thinking this canyon was actually decently fun especially adding in the unnecessary but refreshing, tranquil and scenic kayak component.

This canyon was ridiculously pretty and a lot of fun. Water levels were low, with just one swimmer and a couple of short wades. We brought wetsuits but didn't use them. Anchors were in good condition, including a couple in the main technical sequence that were quite creatively arranged given the lack of material; kudos. 6 raps total and 5:30hrs car to car for the two of us, including the final sand slog.

Explored the canyon and rappelled the first 2 waterfalls. The exit is a bit challenging as I climbed up the way the map shows, but I suspect an easier way would be to state a vehicle at Indian Creek (also shown on map as possible descent)

A spectacular day exploring a very beautiful canyon with exceptional views on the approach hike. The canyon itself is unique and very enjoyable. It's too bad that it is so remote and likely has a very short descent window before it dries up. The exit was brutal. See beta for alternate exit recommendations.

did it with packrafts and decided the scramble over from the little ledge RDC of the splash point looked too sketchy so we put them up in the bushes a few hundred feet to the west. this was fine but beware the many thorns which can make quick work of a pool toy's thin skin. also we then had to swim that whole way back to them, which wasn't much shorter than just swimming back to the other side. there were a couple swimmers though it was actively flowing and I'd imagine these dry up a bit for most of the year. canyon is short and ok but rapping into the lake is cool. the fishermen near the bottom barely looked up from their lines so I guess people are doing some cool shit all the time down here. didn't replace any anchors. no rope pull issues.

Completely dry and really hot. We struggled on the approach - its undefined and full of cacti and sketchy scrambling up exposed rock. We swam across the lake both ways which was doable and a needed cool down. Rappels had some tricky starts. Last rappel into the lake is nice. Doesn’t seem worth doing this time of year.

We took crappy packrafts across the lake, landed at the little cove and then did a short section of heavy bushwhacking near the lake's edge to get into a less brushy area. Instead of doing the exposed class 3/4 route, we just kept going up the drainage to the saddle, then turned right and went over the top to where you drop into the canyon. Raps 1 & 2 have some really awkward starts on chock stones with voids beneath them. Both land you in a pool that is too shallow for jumping. They were waist to chest deep. Rap 3 was an easy two-stage with avoidable water. We put new webbing on rap 4 at a rock pinch directly across from the rappel. Looks like it has been used previously because it had bits of webbing stuck in it, but we added webbing and a ring because the one that was there was crispy and had a rusty ring from being in the water. Right after we made it back to the other side of the lake, we noticed the current pick up a lot, so just be aware that water can be released from the dam any time.

Light flow. A couple swims and deep wades. Water was colder than anticipated and we appreciated having wetsuits, though they weren’t critical. We packed our rafts through the canyon and inflated them at the large staging area above the final rappel.

If I could, I’d give it 2.5. The nasty brush on the far shore, the chossy climb, and the awkward, yet short first 2 raps are all a pain. But the last rap makes it worth doing at least once, and makes for a quick, if scruffy intro to nearby canyons. I recommend leaving watercraft at home. This is by a friendly place for packrafts or other inflatables. I make sure everyone has a 20L drybag, rolled up with lots of air inside. With that (or some other floaty device) in the pack, backstroking to the other side (and back at the end) is no big deal - and I’m a below average swimmer. Webbing on the short raps is getting crispy, will need replacing soon. Big rap anchor was solid. All pulls easy. Some bees near R4 anchor, but they stayed calm.

Explored the canyon in about 45 minutes car to car. Very short, but scenic and interesting, somewhat fragile and unique alluvial conglomerate narrows. The canyon ends shortly after the dryfall in the main arm.
Meh.
See comment at peek-a-boo: https://ropewiki.com/Conditions:Peek-a-Boo_Canyon-20231009172339. 6.69 hours.
Great way to introduce someone to canyons. We saw a baby rattler inside. Beautiful little canyon.

An amazing day in Spooky! We did Spooky, The Beast, and ROC in a day. Time is for all 3. It was a group that was somewhat new to desert canyons so going was reasonably slow. We spent some extra time in Spooky to practice high stemming and moves off the deck. Overall lots of fun features and a cool slot. Bring lots of water obviously. We brought ~2 liters and our party wanted more for sure.

Solo Trip/FDD. Climbed up higher to upper drainage. Two raps in upper section (30' and 60'), left webbing and rapides if anyone wants to repeat. Best bang for the buck is my recommended approach/cables and just doing the final 150' rap.

I took quite a while, combined with nearby canyon, but it was a very fun solo. Not many approaches can be so engaging. The descent was fun, though some changes led me to rearrange things, beta reflected. A traverse line should now be used to access the RDC descent bolts.
Full trip report is listed on URL. Creek will have very little to no running water in subsequent months.

Two hour hike up to the Sidewalk-in-the-Sky. Thrashy approach to the tarns, then down to find the main canyon. Suspect the original crew may have dropped directly from the sidewalk. East Stack Falls impressive, but long, long hike out.

Flow was way too extreme to rappel directly in the flow. For some drops, we were able to skirt along side the falls; for others we bypassed due to unavoidable hazards. The Wilson river was not crossable so we skirted along the cliffs (easy) back to the bridge. Wore a full wetsuit + windbreaker and managed to stay relatively warm except in a few spots.

We only had one car so we dropped in on a tributary on the western side of the creek. It was filled with wood but the approach was only ~10 minutes. We stayed out of the flow for most drops because of high water, but a more adventurous group might have ran some of them. Very fun canyon, I'm excited to come back in the summer!

Near perfect conditions. The center of most flows was pretty heavy so we stayed to one side or the other while still in the flow. We stayed out of the flow on the main Stafford Falls, but noticed after all were down that we could have dropped in the flow DCR of the heavy flow. Fun shit!!
No shuttle so we dropped in the west drainage. Conditions were great, enough water to be fun but not sketchy.
We were able to swim/wade across the Devil's Fork of the Wilson River - no issues.

Always fun -- flow was great today. Stafford is looking a little beaten up; to keep impact to a minimum please stay in the watercourse as much as possible. It's easy to do. We removed a few dodgy anchors. Please rappel DCR on the final falls as there's a confirmed dipper nest on the DCL side again this year. We probably should have removed the DCL anchor.
Removed DCL anchor on the final falls. Please rappel DCR only to avoid the Dipper nest.

Great day on Stafford. Water conditions were on the spicy side, requiring some evaluation of the drops before committing. Found several over-built anchors. Seemed a bit more slippery than I remembered. The big drop should probably be bolted eventually -- put in a bolt station right on the brink would be safer and allow an easier pull. A tree anchor set back from the edge could be the anchor for a retrievable safety line. Kicked back in the sun with a festive beverage back at the lower parking area. Great way to unwind after a great day.

Very fun run off Stafford which is my home area. The added anchors are all in good shape and allowed the waterflow to be rappelled in these conditions. All anchors ideally set and in good shape. Anchor webbing added to Stafford Falls tree anchor. The existing sling started to show it's age from fading, no mechanical or creature damage. The set anchors are really helping to restore the banks and moss on side of the waterflow.
Terrific training day in Stafford skipping the 90-degree temps in town. Replaced several anchors; all are good to go. Great fun to lounge in the pools and introduce some new folks to canyoning.

Good jaunt through today with a two man team. Not sure if it is rap 3 or 4 but someone had a sling around a log center creek. The log was blocked with a large bolder and propped with another log. A slight tug on the main log had the whole system moving including the large bolder. We removed this so others didn’t get the idea it was safe. We retired webbing around the nearby large root center creek. There was a lot of debris build up there. We removed most. Probably best running your rope under the sketch log, just watch too much upward lift on your rope if you run the rope under the log.

Stafford is currently in flood stage; most rappels not suitable for running the flow direct. Probably the highest I've ever seen the creek at. Double falls at the bottom was more of a uni-falls... albeit with light flow in the center. Still lots of snow higher up in the coast range; road was blocked near the bridge. We had to park and hike the last 0.5mi to the usual drop in. Probably would recommend waiting a couple weeks for the snow to melt off and flow to subside to "fun" level. Problematic log in the previous TR has disappeared. Creek still surprisingly clean given the big snow-pocalypse this winter. Found and fixed a few wonky anchors. Need to come back and install a rebelay station for a better way to run the big falls. Looks like a nice rock panel DCL just before the big vertical.

Flow has dropped off tremendously -- back to typical levels for this time of year. Investigated possibility of installing a new rebelay station on the big pitch, but rock is largely poor quality. Found & packed out a mostly-full bottle of engine oil that some idiots were using to light a campfire at the bottom of the pitch. Otherwise, a fun day out.
Great day taking new canyoneers on their first creek. Flow was moderate-low, but seemed normal for this time of year. Rebuilt 3 anchors with new webbing.

All anchors looked good. Fun easy flow, pretty canyon. We used the sneak approach and walked up the road. A nice van lifer gave one of us a ride back to the car. Felt like we had the canyon to ourselves until we saw a rope drop behind us in the last rappel. Never saw or heard the others. Good day.

Definitely on the high side, but rappels are manageable by an experienced team. Creek is pushy in places where it narrows up. Expect to get hammered by the flow on R9 as the shape of the falls takes you right into the torrent at the bottom. At this level, it's best to reach the shelf about 2/3rds of the way down, then move DCR as much as possible to find a good line. Can be beneficial to have a bottom belay as a reverse tag line to slow folks down if they should go for a pendulum swing.
Sunny day and water temp felt nice. Dropped in at R4.
Mazamas class outing. Added new single bolt to R8 that lets you easily rappel the chute.
Fun day out except we returned to the parking lot and found the back window broken in the lower shuttle SUV. Dry clothes backpack and soft cooler were stolen.

Beautiful trip out. extra time as we practiced techniques. Water levels were great for beginner level - not too strong to be risky but high enough to pay attention. The flowers are popping and quite fragrant. The dippers are present along the creek. still nesting in last waterfall DCL, please use caution and avoid rappelling that line.

Great day out at Stafford. Very chill, straightforward canyon with a nice approach and exit if you shuttle. I wore a 5mm, but a 3mm would have been fine; I was getting pretty hot at times. Anchors and webbing were all in good shape. Water was low, but still enough to have a good time.
Added shortcut to R4 to the beta. Flow gage is reading high and I think that's accurate.
The royal approach is worthy of its name. Water levels coming down quickly - solid flow but nothing spicy.

Mission with Dusty Gold to update the anchors on the big pitch. Previously, the tree anchor DCL was in a bad position: no visibility down the pitch, and no way to have eyes on rappellers if there was a problem. Tree anchor is now retired. Beta has been updated.

Ran the full canyon from the tributary drop in (haven't run the top half in a while as I prefer the shortened version). Introduced a friend to this canyon so we took out time and ran the whole thing. I placed a bolt at R2 DCL so now the human anchor is no longer required. It is a Bonier PinGo hanger from HowNOT2 and designed to rappel directly from the hanger so it does not require a quick link. All other anchors in the canyon are good. The webbing at R4 for the inflow rappel is starting to show its age but still good. I added an 8mm Petzl Pulse hole at R5 DCR forward of the anchor so there is some protection for someone (observer/anchor manager) to be able to observe the drop, its about waist high on the rock wall forward of the anchor. The big rap at Stafford Falls is fun even in low flow with the new anchors and of course the pull is a dream now compared to the old tree anchor. We had thin wetsuits but flow is low enough and weather warm enough we were hot most of the time and kept laying in the pools to cool off.
Low flow abounds, but still a little splashy on the bigger rappels. New anchors are great, especially the re-belay on Stafford Falls. Great line right in the water!
There's some cracking in the rock around one of the new bolts at the rebelay station at R9. Something to keep an eye on.
Great run with class, everyone had a great time! We started at R4. New anchor and line at the 75 foot rappel is great! Thanks Kevin and Dusty.

It was a nice winter day in Stafford, running R4-R10. The bushwhack for this shorter entrance is pretty overgrown. Water flow was a little more than moderate but quite manageable, especially if you could stay out of the direct flow. Crossing the Devil's Fork of Wilson River was no problem.

Super fun day - ran full Staff. Team of 3 with 1 noob (first canyon). Flow was moderate-high, water temp very comfortable. R10 bolts DCR webbing was damaged - we removed it. R10 DCR - bolt spins, lifts, but not enough to remove, and is clearly bent.
Took the non-bushwhack route in to run R4 - R10. Creek is exceedingly slippery at present. Flow is at fun level. Another bolt at R9b might make station management a little easier.

Stafford was beautiful today with good flow and lots of sun! The DCR bolt on the DCR anchor for the final wrap of Tin Falls got smashed over during the winter and is no longer functional. We removed the hanger and nut and pounded the bolt in as much as we could. Other than that, anchors are in good shape. The creek is slippery as heck per usual!

Eventful day in Staff. The flow was higher than expected and reported from the previous week; very similar to flow observed in mid-April. Ran full Staff starting at R1. It was a very cold rainy day, and one of the beginners began to suffer from hypothermia. 3 of us exited the canyon between R6 and R7 by climbing the steep mossy wall DCR. We were surprised to find webbing and quicklinks on trees at the top of the climb. The remainder of the team continued down creek and we reunited at the cars.

Used the "Royal Shuttle" entry to R4 and took our time with some show and tell and some teaching. All anchors in a good shape. R9A does not look as if it'scracking more or worrying. Multiple dipper nests, R6. in the big alcove DCR, also suspected in R5. Very slippery conditions.

Stafford was slippery as always, but even more so than normal! The right bolt on the R9 hanging anchor continues to be a concern with expanding cracks. It was stable today after I yanked on it, but I am concerned about what would happen if extra force needed to be put on it (like a haul system). All other anchors are in good shape. Be careful of dipper nests in and around the falls!
Fantastic fun day in Stafford with 4 Mazamas students. Creek was not particularly slippery. We were fortunate to get great sunny weather. The students loved the Stafford falls rebelay.
