While the flow wasn't impressive, Star Creek gets extra points for amazing scenery and gorgeous snow-clad mountain views. Definitely takes home the prize as the most scenic creek near Portland. Even the approach hike is highly photogenic. Star Creek caught us a little off guard with some bigger rappels than we were expecting... and we were not expecting an audience on the 180ft rappel near the end. If you demand beauty in your canyon adventures, this one's for you.

No anchors or signs of previous passage were found, so this may have been a first descent. A couple of nice falls/sequences, but have to knock off a bunch of points for all the fallen trees (some of them ancient) in Steel Creek. Just about every drop had some evidence of vegetative destruction; would bump up to a three star if it ever blows out. There are much better canyon destinations in the Skykomish River Valley. Excess wood in some of the drops makes the canyon less safe than it should be.

The rappels were approximately 40, 30, 20, 60, 110, 50, and 230 feet. Anchors were in good condition and primarily consisted of natural anchors off trees and rock cairn anchors. The final 230 foot rappel is bolted on canyon left.

We descended Stewart Falls on Sunday. There was significant snow as we crossed over the creek, and a fantastic ice bridge and tunnel over the creek after the first rappel. The flow was moderate high and most rappels avoided the flow. We were pounded by water on two rappels. The anchors were all in good condition. A terrific day in the mountains and fun was had by everyone!

Note that many of these rappels put you in the thick of the flow at at least one point on almost every rappel. Probably not a good canyon for those that are inexperienced. The 30' drop near the end, down a crack, with a deadman in the creek, was especially pounding and had an overhang at the top yhat can catch you unawares, with 1-2 other spots that can be problematic. There was an anchor placed out right that avoids this rappel. Many people are doing the final rappel out right from a dirty ledge? Apparently to avoid the flow? Not my cup of tea, but to each their own. We went in the flow and a 200' rope reached for us to the base. Hike out on a trail left to the creek below. Surprisingly good waterfall raps. But as mentioned, probably not for newbs.

Very beautiful set of waterfalls, great flow, high quality rappels and a beautiful approach/exit hike! Approach trail is really enjoyable in it’s own right, first portion on main trail is fairly shaded in the woods. Once you cut off the main trail heading up gets fairly steep and loose, lots of vegetation flanking either side of a narrow canyoneer path, canopy giving way to gorgeous long reaching views as you traverse narrow loose cliff edges. Lots of stinging nettle on approach, my partner who wore shorts, is still recovering from that experience. By the end of the approach we were very hot in full sun wondering if we had overestimated our neoprene as we tried to find a spot to gear up in the brush. 1hr 15min to top of canyon. I wore my 5/4 and it was terrible to put on in the heat but jumping in the freezing snow melt water below made me realize it was the right choice. Flow is strong and pushy but not too overbearing for my partner that had never experienced water of this caliber. With this flow and the canyon geometry, your head is under water for alot of the rappelling, especially on the large ones. A hooded rain shell was nice to have. The technical section is short but packs a punch, the larger raps were really fun and very vertical. By the time we had gotten to the last rap were all very happy about our thick neoprene as the canyon was not in the sun at all and would have been chilly with less. On the big rappel I had heard about multiple parties getting their ropes stuck when rigging from the DCL bolts (the obvious choice). There are also bolts high DCR if you scramble up to them. They have webbing equalized, then a traverse line run along the cliff to another rock pinch. Not sure why… for a dry rap on the other side maybe?? We decided to rig directly to the equalized bolted anchor high DCR, ignoring the traverse line. This rappel puts you through the bushes up top briefly but gives way to a good rappel line on the DCR side, in the flow but not punishing. We rappelled all the way down to the floor combining the final 30ft falls making a 2 stage rap. This used around 270+ ft of rope. Pull from the bottom was no problem using the DCR anchor. Whistles, even our loud Fox40s were hardly any use on this route. The water is too loud. Radios recommended. There is line of sight to the bottom from the cliff edge DCR if following the traverse line over. We did change out of out wetsuits entirely at the bottom and we were glad we did, made the hike back much more enjoyable. Don’t forget your National Parks Pass for the parking lot! Total 6hrs at a leisurely pace.

I mean, I never rappelled from wicker anchors before today, but hanging my body from a few of them gave a convincing validation to the old “You can break one popsicle stick, but not a fistful of them” show. Seriously though. Legit class C drops in an unexpected place. Achingly beautiful views. Back-to-back fun drops, hyphenated by minimal creek walk. A few takeaways: 1. Currently flowing, not pushy. 2. Clipping the extended DCL R7 LAPAR anchor webbing around the small tree on DCR bank made a nice traverse while we used the courtesy-length equalized rope anchor for most of the group. The long webbing also reaches perfectly over the edge for a clean pull, safe from the greedy looking and notoriously rope jamming cracks. Shelves just past the edge made that (discourtesy?) easy-pull long webbing easier to rap from. Long webbing has a hint of fray where it hangs over edge, will need replacement soon. 3. Upvote for skipping R8. It’s an easy class 3 scramble bypass, and even on a Monday, there were multiple hikers literally just below that final 30’ falls. And the rockfall risk around here is real. We heard a couple non-small rocks tumble down the 230’ part just during our rope pull. A small rock went toward R8 while we changed our clothes. We yelled “ROCK!”to hikers below. They would have been less confused if we’d yelled “WE ARE NARWHALS!” 4. We were happy in everything from a 4/3 to a drysuit. Water was below knees all the time, except when falling all over us on rappel. No swimming or floating dismounts. Hooded rain jackets made the waterboardy bits much more fun. 5. The waterfall faces are pocked with hollow bits that swing in, but footing is surprisingly solid all the way down, due to heavy moss growth. 6. Decent approach trail all the way. Upvote pants recommendation for protection from abundant nettle. 7. It’s loud. Radios were awesome.

We replaced the webbing on the first rappel that is wrapped around a bunch of little sticks. Forgot my jacket and regretted it with the water pounding my head. Definite ice cream headache! Flow was a really fun level. A little pushy but not unmanageable. Perfect choice for such a hot day today.
very accessible canyon from river camp. Approach fairly straightforward but some route finding problems on top of approach once you ascend rock slide. Be mindful of stuck ropes on first rap
Very nice canyon. Did it right before the rain started, then went up to Mount Rainier for some idyllic stroll in the rain/snow.
We descended the middle section. It's a scenic/great little section that can be done in a few hours and will be good for Class C beginners when the flow is low/moderate low.

Gorgeous canyon!Water was great. We only descended the middle section. Flow appeared higher than we thought so we elected to rappel out of the water on Thunder Falls. Never did see the exit trail and bushwacked straight up a very steep hill to the road.
Ran the middle section. Replced the webbing on the two bolted raps. Flow was low enough that you could rap in the water course if you wanted.
Ran the middle section. All the anchors are in good shape. Sad to see the campsite has been decommissioned.

Combined nearby Panther Creek in MRNP with the middle section of Summit for an all-around fun day. We dropped in just above the bridge on the south side. Small shallow jump just below the bridge. Below this, there's a short creek walk before action begins. Some great geology and huge fractured blocks with deep pools between. Found a dumped computer that was full of rocks. Finale is rappelling down Thunder Falls which is spectacular and photogenic. Just below the final downclimb, look left for a mossy gully and climb up the user trail (steeep!) to the canyon rim.

Hangers at R2 and R3 have been almost completely flattened by seasonal flooding. I have not seen anything like it. Neither wiggled and we rappelled with no issues. We cut and packed out the old webbing for the bolt and knot chock at R3 DCR as both were in ragged condition and the quicklink was badly rusted. Next group that wants to do the DCR rap will need to bring materials to rebuild the anchor. The DCL rappel was fun and very photogenic.

We set a ghost anchor and dropped in just upstream of the Middle Section, R2. We skipped R1 because it's a downclimb and wanted to eliminate the creek walking. We jumped R2 and rappelled R3. Thunder Falls is one of my favorite waterfalls. Love it!! This is a great place to camp when running creeks as well.

Gorgeous route with great pay:play ratio. The group did two laps of the middle section from the campground. R2 bolts are still flattened, but seemingly sound (we replaced the webbing). If someone is passing nearby with a bolt kit, replacing R2 bolts would be a great (and not very time consuming) idea.
Did the middle section. It's lovely. R2 is a jump, I jumped from DCR. Anchors and webbing all in good condition. The thunder falls rap is spectacular
Checked out the flow after camping nearby. Very high still. Photos attached.

This is an excellent waterfall to rappel. My favorite way to run this creek is to walk down the road from the campground, exit the road on a trail just after the road bends to the left. Find a good tree and rappel line near the top of the elevation gain. I rappel into the canyon here. Swim around the corner and you will arrive at the 15 foot jump/rap. Descend that and you are at the top of Thunder Falls. The single strand of anchor webbing is bouncy, watch out it doesn't damage your rope. I'll replace it with a long quad strand when I return to reduce that bounce.
All anchors are still good
Fun, easy day to start the Rondy. Just did middle section. Used DCL option on last rappel, bolt on R2 is pretty smashed, but feels solid. Might be good to explore a new bolt placement. Jump was in.
Bone dry. Some spider webs. Pretty but short! Road gets bad, park your car before the sand gets deep. Don't drive down into the gully - there is no turnaround point in the sand!

Completely dry. Multiple dead, decomposing cows in the narrows (had to walk through the carcasses). Canyon was full of tumbleweeds. Approach and exit are highly exposed the entire way and the sandy roads slowed our travel. Ran out of water after having to share mine with the others in my group. Lots of misery, though the narrows were quite impressive…

Time includes a descent of Tango, hike down fish creek, visit to Micro, and ascent up Ladder. At least one mandatory swim in Tango with a number of deep but potentially avoidable pools depending on climbing ability. Wetsuits were not worn but would recommend them under these conditions. We replaced webbing at 4 rappels. Fish Creek was flowing high but even at its narrowest was not more than thigh depth.

Multiple anchors replaced with good webbing Rap 4 had webbing cut already and it was a bad anchor since the rock moved easily so converted to rabbit hole and worked out great Multiple drops are perfect for toggles Very little to no water all was easily avoidable by easy stemming or easy bridging 6 hours 45 minutes card a car with a very chill attitude Most of this canyon time is approach and exit Beautiful scenery Top notch equal to or better than Sierra

As with Sierra, being able to start and end from the pullout beyond the Fish Creek Bridge is much less work than the gulley from the vista pullout. Appreciated the nice new anchors all the way down. I don’t usually rush canyons, but today I had to start it at noon, and wanted to finish the hike in daylight, so I ran whenever the trail was flat, and got back to the truck by 6. First time through, and the kml made the nav easy. Minimal, easily avoided water all the way. Enjoyed the views in this fun slice of desert. The final rap is definitely the best of the canyon. Overall I’d put it just below Sierra.
No waterflow; pool water was cold! Lots of climbing along shelves to avoid deep pools. ~5 hours for Tank (car to Cibecue), 10.5 hours for both canyons
River up.
great intro canyon when rafting. Easy approach from camp stemming up a few chimney's. Low water, didn't get wet at all, except if you rap into river

Fun canyon, raps are good, and the pack raft is a nice section of the river. The hike out got interesting with sleet and cold temps. 3 stars for it s great scenery in sections yet it opened up a lot. I think this is a excellent car to car loop that most everyone should enjoy. 5 star trip with the variation of activities

We knocked out Tatahoysa in the same trip taking 1 day to go car to beach in Tatahatso, 1 day to float to Eminence break camping dry at the top of Tatahoysa, and 1 day to descend Tatahoysa, break camp, and continue the hike out Eminence Break to the car. Beautiful Redwall narrows, frigid swims, some of the most scenic packrafting, and an adventurous scramble on exit. A well rounded multisport outing.
great canyon from President Harding Camp! Solid approach trail and good raps. No water

We added this on to our Tatahatso itinerary. After packrafting from Tatahatso, we hiked up to the top of the Redwall and dry camped. The next day we left our camp and descended with day packs to the river, topped up on water, hiked up, broke camp, and continued up Eminence Break to the car. A fast and competent group could do this as a day trip from the rim and most attempting this from the river should have no trouble making it round trip boat to boat during daylight, even in the winter. The canyon is pleasant with some interesting arch features and some polished chutes. The final rap to the river above Harding was the highlight for me.

The logistics are very easy to do this canyon from a rafting trip. You can start and end at President Harding Camp, and the approach is on a trail. The canyon is completely dry, so no cold water. There is one rappel near the end that is drop-dead gorgeous.

First descent took us 8 hours car to car. Those running the canyon will likely complete it in a shorter amount of time. It would be nice it someone set a 2nd bolt for the stations. We dropped in around 2400' feet and the upper portion of the canyon were still covered in snow. The first part of the canyon is quite similar to Deadhorse Creek but with much lower flow.

Icy conditions - the first rappel was completed covered in ice. Ice fall and slippery footage adds additional risk/hazards. Surprisingly, the ledge for the 2nd rappel was NOT iced over when we went. An anchor has been installed from the beginning of ledge to get to the main anchor for added protections to either setup another line or handline. The canyon walls are lined with icicles which makes for some beautiful scenery. Unfortunately my car was broken into at the trailhead. The window was smashed and the theives stole my purse. Be sure to hide all valuable (which were hidden) and know that there's a risk of break-in.

What a stellar canyon! Excellent find, and thanks so much to those who bolted it. It's very well bolted. We did it with a 300ft rap line and a 200ft tag line. Probably could get away with just a 200ft rap line and 200 ft tag line, even on the first rap, especially if you just rapped off the first of the two bolts at the end of that rap. After that first rap, no need for anything over 200ft. Water was borderline warm in most parts. Felt fine even in the flow with just pants and a short sleeved shirt. On the way out, we just crossed the Taylor and bushwhacked a short ways to the Taylor River Trail. Highly recommend doing that at these low flows as opposed to bushwhacking on the left side of the Taylor from the bridge.

A great day out. Flows were perfect. June is prime time for Tesseract. Snow at the drop-in point, but none in the canyon itself. Updated many anchors and backed up single point anchors. The traverse line at the bottom of R1 is now redundant and makes the transition between R1 and R2 much more efficient. Long day, took our time and had a few issues, resulting in a late exit in the dark, but got out safe and sound.
A spectacular day on an amazing canyon. All of the anchors were in decent shape, 3 small logjams to climb over.

A spectacular canyon! The first three rappels are just incredible and feels like you are dropping into the bowels of the earth. 4" of rainfall over the past 72 hours (after an extended dry period) brought Tesseract into perfect condition for a fall run. All anchors are in great condition.

Entered below R3 due to big snow field in the box section. Will probably be a while before that melts out. ~6 hours car to car included some anchor work. Brief ice cold pools but most of the run is out of the water.

Hike up could use some flagging once you get away from the road to better define a trail. Drop into R4 involves two dry canyon Repels to get down. Very Pretty but froze with no sunlight due to North facing canyon. Anchors were well place and no issues with pulls. Water was higher than the trip the week before, used a couple handlines with our 2 newbies.

Really dig this canyon - just beautiful. All bolts are in good shape. Someone linked a bunch of the bolt stations - don't do that, folks! We unlinked the ones we used that shouldn't have been linked. Lots of loose rock - we knocked a ton off the first rap, then some came in from the canyon rim lower down (maybe goats?). Watch that loose rock! There are still some ice spires after R3 - so cool! They were sublimating and putting off cold fog, which was trippy.

A great day in an awe inspiring canyon, even in very low flow. So low the creek entirely disappeared underground in multiple points leaving the canyon dry at the junction with the Taylor River. The loose rock on R1/R2 is a serious issue, people should not wait around at the immediate bottom of the rap, instead keep moving down to R3. We had some near misses as the ropes were pulled. I think some of the bolts *are* meant to be linked - e.g. R6 (if I remember correctly) which is on old moss covered rock, vertically aligned, with a smaller quicklink on the upper hanger, and a fullsized quicklink on the lower. See discussion regarding this layout in Mineral Creek[1] [1] https://www.facebook.com/groups/pnwcanyoning/posts/10159518885585789/?comment_id=10159534099730789&reply_comment_id=10159535970035789 We exited by crossing the Taylor River and picking up the trail on the other side - much quicker than our approach along the river south bank. Will definitely be back when there's more water around!
Clear of snow but there is more avy debris than last year. Anchors are intact. Great rainy day canyon.

Sunny day after a week+ of rain. Canyon was flowing, but surprisingly not very high still. R3 was the highlight. All anchors are good (one of the two bolts at the R2 traverse line has taken a knock, but still functioning). Cold in the water & upper section, warmed up down below.

Fun day! Lower than we had expected given the snow pack. It was moderate-low for tessy… we were hoping for sporty. The canyon is slimier than usual, but it’s still a beautiful canyon! And the slot is just as great as always.

Time was significantly impacted by extra time taken to cross the river. This is my first time through the canyon and I don't have a reference point for staying on river right and avoiding the crossing, but we spent an extra hour finding a spot to cross both in/out of the canyon. We started late and ended late, but there were no significant obstacles or difficulties in the canyon. Last rappel was by headlamp. It was quite warm in neoprene on the first two raps and felt very good to get into the water. All in all, it was a fun day in the canyon.
Crossing the river on approach was a little pushy, made a tension diagonal to assist some of the team. Otherwise a straightforward day in low flow.

A great day out in Tessy. No avalanche snow in the narrows this season, all anchors appear in good condition minus one traverse line bolt at R2 that had previous damage. This bolt while bent, still seems solid in the rock. Flow was low but pleasant and the sunny warm weather made for a great day out.
All anchors in great shape. Lower flow than we hoped for, but still fun. More greenery than we'd ever experienced. Made for a gorgeous day, but also downclimbs were more slippery/spicy than usual.

A nice relaxing day in Tessy - in February! The approach/canyon were entirely clear of snow. Flows were very low, but it was nice to be out in some water with good people, and the canyon is quite beautiful! We doubled up all of the anchors and replaced most quicklinks with ring hangers. We also replaced the previously smashed-up traverse line anchor. Abrasion mitigation is a requirement in many sections. The middle/lower sections of the canyon are ludicrously slippery currently. Important Note: Tesseract is a terrain trap, so if you are doing an early season run, you should be tracking avalanche conditions and snow levels. Today there was little/no snow above us, and canyon was clear of snow.

Used this to access river to float down to Lees Ferry. No issues. Parked at 36.93765, -111.49654. It was 2.3 miles to the river from here. Crossed a fence at 36.92350, -111.49797. First pole is at 36.92027, -111.49247. All coords are WGS84.
First descent. A few swims, but wetsuit not required in hotter months.

Moderate flow through the canyon, ~11cfs in tonto creek, and surprisingly good flow through the exit drainage all the way back to the car. Replaced webbing on all major rappels. Tonto creek had flashed one week prior to this descent, with ~500cfs measured in Tonto near that location. This affected our descent and added substantial time as the creek bottom was still very muddy. Visibility was 1" below water surface at maximum, and boulders in the riverbed were covered in thick mud. Even 11cfs was irritating to fight swimming/slipping upstream in Tonto, do not attempt this canyon if flow is much higher than 30cfs in Tonto. Hazardous and slippery, taking almost 2 hours to get through the 1.5 mile swim up Tonto. R3 was relocated to a large tree roughly 15' back from the edge, adding ~30' total to the final pull as the original anchor (small tree near edge of R3) had been damaged to the point of failure by recent floods.

Wet day, so took the long way in to avoid bushwhacking. Suited up at the end of the logging grade and dropped into the creek right above R1. Pretty easy. Nice falls and fun half-day adventure at the coast. Replaced wonky anchor on R2. Easiest exit seems to be following the creek down until you can cut out to the logging grade on the right.
Had visited the base of the falls by hiking several years previous, but this was likely a first descent. A nice pleasant little canyon outing.
Good creek for high water. Easy to pick anchors to get as much flow as you want.
Fun run. Mostly avoided flow as it was bonkers.
This is a great creek. Short and sweet. Great pay to play if you have a shuttle. I'm trying to figure out how to update the beta to add the shuttle.

Be aware: there's a log down across the road near its end, so you have to park and walk in an extra 0.5mi if you're taking a shuttle. Hike down the road, drop onto the ridge, follow it to its end, and head straight downhill to reach R1. Several core shots have been reported on the main slot -- so this is a good candidate for future bolting - and we may add a redirect further down the slot to keep the rope away from a rope-eating notch (hidden under the water?). Delightful day out. Rigged a guided rappel on R3 just for fun.
Great day with awesome raps. Flow was fun, not too pushy but active. Rapped R2 2x by following goat trail back to top. Log has been removed from the road.

Road up is covered by a snowdrift at the bridge before the turnoff for the lower parking. Additionally, there is a large tree down on the road up to the lower parking. Hiked in through lots of drifts but no snow in the creek. Fun day as always.

Looks like the first trip this year. Three trees across the road about 2 miles from the drop in point. A chain saw would fix those pretty quickly. The road walk was easy. Anchors were in good shape except for the last drop. We replaced the webbing on the dry rappel there. Overall a great day at 3 corner!

We cut the trees and opened the road. R1 we dropped the main waterfall in the flow. There were many many loose rocks we cleaned off and kicked down along the way. Very crumbly basalt. R2 we went DCL at the bottom of the rappel in the rock formation. Right looked a little spicy as most of the flow was dumping in there. R3 we installed webbing on the boulder. Great day in 3CC!

Nice day out in 3 corners. R1 and R2 anchors good. R3 wet anchor from boulder in creek was already compromised despite new webbing being put in a few weeks ago - the little loop in the frost knot had a sizeable hole worn into it. Rather than waste another 10 feet of webbing that wont last until the next group we moved the quicklink to the main loop in the webbing and moved the knot to the backside of the boulder. Next group in should inspect this anchor carefully if using.

Water is getting pretty low but it's still a fun creek. More opportunities to play around in all the grooves on R2. Replaced the anchor on the low flow side of R1 to stay in the flow. Also replaced the anchor around the boulder in the center of the creek on R3.

Nice day out. Road to the top is clear and in great shape (there was still snow on the road mid April last year). Fun flow level, all rappels were quite manageable in the flow. Replaced the R3 webbing anchor at creek center, but I think this anchor would be better served with bolts on top of the boulder as the webbing just gets trashed in the flow. Looks like a few new large hunks of basalt came off the R3 cliff face this winter DCL but nothing in the rappel zone. R3 dry anchor looked to be in good shape tho we didn’t use it.
Great trip. Bolted up R1 and R3. I'll update the beta.
Great day in a beautiful creek. We ran laps on R2. New bolts on R3 are great!

Big water! Three Corner was raging in flood stage after a huge precip dump on the PNW. The creek is a different animal at this level with simple tasks like crossing the creek needing care & caution to avoid getting swept. Rappel lines are good from R1-alt, R2, and R3 (DCR). The creek center anchor on R3 was virtually underwater. Anchor R2 and R3 were damaged and needed replacement. At the bottom of R3 we crossed DCL going under the falls (awesome) and took a user trail along the DCL bank. Took the alt exit directly up the hill from the confluence which was a lot faster than staying in the creek. Bit of a user trail all the way out. Nice! Note for future teams: there are a bunch of old anchors around R1 that should probably be removed. 1) Looked like two anchors on trees DCL of the main drop on R1. 2) Anchor on old snag 50ft back on R1-alt.
There is snow on the road. Lots of water has been in the creek. Log jams have moved around.

Snow lvls currently above the creek. No snow in parking areas. Waterflow is really nice, enjoyable and not extreme. Water temps are cool but not cold, air temps in the early AM are chilly but by mid day is was really nice in creek. Anchors are solid and I flow raps are very manageable but still fun!
Beautiful canyon with three great rappels. Anchors were all solid. Love the smooth, rounded hangers. I considered putting some new webbing on R2, but it’s still solid.
Fantastic day in 3C, despite the overcast skies. Intermittent rain and sun - but the creek and the raps are great. All anchors in great shape. Awesome ending to the 2026 Mazamas Canyon class.

First known descent. We placed 3 bolted anchors and 1 natural anchor. Fun little canyon with some flow after the heavy rains this season. The approach and exit in kayak at 6500 cfs across the Kern River was exciting.

The first known descent of this canyon. I have kayaked and driven by this canyon countless times. Because of the record wet winter, this canyon was flowing. Easter weekend had above-average temperatures so we decided to check it out. Natural anchors were used. The R1 was from a bush on RDC 110ft, but there was too much drag to pull (the last person climbed around). R2 was a short rappel off a pine tree LDC 25ft to a second Pine LDC which was used for R4 100ft. R5 was from a bush RDC 30ft. This was a record-breaking runoff so the river was high. A 2 person Inflatable kayak was used to shuttle gear and people. Three kids were on this first decent. Aspen Hacker (15), Kyla Hacker (13) and Russell Hacker (9). Russell walked R3&R4.

The flows on the Kern were very low so there was not current at the pool used to cross. We shuttled people and gear with a inflatable paddle board. There was a small trickle of flow in canyon. We stayed mostly dry because of cool air temps. The surrounding hills were really green and the nearby mountains had snow which made for a nice backdrop.

There are many deep pools in the canyon, but we were able to avoid getting wet at all. Water conditions will change throughout the year, bring a wetsuit just in case. Bypassing one pool means a traverse through the brush and rappel 20 feet back down after the pool.

Followed the creek to the box and turned around when I saw a few spiders the size of my face. Stayed in the water most the way. Several deep wades and one particularly deep section that I opted to swim. Water temperature was very comfortable. I was the only one out there, definitely a low-trafficked spot. Nothing to write home about but might be worth checking out if you're looking for somewhere in the area to cool off.

Combined with Middle Tortilla and Peter's Cave. Rather lackluster this time of year due to minimal water, would consider returning in the spring. Some hellish bushwacking through cat claw and dense 10' tall reeds. Extra blood supply recommended.

Never put my harness on though I did use a handline to get down a little drop between boulders. This was at the big drop where I found somewhat crispy webbing around a tree canyon center but didn't use it. It's a pretty area but not really much for the canyoneer. The alt exit is long, brushy in places and on steeply angled and unstable ground. I.e. annoying.

Combined with Lower Tortilla and Peter's Cave, starting at Tortilla TH and ending at Tortilla Flat. Didn't need ropes or technical gear (will be updating rating from 3A to 2A). The first thought-provoking obstacle had a fixed handline in place on canyon right. Multiple deep pools which were easily avoided. Minimal brush in the middle section...but lower Tortilla was another story entirely.

Water in the canyon was flowing lightly, was surprisingly cold, wading to waist deep. Staying completely dry was not possible. Was able to climb around all the big pools to avoid swimming, the last bypass is the only real difficult one with a class 4 scramble. Water levels are probably uncharacteristically high. Hiked to big alcove near the end of the canyon on day one; 12.4 miles in 8.5 hours. Finished day two via whiskey spring and dutchman trails 7.7 miles in 6 hours.

Likely the only group in this canyon since the first descent in 2018. It was long but enjoyable with a couple fun rappels and took our time. Might be back someday with hopes of catching it in higher flow. Fairly easy creek walking. Found remnants of some anchors from 2018. Wrote the beta, and added a map. While the shuttle marked on the map is longer than the route to the north, I find the road in better shape.

Very pretty once you get into it but due to how short it is, the effort it takes to get there kind of overshadows it. We put in at Pipe Creek, floated to 91 Mile to camp (after swimming Horn). 2nd day we went up 91 and over into Trinity. Beautiful narrows in Trinity. Floated to 94 mile to camp (without swimming Granite). Floated to Hermit and hiked out Hermit Trail the 3rd day.

Time includes setting a fixed rope at Tufftatella, backtracking and running Nikon Draw, and then ascending out of Tufftatella at a fairly speedy pace. I'd recommend 2-3 pieces of rope protection for the fixed line on Tufftatella.

First descent took 6 hours so likely the canyon can be run in a shorter amount of time. Flow was pushy only if the rappel line was directly in a channeled section, otherwise, flow is probably considered moderate. Great canyon for early season and lots of rappels to make the trip worth while.

Fun addition to the Dark Divide. The creek is pretty action-packed from start to finish. Lots of trees down in the drainage. Creek must see extremely high water from time to time based on the amount of vegetative destruction at the bottom of the canyon.
Tule is a great easy little creek. R6 - We added a high anchor DCL. This splits the drop into R6a and R6b to avoid the sharp edge.

Brrhhhh.... very cold! Nice flow levels however. Group of 9 going at a relaxed pace took 6hrs car-to-car. Blow-down 0.4 miles from parking added a short bit of road walking. Added a single bolt somewhere around the middle of the canyon to protect mid-sized rampy rappel. We re-rigged R6 to go from a tree DCL (photo in beta), as the previously added overhead R6b anchor was now quite marginal. The new anchor location allows both stages to be descended at once without significant edge problems. A spotter can be to be placed on the lip to help facilitate communications down the whole pitch. We cleared the debris we could from the jump pool. One log remains submerged running the length of the pool. It can still be jumped using accurate flat-jumping technique.

Fun trip down a beautiful canyon. Impressive pile of logs and debris after the canyon narrows just before the exit. We removed the log from the J1 pool - watch for rock on jumper's left. Also recommend using upper anchor for first tier of R6 then transitioning to rope shown in picture to avoid a hard start and a collapsing bank.
A scenic canyon with chill vibes at moderate-low flow. At least four raps can be bypassed with down climbing. J1 pool was too low to jump, but did present an opportunity for controlled slides.
Decent flow at all falls but but nothing extreme. Really fun canyon and not super committing, just right for us as beginners!

2nd time running the route. The flow is not any lower than it was in mid-Aug. Donated a long quickdraw to the R2 redirect. Selecting "special challenges" due to the overall nature of the route (e.g. risky start to R2, every pool has a current which will happily snatch ropes & bags). However a fun evening trip in a beautiful canyon. Departed parking lot 3.30pm, back at 6.15pm.

Fun sunny afternoon/evening run. Lots of jumping, and some ascending to lap them. The currents were very manageable but still present. Did the slide on the main falls - that's quite something! The start of R2 is still the crux move (I'm not sure the flow here ever gets below the Mod-High reference photos). Improved the R2 redirect with a proper stainless quicklink & extended webbing, and removed the corroded quick draw.

The R2 redirect was still in place from last season although a little worn. Bringing a brush to clean the rock face near the redirect may prove to be helpful with the footing for all except the first rappeller. Our team opted for the dry line on R3. The waterflow link showed double the cfs today compared to 8/22/23 on all gauges except the SF SNOQUALMIE RIVER AB ALICE CREEK NR GARCIA, WA meter which was about the same as then - mid 20's cfs. Visually, it only looked a little higher today. Pay special attention to the final rappel rigging. The rope pull on this can be difficult due to the spacing between the bolts relative to the rock (see photo). This anchor should be adjusted possibly by simply adding another quicklink to just one of the bolts. Overall, this was a fun mid-afternoon canyon.

The Alice Creek gauge was reading 25cfs. Time in canyon was 2 hours. None in our group could make it to the slide on R4, too slippery on the very mossy rock. We did 6 jumps from 5 to 20 feet. Would like to come in lower flow in order to do R3 in the flow and to have a better chance of getting over to the slide. Also would like to do mid-day when sun is hitting the canyon. Excellent anchor placement throughout, Kudos to the development team.

Awesome day in Twin Falls! Time includes running the whole route twice! We skipped the redirect on R2 for the second lap (and we short rope + jumped R3 each time). Getting to R4 was a relatively trivial swim exit. This route is amazing! We would highly recommend running it twice for maximum fun. We were also unable to traverse to the slide despite a lot of effort... Very, very slippery.

Fun afternoon jaunt through Twin Falls. Team found water features/hazards (ex. Pourovers) very manageable/the water was not very pushy, but the team consisted of generally strong swimmers so future teams should reflect on the own comfortability. Interestingly, the water was surprisingly warm. The webbing at R1 has some minor fraying so future teams could consider replacing depending on comfortability. The quick links at R2 were pretty rusted so again, opportunity for replacement. Overall, a very laid back and pleasant trip with deep pools, a few raps, a few jumps, and some swimming. None of us used the redirect on R2.

First time through this canyon and it’s a stunner. A lot of water moving through this canyon, but very manageable with a little knowledge as long as you don’t put yourself in a bad situation. Thought we could avoid the crowds by going early on a Monday, but we had a surprisingly large audience from the bridge and viewpoint. Good reminder to keep a low profile, use whistles sparingly and try not to attract attention that would encourage casual attempts to access the top of the falls. The spinner bolt at R3 pulled out of the rock with only a little force and we found it was only secured in an unstable flake…yikes. Anchors could use some work.
Thanks to whomever put the new rings / anchors in. It made the pulls buttery smooth. Pulled R4 from the bottom of R4 - as opposed to from across the water.

Amazing day in Twin Falls! Bolts are quite bad at R2. Hangers are flat to the wall, one bolt is jiggling, the other is 1 cm exposed on one side. Both hangers are spinning (sleeved bolts, we did not take photos unfortunately). We did not bring equipment to fix it on this trip but it would be hard/impossible to fix without new holes and bolts. The redirect carabiner has been ripped out of the webbing. We re-tied the webbing shorter and did not leave hardware. The next group should bring webbing and a biner for the redirect. Bolts for R3 are good, webbing is maybe a bit bleached. Gauge was 58 CFS. Conditions were substantially higher than Moderate-High photos. Overall a great day!

Only 4 stars considering the short approach/exit, canyon length, and pothole fun. Couple were keepers but basic escape gear will suffice (every rappel and pothole is passable). Did it early in the morning, so chilly with wind but great romp once sun came out. No wet suits needed. Great quarter day descent if in the swell or passing through.

Probably should have replaced some webbing, but I backed up the ones that were the worst and then downclimbed/slid. There were some deep pools and with the overcast and windy conditions, we were a little chilled in canyon. Very pretty rocks, and was a good way spend a couple of hours before dinner.

Pot holes were mostly full of water. This canyon is a great introductory canyon for new people. Very easy to bail if needed as the canyon has easy spots to climb out at almost every obstacle you find. Follow the KLM map on here to get the easiest spots to cross the barbed wire fence.

Completed this canyon with (4) others, (2) of which we randomly ran into at the TH. Ascent follows a well traveled trail, you then cut off and gain the ridge. We took a straight up approach which was steep but stable. Follow the ridge around to the left and find the drainage. Descent starts on steep loose grade. Careful foot placement. Soon you hit the bare rock and the two rappels were easy and fun. Second rappel needs a 150 ft rope, don't pull rope at the first bottom. You need to go around while still on belay and complete the final 40 ft vertical drop before getting off rope. Third rappel was done with a meat anchor and downclimb. Some fun downclimbs wrapped up the technical descent. Exit is same as approach. Beautiful rocks to view and discuss while exiting back to TH.

Thanks to the FD crew for scouting the way. Hiked up in the drizzle, but it soon cleared off by the time we began our descent. Quite a bit of walking along the sides of the creek, but the waterfalls are a joy to rappel. The big drop is super fun. We didn't have a shuttle, so exited after the big falls on FR 610. Yellow gate is currently open, but it would be best not to drive in as it could be closed without warning at any time.

Finally saw that the dam opened up for vehicle traffic and the small burn from the Labor Day 2020 fires was accessible. Special thanks to the Detroit legend, Richard Horner for the heads up. Karl H was able to hike the way up a weekend prior so props to him for saving us some time. The creek is action packed with many cave like undercut drops that all look similar besides the big drop. This canyon can get very high in the spring so be mindful when looking at it. As a spring run, it’s a 4 because the amount of raps, the big drop, and the relatively low elevation. Not many options like it exist.

We were surprised no one seems to run this creek—it was a great experience and felt unique compared to others in the area. The approach is big but manageable: a steady climb for about 1.75 miles before it levels off. Not terrible. When we arrived, the gate was open, but it was closed by the time we got back to the car. We ran into some forest workers at the drop-in who gave us the classic “you’re doing what?” look. Most of the descent involves walking along the sides of the creek, weaving through vine maples and alder. It’s not bad, though it does feel a little long in spots. The flow was low, which worked well for our first time through. I’d love to come back when it’s running a bit higher. We replaced anchors where needed and trimmed back some branches to clear the path. Recommend creeping the rope—there are some sharp edges that could bite if you're not careful. We ran the entire creek all the way to the lake. Warm, sunny weather made the day even better. A farmer John was the perfect layer.

Great creek. Flow is probably 2x what it was when we ran it last year. Flow was fine but would like more flow. I'm not sure it gets too high to be dangerous. It starts out with a creek walk DCL. Stay high on the side of the hill cause the creek is messy with sticks. The 3 raps are great and then there is a long messy creek walk. The big rap makes the creek worth it. Most of the creek went DCR in the flow. Reports were they liked it. I went DCL and that was a great line as well. Not as much flow but its straight and open so you can drop fast. We continued past the road and did the last couple of raps. The first one is the best. Second one not really worth it. Its fun to run it all the way to the lake so I would do that again.

Dropped in around 2200 feet elevation. This put us just above the big drop. Most raps before the committing section are a nuisance but the canyon is delightful and now that the road is fixed, truck or suv recommended. All bolts are good and still are singles so the next team should plan to add more. Flow was fun.

This is a great canyon! A 4WD with higher clearance is needed to clear one portion of the road otherwise the dirt road is in good condition. The total is not indicative of a "regular" canyoning trip as the team took extra time to find an approach route and set bolts. The water is surprisingly cold even with warmer air temperatures so a dry suit is recommended. There are some rappels where you will rappel directly in the full force of the water. With the current water level, the crux move requires a little stemming and dropping directly in the flow (with an overhang/minimal visibility) so canyoners should be comfortable with that move.

Got to work for it a bit, but a great little canyon! Tip of the hat to the bolting teams -- very nice. Water levels seem to have diminished over the last several weeks. Get a good power-wash to the face on some of the rappels, but otherwise pretty friendly. Was all psyched up for a terrible thrash out and was pleasantly surprised at how nice it was. Jumped on the old logging grade to get back up to the trucks.

Lovely pretty canyon with beautiful waterfalls, some fun sporty flow, a couple of short jumps. Got to see a salamander at the very end. It is not done very often, but it definitely deserves more love. Anchors were in great condition. We got to do some fun problem solving in the lower section, which have some short drops but no fixed anchor material. The approach sucks a bit, beware of hornets. Exit went pretty smoothly

We did an "after" season trip on our way to Gifford-Pinchot. Low water and late day made for extremely underwhelming conditions. Although I still argue it's an overrated canyon. Rap lengths seem to be overstated by a bit, but we were hurrying and didn't measure. I wasn't overly looking to jump, but jumps didn't seem as plentiful and/or as obvious as they are made to sound. Jumper beware. Descent is a bit unpleasant, but fortunately short.

Canyon flow was very low, but still R1 2 and 3 were fun. Water levels this low would make those 2 technical jumps even more precise... Pretty sure the exit is no longer marked as mentions in a previous trip report. The trek down to the creek is no too bad but the exit I chose was quite steep and littered with downed trees. The creek walking is abundant but def has some great little areas to cool off in or just appreciate. YT vid link shared on the main page.

Canyon was fun, although flowing higher than expected. Made dry line rappels on the first and crux rappels, and had to protect for the downclimb. Flow seemed to be diverted more heavily DCR around chockstone rappel. Log jam may have built up more on DCL side. Also created a hydraulic in the pool that pulled back toward falls on DCR side in pool at the bottom.

Nice day out before flow is too low to be fun. We noticed several small siphons; in higher flow special care should be taken as the siphons were located directly below where the anchors are placed. From the top of R3, the rappel looked scarier than it turned out to be. However, special care should still be taken and canyoneers should be confident rappelling in flow at moderate-high or higher levels (avoiding the water was not possible at the moderate-high flow).

Opening this canyon was fun, a very easy run with some quick fire fun rappels. Should be combined with Galena next door for a full day of cold water splash zone action. The last 3 drops had to be done on one rap due to the lack of natural anchors present at the final 70ft waterfall known as washout falls. This canyon would be much more attractive to do if bolts were installed for the last rap and then this could be run with a 70m rope. Def best to run this in winter or spring when flow is mod high or high, it can be run quite safely.

The Supes are super green right now with a lite flow in the canyon. The one spot I saw webbing was super old and unusable. I was able to find bypass down climbs in the boulder jams to avoid needing to rappel. 🤷‍♂️

Let me just say this now: the hike out was long! 9 miles along the Paria River after the technical section made for a long day. We pulled rope at 2 and were done after 6. I'd do it this way again though and shuttle back to the approach instead of obtaining permits and hauling camping gear through Water Pockets. The canyon itself was amazing. Scenic, narrow, fun downclimbs, one small avoidable pool deep in the canyon, and the big rap at the end. The pot holes were easily escapable with partner assists. All anchors were rebuilt, replaced, or backed up with new webbing. Water was flowing at the river but very silty. Fun but long day! Definitely not in R mode. Climb out or easy partner assist out of pot holes. A few narrow tight, dark sections. Did not have to use any special gear like pot shot or sandtrap. Recommend bringing plenty of webbing. About 30' of webbing if replacing last anchor. At least 30-50' feet if replacing rest of anchors. Lots of stemming and downclimbing opportunities. You are welcome, Arizona.
With a larger group that already posted but wanted to add some photos

On the big rappel our rope got cut. Not on the top ledge, which we protected. Not on the second ledge. But on the third lip, it seems some rock recently detached leaving an edge at the last place the rope touches before free-hanging. Recommend protecting top edge and third edge.

Stunningly gorgeous, fun, and engaging! Some of the prettiest sections of slot Ive seen, some parts even prettier than Water Holes! We dropped our shuttle vehicle night before at Lee’s Ferry. Almost 2hrs from Lee’s to drive to camp at the top. The dirt road was long but not bad, high clearance recommended for the last portion. Started hiking from camp at 6:38am. Approach was about 45mins, fairly easy. Tip toe around the crust, down-climbing on loose terrain. The canyon had mosquitos and some of the most putrid, horrible smelling pothole water Ive ever experienced. Still totally worth it!! There are alot of North Wash style sections requiring stemming, spicy moves, elevator downclimbs and moving through very tight sections of slot. We replaced and rebuilt a few anchors. We didnt need our sandtrap or pothole escape tools. All potholes had water but were able to be climbed out of with minimal effort. The water temp was very bearable and I am glad I did not bring a wetsuit. The final rappel has new webbing, but it will probably crisp up quick as it is in direct sun. Roughly 30’ would be needed to replace it. We protected 3 edges with rope pro. Top edge, 2nd ledge, and the edge right before it goes free hanging. Hike out is 9 miles and took us 3.5hrs. The trail came and went, crossing the Paria river periodically. It rained on us while on the exit, making for lots of muddy sand to hike though. Definitely a long tough slough. Overall, it’s an incredible canyon, very worthwhile, a must do for the advanced canyoneer!

Another incredible run through Pockets! Water was much cleaner this time and didnt stink of death! No flies! Keepers not in play yet. All anchors in good shape. Final anchor not deteriorating as fast as I first anticipated but one should still come prepared with 30’ to replace if needed… Wore a 3/2 and felt good about that decision! We did not use rope pro on the big drop and my 8mm Slyther did not suffer any damage. I feel that the edges are not as bad as described but smooth rappelling is key, know your friction settings, do not bounce or feed the rope and you’ll be fine. We took our time to soak up the beauty of this canyon. Truly a special and worthwhile trip!

Back again! 30min approach, 5hr canyon, 3.5hr exit. Water was cleanish and low, multiple wades up to chest deep, 1 brief swim, no wetsuit needed, neo booties were nice. Elbow pads and knee pads are nice to bring for the tight section. Fixed or replaced a few anchors. Many anchors are natural, Challenging to inspect and easily washed out (rock chocks nestled down in the crack) The webbing monstrosity is still holding strong at the last rap, still havent core shot my rope here… fantastic run as always, a top notch slot!

Group of 4 ages 16-45. When we first descended this, we couldn't find any beta on the rappels other than a YouTube video. The approach wasn't bad with a bit of bushwhacking. For the first rappel we slung a chockstone in the water way. We weren't sure how big the second rappel was and whether we would need to do it in multiple stages. We brought plenty of rope for that scenario, but our 300-foot rope reached perfectly to the ground with about 10 feet left over. There are two bolts there at the top of the waterfall with a lot of sun-bleached webbing. We placed our own webbing as well that we used. Some slight issues with having someone rappel with one of the pull cords which extended the trip and the last persons rappel significantly. Not a serious issue and could easily have been avoided with better communication. The pull was easy with the way the webbing hangs over the edge and didn't cause us any trouble. No wetsuits for this trip although it was pretty chilly. The sun didn't hit the waterfall at all while we were on it. Moving quickly, you can avoid being in the water for too long.

Partly cloudy sky's, variable winds 10-15mph, water temp 56F +/- Air temp 67F +/- LATE START & REBUILDING ANCHORS Small team and a long day. Start early because its a very long shuttle on unpaved roads (conditions will vary) and if you choose to do the top down (recommended) =you get about 5 more rappels than if you did the bottom up approach (horrible/not worth doing IMO). We actually followed a grader that was raking the road to the drop in which slowed our approach time but made for a relatively smooth ride. 4x4 is not necessary but high clearance will help for sure. A savvy driver could carefully get up there but low slung vehicles are not recommended. The Start of the canyon is straight forward and offers multiple drops that are dry ranging from 50'-15' and some down climbs. Dawn the wet suites when you come to the slide section (obviously larger pools and polished rock present at this section) Many of the anchors should be checked and monitored and proper rappeling techniques and rope protection used. There are many sharp edges and a few avoidable pendulum issues to consider. Many of the anchors we came across showed signs of critters eating them, UV damage and simply bad anchor building from whom ever built them. There was a lot of unexpected scrambling and down climbing through a huge bolder field and lots of creaking that wasn't mentioned in any beta we found (updated on rope wiki). The canyon is definitely a fun adventure filled with lots of wading, swimming, jumping, sliding and a butt load of creaking. Bring head lamps and be prepared for a night creek adventure if you have a large group and plan on playing in the canyon! We were all pretty warm with 4/3mm wet suits but i would also recommend bringing splash jackets to help with evaporative cooling. I chose to wear 2mm shorts w/ 2mm top under my 4/3mm wet suit because i was in the water a lot! We saw a very cool looking Ring Tail on the way out at night and lots of lightening and some light rain on our exit which put the help conclude our canyon. Enjoy! by the way careful on some of these slides because you may get spanked!

Dirt road was decent for SUVs & Jeeps, 9 hours on the trail, add 3hrs to deal with shuttle + 1-2hrs for drive to camp, start early & plan for a late day & clothing/snacks for the exit if waiting for the return shuttle, rappels are fun and come in quick succession, middles slog was hot and sunny, main slides were deep, some slides repeat as jumps, a 8' jump had a 12" window needing perfect spot to jump in to avoid rocks, a few nice swimmer narrows, nice teacup series of pools, wish the slide/jump sections were longer.
Final 80 or so foot rappel can be done as a moderate downclimb DCR.

Water is very low, but still flowing; the slides are still sliceable (and some are absolutely fantastic, steep and into very deep water), but make sure you aim and bend your knees on some, since a few others are pretty shallow. There are a lot of places where, if not directly in the watercourse, the water is stagnant and filled with bright green scum. You can avoid these with some careful climbing. All in all, very low-key, no water challenges except checking depths of a few pools to make sure you slide into the right place. Don't bother with the "stay-dry" game once you reach water, though; you'll miss all the fun. When you get to the good slides, definitely take the time to do laps on them.
Full canyon. Replaced webbing on 2nd to last rappel. Water level great. Saw a few snakes.

Low flow in waterslide section. Algae beginning to bloom, but mostly avoidable. Slide/jump pools chilly but pleasant due air temps around 85. If anyone finds my Maui Jims at the bottom of the first slide pool, I'd like them back ;)

water levels low, but slides are still deep. probably not for long. very enjoyable route. as mentioned, green algae starting to develop. all anchors looked bomber. enjoyed the shuttle making for an instant approach, and straightforward exit.

Great flow! After an extremely dry winter, canyon flow was perfect. Not really class C, but the slides were flowing and the pools were overflowing! Anchors in great shape, but still take some webbing and a few quicklinks. Water very cold, even with 5 mm, booties and 80F sunshine. One of the most fun canyons in Arizona. Whoever gave this canyon <5 stars is harsh. By chance, hitchhiking out, we met Gary and Katherine, two folks who pioneered the first descent with a guy named Darrell around 2005. Shout out, thanks for picking us up!
Plenty of water for a super fun day of slides & deep cold pools! I had on a 5/4 wetsuit & paddling jacket to help with evaporative cooling & was comfortable all day.

Waterflow was down to a trickle but still enough to grease the slides and keep the pools clean. Not much algae in the upper section, but lots in the lower section after the waterpark. It took us 2 hours from car to 1st slide (including suit-up time). We replaced a couple anchors in the upper technical section; all anchors are in good condition. There are handlines and anchors set up above each slide for anyone who prefers a rope assist. We spent a lot of time playing in the waterpark. Lots of deep pools, lots of big jumps. Take time to redo the slides and find the good jumps... it really makes for a ridiculously fun day. We were in 4/3 or 5/4/3 suits and neoprene socks and were just fine. On the exit, recommend staying in the wash and keeping an eye on the GPS. We were weaving through the brush and accidentally started following a side wash for a while.

What a great canyon! Just right in full body 4/3 wetsuits. Algae getting heavy, but still plenty of clear water for pool landings. Replaced crispy webbing and rusty rapids 2nd to last rap. Took dirt bikes in a pickup, which made shuttling more fun. https://youtu.be/3hzJsuPj_Uk

Lower trailhead is 2WD-friendly. Upper trailhead needs HC or 4x4 and should be driven slowly near the top. No water at the confluence, but we soon picked up light flow below the swamp-walk. Pools were deep enough to slide which was great, but may not be for very much longer. Encountered some bizarre insect wildlife on the way out including a scary giant Sonoran centipede -- largest centipede in North America... apparently venomous. It was about a foot long.

Full route, 7.5h shuttle-to-shuttle moving at a moderate pace. About 30 minute drive from shuttle to upper parking / head of the canyon. Upper parking should be accessible for most stock 4x4s. Wore a 5mm wetsuit, 3mm socks, and windproof pants and jacket. Core was mostly warm but hands and feet were quite cold in sections. Partner was much more content in his 7mm wetsuit, thicker neoprene socks, and neoprene gloves. Measured water temperature in one of the deeper pools. It was approximately swim-like-your-life-depends-on-it degrees.

We did the full route which was 8 or so rappels on top of the numerous slides. Replaced and rebuilt some anchors. Most of the raps were kind of lack-luster in my opinion. The scenery is decent with a few neat spots but doesn't hold a candle to some of the other canyons nearby. The one thing this canyon does have is lots of HUGE nearly vertical slides that end in deep swimming pools. So if big slides and jumps are your thing, in the right conditions, this is the canyon for you!

Had a blast here. Water level was enough to make all the slides and jumps a lot of fun still. Some algae is growing in the pools, but it's not too bad yet. We moved along pretty well until one member of our group rolled an ankle before the slides. That slowed us down quite a bit, but we were happy with 8 hours from top to bottom given the circumstances. While there are more scenic and technical canyons, I haven't done a canyon that's more fun with the slides and jumps. The anchors were all in good shape. There were several rappels we down-climbed or jumped, but we still did 7 or 8, I think. Overall, it was a great time!

Mellow day in the canyon. Took the bottom-up approach to avoid shuttle. Stayed on the ridge north of Hackberry Spring, which made for an easy 2 hours to the top of the first slide. All slides flowing with minimal algae in pools. Some pools seemed slightly shallower than last year, but no incidents to report. Jumped the big slide. Air was warm but water very cold in more shaded sections. Six hours car to car including delay for Gila monster viewing.

Light flow. Water is warming up - 4/3 wetsuit was very comfortable. Set a shuttle and started from the top. 5.5h car-to-car. Encountered two other large groups in the canyon who kindly let us play through.

Light flow, slides were great. Set up a shuttle car. Was able to get a Ford escape up the shuttle road with some care. Upper section was just okay, next time would try bottom up and cut off the annoying shuttle.

Upper canyon fun but long boulder hoping before slide section kinda sucks. Low flow so all pools full, mild algae in all the jump/slide pools, comfortable in 2mm shorty. Shuttle route best suited for trucks. Anchor for final rap is UP CANYON from a mandatory jump pool - make sure you scout the anchor before sending the whole team down the jump. Pretty decent cow trail along the exit, RDC.

Water was flowing, not very high, but still in fun mode. There is a trail to the mouth of the canyon at the moment DCR if you hate rock hopping in the creek. There is quite a bit of catsclaw on it, though, but some of it is avoidable. The water was pretty cold for me, even in a 3/2 wetsuit with a skin under. It was tolerable, except when the wind kicked up. There is a handline on one of the drops, but it doesn't reach far enough to safely enter the pool. We rerigged the anchor here as it was on a rock that rolled in the pinch.

Replaced a few anchors. Canyon was flowing from the start and is in fun mode! Beginning rappels gave us a good showering. We started at 9:30 and got to the waterslides section when the sun was behind the canyon wall would recommend leaving earlier so that part is in the sun. Many of the steeper "water slide"sections have optional anchors for those nervous too commit to a deep plunge.
Still flowing but probably getting low soon. Slides and jumps were super fun. Enjoy

I love to ascend Weavers Needle...then sort of canyoneer back down:). I have free climbed it 3xs no problem. Mostly just 10-20 foot ascents with ample holds. Problem is the peripheral view where it seems like a 400' free fall. 120' static line used to suffice but someone cut and removed the huge antique eye bolts so now a 200' is needed--or 100' w 100' pull cord...if I remember right. Down climbing is harder in my opinion, so the rope descent is great. But I have seen anchored climbers and a free hand down climber on diff trips.

4 stars and special challenges because it’s a really unique day out if you’re mostly a canyoneer like me. Climb-wise, the west side chimney approach is super easy. Big holds all the way. There are exposed segments where a fall would be potentially fatal. Our team had a real climber with the skills, a dynamic rope and a few trad pieces to let the rest of us top-rope up. Rappelling back down was easy and fun.

canyon: Weeping Springs and Bighorn Canyons Loop
Weeping Springs and Bighorn Canyons Loop
comment: Some nice rappels on the way down Weeping Springs with an excellent little packraft connection to Bighorn, which was a scenic and pleasant way to exit. All anchors were present and in good condition, with the exception of the final rappel into the river, which was non-existent. We rigged one to a tree near the edge. Blew up our rafts at the top of the last rappel; this is recommended since the flat section of the rock ledge at the base is not very large and is prone to a lot of spray from the waterfall. Water levels in the Colorado/Lake Mead were very low.

canyon: Weeping Springs and Bighorn Canyons Loop
comment: Awesome day in the canyon. All anchors were in great condition, and kudos to the team before us who set up the triple-redundant knot chock near the end. We skipped the final rappel into the Colorado and traversed the ridgeline above climbing and rappelling down to the mouth of Bighorn Canyon. Saw three bighorn sheep on the approach and collected golf balls on the exit making for a couple of notable end caps to the day.

canyon: Weeping Springs and Bighorn Canyons Loop
comment: All anchors are in good condition, although the last (into the river) is noticeably older. I had a bit of unexpected rain I waited out in a perfectly-placed cave a couple rappels from the bottom. For the last rappel, it was my first time rappelling with a packraft so that was a bit awkward, there is a good landing to get into the water but it is a little hard to get to since the fall line wants to swing you back into the mini-waterfall. I ended up getting a little wet while getting into the raft due to that mini-waterfall. Paddling the river is beautiful ... too short! One climb up through Bighorn Canyon is pretty stiff! Ended up stepping on my huge pack to help me up a bit.

canyon: Weeping Springs and Bighorn Canyons Loop
comment: All anchors are in good condition, although one towards the end of the canyon took a little looking to spot it hidden way up to the right. First chance either of us had to rappel with a pack raft. Awesome experience. Nice short float down to the mouth of Bighorn Canyon. Got quite tired trudging back up to the car with more gear than we're used to, but a great experience overall.

canyon: Weeping Springs and Bighorn Canyons Loop
comment: This time is for an alternate route with a car spot (to enjoy a longer packraft) exiting up Crane's Nest wash. All anchors were present; replaced webbing in several places. Water levels are now so low that there's a nice beach at the bottom of the last rap where you can blow up your raft. Packraft took us about 1 hour (~2.5 miles) from Weeping Springs to Crane's Nest Wash.

canyon: Weeping Springs and Bighorn Canyons Loop
comment: Replaced a few anchors, all others were in good shape. Did the loop as part of a river trip, so our travel time doesn't include the hike to the parking lot.

canyon: Weeping Springs and Bighorn Canyons Loop
comment: The air temperature was ideal. We started around 7am with a quick decent through the rocky slope. The water level in Colorado river was perfect for a rappel with inflatable boat directly into the water. We took the exit of via Big Horn Canyon. It is overgrown in places, but still really pretty.

canyon: Weeping Springs and Bighorn Canyons Loop
comment: Webbing was pretty good with the exception of the difficult-to-get-to anchor high RDC (through the hole). This likely needs replacing soon. We didn't do the last rap to the river though we went down to check it out. We rapped out of a mini-canyon to get off of the bypass cliffs but after we got down we saw how we could have downclimbed a crack closer to Bighorn drainage. We found the biggest climb on Bighorn pretty intimidating but were able to traverse RUC fairly easily.

canyon: Weeping Springs and Bighorn Canyons Loop
comment: Weeping Springs and Bighorn are an epic combination. Two very different canyons, each amazing in its own way. The approach road is in good condition, reachable by passenger vehicles with some care through the rocky portions. There's a little water in some pools towards the lower part of Weeping Springs, but we found it all avoidable. The anchors are (now) in good condition, including a few cairn anchors which we converted from "pile of rocks" style to "Death Valley" style. We did not like either of the obvious downclimbs to traverse into Bighorn Canyon, so we sought out a decent rappel point above the large crack on the Bighorn side. There was an improvised anchor there, which we also had to fix up. Going up Bighorn was wet and strenuously brushy at first, then delightful as the canyon narrowed and obstacles began to appear. None were particularly challenging for our group except the last one, which we were able to solve by sending a climber up RUC and having him meat anchor or top belay others. The final climb out to the parking lot was easier up a lava rock field than it was down the use trail through loose rock. All in all, a highly enjoyable day.

canyon: Weeping Springs and Bighorn Canyons Loop
comment: Probably last run through weeping springs for the summer. All the anchors in the primary part of the canyon have new looking webbing and all cairn anchors are solid. We packrafted to cranes nest by way of the free hanging rappel into weeping springs cove. That’s an epic rappel for sure. Almost no water left in the canyon. Car to river took us 3.5 hours, paddling to cranes nest took 2 hours with no wind.

canyon: Weeping Springs and Bighorn Canyons Loop
comment: This was our first time running this canyon. After hearing all of the horrible stories of exiting up Bighorn Canyon through the thick overgrowth, we decided to scramble back up a route just north of Weeping Springs Canyon. We studied the terrain on Google Earth and felt that it was going to work. Well, it sure did, and it was quick and easy. It was Class 2 scrambling all the way back to the Emerald Pools Canyon and the turn off to Weeping Springs. The route starts LDC, right after the two stage rappel before the plateau area. This would be the second to last rappel if you were going all the way to the river. You don't have to go any farther down canyon. Climb up out of the canyon on an obvious ramp to get started on the exit. It was just over 1/2 a mile to the top of Weeping Springs and with only 600 feet of elevation gain. With route finding, cairn building (for future users) it took just over 1-1/2 hrs to get back to the car at the trailhead. The whole trip was only 4-1/2 hrs. Definitely saved the torture of bushwhacking through Bighorn Canyon.

canyon: Weeping Springs and Bighorn Canyons Loop
comment: Down Weeping Springs and packrafted to Crane's Nest. Air and water temps were great. Anchors mostly new but the last 2. Paddle was all slack water, since Lake Mojave was 4ft below full pool. Fought some wind on the paddle and sun set on us on the river. We were able to park at the bottom of Crane Flat Wash for just a 1/4mi hike out.

canyon: Weeping Springs and Bighorn Canyons Loop
comment: We ran Weeping Springs as the second canyon of the day. We ran Death Pit first and used the class 2 exit route, and the connector route, to put us at rap 1 of Weeping Springs to run it too. The time was 6.5 hours to run BOTH canyons and get back to the TH. Running both canyons added about 2 hours to the time of just running one canyon. Well worth the extra 2 hours and 600 feet of elevation gain to run the two together. The connector route is on the Death Pit map page.

canyon: Weeping Springs and Bighorn Canyons Loop
comment: We were in town for work and chose this as a quick afternoon adventure after landing. Started around noon and it was hot! The canyon was super fun with back-to-back rappels. We brought plenty of water and took lots of shade breaks. A great way to kick off our first day in Vegas. We only ran Weeping Springs and headed back up the car.

canyon: Weeping Springs and Bighorn Canyons Loop
comment: Fantastic canyon. Going up Bighorn was a nice treat after a fun decent, but the first riparian section has a lot of catclaw bushes - group leader brought a handsaw and that was helpful. Very overgrown in that first part of the exit. Consider bringing a sweatshirt layer to help save your fancy sunshirt and skin. We replaced several anchors with new webbing (some of the anchors had UV fading).

canyon: Weeping Springs and Bighorn Canyons Loop
comment: 1 hour approach, 2 hours to last rappel, 1.5 hours to rappel and packraft out, half hour of rest, and 2.5 hours to exit out Bighorn back to car. There is webbing on the intermediate ledge for the 110' rappel as of this report, so only used 2 ropes for the 150' rappel. Lots of growth on the traverse to the last rappel and when starting the Bighorn exit, and lots of velcro/cat's claw plants ready to grab you. No beach at the bottom of the last rappel, so I inflated the packraft at the anchors, but the packraft was short, scenic, and quiet! For Bighorn, I followed the map and took the middle fork when greeted with 3 choices, and that one left me to the spicy 5.8 climb. You can traverse the right side of it pretty easily. I didn't notice any other spicy upclimb, but there were a lot of scrambling to get through the narrow sections.

While scoping this canyon from the top we discovered a major tree fall blocking FS road 5410 just past cat creek. There are two Logs 4 - 5 feet in diameter crossing the road preventing any further motorized travel. This adds about 1.75 miles of walking to the drop in point of Whale creek. We did not do the canyon.

This canyon is impressive - hands down. There are a couple big drops, an enormous 275 rappel,and a narrow chute at the end which jets the water out over a free-hanging rappel. We ran the canyon in miserable conditions where the sun never showed up and it misted/drizzled most of the time. The 275 rappel should not be taken lightly. Despite padding two friction points, we still managed to core shot the rope as there was a third friction point that you don't see until much too late. Setting up a guided rappel would not be a bad idea on this drop. We set up most drops in this canyon except the last one. In the last drop, the water funnel into a narrows section and it should not be taken lightly. Road access is open. Despite all pre-cautious, this canyon is definitely a 5 star canyon and I would happily run it again...when it's sunny.

Bit of a thrash to get in and get out, but some quality (and big!) rappels. Water levels were about perfect today. Didn't bring a 300ft rope, so we ended out doing a dry brushy two-stage rappel immediately DCL. Beware getting your rope stuck on this one. Need to be on your game on the big drops. The last chute where the creek funnels up and horsetails out into open space will get your attention. Long shuttle; possibly best with a vehicle you don't love too much. High clearance recommended.

Good conditions. Big rap is 230' not 270'. Current anchor placement seems to avoid most sharp edges. I cleaned up the first edge to be much more rope friendly- but still exercise caution. Last rap with the rooster tail is no problem. Definitely do NOT need 4x4 for this road.

I attempted to access whale creek by following a game trail up the ridgeline south of whale creek. This ended where it was too steep to continue at 2200 feet. Going more north is impossible. I explored around to the south but did not find a path. The top of the ridgeline is 2800 feet so there was still a ways to go. Back to the drawing board!

It took our group 3.5 hours to complete the sneak route and lower canyon from a base camp very close to the Whispering Falls sneak route. We built an anchor from under a large boulder in the watercourse for the big drop as the only anchor present was the old bolts on the right which takes you completely out of the amazing waterfall rappel - to my mind defeating the entire point of dropping this short section of canyon. The rappel was right around 235 feet from this anchor - the pool at the bottom was waist deep at most. After the canyon descent we made our way up Kanab Creek and Scotties Hollow to get above the chockstone climbs in the redwall for a total of a 10 hour day.
What an incredible place!

Most amazing spot on our rafting trip. Late afternoon light at bottom of whispering falls is just unreal. Pool is deep and rocks have been pushed out from past years. Plan to spend some extra time here taking it all in

3h total for the sneak route, starting from camp in Kanab Creek a short distance upstream of the mouth of the canyon. Anchors were in fine condition. Pool at the base of the falls was a swimmer. It was refreshing, given the warm air temps.
Such an amazing oasis. The water in the pool at base of 250' rappel was clear, cool and clean. We played, jumped and swam.

There were some shallow pools scattered throughout the canyon, though the water could easily be avoided. Highlights were the short narrow section of white rock (from which the canyon must acquire its name) and one fairly large pool that was fed by a small waterfall which has carved a thin, smooth chute in the rock. We went about 1.3 miles up canyon before turning around and heading back the way we came. Though mostly dry and completely nontechnical, I enjoyed this canyon for the scenery and seclusion and will likely be returning.

Hiked the Putvin Trail and dropped into Whitehorse at the toe of the avalanche field. Our time may be a bit longer as every drop had to be assessed (we found all of two anchors from previous parties). Toward the bottom we were getting a bit tired, but it would be fun to set more anchors to run the flow on the lower drops, especially the final section below Lower Whitehorse Falls. Next time need to look at the feasibility of dropping into the creek from the decommissioned road. This would shorten up the descent and allow more time to enjoy the best falls towards the bottom of the creek. Flow was strong/pushy in the narrow sections. Most drops, however, had the option to rappel down the edge of the flow so you could avoid getting blasted. We're probably a little early for Whitehorse; it'll probably be more fun in a couple weeks as water levels continue to drop. There are a of couple deep pools, but no real swimmers on the creek. No serious water hazards were encountered. Bonus note: Just east of the bridge where the Hamma Hamma Rd crosses Whitehorse is a campsite with an enormous old cedar stump. It's ideally placed for canyon teams exiting Whitehorse after a long day.

Returning to Whitehorse this year, we scouted out the lower entrance just off the old logging road. This is a much better approach (it drops you in just above R9) and improves the creek's pay-to-play ratio significantly. Last time we were pretty tired by the time we got to the big rappels.
