We spent some time establishing anchors and installed two bolt stations on R15 and R16 (both outside the wilderness boundary) allowing you to run the flow. Other groups will probably be faster. This canyon has everything you could want: great access, good water, and fun rappels. If camping in the area, try to grab Big Stump Camp (a dispersed campsite marked on the map). This allows you to exit the creek and have a leisurely stroll down the road and right into your campsite. Outstanding!
Ran the main section. Flow was low but still a great time with a couple beginners. Replaced webbing at several anchors and ended up linking R11 and R12.
Ran the main. Flow was low but still great day with a couple beginners. Replaced webbing at several anchors and ended up linking R11 and R12.

Whitehorse full run is an amazing, action-packed creek with almost all rappels coming back-to-back in quick succession. Expect a high-energy descent with very little downtime. The current beta was significantly off—many rappel lengths were understated, which led to some logistical challenges. We took notes but didn’t get everything dialed, so expect discrepancies. Because we based our gear off the existing beta, we didn’t bring many long ropes and ended up short-roped several times, waiting at stations for the long rope to catch up. Most anchors needed to be rebuilt. We used approximately 100 feet of webbing and were starting to worry about running out. Where possible, we ghosted anchors to conserve material. Water wasn’t particularly cold. Air temps reached around 70°F. I ran it comfortably in a 4/3 Farmer John with a rain jacket. While most nearby creeks are running on the moderate-low side, Whitehorse was definitely moderate-high—stronger flow than expected given the conditions in the area. Bring extra webbing, I updated the beta closer to what we found the raps to be. Killer creek with great flow, but be prepared.
Ran the full canyon as outlined in rope wiki. Flow has tailed off now as the snow has mostly melted from up high on the mountains. Still a great run and a fun time in the water!
Fun canyon. Anchors are ok but some needs thicker webbing. Canyon could do with an extra bolt or webbing anchor to avoid a dry rappel dcr. We just did the lower section.

Enjoyable canyon. Not quite as good as Fulton. Starts out a little boring, gets better towards the end. Webbing is in decent condition at all of the anchors. There is a paper wasp nest DCL at the downclimb before the R15 anchor, so treat that with respect.

Water was avoided by stemming. Drainage was a bit brushy in sections. Some fun downclimb challenges but be cautious of loose rocks assuming the identity of secure handholds. Replaced webbing at both anchors. Approach roads were dry and not too rough, but expect some desert pin-striping courtesy of the local catclaw community.
Some fun downclimbing neat short slotted bit at end.

Followed the road up, then took a brushy track south across the lesser trib and dropped into Wieberg. Rappelled another 20ft drop before reaching the rappels in the beta. Nice early season creek. Second rappel needs another bolt to complete the station. The last rappel was super fun. Bit surprising a falls of this height doesn't have a trail leading to it.
Sunny day, 25F at the start. Cut out an anchor station on last rap due to it being tied to a dead looking alder.

Great day at Weiberg. Ice was a bit much and the temps were higher than forecasted so we needed to kick out a few large pieces to make it safe. Rigged R3 from a tree more DCR than the current one for the big drop. Ice was most significant on the last drop and the height at which it could fall led to the new anchor. Old anchor was left alone.
Good flow. Reset anchor DCL on R3 (due to a partial cut or hungry animal). All other anchors are in good shape.
Super fun creek, flow was perfect!
Fun Creek with great rappels. Bushwacked in to R2. Great day! All anchors in good shape.

The creek was great. Sunny day, warm weather, no better place to be. We didn't damage any ropes. There is a sharp spot at the top of R4 kinda where you go over the edge. We creeped and didn't have any problems.

Super fun day! Flow was great! The rappel lines are setup perfectly to either be in or out of the flow. We lowered the rope after each person to prevent rope damage. We removed the webbing from the bolts and would like to keep them unlinked. Pull R3 from across the creek where you have a line of sight on the anchor.
the flow dropped a lot in the last 5 days. Still no rope damage. I think the core shots in the beta were from bouncing on rappel.
Great day in the creek.

All anchors are solid, sharp rock and tough rope pulls make this one interesting. Current water lvls are impressive with snow in the area melting. The final rappel is stunning and eye opening! In lower flow this would not be worth the effort in my opinion! Massive core shot occurred on R3 that happend during the rope retrieval.

Nice leisurely day in Wieberg with good flow and a fun team. We did all four raps and replaced the webbing on R1 and placed it higher on the tree. No core shots to report, though we did creep the rope at R4. Unfortunately there is a lot of marking tape along the lower creek that seems to indicate there may be logging in the near future?

I feel this full featured route is one of the best in the Lake Mead Area! Starting with a long and engaging 4th class approach with some incredible, sweeping panoramic views of the entire area. The route funnels down into a dramatic chasem with alot of downclimbs and multiple large, quality rappels. Lots of unique natural features make this a very memorable route! We shuttled from BoyScout to Gold Strike. Driving to BoyScout TH is doable with Subaru Crosstrek or similar AWD as there are small obstacles that require some care to negotiate and a hill climb out. Once leaving Gold Strike Trail, the approach definitely requires some route finding. We trusted our gut and the topo, working our way around multiple gullys and along ridges toward the coordinates; we didnt have any issue. We replaced alot of webbing and rebuilt a few anchors to be closer to cliff edges with rock pinches. There is one chest deep pool that is gross and unavoidable. The hotspring area is large, snaking around corners with calcite formations everywhere. The natural hotshower alcove is a nice memorable feature! Unfortunately, no pools to soak in. We It took us alomst 6 hours to arrive at the beach where we aired up our rafts and paddled 1000ft down to BoyScout. We spent some time at the BoyScout Hotsprings then tackled the 4th class scramble exit at night. Having done it before, I had route down and an idea of what to expect. There are a few sections on this exit that are low 5th class, namely a 20' chimney climb 3/4 of the way up that is the crux. There is a spot to top rope belay others up from a cammed position. I rather enjoy this engaging exit but I recommend being a confident climber/scrambler and if you havent done it before, route finding would be challenging at night. Definitely a route to put on the top of your list if you are an experienced canyoneer visiting the area!

We approached from Boy Scout TH, going up this side canyon (35.9798, -114.7540) and around the top of "Bolt Me Daddy." **Be aware that there is a cliff band to descend shortly after passing "Bolt Me Daddy that I do not recommend for anyone inexperienced (or possibly for those with experience). There is another sequence of multiple steep/semi difficult DCs descending to Wild Side. The canyon itself has very little noteworthy. The scenery is unique, but not particularly beautiful. The pool at our R4 was pretty easy to avoid while on rappel. The "shower room" near the end was unique but not hot water (if you'd even want to risk it with the amoebas?) Can confirm 120' rap length WITH the webbing extended (about 20' total webbing) to near the edge. The exit from Boy Scout has a bit of route finding and has some fairly legit scrambling. Definitely NOT a boyscout route...

We did this canyon by accident. We were looking at Gaia and Google Earth before attempting, not realizing it's an already established canyon. The rappels were worthy. We were able to skirt around the "swimmer pothole" down canyon left/climbers right. All webbings were in good condition. Bottom/grotto part is beautiful. We had staged a car at AZ to raft out of due to N to S wind 10mph that day. If there were no wind or going opposite direction, we would have exit Goldstrike to avoid having to stage shuttle.
Bolts have been added for both rappels. Single bolt for first rappel. Two bolts for second rappel.

First rappel is closer to 60 feet. Used a fiddle stick with no issues. Bolts are in good condition, but lots of rope grooves at the first rappel. Webbing at last rappel is starting to look discolored but is backed up with cord on the first bolt.
Some shallow but avoidable water. Lots of sand along the walls which made things a bit more interesting. R2 webbing was in good condition. Combined with Cat's Cradle and SHP Canyon.
Night run. All water avoidable very easily. Anchors look good.

Solo canyoneer 2.5 hrs car to car (took a few photos with hoodoos). Few muddy spots. Did not expect a quarter mile of straight up stemming/chimneying up and down so that was interesting and more physically demanding than expected. Enjoyed the parts where the slot opens up and had to drop down like a cat; appreciate the soft sand landing. Bolt anchors were good. Webbing in the last rappel in good condition.
Lots of low penalty fun. Great practice for stemming and bridging. Slightly easier than east of state line. Would be a great warm up for other routes nearby.

Quick, fun route, about as easy as it gets for a no-shuttle loop. We camped near the parking point the night before - beautiful area. Both raps were cool for 50-footers. The unusually prolonged stemming was intense enough to be fun, without becoming exhausting or intimidating. Made a great wrap-up before driving home after doing Shinumo and Waterholes the preceding days.

Great stemmy slot! Actually fairly pretty too! We drove to the top and dropped in from there. Highclearence 4x4 recommended for the trail to the top. There is a bonus freehang alcove rap at the head of the drainage that is about 80ft. There is a bolted anchor In place. We spent 30mins and ran laps on this drop as you can easily scramble back up to the top. The slot has great holds and is very fun to move through, great to teach stemming to beginners. It does have a few open sections throughout. Hike back up to the truck was an easy stair stepper on slab. Loved the giant mushroom hoodoos as a bonus! Total 2 hours with a beginner stemmer at a leisurely pace. Rappel lengths in beta were grossly inaccurate… beta fixed!

Great trip! Some water was flowing over the falls but I was able to stay out of pools and only got my feet & legs wet. The hike up and out to the seven cataracts vista pullout was unpleasantly steep and loose.
Very slippery ice on approach.
Perfect weather today! Good adventure shared with friends.

Water was nice, but breeze outside water made it a little cool. Last pool swim is pretty cool. Might want a rain jacket to be a little more comfortable. Low flow. Will definitely get fully wet at 80' rappel and pool swim on last rappel.

Great day with high flow. Completed all raps but definitely caution those without the experience in class c and rigging accordingly to consider an alternative or a day without as much flow. Last rap into grotto was intense. :) Fun day seeing the whole canyon covered in water.

Multiple great waterfall raps and a fantastic swimming hole accessible with little effort! When we got down into the canyon, waterflow was fairly high and brown. Canyon seemed to be in borderline flash flood conditions so we took caution. The first few raps we rigged just outside the watercourse but as we made our way down we started rigging in the watercourse without issue. I decided to forgo the wetsuit this time, wore a rain jacket, was comfortable enough but started getting chilly near the end. Hike out was a breeze. Took our time, definitely a fun day.
very low flow, 3/4 kept feet dry. minor bushwacking on approach and exit. anchors in good shape.

Water was cold and flowing well from recent rains. We did 5 raps and skipped the last 2, exiting on the nice social trail that goes to the grotto. Everything is bolted now and webbing was looking good.

Strong flow through a couple of falls. Didn't find R6 off the tree, must have DC'ed. Last pool is a swimmer. Never wore wetsuit, really cold through flowing raps and swims however. On approach, I went right of Nancy's Thumb, lost all semblance of trail, and descended through hell to reach canyon costing time. My ascent to overlook was rather heinous as well and went relatively straight up, occasionally on climber's trails.
Great canyon for the area when flowing! Wasnt cranking as much as I thought it’d be. All anchors in good shape. We exited at the grotto and skipped the last 2 drops. Worth doing for a short day.

We entered the canyon from a pull off 1/2 a mile up from windy point which makes canyon entrance quicker. Lots of fall colors which made the canyon beautiful. Took 4 newbies. Everyone had a lot of fun. All anchors were in good shape. Grotto water level was still full. Hike out of the canyon is a scramble in a lot of spots. Is a great option for people wanting to get into canyoneering.

All anchors that were found in great shape. Add alt anchor on rap 1 RDC around a tree to avoid the pool. Couldn't locate anchors for raps 6 & 7. I ghosted rap 6 around a tree LDC. Rap 7 I used a big stone LDC, I think i threw 80ft of rope..... this is a very temporary anchor. It will be gone in the event of heavy water flow. Use at your own risk. I'm 165lbs and held me no problem...

Small trickle for most of the waterfalls / rappels. Grotto is full and lovely. What I believe to be R6 has an easy walkaround. For R7, I did not find a rock pinch RDC, but instead a small slung rock *LDC*. This rock moved slightly while pulling on the anchor. Instead, we used a tree located on a ledge about 10' above this LDC rock - a much better anchor in my opinion. Existing webbing suggests others have used the tree. Exited by climbing directly out of the canyon up to Seven Cataracts parking area: definitely a loose, steep scramble.

Time reflects car park, hitching a ride, and running the canyon. R6 is by passable, found out on accident, no webbing on tree so heads up for folks. R7 can be alternatively rigged off a tree but requires pulling through dirt, not ideal. Overall fun canyon, limited geology but still a sweet short jaunt.
A pleasant diversion on a rainy day, although you'll spend more time hiking than in the creek. Lots of luxuriant ferns in the area. Surprisingly easy access to the top.

Some beautiful waterfalls - particularly the big one - but expect a fairly high pay-to-play. Lots of walking between falls. The lower end of the creek is pretty interesting: swept with lots of exposed bedrock.
No evidence of previous descent in canyon, nor can I find any info of the canyon online. So probable first descent. Ghosted canyon due to being unfamilar with ONP policy on leaving webbing.

Wore a full 5mm wetsuit as the water was cold and this canyon requires numerous swims. There was old webbing around a chockstone at the only rappel. We didn't use this (nor did we bring a rope) and were able to avoid this obstacle downclimbing LDC of the watercourse. Several deep wades and swimming sequences - particular one long, slotted section. The switchbacks along the exit are very overgrown but the trail is easy enough to follow. Movement along the creek was slow. With a big group this would probably be a full day, and you may consider an overnight for a more enjoyable experience.

Rugged and beautiful. 3h approach, 3.5h descent, 4.5h exit. Replaced / rebuilt webbing and anchors at 6 out of 7 rappels. R7 pull was tough and it likely would have been smoother rigging high from a tree. Had an up-close encounter with a very curious coatimundi on the walk out. The exit was an absolute beat down and everything hurts.

Downclimbed first rap, did just 3 raps with first rappel being the 165'. Replaced webbing on rappels 2 and 3. Had a light water flow with heavy rains the day before. Pools never got more than shin deep. Very scenic and interesting hike, with a short canyon.

Replaced R1, R2, and R4 webbing. Some very light flow through the canyon. Didn’t bother trying to keep feet dry as we needed to cross fish creek several times along the exit (which was still flowing quite nicely after recent rains). Very beautiful scenery. Time reflects a relaxed pace.
We were shocked by the beauty of this canyon, which seemed more characteristic of the Cascade canyons than a coast range canyon. Reminiscent of the East fork of Sardine Creek in many ways.

Gate was closed, so we had to hike in and out the extra mile along the road. Doesn't look like anyone's visited the creek in an age, so we replaced all anchors. R2 and R3 are excellent at current water levels -- although water this year may be higher than a typical year. Surprisingly grippy rock. Looks like someone has been camping at the bottom of R2. Followed game trails out on the way out below R4. Steep but doable. Would be good to establish a trail out in future.
Mid 20˚ at night, mid 60˚ and sunny during the day. Bowline is not an acceptable knot for tying onto a pack-toss.
Slotty and stemmy, but couldn't reach the higher potholes without extra challenges. A fun climby challenge with a desert hike.

We hiked in with no gear. Approach is hot and somewhat exposed. Immediately entering the canyon the water was waist to chest deep. It tightened up making for some fun stemming. We ended at the last pothole and hiked back out. great little route.

Nice flow - that might be everything positive I have to say about this one. Let me be clear: this is not a traditional canyoneering route, but simply a sequence of rappels down a big wall. The canyon itself is awful and many groups get ropes stuck on the rappel sequence. I had not been alerted to the fact that nobody goes through the actual drainage to get to the rappel sequence and so made the mistake of going through the horribly brushy, uninteresting canyon - don't make the same mistake. Had a very difficult time pulling the second stage anchor - took at least 1/2 hour to get it. I'll never understand local interest in this route.

First time back to Fish Creek since the landslides closed the Apache Trail. We hiked the short cut which was the orange line on my Gaia avoiding bushwhacking in the other longer route. We had a beginner in our group so it was fun to practice and take our time. There is an established climbing route and chain anchors for each rap, all in good shape. There was a small stream of water from the recent rain. We used a shuttle with one car at the rest area and the other at the trail head. It was sad to see all the landslides and damage as we walked up the road. It was easy to walk through and around the boulders and took less than an hour to walk up to the rest area.

4 bolt / chain rappels. New bolts in the last 3 years. improved trail in with small cairns to keep from trampling the hill side. Pull on first rappel requires due diligence to not cross ropes on last one down, failed and core shot one rope. All good. Hike out is a steady 1.6 mile to shuttle parking lot. Did this twice with in 4 days

Easy approach followed by some cool rappels with awesome views. As you leave forest road 213 stay high and work you way down at the last minute. This will avoid serious bush waking. FYI the third rap is chains DCR not the red webbing. The exit is now hiking up Fish creek hill to the rest area at the top. I recommend having a shuttle vehicle to get back to the trailhead. The road between the lookout and T/H was super busy. Not a destination canyon, but a great one to get some practice.

With the road reopened and shuttling back on the menu, this was a fun, quick day out on the cliffs. Even dry, with its easy approach and easier exit, it’s a decent intro to big drops. R1 triple unlinked bolts/chains are in a great line to pull from. It’s a little airy to rig, but no big deal if station manager has a tether. We used a 300’ rope and 300’ toggle/cord to combine R1 and R2. Used a shorter rope for R3 and R4, but could have combined them too.
Nice trickle from recent rains. Solid chain anchors all the way. Completely staying out of the canyon till basically at the edge is the way to minimize catclaw interactions.

This is a cool and easy rap canyon. If you are looking for some large easily accessible raps this is the one for you. Every anchor is set in a no fall zone so have someone comfortable on ledges that can access anchors. Pro tip, once leaving access trail stay on the ridge to the left of drainage, we did so and had pretty much no bushwacking.
